titleEnglish: Autonomous Agent as a Partner or Enemy in the Co-Production of Public Services: Concerns, Preferences and Artefact Design
abstract: Research on the role and implications of artificial intelligence (AI) in the delivery of public services is evolving rapidly, bringing us closer to what experts call ‘zero bureaucracy’ and ‘intelligent government’. While public administrators are still learning how to ‘dance’ with generative AI, augmenting, automating, or collaborating with it, the arrival of agentic AI – or AI agent – is posing new challenges and opportunities for theory development and practice. AI agent – with its abilities to make independent decisions and self-learn to achieve goals without constantly being told what to do – opens a Pandora’s box of its roles, as either partner or enemy, in co-production and co-creation between citizens and government in public service delivery. As autonomous AI agent becomes gradually integrated into public services, questions arise regarding what its features are deemed valuable to citizens as a ‘partner’ in co-production and co-design. Critically, by having AI agent as a partner, can it actually work to co-create value with citizens as suggested in the literature? We seek to understand how autonomous AI agents may play a role as a partner in the co-production and co-design of public services. As an AI agent has unique characteristics, it is timely to evaluate what technical-values attributes are deemed valuable to citizens as they co-produce and co-design. Taking this a step further, and inspired by the premise of design science, we seek to actualize value co-creation by prototyping and evaluating a real autonomous AI system that draws upon attributes that are important to citizens. Using two complementary studies, we will first conduct a pre-registered discrete choice experiment (DCE) with citizens to examine the relative importance of five major technical-values attributes of AI agent and then use in-depth interviews to probe into citizens’ concerns and preferences. Next, we will design an autonomous AI agent for citizens to report problems and offer suggestions to local (district) governments – Hero Citizen App – and evaluate its efficacy using pre- and post-test design and couple it with in-depth interviews. The content of citizens reporting will be analyzed using topic modeling, corpus linguistics, and inductive coding methods. This proposal aims to make a new breakthrough in the substantive theory of digital transformation and co-production in public administration while attempting to actualize value co-creation with the local (district) service ecosystem to inspire new knowledge creation. N/A

titleEnglish: 香港華商總會的社會角色與政治參與(1925-1941)
abstract: 本計劃以文獻搜集、史料分析為基礎，從社會參與、政商協作、粵港/政商跨界活動以至身份認同等不同議題，研究華商總會於戰前香港的各種活動與發展歷程。自省港大罷工爆發後，華商在中英交惡困境下透過粵港人事網絡和交際手腕，協助粵港雙方解決經濟及政治危機。華總要員佔據社會要津，多被粵港政權體制吸納以助當局改善管治，成為溝通中港的橋樑。但是，1930年代的中國名義上統一，各派勢力爭鬥不休。廣東執政者如走馬燈般來去匆匆，加上全球經濟大蕭條以及接踵而至的日本侵華國難，都為香港政經發展帶來不少挑戰，華總於此不穩定局面下既為港府解決經濟難題，也為中國政府承擔抗日後勤支援的政治角色，值得關注。它未如南洋的商會般因為雙重效忠遭受打壓，所涉及的香港地緣政治、華商的在地殖民協作以及家國、身份認同等複雜問題也是值得研究。要之，研究華總參與在地社會建設、國家建構的角色以及「雙重效忠」的課題，有助我們開拓更深層、更多元的香港史研究。 N/A

titleEnglish: Evaluating and Training Large Language Models for Their Cantonese Abilities
coInvestigators: Prof LAI, Yan Ki Jackie Prof Wang, Rui
abstract: Cantonese, a language spoken by over 85 million people worldwide, is being left behind in the current AI revolution. While Large Language Models (LLMs)—the technology behind tools like ChatGPT—are transforming society, there are no high-quality, publicly available models that truly understand Cantonese. This is reflected in a stark research gap: for every 30 academic papers on German language AI, there is only one on Cantonese. This digital inequity means Cantonese-speaking communities, particularly in Hong Kong, lack access to reliable and culturally aware AI tools for education, work, and daily life. This project will systematically solve this problem. First, we will create two foundational resources: 1) a comprehensive exam or evaluation benchmark with 34,000 questions to accurately measure an AI's proficiency in Cantonese, covering everything from everyday slang and cultural nuances to its unique sentence structures; and 2) the largest collection of high-quality Cantonese text of 100 billion tokens for training new AI models. Second, we will use this benchmark to produce a public white paper on the Cantonese abilities of existing major LLMs. Finally, we will train a suite of more than 30 monolingual and multilingual Cantonese LLMs through a series of well-controlled experiments. All datasets and models will be open-sourced under a suitable license. The impact of the project will be twofold. For Hong Kong and the global Cantonese community, it will deliver a new generation of powerful, open-source AI models that are capable and reliable in Cantonese. This will empower developers to build truly useful Cantonese-aware applications. More broadly, our methodical experiments will create a roadmap for developing AI in thousands of other low-resource languages currently neglected by technology. Thus this research will not only secure a vibrant future for Cantonese in the digital world, but also advance the creation of more inclusive and equitable AI in the world. N/A

titleEnglish: Beijing and the Global Imagination: Memory, Mapping, Translation, 1912–1949
abstract: China’s Republican era (1912–1949) was a period of transformation for Beijing’s built environment and its international image. While Shanghai’s visible modernity, iconic status, and cosmopolitan treaty-port status have attracted both popular and scholarly attention, historical studies of Beijing during this period are relatively less common. Drawing on English-language and translingual sources and archives, the project examines how genres such as travel writing, maps, guidebooks, and novels position Beijing in the global imagination as a city poised between old and new worlds, where memory, modernity, and power intersect. This project will be organized around three related investigations: (1) George N. Kates’s unpublished papers and his memoir "The Years That Were Fat" (1952), which reveal an embodied, preservationist practice attentive to routes and routines, courtyard typologies, furniture, and the everyday infrastructures of Republican Beijing; (2) maps and tourist guidebooks including L. C. Arlington and William Lewisohn’s "In Search of Old Peking" (1935), Frank Dorn’s “Map and History of Peiping” (1936), and related archival materials, which demonstrate how cartographic and artistic practices framed Beijing as both a navigable spectacle and a site of cultural memory; and (3) novels by authors including Harold Acton, Lin Yutang, and Lao She, which dramatize and translate everyday life within old worlds and newly charted urban spaces. Treating these sources as situated mediations rather than transparent windows, the project links practices of memorializing, mapping, and storytelling to broader geopolitical tensions, cultural anxieties, and imagined futures. Through an interdisciplinary method that engages with multimodal and popular texts, literary works, and historical materials, the project will produce three peer-reviewed articles that contribute to urban cultural history, critical heritage studies, and transnational literary studies. N/A

titleEnglish: Why Robotic Journalists Backfire: How Authorship, Transparency, and Platform Reach Shape Bias Perceptions and Responses to AI-Synthesized News
coInvestigators: Dr Zhu, Yuner
abstract: While five finalists in the 2024 Pulitzer Prize for Journalism acknowledged using artificial intelligence (AI) in their work and Bloomberg’s Cyborg system generates thousands of articles within milliseconds, a paradox emerges from this “AI-turn” in journalism: do users perceive AI-synthesized news as more or less biased than human journalism? Competing arguments emerge: users may view AI as objective due to its rule-based operation and freedom from human prejudices, or conversely, as suspect due to algorithmic opacity and its inability to exercise contextual judgment. In politically polarized environments, even balanced reporting can be perceived as biased—a phenomenon known as hostile media perception (HMP)—which leads to news disengagement, selective avoidance, and diminished trust in the broader media environment. This project examines public perceptions of and responses to AI-synthesized news—AI-powered news curation applications—from socio-psychological and journalism studies perspectives. We examine how source-level (authorship: human vs. AI vs. human-AI hybrid), message-level (editorial transparency disclosure: evidence-based sourcing citations vs. process-based fairness claims), and system-level (platform reach: established commercial application vs. student coursework prototype) factors shape bias perception as stipulated by HMP (Objective 1). Informed by human-computer interaction and self-categorization theory, we explore how this news–perception pathway is moderated by three techno-political dispositions—machine heuristics (mental shortcuts about algorithmic objectivity versus inflexibility), affective polarization (in-group solidarity and out-group animosity), and technological nationalism (belief in one’s nation’s technological superiority)—that condition audience responses to AI-authored content (Objective 2). We also examine how such hostile perception predicts several attitudinal and behavioral outcomes, including news engagement, platform trust, and policy attitudes (Objective 3). To ground this inquiry in high-stakes environments, this project implements two population-based online experiments in the United States and Hong Kong, using U.S. federal AI regulation (Study 1, U.S. sample) and U.S. semiconductor export controls on (Study 2, U.S. and Hong Kong samples) as test cases. Both topics provide ecological validity through their sustained media coverage, concrete economic impacts, and geopolitical importance. The findings aim to advance theories in media effects, political psychology, and human-AI interaction (Objective 4), and offer evidence-based insights for media organizations and policymakers (Objective 5). For practitioners, we identify transparency and platform positioning strategies that build credibility. For policymakers, our findings foster democratic media innovation, aligning with the UN Sustainable Development Goals to promote peace, justice, strong institutions, and responsible innovation. N/A

titleEnglish: Transnational Literary Encounters: Stella Benson, Modernism, and Asia
abstract: With increasing global movement, Hong Kong’s literary culture in the early 20th century developed through exchanges that linked local writers with those in mainland China and the wider global networks. However, Sinophone scholarship has often focused on local and ‘Southbound’ Chinese writers during the interwar period, while Anglophone scholarship tends to foreground literature published after the Second World War, especially works responding to the 1997 handover. Few studies have investigated the various forms of literary connections, including modernist networks, that existed between Hong Kong, other Asian cities, and Britain in the 1920s and 1930s. To fill this gap and provide a richer understanding of modernism and early 20th-century literary history in Hong Kong and Asia, this project will examine the creative works and literary connections of a largely forgotten modernist writer, Stella Benson (1892–1933). Born in England, Benson lived in mainland China, Hong Kong, and other parts of Asia from 1921 until her death in 1933. While this study will be author-driven, its broader aims will be as follows: (1) To investigate Benson’s literary innovation and the cross-cultural dimensions of modernist aesthetics by examining how her works were shaped by her experiences in Asia. (2) To shed new light on the literary history of Hong Kong and Asia in the 1920s and 1930s by situating Benson and other Anglophone writers within a transnational literary landscape involving Sinophone and Anglophone networks and encounters. Examining Benson’s connections with writers and artists (including Bella Sidney Woolf, Mei Lanfang, and Virginia Woolf) while in Hong Kong will reveal how cosmopolitan Hong Kong shaped and enabled cross-cultural literary exchanges. The project will draw on Benson’s unpublished diaries (all 41 diaries written between 1902 to 1933 are housed in the University of Cambridge’s Special Collections), focusing on entries about her life in Asia; materials in the archives of the Helena May Institute, where she worked as a librarian when she was in Hong Kong; and her publications in periodicals such as Shanghai’s North China Daily News, Hong Kong’s South China Morning Post, and London’s Time and Tide. This examination will yield new knowledge of Benson’s literary aesthetics, her critical engagement with topics including alienation, uneven development, cosmopolitanism and its limits, and the various transnational literary networks that were forged during the interwar period. N/A

titleEnglish: Living China’s First Imperial Transition: An Urban Biography of the Nan Commandery City
abstract: This project reconstructs the material remains of the Nan Commandery city at Yingcheng to produce an urban biography spanning the third to early first centuries BCE—a period that witnessed China’s first imperial transition but remains insufficiently understood at the local level. Located in the Jiang–Han Plain of present-day Hubei Province, Yingcheng was established in 278 BCE as the southernmost Qin administrative center following the conquest of Chu, the dominant state in South China. It was later incorporated into the Han Empire upon the region’s reconquest in 202 BCE. Among the limited number of Qin–Han commandery headquarters discovered to date, Yingcheng is the most extensively excavated, offering an exceptional case study for understanding local society at the crossroads of imperial expansion. Designed primarily as an archaeological study, this project undertakes a comprehensive analysis of two interrelated bodies of evidence, integrating both published sources and original fieldwork: the settlement remains at and around the Nan Commandery city of Yingcheng, and the contemporaneous tombs surrounding the Yingcheng site. Supported in part by spatial and quantitative methods, the project develops four complementary analytical frameworks derived from landscape and urban archaeology: cityscape, mundanescape, necroscape, and objectscape. While heuristic and exploratory in nature, these concepts enable a multidimensional investigation of space, materiality, social organization, and human experience—shedding light on how both elites and commoners in the Yingcheng settlement navigated the social, political, cultural, and economic transformations of the era. Crucially, this case study does not seek to formulate a new overarching theory to explain the macro-mechanisms that contributed to China’s first imperial transition. Rather, given the current scarcity of comparable archaeological data from Qin–Han commandery cities beyond the Jiang–Han region, it aims to enrich and deepen our empirical knowledge of the complex processes and embodied experiences—at a local scale—that accompanied China’s first wave of imperiogenesis. Ultimately, this research seeks to generate new observations that elucidate local micro-dynamics and, in doing so, inform and support future theorization of early empire formation in China. Beyond its local focus, Yingcheng’s military origins and its successive interactions with major powers of the time also resonate with patterns observed in ancient cities elsewhere in the world—such as Dura-Europos—making it a promising site for future comparative study. N/A

titleEnglish: Assessment criteria in audio description training: Perspectives of visually impaired and sighted people
abstract: Audio description (AD) helps people who are visually impaired (VI) to access audiovisual materials and events such as film, television, and theatre. It can also function as an orientation aid in daily life. To guarantee high-quality training for people learning to produce AD, an effective set of criteria is essential. However, it remains unclear how sighted and VI individuals differently perceive and prioritize criteria for AD assessment, and existing criteria have not been empirically substantiated. Moreover, most AD guidelines are developed by sighted professionals, overlooking the preferences of VI users, the primary consumers of AD. Finally, AD products and services in Chinese are offered mainly by non-governmental organizations, with no agreement on quality standards. These omissions create major barriers to the development of effective AD training and assessment in Chinese-speaking regions. To address these challenges, the proposed study will use a mixed-methods design to explore the perceptions of VI and sighted individuals concerning quality assessment criteria for AD. In the qualitative phase, audio-described film clips produced by 60 postgraduate students will be evaluated. Two groups of 12 evaluators—six VI and six sighted individuals—will assess and deliberate on suitable AD criteria. Their evaluation discussions will be recorded, transcribed, and analyzed using grounded theory and interactive qualitative analysis to identify key AD quality dimensions, leading to a preliminary framework for quality assessment criteria. In the quantitative phase, a follow-up study will be conducted to assess the perceived importance and difficulty of each criterion via a questionnaire distributed to 120 participants (60 VI and 60 sighted) with experience of watching films with AD. The participants will be recruited from Hong Kong and Chinese Mainland. The outcomes of this study are expected to enhance AD training, practice, and research by providing a set of assessment criteria incorporating perspectives from both VI and sighted people. The study will also produce clear evaluation rubrics and marking schemes that can be directly integrated into AD training programs. These improvements will have real-world benefits for the users of AD by enhancing their access to visual materials and involving them as creators of AD materials. Ultimately, the study will establish the first evidence-based quality assessment framework tailormade for Chinese languages, which can be used by relevant organizations or even made openly available online to ensure that future AD products and services suit the Chinese-speaking market. N/A

titleEnglish: A Site of Negotiation for Female Homoeroticism: Baihe Webnovels in Contemporary China
coInvestigators: Prof Bao, Hongwei Prof Yang, Ling
abstract: Baihe webnovels—serialized female homoerotic fiction published on Chinese literature websites—present a compelling paradox. Originating as free, amateur rewritings in the early 2000s, this genre has been dominated by original works since 2010, thriving in China's commercial digital publishing industry. In the 2020s, it has reached a new peak with transmedia adaptations and transnational publishing, even as state-led crackdowns on homosexual content have caused bans and a decline for Chinese danmei (male homoerotic) media. Despite this success, baihe webnovels remain underexplored in scholarship on queer literature, which has privileged print publications, male-centered stories, or non-commercial fanfiction. To address this gap, this project conceptualizes baihe webnovels as "a site of negotiation"—a space where female same-sex desire finds expression shaped by diverse sociocultural and institutional forces. These forces include but are not limited to authorial creativity, reader engagement, state ideologies, feminist and LGBTQ realities, and the constraints and opportunities opened by platform infrastructures. This 24-month project, led by three pioneering scholars in queer Chinese literary, media, and fan studies, adopts an integrative approach that combines qualitative and computational methods. We bridge research on queer Chinese media, literary platforms and fandoms, and gender and sexual cultures, while attending to China's evolving digital economy and censorial practices that structure queer literary production. Focusing on original baihe webnovels on commercial literary platforms, we will conduct textual and discourse analyses of selected webnovels, author interviews, reader focus groups, online ethnography of selected platforms, and text mining and topic modelling of large-scale reader comments and webnovel metadata (e.g., synopses). By analyzing five interconnected processes—narratives, authors, readers, platforms, and regulations—this project asks: How does this genre portray female homoeroticism? In what ways have interplays among authors, readers, platforms, and regulations shaped its characteristics? How does this genre refract or complicate dominant scripts of gender and sexuality, state gender policies, and the lived experiences of gender and sexual minorities? And what forms of agency do authors and readers exercise within this mediated literary space? Ultimately, this project challenges the presumption that queer media culture in digital, commercial settings is dominated by a singular force (authors, readers, or censorship). Instead, it offers a fresh, comprehensive model emphasizing a holistic view of this digital-born genre as a negotiated outcome, revealing how queer female fantasies and subjectivities emerge—though often through compromise—within a heteropatriarchal-normative, state-governed, and market-driven society. N/A

titleEnglish: Applying the CISG to Hong Kong-Mainland Sale of Goods Contracts: Comparative Law and Jurisprudence, Inter-Regional Agreement and Legal Harmonisation
coInvestigators: Prof Tang, Zheng Prof Trakman, Leon
abstract: The proposed project seeks to investigate, using comparative law and empirical methodologies, how the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods (CISG) might best be applied to a contract for the sale of goods between parties from Hong Kong and the Mainland respectively (Hong Kong-Mainland contract). Such a study builds upon the PI’s earlier research and policy report to the Department of Justice and is intended primarily to help Hong Kong to realise and maximise the economic and legal benefits of the 2021 Sale of Goods (United Nations Convention) Ordinance. Due to the overwhelming importance of the Hong Kong-Mainland trade to Hong Kong’s economy, the Ordinance’s future success hinges largely upon whether the CISG might extend its application to Hong Kong-Mainland contracts. The proposed project seeks to produce a systematic, multi-dimensional study of the different policy options, legal routes and implementation measures concerning the extension of the CISG's application to Hong Kong-Mainland contracts. The aim is to reduce the considerable legal barriers impeding an efficient and free flow of goods, to enhance Hong Kong’s status as an international trade and legal centre and hence to boost Hong Kong’s economic growth. Proceeding from the premise established in earlier research and Hong Kong legislation, namely that it is crucial to extend the CISG to Hong Kong-Mainland contracts, the proposed project seeks to answer two inter-related yet distinct 'how' questions: how to make the CISG applicable to Hong Kong-Mainland contracts, and how to ensure the uniformity of interpretation of CISG provisions by Hong Kong and Mainland courts. In answering these questions, this project will launch a comprehensive comparative study (which has not been done before) of the sale of goods laws (including the relevant judicial decisions and legal practice) in Hong Kong and the Chinese Mainland. In particular, this project strives to accomplish a broader and more complicated task of bridging, harmonising and even unifying Hong Kong and Mainland's judicial approach to the interpretation of the CISG, which is expected to furnish an inspirational model for the harmonisation (or at least reconciliation) of contract and commercial laws in the Great Bay Area. N/A

titleEnglish: Public Facilitation of Private Equity and Venture Capital in Asia’s Green Finance Transition
coInvestigators: Prof Lin, Lin
abstract: Hong Kong and Singapore aspire to be global green finance leaders, and both governments have positioned themselves as a (or “the”) leading Asian green finance hub. Both have therefore sought to become “early adopters” of international standards for listed company sustainability reporting and to develop standards to support green finance. Both have also developed regulatory regimes to support venture capital (VC) and private equity (PE) investment. However, how well each jurisdiction aligns green finance regimes with the regulatory supports and other incentives for private capital has not been explored. Because private financing levels globally are insufficient to reach national decarbonization and sustainable development goals (Boffo & Patalano 2020), these are pressing challenges. Our project will therefore develop a conceptual framework to enable us to evaluate the regulatory and financial support governments provide for the green finance transition in private capital markets. Here, we will build on the emerging literatures on policy mix and on the regulatory typology developed by the Co-I (Lin 2022) to identify regulatory supports across all stages of the VC/PE investment cycle (i.e. from fund-raising to investment to “exit”). We will then apply this to analyze the “greening” of VC and PE investment in Hong Kong, and in Singapore, and then compare the two. To place this analysis in context, we will also use a mixed-methods approach to compare the level of private capital activity in sustainable investment in the two markets. For Hong Kong, our analysis will also evaluate the extent to which its regulatory regime for greening private investment aligns with the relevant government supports for sustainable investment and VC/PE in the Greater Bay Area, and with relevant green finance strategies in the PRC as a whole. From this foundation, we will then develop a theoretical framework of the varying roles of government in supporting sustainable investment in PE/VC markets. We will ultimately propose regulatory and policy reforms to promote sustainable investment as a dominant source of private capital financing. This comparative analysis will fill a major gap in the literature on private capital markets, which has largely overlooked the potential role of government in supporting a “green transition” in VC and PE investment. N/A

titleEnglish: TWO-BODY: collaborative improvisation and choreography in motion-capture supported dance
coInvestigators: Prof Leung, Ian Prof Lo, Mimi
abstract: The mirror is a fundamental tool for dancers to get feedback during rehearsal about what they look like to audiences, and how they can creatively present that image. Recent motion capture technology has ushered in digital forms of the mirror where performers can inhabit bodies unlike their own in avatar form, creatively acting out movements in an imaginary landscape beyond what they are used to when looking at the mirror. This has inspired recent growth in motion-capture (mocap) avatar-mediated work that takes the rehearsal process into performance settings, enabling dancers and technologists to build a creative performance context beyond reality. Research has shown that the look of the avatar can affect how people perceive and behave in online settings, leading them to adopt actions more aligned with how the avatar should behave. In mocap-supported improvisation, different avatars like opposite gender, different body weight, and differing abilities and disabilities change the way dancers individually move in alignment with how the digital mirror represents their bodies. However, dance is frequently a collaborative process involving connection and communication between individuals negotiating both diverse and common perspectives. Research has yet to address how collaboration in both rehearsal improvisation and performance choreography contexts take place when a mocap-enabled avatar is the material for creative exercise between dancers. This two-part proposal aims to first understand how performers interact with each other, and with the common avatar they dance as, in mocap-supported improvisation. We will work with a cohort of 25 movement artists in Hong Kong dance community in avatar improvisation workshops. One dancer will take on sensors for the upper-body of one avatar while the other takes on the lower-body of the same avatar while visualizing it on an immersive screen. Dancers can then creatively swap control sensors and work with different types of avatars while we observe and later interview them to understand their creative responses, challenges, focuses of attention, and perceptions of each other and the avatar. Secondly, based on the workshop findings, we invite members of contemporary, partner-dance, and inclusive dance communities of different abilities to co-create choreographies for their particular areas of expertise. These focus groups will enable the creators’ voices in shaping how the interaction with mocap-supported avatars may occur in choreographed performance settings. The research and artistic outputs of the project will be both published in international venues and given public performance highlighting technology in Hong Kong performing arts. N/A

titleEnglish: Multidimensional Housing Precarity and Capability Deprivation among low-income residents in Subdivided Units, Transitional Housing, and Light Public Housing in Hong Kong
department: Department of Social and Behavioural Sciences
coInvestigators: Dr Chan, Crystal Ying Dr Leung, Ka Man Prof Skobba, Kimberly Prof Zhu, Yushu
abstract: Multidimensional housing precarity—encompassing economic, physical, social, spatial, and tenure insecurities—shapes the lived experiences of low-income residents in Hong Kong, particularly those in subdivided units (SDUs), transitional housing (TH), and light public housing (LPH). These dimensions reflect interconnected challenges: financial burdens in the private rental sector, substandard physical conditions affecting quality of life, housing insecurity and eviction risks due to regulatory gaps, geographic disparities limiting access to resources, and social exclusion impacting marginalized groups. Despite its complexity, significant gaps persist in understanding how multidimensional housing precarity varies across housing arrangements and how these dimensions influence residents’ capabilities, including health, social inclusion, and autonomy. This study addresses these gaps by applying the capability approach, which reframes housing precarity as a deprivation of freedoms and opportunities. The capability approach enables an exploration of how systemic constraints and individual agency interact to shape well-being, resilience, and decision-making. Specifically, this study investigates: (1) how multidimensional housing precarity manifests across SDUs, TH, and LPH, (2) how changes within or across housing arrangements affect long-term well-being and capabilities; and (3) how residents with diverse socioeconomic backgrounds and family compositions exercise agency within systemic constraints. The research underscores the dynamic and intersectional nature of housing precarity and capabilities, offering a foundation for rethinking housing as an essential component of capabilities and well-being. Adopting a longitudinal qualitative design, the study will interview 100 low-income residents who have lived in SDUs, TH, or LPH for at least six months. Using the Housing Pathway Approach with Life History Calendar method, participants will create visual timelines of their housing histories. The first interview (T1) will reconstruct lifetime housing pathways, focusing on critical transitions and moments of capability deprivation. A follow-up interview (T2), conducted one year later, will track changes in housing situations, capabilities, and agency over time. Through thematic and longitudinal analysis, the study will examine how multidimensional housing precarity varies across SDUs, TH, and LPH, and how these differences shape residents’ abilities to achieve stability, health, and social inclusion. The findings will contribute to theoretical advancements by applying the capability approach to housing precarity, emphasizing its multidimensional, evolving, and intersectional nature. Practically, the research will inform community-based strategies and policy measures aimed at reducing precarity, enhancing capabilities, and improving well-being for grassroots residents. By offering actionable insights for policymakers and practitioners, this study will support global efforts to address housing precarity in high-density urban contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Phonological development and decline across typical and atypical populations: cross-age group examination of the perception-production link
abstract: This study examines phonological processes characterizing early speech development in children (comparing typically developing children with those exhibiting speech sound disorders [SSD]) and age-related speech attrition in seniors (contrasting healthy aging with individuals diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease [AD] and Parkinson's disease [PD]). The world is experiencing unprecedented aging, particularly pronounced in Asia where Hong Kong will have nearly 40% of its population aged 65 and older by 2050. Within this demographic transformation lies a crisis: communication disorders significantly affect seniors, resulting in smaller social networks and increased social isolation. Remarkably, the speech sounds that become difficult for seniors to distinguish and produce are often the most challenging ones for children to acquire, suggesting fundamental principles governing both acquisition and attrition of phonological abilities. As neurodegenerative conditions like AD and PD continue to rise alongside global aging trends, there is urgent need for linguistic research to address these issues and to help develope effective interventions that preserve communication abilities and improve quality of life. This research examines underlying mechanisms of speech abilities by identifying links between phonological development and decline, and between speech perception and production. We focus on Mandarin and Cantonese speakers in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area, adopting listening and narrative tasks for assessments. Mandarin and Cantonese provide typological diversification to test current phonological theories. Listening tests evaluate participants' ability to discriminate sounds in diverse conditions, while narrative tasks assess articulatory accuracy and intelligibility across communicative contexts. Acoustic analysis quantifies spectrotemporal features and tonal contours to identify patterns of preservation and decline. This project carries theoretical and practical merits across three dimensions. First, it tests the mirror-image hypothesis by examining tonal speakers' speech processing, enriching evidence for constraint-based phonological theories. Applying constraint (re)ranking to model error patterns in children versus seniors in different conditions offers insights into whether phonological deviations reflect stable grammatical reorganization or processing-driven breakdowns. Second, our focus on the perception-production link contributes to phonetic theory by examining how auditory processing and articulatory control interact across developmental stages, addressing the fundamental question of whether perception and production share unified representational systems or operate through distinct but interconnected mechanisms. Third, comparative examination of Mandarin and Cantonese offers theoretical insights into distinctive feature theory, testing how phonological features are processed across age groups and clinical populations, thereby providing empirical evidence for the featural (re)organization of phonological competence and its biological foundations. N/A

titleEnglish: Resisting the Script: Narrative and Participatory Inoculation as Anti-Scam Interventions
coInvestigators: Dr DAI, Nancy Yue Dr Li, Danyang Prof LIU, Xiaofan
abstract: Telecom and online scams represent a rapidly growing global threat, costing individuals and societies billions of dollars annually and eroding trust in digital communication systems. These scams are adept at exploiting victims’ cognitive vulnerabilities through highly immersive interpersonal communication scenarios. Alarmingly, scammers are now employing increasingly sophisticated tactics, such as deepfakes, emotional manipulation, fake authorities, and meticulously scripted narratives. However, despite rising awareness about scams, conventional anti-scam strategies, such as information-based warnings and public announcements, remain largely ineffective, as they fail to engage people at the narrative and emotional levels at which scams operate. The proposed Engaged Inoculation Framework addresses this urgent problem by integrating innovative, immersive communication strategies to develop emotionally engaging, cognitively elaborative, and behaviorally empowering interventions. Unlike traditional approaches, this novel framework leverages inoculation theory alongside mechanisms from narrative persuasion and experiential learning to enhance scam resistance. This framework moves beyond one-size-fits-all solutions by tailoring interventions to high-risk populations, making it both scalable and impactful. Two distinct operationalizations will be evaluated: narrative inoculation (embedding scam warnings in compelling, relatable stories) and participatory inoculation (engaging users in role-play and simulation of scams). Study 1 will compare the effectiveness of narrative and didactic inoculation in enhancing scam detection, resistance intentions, and psychological mechanisms such as narrative engagement and self-efficacy. Study 2 will contrast passive message exposure with active scenario-based role-play on the same sets of outcome measures, exploring for cognitive, emotional and behavioral mechanisms. By targeting cognitive, affective, and behavioral pathways, this project aspires to establish a scalable, evidence-based intervention model to combat scams. The outcomes of these studies will inform the design of interactive educational tools, anti-scam applications, and community-centered campaigns, particularly aimed at high-risk populations in Hong Kong and other regions across Asia. Moreover, these findings could inform policymaking, regulatory frameworks, and international collaborations to combat cybercrime, offering actionable insights for governments, organizations, and other stakeholders More broadly, this research will contribute to the broader global conversation on AI-powered deception and public resilience, offering innovative solutions for combating scams while positioning Hong Kong as a leader in scam resistance innovation. Given the escalating sophistication and prevalence of scams, this research is not only timely but also necessary to safeguard trust in digital ecosystems and support the development of deployable inoculation-based communication systems, early-warning interventions, and digital recovery tools that can be adopted by various stakeholders such as schools, banks, telecoms, and civic organizations. N/A

titleEnglish: Human-AI Collaborative Craft (HACC) in Chinese Ceramics Activation and Integration of Intangible Cultural Heritage through AI frameworks
abstract: UNESCO’s Safeguarding Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH) reports identify "aging practitioners without successors" as a top threat to traditional crafts. Correlating with rapid industrialization, urbanization, and globalization, Chinese Ceramic heritage is at risk of losing millennia of knowledge (He, 2023). At the same time, advancements in industrial Ceramic Additive Manufacturing (CAM) and the application of AI in the generation of design and optimisation of ceramic processes have led to the global CAM market being projected to reach $3.49 billion by 2034 (Precedence Research, 2025). While AI drives innovation in industrial ceramics and additive manufacturing (AM), its role remains mostly passive and preservative in heritage and museological contexts. This gap represents both a commercial missed opportunity and a cultural stagnation for traditional Chinese ceramics. In the long history of ceramics, novel methods, new tools, and materials have been integrated within existing workflows and traditions, making it one of the richest crafts where continuous innovations deepened creative symbiosis between technology and expression. This research builds on the analysis of traditional ceramic craft procedures to introduce Human-AI Collaborative Craft (HACC), integrating custom-made digital fabrication methods, new material applications, and design methods to merge machine precision with human expressiveness, resulting in novel ceramic artifacts. Together with the University of Hong Kong’s University Museum and Art Gallery’s (UMAG) and collaboratively with Hong Kong-based Ceramic craft practitioners, and visits to Jingdezhen, we will identify key methods and processes of traditional ceramic properties, techniques, and tools. The research will integrate 3D-scanned ceramic artefacts, motion capture data, sensor-based monitoring, and interviews into a custom-trained AI model, establishing an AI-Collaborative framework featuring customized tools, ceramic 3D printing, and robotic subtractive and additive tooling. The framework will be tested through iterative prototyping involving Hong Kong-based Ceramic craft practitioners, producing comparative case studies of AI-human and human-only ceramic artifacts. The results will be presented as public exhibitions in 2028/29 in Hong Kong and potentially in Jingdezhen. The exhibitions will showcase the prototypes, newly created tools and frameworks, and resulting artefacts together with traditional ceramic objects. An accompanying symposium with invited guests from the field of ceramic arts, material science, and computer-aided manufacturing will engage a wider public audience, researchers, artists, and designers. An edited transcription of the symposium's recording will form the basis for academic journals such as Leonardo and conferences such as ISEA that address the crossover of science and technology into art. N/A

titleEnglish: Negotiating State-private Dynamics in China's Engagement with Africa: Transnational Entrepreneurship and the Rise of Privately-owned Chinese Industrial Zones in Zimbabwe
abstract: Since the turn of the 21st century, the dominant narrative of China’s engagement with Africa has emphasized state-led infrastructure and resource-based investments. Relatively little scholarly attention has been paid to the private sector, which currently accounts for 70% of China’s direct investment in Africa and is becoming the new driver of China’s industrial engagement with Africa. The presence of private Chinese entrepreneurs is growing in the continent, particularly in China-associated Special Economic Zones, under the framework established by the Forum on China-Africa Cooperation. However, few empirical studies have closely examined the emerging phenomenon of privately-owned Chinese zones. To address this gap, the proposed qualitative research will explore the role of large Chinese private firms in expanding China’s economic interests in Africa through various industrial parks, focusing on China-Zimbabwe cooperation—a relationship often considered a microcosm of broader, resource-driven China-Africa relations. The establishment of privately-owned Chinese zones for mineral resource extraction is a crucial part of China’s growing cooperation with Zimbabwe, and the project will ask how these overseas zones provide a new context for Chinese entrepreneurs to ground their transnational business pursuits and how these transnational actors mobilize resources and navigate the regulatory landscapes of both China and Zimbabwe. Specifically, multi-sited fieldwork, semi-structured interviews, and secondary data analysis will be conducted to examine the complex connections and divergences between private investors, state capital, governments, and communities in China’s zone-based engagement with Africa. Exploring the business and social practices of Chinese entrepreneurs in privately-owned industrial zones in Zimbabwe will illuminate the processes through which the Chinese state at different levels engages Chinese private capital and how evolving transnational processes and networks reconfigure contemporary Chinese entrepreneurship. Going beyond the conventional perception of Chinese enterprises as either instruments of state strategy or independent actors, the project will capture the dynamic reality of transnational enterprise building. Its findings will thereby deepen understanding of transnational entrepreneurship, state-business relations, and globalization at a time when private capital, especially Chinese capital, is playing an increasingly prominent role in shaping global development. The story of these private entrepreneurs as transnational economic agents will demonstrate the need for a more dynamic understanding of the China-Africa encounter in a globalized world. In practical terms, the research will inform policymakers, development agencies, and businesses seeking to design more inclusive and sustainable models of China-Africa industrial cooperation. N/A

titleEnglish: Dialogic Reading for Shared Reading for Young Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder with a Whole-School Approach: A Cluster Randomized Controlled Trial
department: Department of Social and Behavioural Sciences
coInvestigators: Prof Chung, Kien Hoa Kevin Prof Leung, Cynthia
abstract: According to the statistics provided by the Education Bureau (EDB) and the Social Welfare Department, about 16% of the total enrollment in subsidized kindergartens have special needs, among which 46% have a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder. However, the quota for professional development for kindergarten teachers on catering for students with disabilities is about 365 (3% of the kindergarten teacher population), offered by EDB in 2024-25. The figure reflects inadequate training on catering for young children with special needs who often lag behind in literacy, and language development. The study aims to provide training to teachers with dialogic reading strategies for shared reading and facilitate joint attention and social acceptance of young children with autism spectrum disorder. An enriched dialogic reading with emphasis on social interaction and communication for shared reading has been piloted for 44 young children with ASD from six kindergartens, with a random assignment to two groups (intervention and wait-list) who completed eight intervention sessions across four weeks. The experimental group gained significantly in language development (receptive and expressive vocabulary) and social understanding with a large effect size. To adapt the intervention for children with various language levels of ASD, the instructional strategies were further differentiated into three levels: easy, medium, and difficult. A 3-hour training workshop was provided to teachers in the pilot schools. Teacher participants also reported high teacher self-efficacy in supporting the reading and learning of young children with ASD. Teachers highlighted that strategies in enhancing joint attention, emotional understanding and expression effectively addressed the developmental needs of ASD children and also benefited typical peers. In the current cluster randomized controlled trial, about 200 participating children with a whole-school approach (WSA) will be randomly assigned to two conditions (the experimental and waitlist) for an 8-session reading intervention. The objectives of the study include: a) examining the effectiveness of an enriched dialogic reading approach to enhancing language development and social understanding in the inclusive classroom, b) investigating teacher efficacy in using enriched dialogic reading strategies to cater for learner diversity, and c) promoting WSA in achieving inclusive and equitable quality education. Qualitative comments from teachers and parents will also be collected to validate the impact on adopting WSA. If successful, the study will contribute to inclusive, equitable education and effective reading instruction for all children by promoting teacher efficacy in accommodating diverse needs of special learners and realizing the sustainable development goal in quality education. N/A

titleEnglish: Beyond Pity: Fostering Workplace Inclusion of People with Disabilities through Media-Based Intergroup Contact
abstract: Although persons with disabilities (PWDs) account for more than 7% of Hong Kong’s population, only about 39% of those of working age participate in the labor force (Chan, 2017). In recent years, the Hong Kong government has increased funding and policy support to promote the employment of PWDs (The 2025–26 Budget – Budget Speech, 2025). Yet, PWDs continue to encounter deep-seated attitudinal barriers that restrict equal participation. Local surveys reveal that stereotypes and underestimation of competence persist across industries (Hong Kong Blind Union, 2023), which highlights the need for evidence-based strategies to address the social perceptions that sustain inequalities. Prior research shows that PWDs are commonly perceived as warm but incompetent (Canton et al., 2023), a stereotype that evokes sympathy rather than respect. The media, particularly social media, represent a powerful channel for shaping public perceptions of PWDs. Paradoxically, when media portrayals primarily elicit pity, they risk reinforcing dependence-oriented perceptions and status asymmetries that undermine genuine equality. Understanding how media representations can convey competence and respect is, therefore, critical for leveraging media as an effective tool for long-term social inclusion. This project seeks to move public perceptions and behaviors beyond pity by developing and testing communication strategies that foster meaningful inclusion of PWDs in workplaces. Guided by the BIAS Map (Cuddy, Fiske, & Glick, 2007), the project integrates three studies to systematically examine how competence- and warmth-related media portrayals shape behavioral responses toward PWDs, and how these effects are mediated by discrete emotions and moderated by perceived controllability and visibility of different disability types. Study 1 will involve in-depth interviews with 10–15 social enterprise employees who have worked with PWD employees to identify real-world narratives that convey competence and warmth in workplace contexts. Study 2 will use a web-based experiment to test the causal effects of competence and warmth portrayals across four disability types that vary in controllability and visibility. Study 3 will extend this design by manipulating controllability and measuring the durability of message effects through a longitudinal design. Theoretically, the project will refine the BIAS Map framework by identifying mediators and contextual moderators relevant to disability inclusion. Practically, it will generate empirically validated messages that guide governments, NGOs, and media practitioners in designing communication campaigns and workplace trainings that promote sustainable inclusion of PWDs. Overall, the project aims to advance Hong Kong’s progress toward a more inclusive and equitable society. N/A

titleEnglish: A Study of Shell Nouns in COVID-19 News Reports for Application in Automatic Appraisal of Writer Stance
abstract: This project proposes to develop an automatic appraisal system of writer stances and attitudes in news media by way of shell nouns (SNs). As a group of abstract nouns such as fact, problem and suggestion, SNs encapsulates the meaning proposition in their complement clauses. The SN and the complement clause have a characteristic semantic relation of equivalence. The research is motivated by the understanding that SNs represent powerful linguistic expressions of speaker stance and attitude in discourse and, when viewed from the perspective of cognitive construal, afford a great deal of insight into language and thought in general and opinionated linguistic encoding in particular. The current project is additionally motivated by the observation that previous studies have focused on academic discourse and very little is known about their use in the broad coverage of healthcare issues in media discourse as an increasingly important outlet of public sentiment. The study intends to fill this gap and aims to chart an empirically verifiable usage profile that usefully feeds into an automatic stance appraisal system. For this purpose, the project will employ a corpus of media reports covering health issues in general and COVID-19 in particular over the past two years and perform a comparative-contrastive analysis of SNs demonstrating different attitudes across different focal topics such as vaccinations, personal protective equipment, and government recommendations. Methodologically, the present study will adopt a novel approach that treats SNs as grammatical metaphors capable of revealing the speaker’s cognitive construal. In this approach, the SNs will be first classified according to an authoritative semantic scheme of analysis, then mapped to transitivity processes and finally categorized according to a taxonomy similar to that proposed Appraisal Theory. Quantitative analyses such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation and Kullback-Leibler Divergence will be conducted and manual validation performed to identify significant patterns of construal characteristic of attitudinal expressions and contextual topics. Since SNs represent grammatical metaphors underlined by cognitive construal, the project is significant for its application of cognitive principles governing the choice of specific linguistic encodings to evaluate the sentiments of the texts. Results will help to unveil different cognitive patterns of construal in news media leading to better AI systems concerned with sentiment understanding. Findings from the project will also lend insight into linguistic research focused on an improved taxonomy of appraisal. Results arising from the study will be disseminated via academic journals and international conferences. N/A

titleEnglish: Critical Friends of Daoxue in Retirement: Three Cases of Literati Identity in Late-Twelfth to Mid-Thirteenth Century China
abstract: This study examines the emotional and social dimensions of Southern Song literati culture through the writings of Yang Wanli (1127–1206) Lu You (1125–1210), and Liu Kezhuang (1187–1269) primarily. Against the backdrop of political upheaval, geographic displacement, and cultural transformation brought about by the fall of the Northern Song capital and the relocation of the imperial court, literati elites faced new challenges in redefining their identities and maintaining intellectual networks. By focusing on the themes of mobility, distance, and self-fashioning, this study explores how Southern Song literati navigated the complexities of separation, social instability, and personal transformation. The concept of distance, as explored in this project, extends beyond physical separation to include temporal, perceived, and ideological dimensions. For these literati, temporal distance from their earlier official lives shaped their emotional landscapes, while perceived distance—rooted in social status, personal relationships, and political estrangement—profoundly influenced their connections to others and their sense of self. Their writings in retirement also reflect a nuanced relationship with Daoxue leaders and ideals. They maintained critical distance from their orthodox view of activist politics, resisting full alignment with its ideologies. Yang Wanli’s letters and poetry reflect his withdrawal from court politics and his pursuit of inner peace through simplicity and nature. Lu You’s express a tension between his rural seclusion and his longing for irredentist action, while Liu Kezhuang’s later works reveal an intellectual from the next generation who, though distanced from court, remained much engaged in cultural production. Together, their writings illustrate how distance—physical, emotional, and ideological—shaped the experiences of Southern Song literati as they navigated the shifting dynamics of their era. This study also highlights how textual practices—letters, essays, and poetry—served as critical tools for negotiating distance and maintaining intellectual and social networks. By examining the works especially of the late years of Yang Wanli, Lu You, and Liu Kezhuang, this project underscores the diversity of strategies to reconcile aspirations with the demands of a changing political and intellectual landscape. Ultimately, this study deepens our understanding of middle period Chinese literati culture, emphasizing the role of distance, mobility, and selective engagement with Daoxue in shaping intellectual and personal lives. It shows how textual practices mediated displacement and change, offering insights into those literati navigating complex social, political, and emotional landscapes. N/A

titleEnglish: Foundations of Character Studies - Towards a Panmedial Theory of Characters
abstract: The overall goal of this project is to create, nurture and grow the nascent field of Character Studies. Characters, whether mythical, historical, fictional, or contemporary, are everywhere in human communication. Not only do they form a cornerstone in the entertainment media such as song lyrics, film/TV, games and literature, but also in religion, history, politics, news, painting, children’s toys, sports, social media, advertisement – in short, they can be found as a central if not always crucial element in every mediated walk of life. One could even claim that they are more important than narratives, because there can be a character independent of a particular story, but there can’t be (successful) stories without characters. And yet, the general study of characters (here defined as the mediated representation of a person) as such does not exist as its own field. The theoretical aim of the project is to create a general and interdisciplinary theory of character and characterisation - the mediated representation of a person - by comparing and collating theories and media practices from three media sectors (literature, AI Chat characters, and games) and three disiplinary fields (Generative AI, narratology, game studies). The project will break new ground by focusing on character as a panmedial phenomenon, not springing from or limited to any one medium or genre, but as a fundamental, cognitive unit of representation, and will foreground characterization as a crucial media practice driving entertainment franchises, social media, news, historiography, sport, biography, and marketing, to name a few. The resulting theory should contribute to the specific media disciplines and also provide insight to media industries and practitioners, and pave the way for larger investigations of character in other aspects of contemporary culture. The societal impact of this project may be significant: the new field will provide highly useful expertise for the entertainment industries, and for the public in terms of improved character literacy. By understanding how characters are constructed to work persuasively, scams can be prevented and harmful practices such as AI-based predators can be legislated against, thus protecting voulnerable population groups such as children and the elderly. N/A

titleEnglish: Neurocomputational Mechanisms of Age-Related Changes in Model-Based Decision-Making: The Role of Prosocial Motivation
department: Department of Social and Behavioural Sciences
coInvestigators: Dr Chau, Ka Hung Bolton Prof YEUNG, Yuen Lan Dannii Prof YU, Rongjun
abstract: The rapid growth of ageing populations raises urgent questions about how the ageing brain supports adaptive behaviour in complex environments. Ageing is associated with declines in prefrontal function and cognitive resources, potentially reducing engagement in goal-directed model-based strategies, which rely on effortful planning and the use of an internal model to predict future outcomes. Reduced reliance on these may limit the capacity to plan and adapt to changing circumstances in daily life. Our recent electroencephalography (EEG) findings and other studies indeed show that older adults engage less in model-based strategies than younger adults. Understanding the processes that enable older adults to re-engage in model-based strategies is crucial for identifying mechanisms of cognitive resilience and developing strategies to sustain adaptive behaviours in ageing. Socioemotional selectivity theory proposes that with age, people increasingly prioritise emotionally meaningful and prosocial goals. Resource-rational theory further suggests that cognitive effort is directed toward goals with higher expected value, implying that motivational shifts—not only cognitive decline—shape when people engage in effortful planning. Prosocial motivation may therefore provide a key route to sustain model-based planning in ageing. Evidence shows that older adults care more about others’ outcomes and that prosocial motivation enhances reinforcement learning when deciding for others. Our findings in young adults also showed that individuals with stronger prosociality exhibited better model-based strategies when deciding for others. To date, it remains unclear whether prosocial motivation can counteract age-related reductions in model-based decisions and what neural mechanisms functionally and causally support this effect in ageing. Study 1 will use functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) with computational modelling to compare self- and other-regarding decisions in younger and older adults, testing whether prosocial contexts enhance model-based decisions in older adults (supported by piloting) and whether social brain regions, such as the temporoparietal junction (TPJ), support this motivation-enhanced engagement. Study 2 will apply high-definition transcranial direct current stimulation (HD-tDCS) to the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and TPJ, regions supporting cognitive control and social cognition, to test their causal contributions to self and prosocial learning. In both studies, we will test whether older adults with greater social connectedness show better model-based decisions, as connectedness relates to prosociality and represents a potential intervention target. Together, these studies will elucidate how prosocial motivation mobilises neural systems that sustain goal-directed and adaptive behaviours in ageing, advancing resource-rational accounts of social decision-making and informing behavioural-neural strategies to foster resilience in later life. N/A

titleEnglish: Hybrid spaces of artistic collaboration: Revisiting the production, painting technique and style of Qianlong’s officer portraits
coInvestigators: Dr Tang, Raymond Prof Walton, Marc Dr Yiu, Josh Dr Zhou, Jin
abstract: During the reign of the Qianlong emperor (1736-95), the Manchu government expanded its rule in Central Asia by a series of conquests, the Ten Glorious Campaigns. To celebrate the conquests and reward its outstanding participants, the Qianlong emperor commissioned astonishing quantities of officer portraits in various formats. In total four portrait cycles representing 280 officers were commissioned probably between 1760 and 1792, known as the East-Turkestan, Jinchuan, Taiwan and Gurkha series. The first two series have received continued scholarly attention because both are considered the collaborative work of Chinese court artists and European missionary-artists, such as Giuseppe Castiglione. Consequently, the portraits are regarded as a product of a unique moment of cultural exchange at the Qing court. These groups of artists had little in common since they were trained in settings with a distinct visual culture and artistic tradition. The Jesuit missionaries had been trained in the practice of oil painting with its visual modes of realism, and its distinctive techniques and styles of brushwork, which was considered the antithesis of Chinese painting. Significant challenges must have arisen from the collaboration as both groups of artists needed to absorb and bring together two different ways of painting. While being valued as an artistic co-production, we know little about what collaboration practices informed the portraits’ production. The reason is that information on the artists who made the portraits and, on their production, has been recorded sparingly in Jesuit accounts and imperial edicts. Yet, since the 1970s, historians and art historians have developed hypotheses based on the limited available sources to propose reconstructions of the collaborative making procedures of the officer portraits. Still, there remain ambiguities about how these portraits came about through their collaborative practices. The project aims to address these ambiguities by revisiting existing hypotheses using the interdisciplinary approach of technical art history. The material composition and the making procedures of a set of eight officer portraits held in the R&J collection in Hong Kong (currently the largest collection of these portraits in China) will be examined by using natural science methods. The scientific data will be collected and contextualized by an interdisciplinary team of Western and Chinese art historians and conservation scientists. The main contribution to Chinese art history is further corroborating the hypotheses on artistic collaboration at the Qing court and to demonstrate in general how technical art history can be used to study Chinese painting. N/A

titleEnglish: Cui Jian and Chinese Rock: A Cultural Memory
coInvestigators: Prof Field, Andrew
abstract: Chinese rock music is widely recognized to have emerged on May 9, 1986, when Cui Jian performed “Nothing to My Name” at the concert commemorating the International Year of Peace. However, the cultural roots of Chinese rock trace back to the mid-1970s, when Western music began to infiltrate the PRC through unofficial channels. Cui Jian, often referred to as “the father of Chinese rock” or “China’s Bob Dylan,” pioneered the genre and its fusion with folk, funk, punk, hip hop, blues, and jazz. He categorizes music into three types: the first, official “main melody” music promoting patriotism and socialist values; the second, industry-driven pop music; and the third, which addresses heavier themes like human existence, pursuit of freedom, and individuality versus collectivism. As a relatively new field of study, literature on Chinese rock has largely emerged since 2000, focusing on its origins in the 1980s, commercialization in the 1990s, decline in the 2000s, and moderate resurgence during the pandemic. While Cui Jian is recognized for shaping a rebellious youth culture in the late 1980s and respected by many top artists, few studies explore his 40 years of musical and philosophical evolution. This study will be the first major analysis of Cui Jian as a cultural icon, and of Chinese rock music as cultural history, along with cultural exchanges that occur between Mainland China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, and elsewhere. Utilizing existing research by both Chinese and Western scholars, along with interviews we conducted over the past 20 years with over 100 musicians, cultural critics, filmmakers, sound engineers, and fans from three generations, the project will conduct an in-depth analysis of Cui Jian’s influence on Chinese music, youth culture, and live performance. We will break down this large task by presenting two conference presentations, writing two journal papers, and making a one-hour educational documentary to recap the key events and figures in the Chinese rock scene from the 1980s to the present, complete with subtitles in Chinese and English. The presentations, publications, and the documentary film will form the basis of a book manuscript. N/A

titleEnglish: Navigating the State-Market Interface in China’s Net-Zero Transition: Legal and Institutional Analysis of the Green Electricity Certificate Regime
coInvestigators: Dr ANDREWS-SPEED, Philip
abstract: China’s net-zero transition is driving a strategic institutional reconfiguration in energy governance, where market mechanisms are deliberately constructed by the state to reduce historical dependence on a command-and-control model. For decades, China’s energy sector has been managed through administrative directives, state financing, and centralized planning, which undermines long-term efficiency. The current shift toward embedding market mechanisms under continued state oversight exemplifies a hybrid governance model that transcends conventional state-market dichotomies and leverages supply- and demand-side interventions. A central example of this hybridization is the Green Electricity Certificate (GEC) trading system. Although initially slow to develop, the GEC market has expanded rapidly since the central government’s 2023 decision to designate GECs as the sole legal proof of renewable electricity’s environmental attributes, followed by regulatory and policy measures. Investigating the evolution of GEC trading offers insight into how China is recalibrating its energy governance within a state-centric institutional framework that continues to mediate the boundaries of market activity. Despite the growing policy relevance of GECs, academic analysis of China’s GEC regime remains scarce. This research addresses that gap by positioning GECs as a case study to test and refine institutional theory, drawing on Williamson’s foundational framework alongside subsequent contributions by Mahoney and Thelen. It examines how legal and organizational innovations both enable and constrain market reforms amid persistent structural and normative challenges, including regulatory fragmentation, entrenched state intervention, and conflicting stakeholder interests. The coexistence of market mechanisms and state intervention through incremental institutional layering and conversion within China’s GEC regime is particularly intriguing. Additionally, it assesses the extent to which GEC trading rules and policies enhance China’s energy governance capacity in the context of these entrenched regulatory and structural challenges. This research employs a multifaceted methodology, integrating doctrinal analysis of laws, regulations, and policies with semi-structured interviews of Chinese legal scholars, energy regulators, industry participants, and international experts familiar with China’s energy sector. Ultimately, it seeks to generate actionable recommendations to strengthen GECs’ contribution to China’s renewable energy development, ensuring they promote genuine decarbonization rather than formal compliance. By advancing a comprehensive institutional analysis, the project deepens our understanding of China’s evolving energy governance and provides broader insights for scholars and policymakers examining the complex interactions between state power and market instruments in the energy transition. N/A

titleEnglish: Language, Emotion, and Selfhood in the Poetry of Wang Fuzhi (1619-1692)
department: Academy of Chinese, History, Religion and Philosophy
coInvestigators: Prof Zhang, Hongsheng
abstract: Known primarily as a towering philosopher during the Ming-Qing transition, Wang Fuzhi王夫之 (1619-1692) also wrote poetry and extensive literary critical commentary. A mutually formative relationship between “theory” and “practice” has often been proposed, sometimes even taken for granted, for writers in imperial China who were both poets and critics, but this aspect has been strangely overlooked in modern studies of Wang. His poetry has largely been ignored in the vast body of scholarship seeking to reconstruct his poetic thought. This project proposes to reexamine Wang’s poetics by taking his poetry as the primary text, aimed primarily at addressing two aspects that by studying his literary criticism and philosophical writings alone cannot satisfactorily resolve: its practicality in the world of experience; and the changes over the course of his life. For Wang, poetry confirms the inevitable physicality of human existence but also plays a role in the individual’s efforts to transcend it. His insistence on self-discipline in the face of calamity highlights the opposite pulls in individual selfhood that is both necessary and problematic for his moral universe. This, I argue, is what makes poetry necessary and meaningful for Wang, and what underlies his belief for the role that lyrical expression can uniquely fulfill. Wang’s poetics, developed in the same historical moment when the Ming yimin poets widely claimed narrative and history for the poetic demesne, is a poetry of heightened consciousness of being in the world: the poet feels everything in full force and allows nothing to distract or blunt the impact of the moment. While modern scholarship on Wang’s literary critical writings has provided comprehensive reconstructions of Wang’s poetic paradigm, his poetry sheds fresh insight. First, dated and produced in response to real-life situations, it supplies the immediate context to study the changes in his poetic thought over time. Second, by foregrounding the limitations imposed by the human condition, it furnishes us with the material to focus on the practical orientation of his poetics, which tends to be obfuscated in his literary criticism and philosophical writings. A study of his poetry will present a more nuanced picture on the problem of emotion, selfhood, and language in Wang’s poetry and poetics, and deepen our understanding in the role of literature in his intellectual universe. N/A

titleEnglish: Platformized News and Scientific Misinformation in Hong Kong: Mapping and Mitigation
abstract: When scientific information circulates online, accuracy does not always keep pace. In the platformized media landscape, dense with messaging apps, forums, and algorithmic feeds, echo chambers can intensify and accelerate the spread of scientific misinformation. This study maps cross-platform narrative diffusion, identifies the interpretive frames that gain traction, and pinpoints the community and platform characteristics associated with heightened vulnerability. Study 1 will examine the pathways by which scientific reports are interpreted, reframed, and converted into misinformation across social media, news portals, and messaging apps, with attention to variation by topic, subtopic, and community worldview. It will systematically trace the evolution of original reports into misinformation variants, map diffusion pathways across platforms and communities, identify recurrent interpretive frames, and model their dynamics across platforms and over time. Study 2 will implement a survey experiment with Hong Kong residents to assess how online community engagement, news consumption patterns, and science attitudes relate to susceptibility to particular misinformation types. Grounded in the interpretive frames from Study 1 and designed to test echo chamber effects, it will produce actionable insights to reduce susceptibility and mitigate misinformation spread. This research addresses two major gaps in the literature, namely the lack of cross-platform insight into decentralized information flows and limited understanding of susceptibility to diverse forms of scientific misinformation. It contributes to scholarship on platformization and echo chambers by evidencing how platform affordances and community structures intensify particular interpretive frames and accelerate diffusion across platforms. Expected outputs include a typology of interpretive frames in scientific misinformation, topic-specific diffusion maps, and comprehensive susceptibility profiles outlining risk patterns across community worldviews, news diets, platform ecosystems, and interpretive frames. Building on these findings, the research will develop targeted interventions, including pre-bunking and debunking guidance for fact-checkers, media literacy resources tailored to Hong Kong’s platform ecosystem, and recommendations for cross-platform collaboration, to strengthen science outreach and support science-based policymaking. Findings will be disseminated through comprehensive reports and practitioner-facing materials. Ultimately, the project provides actionable pathways to cultivate an informed, resilient public and to bolster accurate perceptions of science within Hong Kong’s dynamic digital landscape. N/A

titleEnglish: How Music Shapes Thought and Imagination: A Data-Driven and Conceptual Framework
coInvestigators: Prof Bhattacharya, Joydeep Prof d'Inverno, Mark
abstract: Listening to music profoundly influences our internal mental experiences, allowing us to immerse ourselves in the narratives of songs, imagine vivid scenarios, and drift between past memories and future anticipations. Many listeners engage with music to support various cognitive functions such as fantasizing, introspecting, and recalling memories, making music an integral part of their inner worlds. The heterogeneity of these mental experiences, such as earworms, absorption, mind-wandering, autobiographical memories, and trance (among others), highlights music’s fundamental role in human cognition, but also presents important challenges for scientific study. Currently, research in music psychology remains fragmented, with diverse terminologies, theories, and methods. Developing a unified conceptual framework that delineates the core dimensions of subjective experience is crucial for advancing the field, resolving terminological ambiguities and operationalization challenges, and providing the foundational setting for future research. Additionally, integrating existing consciousness theories can help create both a comprehensive and parsimonious model. With regard to empirical findings, the influence of music features and listener traits on mental experiences remains unclear and ecological data from naturalistic listening settings are needed to complement traditional lab-based studies, providing a more accurate understanding of music’s impact on consciousness in “real life”. The goal of our project is to develop a novel, conceptual, and data-driven framework for understanding the relationship between music listening and internal mental experiences. Combining expertise from music psychology, cognitive science, and formal modeling, we will undertake comprehensive literature reviews to inform our conceptual modeling, at the same time validating it through systematic empirical data collection. Results will be used to iteratively test and refine the model, ensuring that it captures as much of the full complexity of music-related mental experiences as possible. In four empirical music listening studies, we will employ multidimensional experience sampling to characterize subjective changes, across various music styles, listener profiles, and listening contexts (both naturalistic and laboratory settings).
