Data analysis will include principal component analysis and psychometric network analysis to identify core dimensions and clarify their relationships. Ultimately, this project will deepen our understanding of how music influences conscious experience in everyday life, thereby impacting research on consciousness, emotion, creativity, and other related areas. Beyond academia, the findings will benefit therapists and health professionals by informing music-based interventions, help the general public use music more intentionally for well-being, and support artists in harnessing music’s power to influence conscious states for creative purposes. N/A

titleEnglish: Listening Under the Banyan Tree: Plant Bioelectric Sonification in Arts and Ecology
abstract: Listening Under the Banyan Tree: Plant Bioelectric Sonification in Arts and Ecology is an interdisciplinary project that translates the bioelectrical activity of Hong Kong’s banyan trees into sound. At its core, the project uses listening as both method and outcome, creating new ways of experiencing more-than-human life. Banyans (Ficus microcarpa) are keystone species that provide shade, habitats, and urban cooling, while serving as cultural landmarks and gathering places. Their ecological and cultural presence makes them anchors for exploring how artistic research and ecological science can come together. All plants generate tiny electrical signals as part of their lives, regulating photosynthesis, responding to stress, and adapting to their environments. Normally invisible, these signals can be translated into sound, making plant vitality perceptible. In recent years, artists and scientists have approached this in different ways: some have turned signals into music to spark engagement, while others have used high-fidelity instruments to study physiology in controlled laboratory settings. Artistic approaches risk treating plants as abstract data or performers, while scientific work remains highly specialised. Both perspectives are valuable, yet rarely combined. This project bridges those traditions. It develops a portable, open-source biosensing system to capture banyan bioelectrical activity in real time, with a flexible sonification framework that balances clarity with resonance— combining methods such as parameter mapping, audification, and spatialisation to create immersive soundfields. Fieldwork will focus on ecologically and culturally significant banyans identified through Hong Kong’s Old and Valuable Trees Register, including clusters of trees that allow listening “across” multiple organisms to reveal patterns of interdependence. The project also extends cultural memory. Oral histories gathered under banyan canopies will be recorded in ambisonic format and integrated into the existing 3D Hong Kong Topophonies sound map, a previous funded project. By weaving community stories with real-time plant sonifications, the project creates a layered archive where human and vegetal voices meet. Public listening sessions will share these experiences, inviting communities to gather under banyans once again and explore new forms of empathy, reflection, and dialogue. By combining ecological science, cultural practice, and artistic research, Listening Under the Banyan Tree offers an original model for open, accessible, and situated listening—one that is scientifically grounded, culturally resonant, and widely shareable. N/A

titleEnglish: Towards Automatic Accessibility for the Blind and Partially Sighted with Large Multimodal Models: LMM-based Generation of Audio Description Scripts via Prompt Engineering
department: Department of Translation, Interpreting and Intercultural Studies
coInvestigators: Dr LEUNG, Hoi Ching Dawning Prof Pan, Jun
abstract: According to the World Health Organization, “at least 2.2 billion people have a near or distance vision impairment” in the world (WHO, 2023). Population growth, ageing and life expectancy increases are expected to escalate the risk that more people acquire vision impairment. Therefore, the need for accessibility measures for the blind and partially sighted (BPS) will become even more pressing. This project aims to contribute to accessibility measures for the BPS that support digital content understanding and enjoyment. The project will be based in Hong Kong, where over 199,600 citizens or more than 2.7% of the population, have visual impairments of differing degrees (Census and Statistics Department, 2021). Access to information and participation in cultural life are fundamental human rights. Audio description (AD), which uses voiced-out narratives to describe relevant on-screen actions, is a key accessibility measure in a world where video consumption keeps growing exponentially every year (Sandvine, 2025). The limited availability of AD is further compounded by the fact that, currently, AD is a time-consuming and costly process, including script creation and recording or live voice acting. Partially automating the AD process could significantly expand access to audiovisual media for BPS individuals, enhancing their quality of life. This project proposes the use of Large Multimodal Models (LMM), artificial intelligence systems designed to process audiovisual data, to generate AD scripts in the Cantonese language. It will do so via prompt engineering, the practice of designing and refining input prompts to effectively guide the behaviour and outputs of AI models. The project aims to 1. develop prompt engineering guidelines to enhance the innate capabilities of LMMs to generate AD scripts; 2. assess the way LMMs can assist human audio describers to create AD scripts, and 3. evaluate the way LMM-generated AD scripts are received by BPS individuals. The project collaborates closely with The Audio Description Association (Hong Kong) (AuDeAHK), a Hong Kong-based NGO. LMM-generated scripts will be evaluated using a mixed methods approach, including both automatic and human evaluation. A set of prompting guidelines will be drafted according to the findings. Finally, BPS participants will assess different types of AD scripts (LMM-generated, post-edited, and human-generated) in a reception study. Combining the input from AD creators and AD users will provide a comprehensive understanding of the potential of LMM-generated AD scripts. N/A

titleEnglish: Unbounded Labor: Life on the Digital Frontier of China’s Platform Economy
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
coInvestigators: Prof Tang, Shuangshuang
abstract: The global proliferation of platform-based work signals a profound transformation in the contemporary labor landscape, creating a reality of what we term “Unbounded Labor.” This shift moves beyond flexible hours to a fundamental re-spatialization of work, transitioning from bounded workplaces to “workplaces as infrastructure.” In this model, the platform becomes the central node, generating the unique and flexible geographies of this digital frontier that actively blur the boundaries between work, home, and social life. While promising new avenues for livelihood, this unbounded reorganization is underpinned by a pervasive regime of algorithmic control, creating a new political economy that inevitably transforms the meanings, practices, and experiences of work. Platform jobs often appear to democratize access, yet algorithms mediate interactions, job dispatching, and performance ranking under a veneer of digital objectivity. This raises a critical question: does the perceived freedom and flexibility genuinely enhance empowerment, or do these systems, despite claims of neutrality, encode and perpetuate social biases? Drawing on theories of intersectionality, we interrogate how gender, age, and socioeconomic class shape experiences on this frontier. Furthermore, the study investigates the erosion or reconfiguration of worker agency, asking how algorithmic management constructs divergent workday realities and fosters new, contingent work identities. To investigate these complex socio-technical relations, this project employs a mixed-methods approach in Jiangsu, China, combining a quantitative questionnaire of 2,000 workers with in-depth qualitative methods: participant observation and mobile interviews with 24 workers across four distinct platform roles (food delivery, ride-hailing, vlogging, and livestreaming). This comparative design is crucial for analyzing three key dynamics: the selection and self-selection into different platform roles; the navigation of intersecting demands like migration and family; and the ways workers’ daily tactics negotiate the tension between the platform’s regime of mobility/fixity and the mobilities/immobilities of personal life. As digital technologies relentlessly remake labor markets worldwide, China’s vast and dynamic platform economy offers a critical, instructive case study of this global shift. Our analysis provides a nuanced understanding of how workers from diverse backgrounds strategize within platform systems, and how new subjectivities are forged at the nexus of technology, space, and labor. The findings aim to generate actionable insights for policymakers and platform designers, contributing to more equitable regulatory frameworks and technological designs that genuinely enhance worker safety and well-being, mitigate embedded biases, and reduce adverse social consequences for this expanding workforce of “unbounded” laborers. N/A

titleEnglish: A Study of Assistive Technologies for Supporting Family Caregivers of Older Adults in Hong Kong
department: Academy of Wellness and Human Development
coInvestigators: Prof BAI, Xue Prof Cheng, Reynold Chun-Kong Prof KAN, Wing Shan Prof LAI, Wing-Leung Daniel Prof Wong, Yu Cheung
abstract: Family caregivers in Hong Kong face heavy strain with insufficient tailored support. While software-based assistive technology may help with accessing community information, reaching out to support services, and peer connections, many caregivers face challenges in adoption due to usability, confidence, cost, and attitudes towards technology. The proposed study will focus on software-based assistive technology in Hong Kong—specifically, mobile apps, websites, and online platforms—to examine how caregiving demands, the nature of the care recipient’s primary impairment (cognitive or physical), and anxiety about technology shape caregivers’ views on usefulness and ease of use. The study will also examine how these views influence caregivers’ actual use of these digital platforms and how such use relates to their well-being. The study will also consider the reasons behind adoption or rejection, as well as what caregivers want from the design and support of these tools. Through an explanatory sequential mixed-methods design, Phase 1 will include a quantitative random telephone survey of 800 Hong Kong caregivers aged 40 and above caring for older adults with long-term needs, technological anxiety, technological perceptions, usage of major assistive platforms in Hong Kong (e.g., HA GO, eHealth, Information Gateway for Carers, OK Care, Evercare, and others), and their well-being. This will be followed by Phase 2, which will comprise qualitative in-depth interviews with 40 family caregivers to identify the contextual barriers and facilitators they experienced in using assistive technologies. The expected outcomes include clear, practical recommendations to improve the design of applications and training, outreach for low-literacy users, and policy actions to reduce barriers—ultimately helping caregivers use digital platforms more easily and thereby enhancing their daily care. N/A

titleEnglish: The Impacts of Social Media-Based E-Portfolios on Writing Performance and Writing Anxiety among Hong Kong Tertiary EFL Students
department: Academy of Wellness and Human Development
coInvestigators: Dr GAN, Ling
abstract: In English as a second or foreign language (EFL) writing pedagogy, significant attention has been paid to electronic portfolio (e-portfolio) assessment through social networking sites in the past two decades. Nonetheless, little is known about how WeChat (i.e., an instant messaging cum social networking app), which has 1.15 billion active monthly users, can be used as a platform for e-portfolio assessment to identify junior undergraduates’ learning of academic writing and their levels of anxiety when handling programme-based writing tasks. This proposed study intends to investigate the impact of a WeChat-based e-portfolio assessment, one popular social networking tool in Asia, on L2 writing performance and writing anxiety levels among Hong Kong undergraduates, namely in their freshmen and sophomore years, as well as their perceptions of using this assessment tool. A quasi-experimental design will be employed, recruiting 320 freshmen and sophomores from two public-funded, comprehensive universities in Hong Kong, which represent a total of eight public-funded universities in the territory. Participants will be randomly assigned to either an experimental group that adopts WeChat-based e-portfolios (N = 160, comprising four intact classes from one university) or a control group using paper-based portfolios (N = 160, comprising four intact classes from another university). Both groups will undergo pre-test, intervention, and post-test procedures, and their L2 writing performance and anxiety levels will be measured before and after the intervention by using the International English Language Testing System (IELTS) Writing Task 2 with its rubrics and Second Language Writing Anxiety Inventory (SLWAI), respectively. After the post-test, semi-structured focus-group interviews (n = 80) will be conducted to gather insights into participants’ perspectives on utilizing WeChat-based e-portfolio assessment. The quantitative data will be scrutinized via the paired t-test and the independent sample t-test. Concurrently, the qualitative data will adhere to the six prescribed procedures put forth by Creswell and Clark (2011). The findings of this study can enrich the existing knowledge base of e-portfolio assessment, shed new light on the effectiveness of using social media-based e-portfolios for L2 writing assessment, and provide scientific empirical evidence for the use of alternative assessment approaches along with the trend of integrating information and communication technology into L2 writing assessment in the Hong Kong higher education landscape and beyond. N/A

titleEnglish: Framed and Fighting Back? Media Sexism and Women Leaders' Digital Strategies in Asia
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
coInvestigators: Prof Rowlett, Benedict
abstract: This project addresses gaps in our understanding of media sexism and the strategies employed by political and business leaders, with a focus on women in South Korea, Taiwan, Singapore, and Hong Kong between 2020 and 2024. Most research on media sexism centres on Western contexts and political figures, leaving the experiences of women leaders in other regions and sectors insufficiently explored. By broadening empirical inquiry to these East and Southeast Asian settings and including both political and business sectors, this study aims to provide a more comprehensive account of gender bias in media. First, the research will undertake a comparative analysis of traditional news media portrayals, examining how key women leaders are depicted relative to male counterparts. Specifically, the project will investigate different facets of gendered coverage, including attention to feminine/masculine policy issues, personal life or appearance, use of gender identifiers, tone, and coverage volume. This multidimensional approach enables a nuanced assessment of media bias. Moreover, the cross-national and cross-sectoral design will reveal whether similar stereotypes persist across democratic and authoritarian regimes or differ between political and business sectors. Second, given the growing influence of digital media platforms, the study also examines how leaders use social media such as Facebook and LinkedIn to respond to biased portrayals in traditional news media. By analysing their digital communication strategies—such as the content they share online—the research explores how women leaders challenge stereotypes and how their approaches differ from those of their male counterparts. For example, they may highlight agentic qualities or emphasize engagement with policy issues that are traditionally associated with men. This analysis will shed light on how digital communication can be leveraged to counter or reshape dominant narratives. Methodologically, quantitative analysis will be applied to the coded dataset of news articles and social media posts, using descriptive statistics and regression models to identify patterns and associations between gender, sector, and coverage characteristics. Qualitative corpus-assisted critical discourse analysis will then be conducted, allowing for a close examination of how language and framing construct or challenge gender stereotypes. The study promises a substantial contribution to understanding media sexism and gendered leadership image management in Asian contexts. Beyond advancing theory, it will produce a new dataset on gendered media coverage and digital self-presentation strategies. The findings will also offer practical guidance for policymakers, media organizations, political parties, and businesses seeking to monitor and mitigate gender bias in leadership representation. N/A

titleEnglish: A dual-focused interpersonal emotion regulation model for parents of children with special educational needs: A mixed-methods longitudinal network analysis study
coInvestigators: Prof Epskamp, Sacha Prof Hofmann, Stefan G. Prof Kwok Lai, Sylvia Yuk Ching Prof Pan, Jiayan Dr Samson, Andrea
abstract: In Hong Kong, there has been a steady increase in the prevalence of children with reported special educational needs. Internationally and locally, approximately 30% to 40% parents of children with special educational needs experience elevated depressive and anxiety symptoms. High parental stress and mental health problems can lead to negative parenting behaviours and adversely impact children’s development. Unfortunately, several incidents of abuse of children with special educational needs and even homicide due to adverse parental mental health have been reported in Hong Kong in recent years, raising significant concerns about parental stress and mental health. Current interventions for parents of children with special educational needs typically focus on either parental stress management or parenting skills training. These approaches demonstrate inconsistent effectiveness in improving parental mental health and may not adequately secure healthy functioning in the parent-child dyad. Emotion regulation (ER), a crucial transdiagnostic factor linked to mental health and a promising intervention target, remains understudied in this population. Grounded in the recently developed interpersonal ER framework, this study proposes a reciprocal interpersonal model of dual-focused (i.e., self-focused and child-focused) ER for parent-child dyads in families with a child with special educational needs. Using both advanced network analysis and qualitative inquiry, we will investigate the reciprocal mechanisms between parents’ and children’s ER. This is a 3-year mixed-methods research design comprising a 3-wave longitudinal observational study and a qualitative study. Stratified cluster random sampling will be adopted to recruit 650 parents of a child with special educational needs in Hong Kong. Network analysis will be adopted to explore how parents’ self- and child-focused ER strategies connect to children’s ER and parents’ mental health, as well as how parents’ and children’s ER reciprocally impact each other. Semi-structured qualitative interviews will be conducted among 45 parent-child dyads to enhance the richness and in-depth understanding of the contextual factors influencing the interpersonal ER process. This study aims to provide an in-depth understanding of the reciprocal dynamics between parents’ and children’s ER and mental health, contributing to ER theory in parent-child interactions. If supported, this model will address a critical gap in interventions by informing the development of a reciprocal, interpersonal, and dynamic ER coaching programme. This avoids negative consequences, such as family tragedies, and will potentially benefit over 80,000 families in Hong Kong. N/A

titleEnglish: Representations and Cultural Politics of Aging in Hong Kong Cinema After the Year 2000
abstract: Hong Kong cinema has been one of the most widely researched and discussed cinemas, but not many publications take aging and elderhood as the center of inquiry. In order to fill the gap, this book-length project is set to probe the representation of aging in the Hong Kong films produced after 2000 and uncovers the cultural politics involved with respect to the city’s changing socio-political realities in the new millennium. This study poses several questions: first, what are the narrative and aesthetic strategies to represent old age in contemporary Hong Kong films? Second, what concerns, sentiments, and power dynamics evoked by aging are unfolded in those films? Third, in what ways do the cinematically portrayed aging experiences have intersections and dialogues with the wider socio-political and cultural milieus of Hong Kong? Finally, how do the outcomes of study inform and mobilize the theoretical debates in the studies of Hong Kong cinema? These questions need to be answered with the help of systematic research of the cinematic discourse of elderhood in Hong Kong informed by theoretical formations of aging and cinema. Employing a qualitative, textual-cultural approach, the proposed project centrally explores how aging is represented in contemporary Hong Kong films and how the representations intersect and interact with pertinent cultural discourses by unraveling the complexities, perplexes, and tensions involved. By pursuing the methodological lens of age, this project traces and negotiates gerontological imaginaries as a form of texts that prospered in popular entertainment alongside socio-political, cultural, and industrial transformations. The PI renders textual analysis and cultural reading to anlayze the filmic image of aging, in association with theoretical inquiry of disability, fidelity of care, gerontological patriarchy, and gay sexuality. Building on the thriving area of research of Hong Kong cinema, this project intervenes into the current debate of popular representations of late life, which is seriously under-studied. It illuminates an effort to disrupt a Western-inclined critical mobilization of studying aging and cinema, offering an alternative to the existing scholarship in the context of Hong Kong. This project not only recalibrates the discursive vigor of elderhood in cinematic discourse but also reveals the potential to expand the paradigmic trajectory in the study of Hong Kong films. The research outcome leads to one international conference presentation and an academic monograph published by a university press. N/A

titleEnglish: Sensing Difference: Blindness, Deafness, and the Canon of Art History
abstract: Abstract: The proposed project investigates intersections between art practice and sensory disability in Renaissance and Baroque art. Situated at the crossroads between art history, social history and disability studies, it locates understandings of sensory loss in the context of history, social and religious practices, literature, visual and material culture. By looking at documentary sources, including first-person narratives, legal documents, poetry, sculptures, drawings, and paintings, it interrogates how early modern artists with sensory disabilities navigated their daily lives, professional careers, and personal relationships. While doing so, it highlights the fluidity of disability experiences, challenging enduring stereotypes and emphasising that in premodern societies responses to impairment were shaped by a variety of intersectional factors. While disability studies have recently become a robust aspect of historical research, they remain a neglected topic within early modern art history. Opening a new field of enquiry, the proposed project retraces the life experiences and professional careers of seven artists who experienced congenital or adventitious sensory loss: Benedetto Grazzini (1474–1552), Giovanni Paolo Lomazzo (1538–1592), Girolamo Padulo (ca. 1550–1600), Hans Verhagen (1554–1600), Giovanni Gonnelli (1603–1664), Wolfgang Heimbach (1615-1678), and Giovanni Lo Coco (1667–1721). Each career is presented as a microhistory: a case study that opens a window into broader historical and art historical questions. The project interrogates matters of artistic training, public reception, and patronage. It unravels the strategies adopted by artists with sensory loss to advance themselves both socially and professionally, debunking assumptions that identify them as self-taught outsiders. The study challenges the idea that early modern people with disabilities were necessarily living on the fringes of society and depended on the charity of temporarily abled people. It replaces this ableist notion with individual case studies that highlight the agency of artists with sensory disabilities in the workplace and within their family networks. Through in depth articulation, art historical, visual, cultural, and socio historical analysis, the project (i) challenges the exclusion of artists with disabilities from the canon of art history; (ii) presents a different way to look at how sensory disability was perceived in the early modern period; and (iii) investigates concepts of disability identity and disability gain, supplementing analyses and debates conducted in our contemporary world. N/A

titleEnglish: Remembering Axayacatl: The Life and Afterlives of an Aztec Emperor
department: Academy of Chinese, History, Religion and Philosophy
abstract: 'Remembering Axayacatl' is a project focused on writing a biography of an important, but little studied, Aztec emperor, Axayactl, and how he was remembered for approximately 130-years after his death. 'Remembering Axayacatl' will utilize sources found in colonial archives, rare and published manuscripts, and frame them within the context of popular Nahuatl language songs. These songs, found in the 'Cantares Mexicanos' manuscript, are central to this project because they are part of an oral tradition which was transcribed in the mid-sixteenth century, but had roots from long before the arrival of the Spanish. Song lyrics, and evidence about their performances from other sources, are ideal for framing this project for two reasons. First, the lyrics capture specific emotional and popular commentaries about Axayacatl that were handed down over generations. Second, these commentaries represent perspectives of a more diverse segment of the Nahua population (audience and performers) than other sources. The majority of other sources about Axayacatl were prepared by Indigenous nobles who wrote for other Nahua intellectuals, friars who focused on conversion efforts, or the second or third generation of mixed-ancestry nobility (Spanish and Nahua) invested in better positioning their home cities in Spanish political debates. The songs provide a unique and little studied entry point to analyze Axayacatl’s life and how it was remembered during the colonial period by people whose perspectives are normally considered to be lost to history. 'Remembering Axayacatl' will trace the communal memory and emotions of the residents of the Aztec city of Tenochtitlan and the transition to the Spanish run Mexico City from the 1460s to the late-sixteenth century. This focus will expand our understanding of how layers of communal memory and rulership worked in Nahua society that bridges the precolonial and colonial periods. Disrupting the commonplace division between colonial and precolonial will promote a new methodology from which to engage with song lyrics as political critiques based on historical memories while acknowledging the traumas of conquest and colonization. By emphasizing Indigenous voices as well as their own structures of historical memory, this study will show how an Indigenous people applied their own traditional knowledge and forms of history keeping to a quickly changing world. 'Remembering Axayacatl' engages the accessibility of a biography to discuss important scholarly conversations related to the knowability of the precolonial period while proposing and applying important theoretical framings in memory studies and the history of emotions. N/A

titleEnglish: Vivid Images: The Aesthetics of Animation in the Figural Media of Early Modern Japan
abstract: This project will provide a new understanding of figural media produced in the urban centres of early modern Japan. These included bidimensional figure paintings in manuscript (hanging scrolls, handscrolls, folding screens), figural depictions in print (single-sheet prints, book illustrations), and anthropomorphic three-dimensional media (dolls, puppets, ceramic statuettes). All of these figural media belonged to what came to be known as ukiyo 浮世 (‘the floating world’): a construct that celebrated the cultural practices associated with urban entertainment. The term ‘figural media’ is used because either specific named individuals, such as actors or prostitutes, or generic types were depicted. This project’s working thesis is that the effectiveness and appeal of these figural media relied on a slippage between reality and representation. The phenomenon amounted to an ‘aesthetics of animation’: the appreciation of images as potentially imbued with life, or having ontological potential. The term ‘animation’ is used here in the anthropological sense of attributing qualities of aliveness to inanimate objects, and should not be confused with the term from media and film studies denoting a moving-image medium. The main questions are: what were the roles of figural media in the ‘aesthetics of animation’ of early modern Japan? What were the patterns of interaction between figural media and their users? To investigate the questions above, the PI will deploy an interdisciplinary approach to discussing terms used to describe figural media in this period, such as utsushi, which will enable a theory of ‘floating world’ images. This will be complemented by analyses of stories and images of the time featuring figure paintings interacting with their users as if alive. The main outcome will be the publication of a monograph by a leading academic publisher. This objective will be achieved by applying the PI’s expertise in the history of the art and visual culture of Japan. The first phase of the project (4 months) will involve literature review and fieldwork to consult primary sources in Japan. The second phase (12 months) will consist of conference presentations, writing of the monograph manuscript, and securing the publishing contract, starting by proposing the book to Brill for inclusion in their Japanese Visual Culture series. The third phase (8 months) will comprise rewriting based on peer reviewers’ comments, resubmission of the manuscript and final editing for publication. N/A

titleEnglish: Framing European Union enlargement, 2004-2028: The EU between geopolitics, idealism and proceduralism
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
abstract: Whether, when, and how the EU should expand is among the most pressing questions facing EU policymakers. Russia’s February 2022 invasion of Ukraine put enlargement firmly back on the agenda, following a decade of ‘enlargement fatigue’. The post-invasion extension of a membership perspective to Ukraine, Moldova, and Georgia has also brought renewed attention to the Western Balkans candidates, and to the larger issue of the EU’s ‘absorption capacity’. The proposed project will provide a deeper understanding of present trends and likely future directions via the contextualisation of EU actors’ reactions to the invasion in a broader timeframe. Following the insights of discursive institutionalism, the project focuses on enlargement discourses, which constitute the space for political action. Using the method of framing discourse analysis, the project will track changing attitudes towards enlargement in three institutions – the European Council, the Commission, and the European Parliament (EP) – from 2004 to 2028. The EP’s inclusion is particularly novel. Evidence indicates that it is an increasingly important foreign policy actor and that party groups play a central role as inter-institutional mediators. Yet, the EP remains understudied in the enlargement space. Researchers will gather quantitative and qualitative data on the salience of enlargement, sentiment towards it, and the prevalence of identified frames from a range of primary sources, including European Council conclusions, speeches of the Commission President and High Representative, EP party group manifestos, EP resolutions, and semi-structured interviews. Analysis of the data will yield significant insights into attitudes towards enlargement within and across institutions, and how these have changed over time. Researchers will investigate whether and how exogenous shocks, including Russia’s invasion of Georgia (2008), the Russian annexation of Crimea (2014), the full-scale invasion of Ukraine (2022), and Donald Trump’s return to the US presidency (2025) have affected actors’ perspectives on enlargement. Beyond the compilation of a comprehensive dataset on discursive framings of enlargement, the project’s value-added is both empirical and conceptual. First, it will advance scholarly understandings of the EU’s strategic capacity, contributing to efforts to evaluate the EU’s ‘geopolitical turn’ amidst a weakening of the liberal international order. Second, it will investigate the dynamic interaction between enlargement frames, including the shifting construction of ‘merit’ as a geopolitical, normative, and procedural concept. N/A

titleEnglish: Of Myths and Mines: translating the mythological origins of gems in Sanskrit medical and gemological literature
department: Academy of Chinese, History, Religion and Philosophy
abstract: The project examines alchemical and gemological materials in the Sanskrit language dating from the ninth to seventheenth centuries that focus on the mythological origins and medical usage of nine gems: diamond, pear, coral, garnet/zircon, sapphire, cat eye, topaz, emerald, and ruby. I have identified 20 Sanskrit texts that contain material on the mythological formations, detailed descriptions, and preparations of gems for medicinal applications. The texts range from alchemical works (Rasapaddhati, Rasarnava, Rasendracudamami, and Rasaratnakara) to religious (Garudapurana) to gemological (Manimahatmaya, Manasollasa, Ratnadipika) to ayurvedic (Todaranda, Ayurvedaprakasa), demonstrating textual interconnectedness of works previously studied in isolation. A preliminary examination finds fantastical tales of jewels forming from the mouths and bodies of both gods and demons. Diamonds, for example, are said to have formed during the creation of the universe, when the gods drank the nectar of immortality which arose during the churning of the Ocean of Milk. As they drank, drops of the nectar fell to the ground and became diamonds. This myth uses the standard Hindu creation tale to explain a natural phenomenon in a new and unique way. Alchemical works differ from other medicinal manuals with their rich literary sections that use prevailing religious and mythological themes interwoven with more traditional explanations of disease, plants, and mineral materials. The detailed explanations of the characteristics of gemstones fit into the Hindu mythological worldview but are also analogue with modern scientific understandings of carat, faults, and other qualities. To date few studies have focused on gemological materials in Sanskrit, despite the importance of the Subcontinent to the global gem trade (India is one of the largest centres in the world for the polishing of precious gemstones). The most detailed study of gemological material related to this project is Louis Finot’s French language 1896 work Les Lapidaires Indiens. Dagmar Wujastyk’s 2023 article “Making Gems in Indian Alchemical Literature” discusses the making of artificial gemstones in a 15-17th century alchemical work. Neither of these works explore the myths and usage of medicinal gemstones. Finot is largely an edited edition and partial translation of several gemological works and Wujastyk states artificial gems recipes do not contain any medicinal usages. Through this study, I hope to demonstrate that in fact the alchemical works cross the boundaries of scientific, medicinal, geological, and social traditions that can help us understand the broader significance of healing within Subcontinental history. N/A

titleEnglish: Multi-scale assessment of methane emissions from coal mining operations in China
coInvestigators: Prof Lo, Tek Sheng Kevin Prof Lu, Xi Prof Qin, Kai
abstract: This project employs integrated multi-scale monitoring to quantify methane emissions from China’s coal mining sector, where current estimates rely predominantly on IPCC Tier 1 default emission factors with substantial uncertainties. It addresses critical data and knowledge gaps constraining evidence-based mitigation strategies needed to support China’s methane action plan and carbon neutrality commitments. Methane emissions from coal mining are a major climate concern, with CH4 having 80 times the warming potential of CO2 over a 20-year period. China produces nearly half of the world’s coal, yet atmospheric observations show methane levels that exceed inventory-based estimates. The lack of mandatory monitoring creates a regulatory gap, where operators prioritize safety-grade sensors over climate-relevant measurements, and reliance on self-reported data prevents accurate emission quantification. This measurement shortfall hampers China’s ability to establish empirical baselines and set quantitative reduction targets. The research methodology entails three interconnected tasks: (i) establishing empirical emission factors through hierarchical field measurements at 60 strategically selected mines in Shanxi, Inner Mongolia, and Shandong, using vehicle-mounted laboratories and statistical scaling relationships to extrapolate results to unmeasured mines; (ii) validating the multi-scale monitoring framework by reconciling mine-level measurements with regional atmospheric observations, incorporating uncertainty analysis across scales; and (iii) developing science-based policy frameworks that quantify technical reduction potentials using marginal abatement cost curves and evaluate differentiated regulatory pathways by mine size and ownership. Strategies to mitigate potential risks related to methodology and logistics are also proposed. The PI’s preliminary field studies in Shanxi (August 2024) demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed methodology through successful seven-day mobile monitoring campaigns at multiple operational mines, quantifying emissions from ventilation shafts, drainage systems, and coal storage areas. These pilot measurements identified previously unaccounted emission sources, underscoring the need for comprehensive field-based quantification. The PI’s team has the required instrumentation and technical expertise to implement the multi-scale monitoring framework. Established partnerships with local coal mining operators and environmental bureaus ensure site access and operational data sharing across the target mines. The expected outcomes will directly support China’s methane action plan by providing validated emission factor databases, provincial inventories with transparent uncertainty bounds, quantified reduction potentials by mine characteristics, and policy frameworks that guide measurable emission reductions in line with national climate commitments. These methodologies will also advance international climate mitigation efforts, particularly in countries facing similar data gaps when developing comprehensive methane management strategies. N/A

titleEnglish: Degrees of Inequality: University-to-work Transitions of Direct-entry and Transfer Students in Hong Kong
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
coInvestigators: Prof Postiglione, Gerard Dr Tang, Hei Hang Hayes
abstract: Hong Kong’s higher education system has undergone significant structural changes over the past two decades. In the early 2000s, sub-degree programmes were introduced primarily to provide students who fail to enter university some postsecondary education. For many years, only a small proportion of sub-degree graduates successfully transferred into undergraduate programmes. However, since 2014, the expansion and formalisation of articulation pathways have enabled a growing number of students to enter public universities through transfer, steadily increasing their representation within the university population. After more than a decade of policy implementation, it is timely to evaluate the equity implications of this shift. This research project examines whether the increasing presence of transfer students in public universities leads to equitable educational and employment outcomes. Although the expansion of higher education is often linked to greater social mobility, not all students benefit equally. The massification of undergraduate degrees has contributed to credential inflation, allowing students from privileged backgrounds to leverage additional resources and maintain a competitive edge in the labour market. As a degree alone no longer guarantees meaningful employment, unequal access to career preparation and institutional support may deepen existing disparities. This raises important questions: Are students from different entry pathways—direct-entry and transfer—equally prepared for the transition to work? Do their university experiences yield comparable advantages in the labour market? Focusing on the university-to-work transition—a critical phase where inequalities may be reinforced or reduced—this study adopts a qualitative, longitudinal design. It will conduct two rounds of in-depth interviews with both direct-entry and transfer students: first during their final year of university, and again within one year after graduation. This approach captures rich, contextualised narratives and reveals how students prepare for and navigate their post-university trajectories. Unlike quantitative studies that highlight broad trends, this method offers a nuanced understanding of how social, familial, and educational backgrounds shape initial labour market outcomes, providing deeper insight into the lived experiences and institutional dynamics that influence graduate success. By comparing learning experiences, access to resources, and employment trajectories, the study contributes to theoretical debates on the equity implications of higher education expansion. It also offers policy-relevant insights into how universities can better support diverse student populations in preparing for employment. Ultimately, this research seeks to inform institutional practices and policy development that promote more inclusive and equitable outcomes in Hong Kong’s evolving higher education system. N/A

titleEnglish: Can Large Reasoning Models Think Critically and Creatively? A Fundamental Evaluation of Faithful Reasoning, Anthropomorphic Tone, and Abduction
coInvestigators: Prof Beni, Majid D. Prof Copeland, Jack
abstract: Evidence suggests that large reasoning models (LRMs)—AI systems trained to reason—lack robust critical thinking abilities. They produce persuasive chains of thought not faithful to underlying inference, perform inconsistent reasoning, fail to use explicit procedures, overthink with missing premises, and exhibit motivated reasoning fallacies. If AI systems cannot reason critically and logically, the future risks intellectual contamination: confident synthetic text without substance. We will test whether today’s LRMs, which may sound convincing, can truly reason critically and creatively. Our logic-first project asks three questions: Do LRMs follow valid steps from premises to conclusions and handle tricky cases correctly? Can they keep explanations consistent, checkable, and backed by real evidence? Are they capable of abductive reasoning—proposing a plausible hypothesis for a surprising observation and outlining what to check next? We focus on separating faithful reasoning from anthropomorphic tone, ensuring that evaluations reward warranted inference rather than polished rhetoric. We will develop: (A) clear, testable standards for good reasoning—validity, correct use of conditionals and modality, transparency of warrants (why a claim follows), and creative hypothesis making with a concrete inquiry plan; (B) a task suite that separates substance from style by testing LRMs under tone-neutral prompts; (C) faithfulness audits that track each step in a chain of thought, verify outputs, and compile a failure atlas explaining where and why LRMs go wrong (e.g., fallacies or untestable storylines); and (D) “deliberation as inquiry” scaffolds—lightweight prompts requiring a hypothesis, reasons, and a testable next step—to determine whether structure improves reasoning quality beyond mere fluency. Audits will flag missing premises, non sequitur steps, and fabricated tool-traces with scoring protocols. The project will contribute: (1) philosophy and logic benefits—a rigorous account of what should count as reasoning in modern LRMs; (2) education benefits—open exercises, fallacy labs, and rubrics that help value process, verification, and originality; (3) policy and procurement benefits—simple checklists and open benchmarks that distinguish reliable reasoning from polished rhetoric; and (4) dissemination—three peer reviewed open access journal articles, an open repository with tests, scoring scripts, and an educator kit, plus a workshop to support adoption of findings and policy recommendations. We will engage academic, policy, and education communities through active dissemination and outreach. All releases will be open access, and negative results will be documented to build trust and reproducibility. N/A

titleEnglish: Promoting stress resilience: Investigating the effects of a theory-based stress optimization mindset intervention for university students in Hong Kong
department: Academy of Wellness and Human Development
coInvestigators: Prof HUANG, Yi Prof Jamieson, Jeremy Dr ZHEN, Shanshan
abstract: Academic stress is a pervasive issue in Hong Kong’s competitive educational landscape, with over 40% of university students reporting moderate-to-severe depression and anxiety, directly impairing academic functioning. While traditional interventions focus on stress reduction, contemporary theories suggest that stress can be a pathway for enhanced performance and growth. Our approach is grounded in the synergistic mindset model, which integrates two powerful psychological frameworks: a growth mindset, which reframes challenges as opportunities for development, and a stress-can-be-enhancing mindset, which reinterprets physiological arousal to stress as a source of energy. While effective in Western contexts, a direct application faces limitations due to cultural differences, the transient nature of single-session interventions, and an unclear understanding of its underlying mechanisms. To address these gaps, this project aims to firstly, culturally adapt and deliver a multi-session synergistic mindset intervention. We will reframe the intervention to align with collectivist values, positioning the growth mindset not only as a tool for personal improvement but also a modern embodiment of the Confucian ideal of self-cultivation. We will also conceptualize the stress-enhancing mindset using culturally congruent concepts, portraying the physiological sensations of academic pressure as a state of being inspired and energized. Additionally, the multi-session format will reinforce the mindset over time, embedding it as a habitual response to real-world academic stressors. We will adopt a 3-arm randomized controlled trial with pre-, post-, and 6-months follow-up assessments to examine the effectiveness of the intervention. The primary outcomes include academic achievement, stress mindset, and cognitive stress appraisal, the last of which will be measured via physiological responses (heart rate and skin conductance). Secondly, we will identify key moderators to understand for whom the intervention works best. We will investigate baseline stress levels, socioeconomic factors, and culturally-specific thinking styles to quantify heterogeneity in treatment effects. Thirdly, using fMRI combined with a stress-inducing task, we will uncover the underlying mechanistic evidence for how the intervention works. It allows us to identify the specific neural pathways of change, which is critical for optimizing the intervention’s core components and predicting for whom it will be most effective, thereby enhancing its future application and personalization. By employing a Randomized Controlled Trial combined with physiological and neural measures, this research will not only provide a validated tool for promoting student well-being and academic resilience but will also yield important insights into the brain mechanisms that facilitate stress optimization. N/A

titleEnglish: Applying the Risk Information Seeking and Processing (RISP) Model to Explain Cancer Information Acquisition and Processing
coInvestigators: Prof Griffin , Robert J. Mr ZHU, Yuanxing
abstract: The project is in communication studies’ subfield: health communication. To explain cancer information acquisition and processing behavior, our project adopts a social scientific, etic approach and uses the Risk Information Seeking and Processing (RISP) model as the theoretical framework to conduct surveys with the general public and individuals with cancer experience. To deepen understanding, we also conduct in-depth interviews with both groups to triangulate the survey results. Cancer is a leading cause of death worldwide including Hong Kong. To address this rising cancer burden, Hong Kong’s government formulated the “Hong Kong Cancer Strategy 2019.” One key strategy is to effectively disseminate cancer messages through an approach consistent with the paradigm of people as consumers of information and as active participants in the health decision-making process. However, cancer communicators face the challenges of how to design audience-centered interventions to effectively address different target audience groups’ information needs. Crucial to addressing the challenges is the understanding of how people acquire and process cancer information. People can acquire information through two modes: information seeking (volitionally attend messages from selected communication channels for reaching a desired informational goal) and information scanning (spontaneously obtain information of interest). People can process cancer information also through two modes: systematic processing (more cognitively energetic) and heuristic processing (superficial and less effortful). More or less effort in acquiring and processing information can lead to varying degrees of health behavior engagement. The literature on cancer information acquisition and processing has several weaknesses: (1) lack of theory-driven studies, (2) little research compares the differences of information acquisition and processing behavior across different target audience groups, (3) virtually no research to examine information processing behavior in the cancer domain, (4) lack of a theory-driven audience segmentation tool to design more tailored intervention messaging, and (5) variation in prevention and control behaviors across acquisition and processing modes remains unexplored. Our proposal addresses these research gaps by conducting two surveys and two in-depth interviews—with the general public and individuals with cancer experience. We aim to identify factors influencing modes of cancer information acquisition and processing, and to compare prevention and control behaviors across these modes. This project will not only advance the RISP model by examining its capacity as an audience segmentation tool, but it will also guide cancer communicators to design audience-centered informational campaign to motivate people to acquire and process cancer information. N/A

titleEnglish: Empowering linguistic and ethnic minorities in Hong Kong through community translation: A communities of practice framework
department: Department of Translation, Interpreting and Intercultural Studies
coInvestigators: Dr Bartindale, Tom Dr Neather, Robert John
abstract: This project examines community translation in and for Hong Kong’s South Asian (SA) communities, particularly those whose participation in society is hindered by a lack of proficiency in Chinese and/or English, the city’s official languages. Community translation is important for debates around social inclusion, the relationship between language and identity, and the impact of technology on communication involving minority languages. Research on community translation has hitherto comprised largely product-oriented studies focusing on its social function, power asymmetries between public service providers and minority language speakers, and the modalities of community translation. Translation policies have also gained scholarly attention. However, several issues remain unexamined. These include: the process of community translation as a collaborative act, the ways in which identity is manifested through participant interactions, the power dynamics between majority and minority languages and their speakers, and the meaning of community translation as a practice and a concept. These issues have been particularly under-researched in Hong Kong, not least because multilingualism and multiculturalism are generally less visible than in societies where immigration is more high-profile. This project approaches these issues by focusing on community translation undertaken collaboratively by different stakeholders, including SA communities, a partner NGO, professional minority language translators, and trainee translators. Employing an ethnographic action research methodology, the project investigates community translation from three dimensions – its definition, the collaborative process, and the identity of the participants. It also examines how SA participants’ self-perceptions can be leveraged to develop more effective and inclusive minority language provision. The project will firstly evaluate the needs of SA populations by reflecting on experiences and findings from previous collaborations and discussing with stakeholders. This will inform the subsequent design of translation and research workshops involving human-to-human and human-computer interaction, implemented with the partner NGO and focusing on two key domains: education and healthcare. Thematic analysis, combined with sociolinguistically informed narrative analysis, will be applied to examine the data collected from ethnographic observations, interviews, focus group discussions, written reflections, workshop materials and translated products. ‘Communities of practice’ theory will be applied in interpreting the data. The findings will be communicated through scholarly publications, the project website, and a policy report written jointly with our partner organisation. This project will significantly contribute to expanding existing conceptualisations of community translation and produce a ‘communities of practice’ theoretical framework for studying translation in and for linguistic minority contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Writing Between Cities: A Literary Exploration of Non-Place, Liminal Space, Dis-appearance and Transience in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay
coInvestigators: Prof Lo, Kwai-cheung
abstract: This practice-led research project explores how creative writing might respond to the loss of traditional, anthropological place in the age of supermodernity, when discrete place, and the communitas and identities it generates, has traditionally been the chief driver of story. Hong Kong will be taken as primary focus, as a paradigmatic supermodern city characterizable by proliferating non-places (such as airports, chain hotels and motorways) per Marc Augé’s (2008) designation, characterizable, too, by transience and dis-appearance (due to mobile residents) per the writing of Akbar Abbas (1996). Patrick Shanahan refers to the environs observed by Augé and Abbas—where we never feel estranged, yet never at home—as the ‘post-landscape’; Manuel Castells (1997) adds that a constellation of sleepless nodes he calls the “global capitalist space of flows” has usurped the space of places and traditional cities. The project, then, extends to the post-landscapes of Greater Bay Area—especially to Shenzhen and Macau—to examine how these nodes of global transit communicate and what representational strategies are demanded for sympathetic depiction and understanding of both the built environments and the people who reside in and transit through them. So the project asks: What formal, aesthetic, and stylistic literary strategies might sympathetically depict the supermodern post-landscapes of Hong Kong and Greater Bay Area cities (Shenzhen, Macau), and how might these depictions reveal insights into residents’ reclamation of belonging, identity, telos, and sacred connections in transient urban environments? To address this question, the project adopts a mixed-method approach, developing Narrative-led Spatial Inquiry (NSI), a bespoke adaptation of practice-led research, where interpretive phenomenology and embodied, in situ writing are complemented by iterative storytelling. Combined with stylistic analysis of Greater Bay Area post-landscapes and phenomenological fieldwork, NSI aims to reveal new representational strategies (aesthetic, stylistic, formal), new modes of belonging, and cultural meaning—even the potential re-discovery of sacrality—in apparently banal, cultureless spaces. The findings will be disseminated through two critical papers and an edited volume (Writing Non-places, Bloomsbury/UWA Press) on non-places and transient spaces, and the novel One Thousand & One Nights in Hong Kong. These will inform the PI’s teaching at HKBU, workshops at the Australian International School of Hong Kong, and public talks at such as the Hong Kong International Writers Festival and Brisbane Writers Festival. N/A

titleEnglish: Scrolling into Distress? A Three-Wave Longitudinal Investigation of How and When Short-Form Video Affects Hong Kong Adolescents’ Well-being
abstract: The dominance of short-form video has raised urgent concerns for adolescent well-being, primarily through the rise of “mindless scrolling”—a passive, habitual form of consumption linked to negative mental health outcomes. Adolescents are uniquely vulnerable to this behavior due to neurodevelopmental factors that make them highly susceptible to the platforms’ immersive, reward-driven designs, which can lead to fragmented attention, guilt, and a decline in psychological health. Despite these mounting concerns, a clear understanding of the specific mechanisms through which short video use impacts adolescent well-being remains lacking. Current research is limited by two critical shortcomings: a conceptual gap, where an over-reliance on addiction models pathologizes common behaviors and overlooks both mundane harms and protective behaviors; and a methodological gap, where a lack of longitudinal, within-person studies prevents an understanding of the dynamic, reciprocal, and person-specific effects of short-video use. Drawing on the Differential Susceptibility to Media Effects Model (DSMM) and the Integrative Model of Mobile Media Use and Need Experiences (IM3UNE), this project will provide a more precise, nuanced understanding of how, when, and for whom short-video consumption impacts adolescents in Hong Kong. Specifically, the study will identify key differential-susceptibility variables (trait self-control, habit strength, affiliative tendency) that predict mindless and procrastinatory short-video watching behaviors. It will also investigate potential reciprocal relationships between mindless and procrastinatory short-video use and adolescent well-being overtime. It will further examine the mediating role of media-induced recovery experience and digital harms (distraction, fatigue, time wasting) in this process, while also investigate whether sense of coherence moderate these effects to explain why some adolescents are more vulnerable than others. To achieve these aims, the project will employ a three-wave longitudinal panel design, surveying a sample of adolescents recruited from secondary schools in Hong Kong using a cluster sampling. Findings will clarify the heterogeneous, person-specific, and dynamic effects of short-form video, informing the development of targeted, evidence-based interventions that move beyond blanket bans. Ultimately, this research aims to empower adolescents, parents, and educators with the nuanced knowledge needed to foster healthier digital habits and support adolescent well-being in an increasingly saturated media environment. N/A

titleEnglish: At the Margins, Across Borders: Translocal Uses of Preexisting Music in Postwar Hong Kong's Cantonese Social Realist and Populist Urban Comedy Films, 1953-1968
coInvestigators: Dr Law, Tsin Fung Angela
abstract: This project explores the translocal use of preexisting music in postwar Hong Kong Cantonese films from 1953 to 1968, focusing on two contrasting genres: social realist dramas and populist urban comedies. Cantonese films were central to Hong Kong’s popular culture, often surpassing Mandarin and Hollywood productions in local popularity. While the filmmakers often worked under tight budgets and relied heavily on borrowed music, these soundtracks played a key role in storytelling, shaping emotions, and reflecting Hong Kong’s unique translocal cultural identity. Our study challenges the common dismissal of preexisting music as cheap filler or ‘canned music.’ Instead, we position it as a site of translocal creative production. Borrowed music was not simply a cost-saving measure but a dynamic, artistic element shaped by the interplay of political ideologies, market forces, and the visions of directors and music editors. These forces combined uniquely in Hong Kong cinema to create mixed soundscapes that integrated Hollywood scoring techniques with diverse musical traditions including classical, jazz, Latin, pop, and rock. The study uses a purposively selected sample of 30 films—16 social realist dramas from the left-wing Zhong Lian studio and 14 populist urban comedies starring Sun Ma Sze Tsang—to ground our analysis in concrete cases. Building on this foundation, the research articulates two interrelated theoretical advances: first, distinct translocal mix variants—patterns of how borrowed music is selected, adapted, and integrated across genres; and second, the conditions under which these variants arise—political ideologies, market dynamics, directorial visions, and the networks of music editors. Methodologically, our approach combines detailed film-by-film coding of musical cues, close textual analysis, and archival research. The resulting insights connect genre-specific musical practices to broader political and economic contexts, offering a theoretical framework for Cantonese film history built on the overlooked ingenuity embedded in preexisting film music during a pivotal era. It also has broader impact, encouraging re-evaluation of peripheral cultural forms and enriching transnational perspectives on creative production in postwar Asian cinemas. The project will generate open-access outputs, scholarly articles, and public-facing material that illuminate the creative genius hidden at the margins of Cantonese cinema. N/A

titleEnglish: Assessing Inequalities in Objective and Perceived Healthcare Accessibility and Environmental Exposure Leveraging Large Language Models
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
coInvestigators: Dr Guan, Xiaodong Dr Ou, Yifu
abstract: Since the United Nations introduced Sustainable Development Goal 3, "Good Health and Well‑being," a growing body of urban research has focused on improving healthcare accessibility to reduce public health risks. However, better access to healthcare does not automatically lead to better health outcomes. Areas with strong healthcare access can also suffer from environmental degradation: residents in urban centers may enjoy convenient medical services while facing higher air pollution and intensified urban heat island effects. Objective measures of healthcare accessibility and environmental exposure often diverge from people’s own perceptions, and those perceived measures—shaped by individual experience and judgment—can have a stronger influence on health behaviors and outcomes. Research that systematically quantifies gaps between objective and perceived indicators remains limited, largely because of data and methodological challenges in capturing subjective experience at scale. Moreover, spatial and social inequities can undermine potential public‑health gains. Because populations are unevenly distributed across cities, socially vulnerable groups are more likely to face both limited access to healthcare and greater exposure to environmental hazards, compounding health risks. To address these gaps, this project will: (1) identify spatiotemporal (mis)matches between objective measures of healthcare accessibility and environmental exposure; (2) develop a large language model–based approach to measure perceived accessibility and exposure and compare perceived and objective metrics; and (3) evaluate spatiotemporal and sociodemographic inequalities in the convergence or divergence between accessibility and exposure. Shenzhen is selected as the case study due to its diverse socioeconomic composition, the availability of detailed healthcare, environmental exposure, and population data, and its pronounced spatiotemporal variation in health risks. We will collect multisource data, including remote-sensing imagery, population datasets, points of interest (POIs), multimodal travel times, and healthcare capacity information. Objective healthcare accessibility and environmental exposure will be assessed using the Gaussian two-step floating catchment area (Ga2SFCA) method and resampling techniques, which better capture supply–demand dynamics in medical service allocation and reflect patients’ search behavior than conventional accessibility measures. This research offers two main contributions. Academically, it advances the urban health and social equity literature by uncovering spatiotemporal and sociodemographic inequalities in healthcare accessibility and environmental exposure using both objective and perceived metrics, and it is among the first to leverage large language models to analyze perceived accessibility and exposure. Practically, it provides actionable guidance for creating socially inclusive, healthier cities, shaping urban planning and public health policy. N/A

titleEnglish: Animal Representations in Chinese and Hong Kong Literature and Film (1980s–Present): Anthropomorphism and the Anthropocene
coInvestigators: Prof Zong, Emily Yu
abstract: This research explores how nonhuman animals are represented in literature and cinema from the 1980s to the present in mainland China and Hong Kong. Drawing on the emerging animal studies and environmental humanities, the project investigates how these representations shape—and are shaped by—human understandings of ecological issues mediated by human-animal relationships in rapidly modernizing societies. As modernization displaced animals from everyday life, their symbolic and material significance underwent a shift. Yet, in China, literary and cinematic portrayals of animals have persisted, reflecting complex societal transformations that extend beyond the Western context. From the resource exploitation of the 1980s to the ecological conservation campaigns of the 2010s, Chinese writers and filmmakers have used animals to allegorize social change, critique industrialization and bureaucracy, and explore human-animal relationships to rethink humanity. These narratives are especially prominent in works by ethnic minorities and rural communities, where human-animal bonds continue to hold cultural significance. In (post)colonial Hong Kong, animals serve more as metaphors for urban alienation, political resistance, and hybrid identities, though animal welfare also matters in some works. Representation, in this context, is not merely about depicting animals but about speaking for and to them—making animals matter in cultural discourse and matter to animals themselves. This project, by examining selected texts and films, seeks to discover new conceptual paradigms and imaginings of the human-animal relationship. By engaging with animals as active subjects rather than passive symbols, Chinese writers and filmmakers challenge anthropocentric worldviews and reimagine the complex connections between species. This project argues that anthropomorphism, often dismissed as unscientific, can be a vital tool for bridging human and nonhuman experiences. Rather than reinforcing human dominance, anthropomorphic representations may reveal affinities and shared vulnerabilities, particularly in an era of uncertainty brought forth by geopolitical tensions, climate crisis, and ecological collapse. Ultimately, this study contributes to environmental humanities by examining how Chinese and Hong Kong cultural producers envision a more-than-human world—one where animals are not just symbols but co-participants and co-agents in shaping ecological and ethical futures. N/A

titleEnglish: Mattering on the Margin: A Cultural Study of Ethnic Minorities’ Digital Creative Media Practices in Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Erni, John N Prof Fung, Anthony Ying-him
abstract: This proposed study aims to obtain an in-depth cultural understanding of how South and Southeast Asian ethnic minorities (EM) in Hong Kong utilize digital media for creative purposes and develop content strategies in a hybrid cultural context of Hong Kong. Although EM in Hong Kong are still marginalized from mainstream Chinese society in various aspects, the younger generation have become increasingly active on digital media. New means of digital creative practices have noticeably reshaped the nature, form, and structure of their cultural activities. The young generation of EM have spent their formative years to localize themselves in Hong Kong, and in the process witnessed and participated in the booming development of digital media. Are they becoming adept at utilizing multiple digital tools to create their own forms of mediated storytelling, in order to strive for social recognition? Theoretically, the project fills a research gap by articulating the notions of ‘marginality’ and ‘mattering’ as a critical lens of inquiry to guide the analysis of the EM's active digital practices. Originating in Education research, these interrelated concepts have been widely adopted in different minority studies. ‘Marginality’ is felt when an individual senses separation from others within the larger social system. When the need to matter is not fulfilled by the social environment, individuals may feel pushed to the margin of society and believe that they do not fit in. Meanwhile, ‘mattering’ reflects the relational process of developing one’s positive identity development and encompasses how individuals understand themselves vis-a-vis the others. Adopting a mixed methods approach, this study asks: 1) How do ethnic minorities in Hong Kong develop their sense of mattering through creative digital media use and content strategies despite, and because of, their marginality? 2) How does their participation in creative media help them overcome their social and linguistic limitations and strike alternative social and career pathways on the margin? 3) To what extent do their digital practices inspire their peers in their own search for robust self-understanding, social connectedness, and cultural belonging? The findings will provide important intercultural bearings for understanding the complex interplay among cultural alterity, creativity, active agency, and identity building. By doing so, it will generate a new direction for future societal discussion on the role played by savvy EM digital citizens in Hong Kong’s multicultural development. N/A

titleEnglish: Scaling up decarbonisation through voluntary, AI-assisted simplified energy auditing (AI-SEA) in the residential sector in Hong Kong
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
coInvestigators: Prof Chen, Yi-Ru Regina Dr Cheung, Darren Man-wai Prof LEUNG, Kwok Hi, Michael Prof Tai, Samson Prof Yun, Sun-Jin
abstract: The urgency of climate risks and energy insecurity heightens the importance of Hong Kong's 2050 carbon neutrality goal. Low-carbon technologies alone are insufficient for full decarbonisation. Artificial intelligence (AI) offers potential to scale energy-saving solutions, particularly in the hard-to-reach residential sector. In Hong Kong, while energy audits can address information gaps that hinder behavioural change, a major shortage of certified assessors makes large-scale audits costly and time-consuming. This raises critical questions: Can AI mainstream residential energy auditing to support Hong Kong’s 2050 target? Who, besides professionals, can AI empower to conduct audits effectively? How can Hong Kong leverage AI to deliver economic and environmental benefits while promoting digital inclusion? This interdisciplinary study aims to explore how a voluntary, AI-assisted simplified energy auditing (AI-SEA) programme can mainstream energy saving in the residential sector. Using a participatory mixed-methods approach, we will provide digital green-skill training to approximately 150 members from socially excluded and youth groups (including mentally disabled people, intellectually disadvantaged people, new immigrants, and ethnic minorities). Our trainees will conduct audits in 12 residential sites that represent diverse building and demographic features across Hong Kong. We will develop and test the effectiveness of our collaborative model of AI-assisted voluntary energy auditing, which can be replicated across sectors in Hong Kong. This study is novel in three key areas: First, it will test how integrating five AI methods (i.e. text analysis, object detection, thermal imaging, conversational AI, and natural language generation) can simplify energy audits, making them faster, cheaper, and more collaborative than traditional techno-centric approaches. Second, we will develop a three-stage capacity building and engagement programme that emphasises human inputs to improve the outcomes of AI methods. Thirdly, we will showcase how a voluntary, multi-sectoral collaborative initiative can align diverse interests, and ultimately enhance the government’s governing capacity. Our multiple case study across 12 residential sites will generate robust, new datasets, including real-time electricity profiles, building and occupant metadata, equipment inventories, quantitative survey data, and qualitative data from interviews, community dialogues, and a focus group. These datasets will provide a foundation for benchmarking residential energy performance, and enhancing policy developments. In collaboration with 4 organisational collaborators, a five-member multidisciplinary research team with expertise in AI, big data analytics, smart energy technologies, energy auditing, and science communication will deliver the project. Outputs include training handbooks, toolkits, workshops, focus group meeting, policy action plan, dashboard, and two journal articles. N/A

titleEnglish: Towards optimal AI-augmented simultaneous interpreting: Customizing tools and integrating cognitive individual differences
coInvestigators: Prof Cai, Zhenguang
abstract: High-quality real-time language services, such as simultaneous interpreting, are essential for effective global communication. While artificial intelligence (AI) has made impressive progress, it still struggles to match human interpreters, especially in situations where accuracy and cultural sensitivity are critical. Current AI interpreting systems often miss subtle cultural meanings or a speaker’s true intent, leading to serious misunderstandings. They also tend to produce longer delays than human interpreters, who typically maintain a brief lag of only a few seconds. Although human interpreters remain vital in high-stakes environments like diplomacy, law, and healthcare, there is growing interest in how advanced AI tools can support them effectively and reduce mental strain. This project aims to explore how different AI-assisted tools and interpreters’ individual skills, such as language proficiency, working memory, and stress levels, affect interpreting quality. The goal is to find the best combination of AI support that helps interpreters perform at their peak. To do this, the study will compare four types of AI tools against a no-tool baseline: live transcription, machine translation, keyword prompting, and an innovative multi-agent system developed by our team that allows interpreters to customize the type(s) of AI assistance they need. Thirty professional simultaneous interpreters from major organizations, including the United Nations and the Hong Kong government, will take part in realistic interpreting simulations to assess how humans and AI can work together most effectively. The study has three main objectives: first, to evaluate the overall effectiveness of each AI tool; second, to measure how these tools influence key aspects of interpreting—latency (speed), accuracy, and fluency; and third, to understand how an interpreter’s personal abilities and cognitive traits interact with these technologies. The findings will help interpreters choose and adapt AI tools that best fit their individual strengths, forming the basis for a new model of global competence in the language profession. This project matters because it will provide solid, research-based guidance to organizations around the world, helping improve communication accuracy in crucial settings like international hearings or courtrooms. It will also support interpreters’ well-being by reducing mental fatigue and the risk of burnout. Led by an experienced interpreter and psycholinguist who developed the customizable AI tool, our team has the expertise and proven methods needed to deliver meaningful insights into the future of human-AI collaboration in interpreting. N/A

titleEnglish: Migrant Density and Labor Market Outcomes of Migrant Workers
coInvestigators: Prof Kaushal, Neeraj Prof Pang, Yu Prof Ye, Han
abstract: Migrant density has grown substantially in many Western countries in recent decades, raising an important question: How does rising migrant density affect labor market outcomes for migrant workers? This issue remains underexplored in economics and social policy for two reasons.
