First, most migration studies focus on impacts to native populations, reflecting political concerns over immigration’s effects on citizens. Second, studies that do examine migrant density’s effect on migrants themselves mostly focus on earnings, with mixed results. Some suggest higher migrant density lowers wages, others find the opposite—likely due to limitations in measuring earnings. To address this gap, our project shifts focus to migrant overeducation—when workers hold qualifications exceeding job requirements. Overeducation is widespread among migrants and often results in lower earnings and underused skills, signaling labor market mismatches that prevent skilled migrants from using their full potential. We examine, both theoretically and empirically, how migrant density influences overeducation. Our theoretical model builds on our earlier work—Fan, Kaushal, and Pang (2025)—which employs a dynamic job search framework incorporating Becker’s theory of taste-based discrimination. That study shows that when wage equality is enforced, higher migrant density can reduce inequality by limiting discriminatory hiring. We extend this framework in two key ways. First, we account for not only employer bias but also potential productivity gaps among migrants due to language or cultural barriers. Second, we introduce educational heterogeneity, assuming that more educated workers are more productive. This forces employers to weigh their biases against potential productivity gains. We hypothesize that when migrant density is low, employers prefer natives and hire only highly educated migrants. As density increases, employers become less selective, narrowing the education gap between migrants and natives—our central hypothesis. Empirically, we use data from the Integrated Public Use Microdata Series (IPUMS) American Community Survey (ACS) from 2005 onward, combined with occupational data from O*NET. These sources allow us to construct two indicators of overeducation. Using instrumental-variable regressions, we will test two hypotheses: (1) Working-age male immigrants in the U.S. are more likely than natives to work below their qualifications; and (2) this overqualification gap narrows as migrant density rises. This approach introduces a novel lens on migrant labor market integration, highlighting how migrant density can shape labor market mismatches and influence the effective use of migrant talent. N/A

titleEnglish: Forging the 'Creative Proletariat': Vocational Education, Aspiration and Skill in China's Digital Economy
coInvestigators: Prof Koo, Ching Hua Anita Prof PUN, Ngai
abstract: Amid China's transition to a digital economy, this project investigates a state-led venture: the repurposing of the vast vocational education (VE) system to forge a new 'creative proletariat'. This inquiry is urgent as China confronts the dual imperatives of industrial upgrading and soaring youth unemployment, positioning VE as the primary engine to solve both. Providing the first bottom-up, ethnographic account of this venture, we analyse how VE attempts to transform youth from working-class and rural-migrant backgrounds into precarious digital creative workers. This project highlights vocational education for two critical reasons: 1) the VE system, with its vast scale and role in class stratification, is the key institutional site where the base of the creative economy is being systematically formed; 2) this process raises a core paradox that animates this study: how can an educational system historically oriented toward producing disciplined industrial bodies be radically retooled to cultivate entrepreneurial and creative subjectivities suited for a precarious creative industry that relies heavily on social and cultural capital? We approach this paradox not as a predetermined outcome, but as a complex, power-laden process of contestation. Our inquiry interrogates: 1) how an ethos of entrepreneurialism – encompassing autonomy, self-branding, and risk-taking – is instilled in students whose class background and educational trajectories offer limited safety nets against market precarity; 2) how schools attempt to mass-produce a ‘creative habitus,’ contested by new technologies and the students’ own class-based habitus; 3) how ‘aspirational indebtedness’ is produced and experienced as a precondition for market entry; and 4) what forms of collective consciousness and everyday resistance emerge amidst intense precarity. To answer these questions, this study employs a multi-sited comparative ethnography, contrasting a state-run 'flagship' college in Shenzhen with a private, market-driven one in Chengdu. Through intensive fieldwork, in-depth interviews tracing the student-to-worker trajectory, and innovative qualitative digital diaries, we will capture the textured reality of precarious creative work and the hidden costs of market entry. Academically, this project will substantially contribute to: 1) the global sociology of labour, by advancing an ethnographic theory of creative subjectivity formation outside a middle-class, Global North context and by theorizing ‘aspirational indebtedness’ as a novel mechanism of precarity; and 2) the sociology of education, specifically to the literature on school-to-work transitions and the political economy of skill formation, by offering a critical account of VE’s role in class formation for digital economy. N/A

titleEnglish: Inadequate housing and children's well-being in Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Gordon, David
abstract: This research concerns the links between poverty, housing and neighbourhood deprivation, and the well-being of children during the primary-secondary transitions. Hong Kong is a rich society with some of the least affordable housing in the world and housing inadequacy is one of the most pressing issues in exacerbating disadvantage among children. However, there is a scarcity of local literature incorporating the effect of inadequate housing on child well-being from children’s own perspectives. Since family resources, housing adequacy and employment opportunities have been significantly reduced and constrained due to the pandemic recession, it is believed that child poverty has increased significantly. Based on deprivation and exclusion frameworks, this study aims to fill the research gaps by simultaneously examining the links between child deprivation, deprived home and neighbourhood environments and child outcomes mediated by children’s social relationships. The research aims to examine how the assessment of such a link can be informed by the children’s voices. This study will address three important research questions: (1) What does ‘inadequate housing’ mean to children in middle childhood?; (2) How does poverty and inadequate housing affect children’s social-emotional functioning and self-reported well-being? ?; and (3) What do the empirical findings imply for policy and practice related to child poverty, well-being and housing design in Hong Kong? A mixed-methods approach will be adopted to gather data directly from children aged 11-13 years, through focus groups and school-based surveys for a holistic understanding of their views and lived experience. Focus groups are an integral part of the survey development process and will provide insights into the aspects of housing inadequacy which are of greatest importance to children. A child advisory group will help with interpreting the survey findings. A paired sample design is used. Primary caregivers will be invited to complete a short questionnaire which includes some housing history and affordability questions to obtain some insight into the cumulative effects of deprivation over the life course. This research will capture children’s views about their actual lived experiences and provides robust evidence about how multifaceted disadvantage at the child-, household- and neighbourhood-level shape children’s lives. This study has greater relevance to children themselves and will assist stakeholders from the education, housing, health and social welfare sectors to take evidence-based concerted actions that effectively and efficiently target children who are in the greatest need. N/A

titleEnglish: Meaning, Value, and AI Technology
abstract: Many of us find meaning in life through achievements, relationships, pursuing knowledge, creating art, developing skills, moral growth, or contributing to society. Until recently, these activities were uniquely human. Recent advances in AI are changing this picture. AI systems are now advancing science, creating art, even forming relationships with us. AI may increasingly displace humans from many activities which make our lives meaningful. Some theorists even speculate that AIs could eventually surpass us at all tasks (Bostrom, 2024). This raises pressing questions: how can humans live meaningful lives in an AI-dominated world? What becomes of human significance when our characteristic activities are no longer distinctively ours? These questions lie at the intersection of two active areas of philosophy: theories of meaning in life, developed by Wolf (2010), Metz (2013), and others, and the field of AI ethics. Theories of meaning vary, but most agree that (i) meaning in life is good for its own sake (ii) comes in degrees and (iii) is logically distinct from other normative categories, such as happiness or justice. Most theories converge on the idea that meaning arises from active, positive engagement with valuable things beyond ourselves, often characterized as ‘the good, the true, and the beautiful’. A life wholly lacking in meaning - even if filled with pleasure - appears radically impoverished. AI ethics has thus far focused on issues like existential risk, distributive justice, and AI suffering. Only recently has attention turned to the relationship between AI and meaning in life (Danaher, 2019; Nyholm & Rüther, 2023; O’Brien, 2025). While this literature identifies some threats, analyses remain piecemeal, and few solutions have been proposed. This project will address that gap. First, I will introduce the concept of M-Risks - risks that threaten the meaning in human lives - and develop a systematic taxonomy of how AI creates them. Second, I will evaluate the impact of different AI applications on human meaning and propose normative principles for the design, deployment, and governance of AI that safeguard and promote meaning in life. Third, I will address the broader question of how AI may reshape the cosmic significance and distinctive value of humanity itself, a question not yet directly examined in the literature. In doing so, the project aims to advance both philosophical theory and practical policy, ensuring that meaning in life remains central in the age of artificial intelligence. N/A

titleEnglish: Neural Heterogeneity in Youth Smoking: Rest-Task Vulnerability, Psychological Flexibility Protection, and Former-Smoker Validation
department: Wofoo Joseph Lee Consulting and Counselling Psychology Research Centre
coInvestigators: Dr Mak, Yim Wah
abstract: Smoking is the foremost preventable cause of morbidity, with 70–90% of quit attempts failing even among motivated individuals. This high failure rate suggests that uniform treatments overlook heterogeneous neurobiological mechanisms in tobacco use disorder. Converging evidence points to a Dual‑Route Neural Framework involving two distinct pathways: a Rest (state) route, representing chronic baseline neural dysregulation and internal distress, and a Task (cue/control) route, representing heightened cue reactivity and impaired inhibitory control. While these routes suggest distinct vulnerability profiles, critical gaps remain, which this study aims to address using EEG in young adults (18–25; N≈300): Within‑Person Route Independence and Specificity. No study has measured both routes within the same individuals to determine their partial independence. It remains unknown whether Rest‑ and Task‑route vulnerabilities show differentiated associations with baseline use and 6‑month abstinence. This study will derive stable within‑route Rest and Task composites and use regularized canonical correlation analysis (rCCA) descriptively to quantify cross‑route coupling within individuals and test partial independence. Route‑Matched Protection. Motivation alone is often insufficient. Psychological flexibility (PsyFlex)—the capacity to remain open to difficult experiences while pursuing valued actions—may offer protection. This study will systematically test the hypothesis that the acceptance dimension of PsyFlex preferentially protects the Rest route, while the values‑based action dimension preferentially protects the Task route. Neurobiological Subtyping and Validation in Former Smokers. The field has largely ignored former smokers, missing the opportunity to identify which neural changes normalize with quitting (state‑dependent therapeutic targets) versus persist (trait vulnerabilities). Leveraging unique access to a Youth Quitline, this study will use Gaussian Mixture Modeling to identify distinct subtypes (e.g., Rest‑dominant, Task‑dominant) and compare current, former, and never smokers to validate cessation mechanisms. Originality and impact. The originality of this study lies in its systematic, within‑person dual‑route neural subtyping in a critical developmental population. By including former smokers, we uniquely validate which neural markers enable versus follow successful cessation. This research provides the translational foundation to move beyond one‑size‑fits‑all approaches toward personalized, neuroscience‑informed cessation strategies matched to individual vulnerability profiles. N/A

titleEnglish: The Heart of Altruism
abstract: Altruism is fundamentally grounded in empathetic and compassionate concern for the well-being of others. However, ethical discourse concerning our duties of beneficence has tended to prioritise questions about what one ought to do over questions about what kind of person one ought to be when confronted with others’ needs. The growing influence of Effective Altruism, a relatively recent philosophical and social movement which encourages people to use evidence and careful reasoning to do the most good, underscores this tendency. Effective Altruists are notoriously dismissive of the motivational and affective aspects of philanthropy, advocating impersonal, ruthless decision-making, with the heart firmly reined in by the head. This research project seeks to redirect the discussion by shifting from an exclusively action-oriented framework to one that emphasises the moral significance of character and motivation in shaping our obligations of beneficence. Motives for acting have intrinsic significance independent of their outcome, and acting with the right motives is just as crucial to ethics as achieving the right outcomes. By integrating insights from virtue ethics with contemporary debates in normative ethics, the project aims to develop a more nuanced and psychologically informed account of beneficence—one that takes seriously the role of character and affect in ethical life. This philosophical inquiry is situated against the backdrop of overwhelming global and intertemporal suffering. Approximately 800 million people currently live in extreme poverty. At least 1.6 trillion animals suffer annually as a result of human activity, and wild animal suffering, though less well understood, may exceed human-caused suffering by orders of magnitude. Furthermore, if demographic projections prove accurate and the human population stabilises at around 11 billion by the end of this century, the long-term future may contain upwards of 100 trillion people over the next 800,000 years. The moral enormity of these facts raises urgent questions about the scope, content, and prioritisation of our altruistic obligations. The research will be structured around three core questions that will be approached from this alternative perspective: (1) What is the extent of our obligations to others? (2) Who or what should be included within our moral circle? and (3) Should considerations of effectiveness or sheer numbers influence our philanthropic priorities? In addressing these questions, the project aims to offer a comprehensive and virtue-informed account of beneficence that bridges the gap between ethical theory and real-world moral complexity. N/A

titleEnglish: Desire and Welfare
abstract: According to a family of views falling under the heading "desire satisfactionism," well-being consists, somehow or other, in the satisfaction of desire. In other words: what ultimately makes our lives good is getting what we want. This family of views holds pride of place in economics and decision theory, and many consider it the dominant approach among philosophers, economists, and others. Despite countless attempts to offer a more precise version of this approach, however, no attempt has gained widespread acceptance. Most everyone agrees that not every desire is relevant to well-being. The main disagreement--yielding countless versions of the view--concerns which subset of desires is relevant to well-being. My project's first aim is to show that, in fact, every desire is relevant to well-being. I'll thus argue against a fundamental assumption and starting point for theorizing in contemporary debate over well-being. Desires that don't seem relevant to well-being can always be made to seem relevant under appropriate conditions. I'll offer a systematic account of these conditions, and thereby show that all desires are indeed relevant to well-being. My project's second aim is to build on the foundations of the first, and to address one of the most persistent and damaging objections to desire satisfactionism: the problem of "remote desires." Intuitively, some desires--such as desiring that Napoleon's favorite color was blue--seem "too remote" from our lives to affect our well-being. Despite many attempts to solve this problem, no solution has emerged. Rather than proposing yet another solution, I will break the deadlock here by analyzing the problem itself. Specifically, I will provide the first systematic analysis of what makes a desire "remote." I argue that remoteness is a matter of how likely it is that the desirer will form a belief about whether they are getting the thing they want. This analysis reveals why the problem is so difficult to solve, and also provides an adequacy constraint on all desire satisfactionist theories--a constraint that rules out several important versions of desire satisfactionism, including those dominant in economics and decision theory. These contributions have important practical implications. The remote desires analysis shows that economists and decision theorists working on welfare policy may need to reconsider their basic theoretical assumptions. More generally, by clarifying which desires matter for welfare and under what conditions, the project offers both theoretical insights and practical applications for measuring and promoting human flourishing. N/A

titleEnglish: Trustworthy Stablecoins: Coordinating Centralized and Decentralized Finance at Scale
department: Department of Sociology and Social Policy
coInvestigators: Dr Wang, Zining
abstract: Stablecoins (STC) are digital assets layered on blockchains, on par with and backed by a basket of high quality, liquid traditional assets such as USD or US Treasurys. Licensing regimes for STC have been adopted in 2025 in several jurisdictions, including Hong Kong and the US (UK and the EU are following suit). This opens a broad avenue for STC institutionalization. Can stablecoins be trusted? They are often described as trustworthy because they are backed by high-quality collateral such as Treasurys. Yet in practice, trust in stablecoins cannot be reduced to collateral alone. The real challenge lies in the interface between two fundamentally different systems of finance: centralized ones, where trust is built through reputation, regulation, and professional expertise; and decentralized systems, where trust is anchored in code, cryptographic proofs, and software protocols. Stablecoins connect these two worlds directly: they are digital tokens circulating on blockchains, but they depend on collateral that exists within traditional, centralized finance. This interface generates a new kind of trust problem—one of coordination across systems that operate on different principles and at different scales. Digital wallets and bank accounts must interoperate securely. The parity between digital tokens and their collateral must be maintained continuously across both ledgers, avoiding risks such as double-spending, or deviations from parity. Stablecoins must navigate regulations across jurisdictions, with different expectations about what counts as credible collateral, secure code, or valid reporting. If stablecoins are to be successful, coordination must be achieved on all levels—micro, meso, and macro—and at scale, supporting millions of daily transactions. For social science, this is significant because existing theories of financial trust have largely focused on centralized finance. Much less is known about how trust is built and maintained when two distinct trust regimes must continuously work together. Stablecoins force us to reconceptualize trust not as something embedded in either institutions or code alone, but as a sociotechnical coordination problem spanning both domains. Adopting a mixed methods strategy spanning ethnographic and quantitative approaches, the project focuses on the following objectives: (1) To theorize trust sociologically when it is no longer “inside one domain” but “between radically different domains”. (2) To outline solutions for interfacing two (financial) systems that define, evaluate, and enact trust differently. (3) To identify the conditions under which these solutions work together at scale without breakdown. N/A

titleEnglish: After revolution, after narrative: problems of form and the quest for de-familiarization in roots-seeking literature
abstract: What happens to literature when the narrative certainties of revolution fade? This project seeks to answer this question through the frame of developments in 1980s Chinese literature. It does so with particular reference to the roots-seeking group of writers, consisting of writers who, from the mid 1980s, turned towards the resources of the Chinese countryside and ethnic minority communities as a source of inspiration and renewal in their literary experiments. In particular, it examines how, in theoretical writing as well as in their literary experiments, these writers came to re-imagine literature as no longer strictly bound to the category of narrative, meaning the recounting of events in time in their rational causative relations with one another. In place of narration, roots-seeking formal experiments came to imagine literature as possessing a capacity for “de-familiarization”, meaning the critique of established modes of sensation and cognition. In a search for non-narrative modes of writing, they recovered the cultural possibilities of the pre-modern Chinese essay form (sanwen, biji) as that form most adequate to responding to the cultural demands of the post-socialist moment. N/A

titleEnglish: Curating Art in East Asia: Exhibition as “Worlding” Practices in Global Museums and Contemporary Art Institutions
department: Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries
abstract: This research examines how exhibitions are curated to foster a critical discourse of “worlding” in East Asia, with a focus on Hong Kong and Taiwan. It considers the notion of “worlding” as a critical and creative intervention in knowledge generation, aimed at addressing the unfinished project of decolonizing and de-imperializing, as well as developing post-colonial subjectivity, in the region's global context. Recognizing world-making as practices to reveal multiple geo-cultural perspectives in imagining and pluralizing the “global,” as well as agencies to counter the dominant worlding, this research investigates how world-making has been practised in exhibition, as a critical tactic to de-colonize and develop a “world-in-becoming” that can deconstruct ideological power and radically create new signification for a specific condition. The research will also analyze the historical contexts in which diverse institutional, cultural, and socio-political forces have shaped the role and manifestation of exhibitions. It seeks to assess the impacts of these exhibitions on the "global art" discourse, and the address of new realities and the contemporary condition. This research extends the study of worlding practices to exhibition studies, identifying and constructing a new typology of “worlding” curatorial practice, that represents a significant shift in the region’s exhibitionary discourses in the twenty-first century. The study includes global museums and contemporary art institutions, to see how their exhibitions generate re- or counter-worlding discourses. These areas have received little attention in existing scholarship in Asia or Asia-Pacific, with most studies focused on artwork analysis while overlooking the agency of exhibitions in the region. This research will draw from exhibition case studies in Hong Kong and Taiwan, using ethnographical methods including textual analysis of exhibition-related materials, participant observation, media review analysis, and qualitative analysis of interviews with creative professionals, art communities, and exhibition visitors involved. The research will aid in understanding the contested discourse of worlding curatorial practice in the region. In particular, it will provide a critical understanding of the roles played by key cultural actors and institutions in Hong Kong and Taiwan and their curatorial practice in remaking the world in response to the challenges specific to their regional and global contexts. The studies will contribute to the unfinished project of decolonizing and de-imperializing in East Asia and offer a new point of departure for inter-referencing on counter-worlding activities in the region. N/A

titleEnglish: Dimensions of Pleasure: Qualitative Hedonism Revisited
abstract: The concept of pleasure features importantly in contemporary philosophical debates about well-being, or about what makes a person’s life go better or worse for them. Almost all philosophers of well-being agree that, at least in general, one’s life goes better for one to the extent that it is pleasant. But philosophers disagree about which experiences of pleasure are most beneficial from the standpoint of well-being. Some hold that only the quantity of pleasure matters, where quantity is traditionally understood as a function of pleasure’s intensity and duration. Others hold that some pleasures are higher quality than others, and are therefore superior to lower quality pleasures of equal intensity and duration. I aim to move this debate forward by bringing together contemporary research on well-being, consciousness, and affective neuroscience. In particular, the pioneering work of Kent Berridge and Morten Kringelbach has uncovered two distinct systems in the brain — the so-called "wanting" and "liking" systems — which are operative in many or all cases in which we pursue pleasurable activities. Roughly, “liking” is responsible for making it the case that mental states are experienced as pleasant, and “wanting” is responsible for producing a feeling of motivation or compulsion. I will argue for a distinction between what I call static pleasures and dynamic pleasures: static pleasures are experiences of pure “liking”, whereas dynamic pleasures are experiences of mixed “liking” and “wanting”. I will further argue that dynamic pleasures are more beneficial, or more conducive to well-being, than static pleasures. The difference in quality is explained by an underlying difference in quantity: if two subjects experience pleasures of equal intensity and duration, but the former subject’s pleasure is dynamic whereas the latter subject’s pleasure is static, the former subject experiences more pleasure. Thus, my project will attempt a grand reconciliation of quantitative and qualitative views about pleasure’s impact on well-being: quality of pleasure matters, because higher quality pleasures involve a greater quantity of pleasure. N/A

titleEnglish: Feeling Connected to My Conversational Artificial Intelligence (CAI): A Mixed-Methods Study on the Roles of Attachment to CAI in Adolescent Well-Being
coInvestigators: Prof Ni, Shiguang Dr Wang, Qi
abstract: Contemporary adolescents, who are growing up with ubiquitous digital technologies, are prone to form an emotional connection to conversational artificial intelligence (CAI) agents—AI systems that mimic human conversation (e.g., ChatGPT, Doubao). As adolescents are often under personal and interpersonal pressure while still developing their cognitive, social, and emotional capacities for coping, there have been public concerns about the risk that such emotional connection may pose to their well-being. However, little is known about how adolescent users’ emotional connection to CAI may influence their well-being. Although preliminary research has suggested that adults’ connections to CAI are associated with both positive and negative well-being outcomes, little research has elucidated the mechanisms through which these divergent outcomes occur. We will address these gaps through an attachment lens, conceptualizing adolescents’ connection to CAI as CAI attachment—an emotional bond that affords a sense of security and safety. Specifically, this research aims to address three questions: (1) How does CAI attachment influence adolescent well-being? (2) Who receives more benefits or fewer harms from CAI attachment? (3) How do attached adolescents experience CAI’s influences in daily life? By integrating attachment theories and media use theories, we propose a dual-path model, positing that CAI attachment will benefit adolescent well-being by enhancing socioemotional competence (i.e., social augmentation path) and impair it by increasing CAI dependence (i.e., social displacement path). Furthermore, we will examine the moderating roles of social richness and AI literacy in the aforementioned paths. Finally, to complement the quantitative evidence, we will qualitatively investigate the lived experiences of adolescents with strong CAI attachment. We will employ a mixed-methods approach by incorporating a three-wave longitudinal study (Study 1) and a semi-structured interview study (Study 2). Study 1 will test the hypothesized dual-path model based on high school students who use CAI (N = 860 at Time 1). Study 2 will provide qualitative evidence on the dual-path model and additional insights beyond the structured survey based on highly attached adolescents (identified from Study 1). The findings of this research will provide a nuanced understanding of how CAI may present developmental opportunities and risks to adolescents. These insights will help construct a novel theory that elucidates the effects of CAI attachment on adolescent well-being. They will inform the design and implementation of prevention and intervention strategies to help adolescents develop healthy relationships with CAI while cultivating technologies that promote their well-being. N/A

titleEnglish: Silver Threads: Weaving Narratives of Aging, Illness, and Identity across Sinophone Literature
abstract: East Asia is undergoing the world’s fastest demographic transformation: by 2050, more than 35% of the populations of Hong Kong, Taiwan, and Singapore will be over the age of 65. Yet modern Chinese and Sinophone literary studies have strikingly neglected aging. For over a century, youth has been celebrated as an emblem of progress and modernity, while the elderly have been cast as obstacles to renewal or symbols of tradition. Their lived experiences are rarely explored, leaving a glaring disconnect between demographic realities and literary scholarship. This project addresses that gap by reframing aging as a critical category in Sinophone literary studies through an interdisciplinary framework that integrates cultural gerontology (the study of aging through cultural perspectives), medical humanities, and literary criticism. It examines how Sinophone writers transform embodied and intersectional experiences of aging into innovative narrative forms. Offering the first comparative study of aging in Sinophone literature, the project focuses on three acclaimed authors: Hong Kong writer Xi Xi, Singapore writer Yeng Pway Ngon, and Taiwan writer Huang Chunming. Their careers exemplify the project’s central argument: that illness and aging, rather than signaling decline, can spark new literary vitality. Each writer confronted serious illness while writing about late life, creating a rare biographical convergence that shows how aging, understood both as a lived condition and literary theme, is experienced, narrated, and can be theorized in Sinophone contexts. Methodologically, the project combines close readings of their literature with intermedial analysis of their broader creative outputs, such as Huang Chunming’s torn-paper collages and Xi Xi’s handcrafted stuffed toys. This approach traces how illness reshapes artistic expression, revealing close links between personal vulnerability and late-life creativity. Drawing on narrative analysis, embodiment studies, and comparative Sinophone analysis, the project shows how illness catalyzes new ways of engaging aging, transforming vulnerability into a site of literary experimentation and self-redefinition. The project has three interrelated aims. Firstly, to reframe aging as a central analytical category in Sinophone literary studies; secondly, to expand cultural gerontology beyond its Euro-American foundations by analyzing how Sinophone literature complicates discourses of aging, illness, and identity; thirdly, to demonstrate how literature illuminates demographic and social transformation by revealing how individual stories of aging yield collective reflections on late life. By linking intimate illness narratives to broader socio-cultural changes, the project highlights aging as an issue of urgent significance. N/A

titleEnglish: How Long-Term Care Insurance Reshapes Care Networks in Chinese Families: a longitudinal mixed-method study
coInvestigators: Prof CHAN , Wing-kit Prof Fernández, José Luis Prof Li, Bingqin
abstract: As China’s population ages rapidly, families face growing pressure in providing care for older relatives, challenging traditional support systems. In response, the Chinese government introduced the Long-Term Care Insurance (LTCI) pilot scheme in 2016, designed to share care costs and alleviate family burdens. After nearly a decade of implementation, a critical question remains: How does LTCI scheme reshape care arrangements? Current research often overlooks the complex reality of multiple caregivers and fails to distinguish between different benefit types, particularly cash benefits versus in-kind services. This study addresses these gaps through a care network perspective, investigating three key questions: How does LTCI reshape care network composition and structure? Does it trigger renegotiation of care roles and responsibilities, and how do family caregivers perceive monetary compensation for care? How do these changes ultimately affect the well-being of both care recipients and caregivers? To address these questions, we will conduct a longitudinal mixed-methods study in Guangzhou, a leading LTCI pilot city providing a flexible choice between cash benefits for informal caregivers and in-kind services. While building directly on three previous waves of data collection, we will introduce two new survey waves (Waves 4 and 5) with a one-year interval. A key enhancement of the new data is the use of purposive sampling to ensure 480 care recipient–caregiver dyads from three balanced comparison groups previously underrepresented in the cohort: (1) cash benefit recipients who are family members (n=160), (2) cash benefit recipients who are domestic helpers (n=160), and (3) in-kind service recipients (n=160). Complementing the survey, in-depth interviews will be conducted with multiple family members from 20 families, as well as with 20 key stakeholders including LTCI policymakers, assessors, and service providers, with follow-up interviews after one year. The qualitative approach will capture evolving family dynamics, such as role renegotiation and responsibility shifts, while stakeholder perspectives will provide crucial insights into policy implementation and institutional challenges, ensuring our findings are grounded in real-world contexts. The project will deliver crucial new knowledge for policymakers in China and other aging societies. It will reveal how different LTCI benefit types lead to distinct care arrangements, identify new models of formal and informal care collaboration, and demonstrate how the reconfigured care networks impact family well-being. Our findings will provide an evidence base for designing more effective and family-sensitive long-term care policies that better support both older adults and their caregivers. N/A

titleEnglish: Courage, Conviction, and "Scary Crazy" Inquiry: Conspiracy Theorizing as Social-Epistemic Threat
abstract: In this eight-chapter monograph expansion of my doctoral dissertation, I will develop a view of conspiracy theorizing—the activity of developing, investigating, pursuing, debating, and professing a conspiracy theory—which takes seriously the idea that it poses a characteristic threat to our social-political stability and epistemic flourishing. I will do so from the perspective of social virtue epistemology and in the specific context of my doctoral dissertation, wherein I argue that "doxastic courage" (proper firmness of conviction in the face of fear at the level of belief) is an important and distinctive epistemic virtue. Revised chapters of the dissertation will form the first four chapters of the final monograph; the remaining chapters, which will be the central focus and principal research output of the funding period, will be new. The project will thus explore conspiracy theorizing as it intersects with doxastic courage and the closely-related epistemic virtues of open-mindedness (hearing someone out) and intellectual humility (admitting one's own epistemic limitations). My goal is to show that conspiracy theorizing, as an social-epistemic practice, threatens these and other virtues of inquiry and belief by jeopardizing social features of a healthy epistemic environment. In particular, I will argue that conspiracy theorizing "hijacks" the ordinary social dynamics of good-case inquiry, making us all more liable to epistemic vice. I will argue that conspiracy theorizing requires a kind of “going rogue” in the face of disconfirming evidence and social-epistemic pressure from others in the community. Such “going rogue” renders even the best-case scenario—where the conspiracy theory in question is actually true—socially and epistemically *risky*, as it characteristically requires defying social-epistemic norms of whom to believe, whom to dismiss, and when to suspend or give up inquiry. But in the worst cases, I will argue, conspiracy theorizing actively exploits these norms, distorting and destabilizing them. Conspiracy theorizing thus has the normative power to render normally-virtuous social-epistemic impulses (like deferring to others who seem to have more information) not just epistemically defective, but, in the scariest cases, morally and politically corrupt. This project will thus build on the growing philosophical literature connecting conspiracy theories and moral-epistemic vice. But rather than focusing on whether conspiracy *theories* are epistemically vicious, or whether conspiracy *theorists* are necessarily epistemic bad actors, this project will instead focus on conspiracy *theorizing* as a social-epistemic practice — and a characteristically dangerous one, at that. N/A

titleEnglish: Multimodal Writing, Visual Art and Mental Health
coInvestigators: Prof Jaworski, Adam Prof Ravelli, Louise
abstract: With mental health becoming an urgent global topic, artists from different parts of the world are also contributing to the discussion of related subjects. This interdisciplinary research project investigates how contemporary visual artists with diverse cultural and personal backgrounds incorporate multimodal writing into their work to engage with mental health topics—specifically mental distress, mental illness and mental resilience. Further, it examines the creative, therapeutic and socio-political affordances of text-based visual art in shaping mental health discourse, knowledge and experience. Drawing on theoretical and analytic approaches from social semiotics, sociolinguistics, and critical multimodal discourse analysis, the project examines how multimodal writing—through its integration of linguistic content and style, typography, graphic design, spatial arrangement and materiality—functions in visual artworks to produce mental health knowledge, foster viewer interaction and construct affective experience. It also investigates how artists use writing to enrich creative communication about mental health, enact healing and empowerment and contribute to mental health education and activism. In doing so, the project highlights the role of writing as a multimodal medium that can articulate, construct and transform lived experiences, reduce suffering and stigma and foster empathy and dialogues across diverse audiences. The study will analyze artworks by up to 30 contemporary artists based in Hong Kong, Australia and the United Kingdom, selected for their cultural diversity and active engagement with mental health themes through written text. These include installations, paintings, collages, projections and digital art that utilize various textual strategies such as expressive lettering, word-based graffiti and handwritten narratives. Methodologically, the project will combine in-situ fieldwork in museums, galleries and public sites and digital data collection with multi-layered analysis, integrating social semiotic analysis, recontextualization analysis and critical multimodal discourse analysis. Ultimately, the project seeks to develop an interdisciplinary, culturally sensitive framework for understanding, analyzing and practicing multimodal writing in mental health communication. By foregrounding the intersection of art, language and mental wellbeing, it will contribute to broader theoretical and social conversations on creativity, resilience and social change in visual art and in mental health discourse. N/A

titleEnglish: Bridging social capital, health literacy and health-related quality of life of ethnic minority and Chinese older adults in Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Dune, Tinashe Moira Prof Leung, Angela Yee Man Prof LEUNG, Lisa Yuk-ming Dr Tang, Mun Yu Vera
abstract: Abstract Many older adults, especially ethnic minorities (EM), face significant social disadvantages because of personal (e.g., health knowledge deficits) and institutional (e.g., culturally insensitive services) barriers. These barriers exacerbate the health-related disparities that minority and majority ethnic populations experience. Bridging social capital—that is, resources derived from weak social ties, such as friends of friends—can be crucial to healthy ageing. However, its potential remains understudied and underutilised in health-related policy and practice. Indeed, there is a critical need for theoretical and empirical evidence on how specific types of social capital can be effectively mobilised and sustained to improve health-related outcomes for EM and other older adults. This study will examine how EM and Hong Kong Chinese older adults generate and sustain bridging social capital, the hindrances involved and the influence it can have on their health literacy (i.e., their ability to access, evaluate and apply health information) and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) in Hong Kong. The study will also explore how these older adults manage any adverse influences of bridging social capital on their health-related outcomes—a woefully under-studied issue. By including Chinese older adults, the study will provide comparative and contextual perspectives on the experiences of EM older adults, while creating avenues to expand the theoretical and practical relevance of the findings. The study will employ an exploratory sequential mixed-method design to gather qualitative and quantitative data grounded in the lived experiences of participants. Data will be drawn from the predominant EM population groups, specifically South and Southeast Asians from India, Pakistan, Nepal, Thailand, and the Philippines, as well as Hong Kong Chinese people aged 55 years and above. The study will commence with semi-structured in-depth interviews, including sociogram development, with older adults (n=35 EM and n=35 Chinese) and eight key informant interviews with social service practitioners. The sociograms will provide visual and novel insights into the network structures that produce bridging social capital and inform interventions to enhance it in the context of health literacy and HRQoL. A survey will then be conducted to assess the characteristics and associations among bridging social capital, health literacy, and HRQoL of EM (n = 300) and Chinese (n = 300) older adults. This study will expand theoretical debates on social determinants of health. It will provide evidence-based recommendations co-produced with participants to design effective frameworks that foster social cohesion and healthy ageing in multi-ethnic societies. N/A

titleEnglish: Metaphors of language and their significance: a cross-cultural survey and analysis
abstract: Recent research work on metaphor suggests that, rather than being merely a decorative or stylistic flourish, it is in fact a fundamental aspect of human cognition, based on principles of comparison and blending. However, partly because they are so fundamental, metaphoric representations and the inferences they generate often pass unnoticed. Given that they have been shown to exert considerable influence on how concepts and issues are perceived, it is important to analyse the use of metaphors in particular contexts, aiming to understand their origins and effects. This study will focus on metaphors of language, analysing databases of media articles to see how language is conceptualised in three different cultural and linguistic contexts (Europe, with a focus on France; the United Kingdom; and Asia, as represented by Hong Kong and Singapore). Europe is included because previous research has linked it with one of the two main cultural models of language, the ‘romantic’ model that emphasises the historical, organic roots and continuity of national languages. The UK provides a possible example of the other main cultural model identified in research, the ‘rationalist’ mode which foregrounds the instrumental or tool-like qualities of language. In practice there is considerable hybridity between the romantic and rationalist models, and the project will take what has been learned from these contexts and extend the analysis to Asia (Hong Kong and Singapore). It will collect databases of articles from each context and identify the metaphors within them, while also remaining sensitive to the relevance of non-metaphorical representations. The significance of the metaphorical and non-metaphorical representations will then be interpreted, aiming to understand how metaphor may contribute to public perceptions of language-related issues. It will also investigate the historical and cultural backgrounds to these metaphors, comparing and contrasting the different contexts and applying the analytical concepts of scenarios, narratives, and cultural models. The project will have generate two journal articles and a scholarly book, and will also have impacts beyond academia by, for example, increasing awareness of metaphorical language amongst teachers and students and encouraging reflection on a range of language-related issues, including those of language education and the role of English. By comparing different language-using communities the project will also provide insights into shared human experience and the possibilities for a sense of global citizenship. N/A

titleEnglish: Higher-order Metaphysics and Nominalism
abstract: Over the last decade, there has been a revolution in metaphysics with the previously dominant neo-Quinean orthodoxy of first-orderism being overthrown, or at least being driven close to overthrow, by the rival doctrine of higher-orderism (also known as higher-order metaphysics). First-orderism holds that in order to formulate our theories perspicuously, and hence determine what they are committed to and whether we should believe them, we need to formulate them so that the only quantificational expressions they contain are first-order quantifier expressions, where first-order quantifier expressions include ‘every’, ‘some’ and ‘none’. In contrast, Higher-orderism holds that perspicuously formulated theories can also contain primitive higher-order quantifier expressions. Due to its methodological centrality, the replacement of first-orderism with higher-orderism promises to profoundly transform many debates in metaphysics. It also promises many theoretical benefits, such as greater expressive power, more accurate modes of presentation and new solutions to old problems. While it has such virtues, however, higher-orderism also faces at least two serious objections: that its higher-order quantifiers are obscure and that they are unparsimonious. Based on these objections, this project will develop several arguments that higher-orderism should be rejected and that we should instead endorse a novel third view that also rejects first-orderism. First, it will be argued that all existing attempts by higher-orderists to non-obscurely introduce or explain their higher-order quantifiers have failed. Secondly, it will be argued that there is a way to successfully introduce higher-order quantifiers so that they play the same theoretical role that higher-orderists want them to play, but on which they are not primitives but instead in effect infinite conjunctions and disjunctions. Third, it will be argued that, even if higher-orderists reject the project’s attempted non-primitivist introduction of the higher-order quantifiers, they should reject their primitivism. It will be argued that this is because, from their own perspective, it is possible to use their higher-order quantifiers to formulate an attractive reduction of them that higher-orderists have no good reason to reject. Finally, the project will argue that, rightly understood, employing higher-order quantifiers can make a big difference to metaphysics by arguing that they can allow a powerful defence of nominalism, where nominalism is the view that there are no abstract objects such as properties, numbers or sets. N/A

titleEnglish: The Forgotten Tribunal: American War Crimes Trial of Hankou
abstract: This study examines the 1946 Hankou Trial, an unprecedented yet understudied war crimes tribunal where US military authorities prosecuted 18 Japanese officers for torturing and killing 3 American B-29 aviators in China. As the first war crimes trial in post-WWII China and one of the earliest instances of an Allied power conducting proceedings on another Allied nation’s soil, the trial set significant legal precedents while generating diplomatic tensions. Moving beyond the traditional legal analyses common in Tokyo Trials scholarship, this research investigates how the Hankou Trial became a nexus of legal, political, moral, and emotive controversies in postwar China amid intense civil war struggles and heightened nationalist sentiment. Drawing on extensive English, Chinese, and Japanese sources, the study reveals how various stakeholders—American authorities, the Nationalist government, the Chinese Communist Party, and the Chinese public—advanced competing interpretations of international law, national sovereignty, and diplomatic power relations surrounding the trial. Through a comparative lens that bridges micro and global historical approaches, this research demonstrates how postwar justice emerged from a complex interplay of legal procedures, political objectives, and moral imperatives, rather than conforming to a single framework of victor’s, hegemon’s, or victim’s justice. The findings will contribute to broader scholarly discussions on Allied war crimes tribunals, Sino-American relations, and the contested notion of justice in the evolving global order of the early Cold War era. N/A

titleEnglish: Convention, Creativity, and Identity: An Interdisciplinary Study of Dialect Writing in the Digital Age
abstract: This project originated from my attempt to understand the striking divergence in attitudes towards Chinese dialects that I observed in my last GRF project. On the one hand, many rural dialect speakers, lacking confidence in Putonghua, gravitate to lip-sync features on platforms such as Douyin to avoid exposing their accent. On the other hand, highly educated influencers increasingly deploy hybrid repertoires, mixing English and dialects, while bypassing Putonghua altogether. This paradox signals a broader shift in language attitudes and ideologies against the historical background of language standardization, recent technology development and trends of language commodification. Aiming to gain insights into these changes, this project adopts a focus on dialect writing. For the first time in Chinese history, ordinary speakers are writing dialects with digital technologies. These emergent practices are inventive and reflexive: not having access to established conventions due to historical and political reasons, they experiment with new forms, explore sound-meaning gaps, forge new semantic links, and reconstruct accumulated meanings. They utilise writing together with other resources in complex audiovisual configurations for humour and attention, but also for demonstrating expertise, taking stances, and performing hybrid identities. A focus on writing allows me to capture micro linguistic phenomena, while demonstrating the scale, historical and sociolinguistic significance of technology-afforded changes. Drawing on interdisciplinary scholarship, my project combines analysis of video data, comments and interviews of dialect ‘writers’. I address three questions: (1) How are dialects being written, stylized, and circulated on major platforms and beyond? (2) How do these practices, involving both vloggers and commenters, produce emergent conventions, establish indexical links between people, place and language, and reshape language ideologies? (3) To what extent do these writing practices, with heightened metalinguistic awareness, feed back into language change and public representation of dialects? I connect micro-level practices with macro-level changes to understand processes of standardization and shifts in China’s language ecology. Moreover, I read these practices against the political economy of platforms, urban-rural dynamics, rural development and emergence of regional identities. This large-scale, systematic study will lay groundwork for comparative projects across the broader Sinosphere, where Chinese dialects had a different writing history and digitalization trajectory. Complementing a literature that has focused predominantly on alphabetic languages, this project, accentuating the affordances of characters and sociolinguistic conditions in China, makes significant contribution to scholarship on language attitudes and ideology, dialect mediatisation, sociolinguistics of writing, multilingualism, and digital literacy practices. N/A

titleEnglish: Peer-led Motivational Interviewing and Implementation Intentions Intervention for Men Who Have Sex with Men in Hong Kong: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Promote Condom Use
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Berman, Anne Prof Chan, Siu Wing Alex Dr Chong, Ka Chun Miss Ip, Tsun Kwan Mary Prof Kai, Jonas Dr Tse, Ka Wo Prof Wang, Zixin Mr Wong, Timothy
abstract: HIV is an important public health concern over the globe. Men who have sex with men (MSM) play a critical role in HIV transmission in Hong Kong as a high level of condomless anal sex (CAS) is documented in this population. Evidence has shown that motivational factors, such as risk perceptions and attitudes to condom use, are strongly associated with the intention to use condom among MSM. However, a huge intention-behavior gap is observed and intention to use condom does not always translated into action. Volitional factors, such as planning and preparatory behaviors, are important in filling the intention-behavior gap. Meanwhile, there is extensive evidence that motivational interviewing (MI) is effective in promoting motivations, and implementation intentions intervention (III) is effective in facilitating action, and peer-led interventions can effectively facilitate uptake of the intervention. The proposed study aims to test the efficacy of an integrated peer-led MI with III (MI-III), an intervention that targets both motivational and volitional stage, in promoting condom use among MSM in Hong Kong. A 3-armed randomized controlled trial is proposed, Chinese men who self-reported having CAS with another man in the past month will be recruited and randomly allocated into the integrated MI-III group (N=182), MI-only group (N=182) or the III-only group (N=182). Participants in the MI-III group will receive four weekly telephone-based, peer-led MI sessions, and will be asked to take part in the III exercise on the Internet after each MI session, Participants in the MI-only group will receive four weekly telephone-based, peer-led MI sessions, and participants in the III-only group will received four weekly III exercise sessions. Participants will be evaluated at baseline, at the end of intervention, and six months after the intervention. The primary outcome will be CAS reduction; secondary outcomes will include attitudes, perceived norms, perceived behavioral control, risk perceptions, condom use intention and condom preparatory behaviors. The mechanisms which the intervention reduces CAS through motivational and volitional factors will also be tested. The proposed intervention, if found successful, will have significant implication for implementation as the it can be upscaled to other NGOs that provide HIV prevention services to MSM in Hong Kong. It can also be promoted among MSM in the wider community and has the potential to reduce the increasing HIV epidemic among MSM in Hong Kong. N/A

titleEnglish: Assessing the Impact of RSV Vaccination on Reducing Disease Severity of RSV Infection: A Cross-Regional Study in Hong Kong, the US and the UK
department: The Jockey Club School of Public Health and Primary Care
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Chong, Ka Chun Prof Wang, Haitian Maggie
abstract: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a leading cause of acute respiratory infection globally, particularly affecting infants, older adults, and individuals with chronic diseases. Following the COVID-19 pandemic, shifts in viral circulation, host immunity, and healthcare dynamics may alter the epidemiology of respiratory pathogens. Recent increases in RSV hospitalizations and severe cases worldwide highlight its growing public-health burden. Although RSV vaccines have been introduced in the United States and the United Kingdom, they have not yet been widely implemented in Hong Kong. The lack of local evidence on RSV disease burden, vaccine impact, and viral evolution constrains timely and evidence-based policy decisions on immunization strategies. This project aims to evaluate RSV disease severity and vaccination impact through comparative, cross-regional, and computational analyses. Specifically, we will (i) assess RSV disease severity in Hong Kong relative to COVID-19 and influenza to contextualize its clinical burden; (ii) compare RSV severity across Hong Kong, the United States, and the United Kingdom, where vaccination coverage differs, to quantify the effect of immunization on disease outcomes; and (iii) develop a computational model integrating epidemiological and genomic data to predict vaccine effectiveness (VE) and evaluate potential breakthrough risks associated with viral mutations. The study will analyze more than ten years of clinical data from the Hospital Authority Data Collaboration Lab (HADCL) in Hong Kong, Medicare and Medicaid administrative datasets in the United States, and the UK Biobank. Multilevel regression and machine-learning approaches will be applied to quantify associations between vaccination, patient characteristics, and severe outcomes such as ICU admission, ventilator use, and mortality. Genomic analyses will identify mutations potentially linked to immune escape and reduced vaccine effectiveness. The project will generate the first cross-regional evidence on RSV severity and vaccination impact relevant to Hong Kong, providing quantitative guidance for local vaccine introduction. By combining epidemiological, clinical, and genomic data, this study will establish an adaptable analytical pipeline that can support data-driven vaccination strategies for RSV and other emerging respiratory pathogens. N/A

titleEnglish: Embedding AI Literacy in School Mathematics: A Design-Based Study of Curricular Innovations in Data Handling and Probability
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Dai, Yun Prof Ka Hio, Lei Prof Siller, Hans Stefan
abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI) becomes increasingly embedded in society, cultivating AI literacy at an early age, particularly in K–12 education, has become an urgent priority. Current research efforts often emphasize technical fluency with AI tools and investigate teachers’ perceptions and students’ usage of them, lacking focus on sustainable curricular transformation. AI literacy encompasses not only technical skills but also conceptual knowledge and critical values and attitudes, including ethical considerations. These dimensions remain underexplored, particularly in subject-specific contexts like mathematics education. In mathematics classrooms, AI integration should move beyond merely tool usage, toward supporting authentic connections between AI literacy and mathematics learning outcomes. In response, this design-based study envisions a mathematics curriculum centered on data handling and probability (DHP), embedding real-world AI contexts into conventional mathematical topics. Rather than focusing on introducing new and rapidly evolving technologies, this study adapts existing curricular materials to reflect students’ everyday experiences with AI. Topics such as statistical charts, central tendency, data dispersion, and probability are reframed using familiar AI-related scenarios. For instance, probability problems are redesigned around systems like YouTube’s recommendation algorithms, making AI processes more transparent and relatable. This approach allows students to implicitly engage with AI concepts, processes, and ethics within regular mathematics lessons. The study will iteratively co-design and refine curricular materials in collaboration with 8 mathematics teachers, implementing them with grade 7–10 students (n=200) across 4 secondary schools in Hong Kong. Videotaped classroom enactments will be analyzed to examine how students and teachers interact with the designed materials. Particular attention will be paid to how students articulate AI concepts, identify biases through mathematical reasoning, and how teachers facilitate classroom discourse that bridges mathematics and AI literacy. The pedagogical approach draws on the framework of Realistic Mathematics Education to ensure contextual relevance and accessibility. Theoretically, the study contributes to understanding AI’s interdisciplinary nature through the lens of boundary crossing, emphasizing the foundational yet often implicit connections between AI and mathematics. Practically, it offers an immediate solution and model for integrating AI into mathematics education without requiring extensive retraining or new technologies. By leveraging existing curricular structures and aligning them with contemporary AI applications, this study supports the broader STEM education goal of preparing students to critically engage with AI in society. Ultimately, it will enhance the relevance of mathematics education and promotes technological competence through meaningful, context-rich mathematics learning experiences. N/A

titleEnglish: The Evolution of Air Services Agreements: Implications for Hong Kong
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Any international movement of people or goods by air is governed by an air services agreement (ASA). Before an airline can operate international services to another country, its government must first conclude an ASA with the government of the destination country. The bilateral ASA between the United States and the United Kingdom signed in 1946 served as the paradigm for subsequent ASAs. Over the last eight decades, the number of ASAs has increased, and the contents of ASAs have expanded. A complex web of approximately 4,000 bilateral ASAs with variations in substance and style are in existence today. Historically, international air transport was governed by protectively written bilateral ASAs. Protectionism had steadily declined while liberalization has spread over the world. From the groundbreaking ASA between the United States and the European Union in 2007, a number of ASAs have started to cover new issues, such as the environment, government subsidies, and consumer protection. This project aims to trace the evolution of ASAs and to anticipate their future. The PI will review approximately 4,000 bilateral ASAs from the World Air Services Agreements (WASA), the most complete database of ASAs worldwide managed by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO). This empirical study will be supplemented by expert interviews. In the scholarly monograph and journal article that will be published as part of this project, the PI will analyze how ASAs have been evolving, examine potential conflicts and overlaps with other legal frameworks, and anticipate the future of ASAs, which will require a discussion of new technologies, including remotely piloted aircraft and autonomous aircraft. Despite their relevance and importance, ASAs are underrepresented in the academic literature. Their underrepresentation is largely due to the self-contained nature of international aviation, but it also has to do with the lack of experts in the field. The PI’s background, expertise, and experience make him particularly well-suited to undertake the proposed project. This project will make a significant academic contribution, presenting new insights into legal principles and applications for ASAs. It will offer practical implications for policy makers in aviation and trade authorities as well as businesses in the airline industry. The Hong Kong Government emphasizes the significance of ASAs that improve global air connectivity, especially with Belt and Road partners. As maintaining Hong Kong’s status as an international aviation hub is an important policy goal, Hong Kong will benefit from this research. N/A

titleEnglish: Reconstructing Early Sinitic Loanword Strata in Kra-Dai Languages: Implications for Contact History, Cultural Transmission, and Old Chinese Reconstruction
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Liao, Hanbo
abstract: The primary objective of this project is to investigate the early Sinitic loanword strata in the Kra-Dai languages spoken in southwestern China and Southeast Asia. By analyzing these loanwords, we aim to elucidate the historical transmission of early Chinese (Han) culture to Kra-Dai-speaking regions. Additionally, we will examine linguistic evidence from these loanwords to contribute to the reconstruction of Old Chinese. The Kra-Dai language family comprises several well-defined branches, including Kra, Hlai, Kam-Sui, Ong-Be, and Tai. These branches contain numerous 'related words' shared with Chinese, such as the numerals 'three' to 'ten' in Tai. Early studies often interpreted these shared words as evidence of a genetic relationship between Sino-Tibetan and Kra-Dai. However, with a deeper understanding of Kra-Dai and language contact, scholars now attribute these similarities to prolonged contact between Chinese and Kra-Dai, treating these words as loanwords, with Chinese as the donor language and Kra-Dai as the recipient. Unlike previous studies, which typically focus on loanword stratification in individual Kra-Dai languages or dialects, this project adopts a novel approach by extracting Sinitic loanwords directly from the proto-language of each Kra-Dai branch, excluding recent borrowings. To achieve this, we will analyze sound correspondences across each Kra-Dai branch and expand the reconstructed proto-language vocabulary to identify additional Sinitic loanwords. This will be followed by cross-branch and intra-branch comparisons to stratify loanwords and establish the relative chronology of their transmission. These findings will enable us to explore the historical transmission of Chinese (Han) culture to Kra-Dai-speaking communities, as many loanwords are cultural terms. Special attention will be given to loanwords related to metal, given the ongoing debates about the timing and routes of metal technology transmission from the Han to Kra-Dai groups. We will also investigate how these loanwords, particularly those preserving preinitials in conservative Kra-Dai languages (e.g., Buyang), can contribute to reconstructing Old Chinese. In summary, this project is expected to significantly advance our understanding of historical Kra-Dai and Chinese linguistics, as well as early cultural exchanges between these groups. N/A

titleEnglish: Journey to the West: The Life and Times of Shen Fuzong
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Shen Fuzong 沈福宗 (c. 1658-1692) is the best documented Chinese visitor to early modern Europe, whose life and times shed light on the possibilities of early modern travel and cultural exchange. Born in Nanjing to Christian parents, he travelled via Macau to Europe with his Jesuit teacher, Fr. Philippe Couplet (1623–1693). In 1684, he visited Versailles where he was given an audience with Louis XIV (1638-1715) and demonstrated how to use chopsticks. While in Paris he also assisted in the publication of the ‘Confucius Sinarum Philosophus’, the first European translation of the ‘Four Books’. From Paris, he travelled to Rome via Florence and was received by the pope. In early 1687, Shen travelled to London and then Oxford where he catalogued the Bodleian Library’s collection of Chinese books and tutored the Professor of Oriental Languages in Chinese language and culture. In England, he also caught the attention of King James II who commissioned a portrait of Shen. While Shen is often mentioned in passing in histories of Sino-Western relations and has been the subject of scattered articles treating specific aspects of his visit to Europe (e.g. specialists in English history discuss his time in England, French historians his time in France, etc.), there has been no attempt to write a full-scale biography of this pivotal East-West figure. Many larger research questions (both historical and theoretical) raised by his experiences also remain unanswered. What role did he play in the transmission of Chinese culture in early modern Europe? How was he viewed by observers in the different Catholic and Protestant European contexts he passed through? To what extent does his story provide evidence for commensurability across cultural boundaries and the factors that facilitated or limited this? What did he himself think of Oxford, Paris and Rome? What light does Shen’s story shed on the history of orientalism, ideas about race and human difference and the role of ‘brokers’ or ‘intermediaries’ in cross-cultural exchanges? This research project will culminate in a book-length biography that will use the various steps of Shen’s life and journey from East to West as an opportunity to ask deeper questions about cross-cultural exchange. To broaden its public impact, the book will be accompanied by a lecture series and an exhibition on Shen’s life. N/A

titleEnglish: Predictive neural processing in naturalistic reading: Testing low- vs. high-level impairments in children with dyslexia and children with poor linguistic comprehension
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Dimigen, Olaf Prof Gagl, Benjamin Prof Li, Su Dr Liu, Duo
abstract: Around 25% of children have difficulties understanding the basic information when reading a text. Difficulties with reading comprehension can be the consequence of difficulties at the level of word decoding (dyslexia) or difficulties in understanding language in general (poor linguistic comprehenders). While the two groups have different weaknesses at the cognitive level, little is known about the underlying impairments during text reading at the level of brain function because of methodological challenges. Until recently, fast brain processes could only be investigated with single word presentations, because eye movements create large artefacts in the EEG. With methodological advances, EEG can be co-registered with eye tracking, and eye movement artefacts can be corrected during activities, where eye movements occur naturally, such as reading text. Moreover, reading text is different from single word reading, because readers develop expectations based on the sentence and text context. Recently, Large Language Models (LLM), such as new versions of GPT, have been developed that predict the next word based on previously presented words with high accuracy. Thus, the surprisal value of subsequent words can be used to calculate the neural correlation of expectation. This can be contrasted with neural correlates of word-level properties, such as visual-orthographic properties or word frequency. The first objective of the current proposal is to measure fixation-related potentials of the EEG in children when reading text in order to contrast word-level and context-based predictors and assess their effects during development. After having identified the neural markers of these predictors and their development in typically developing children, the second objective of the proposal is to test whether word-level or context-based neural correlates are impaired in children with dyslexia and in children with poor linguistic comprehension. It is expected that children with dyslexia are impaired in neural correlates related to word-level processing, and children with poor linguistic comprehension show difficulties in engaging neural processes that predict upcoming words. The results are expected to provide a better understanding about the neural processes that are involved in naturalistic reading in children and about the levels at which impairments occur in children with text reading difficulties, such as those with dyslexia or those with poor linguistic comprehension. The knowledge gained in the study will also guide future reading intervention programs for children with text reading difficulties. N/A

titleEnglish: The Chinese Development Experience and the Changing Rule of Law Consensus
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Liu, Lianlian Prof Xia, Ying
abstract: The proposed project examines the impact of the Chinese development experience on legal development policies of prominent international development organizations. My earlier RGC-funded research studied the significance of Chinese legal thought on the global stage. The proposed project takes a more focused approach by describing how international development organizations, such as the World Bank, the United Nations Development Program (UNDP) and the Asian Development Bank (ADB), have responded to China’s development experience (comprising received information and perceived lessons regarding China’s economic and social development) in their legal development policies. The proposed project also considers possible areas of impact for the Chinese development experience in international development organizations. The proposed project comprises three interrelated inquiries. First, the project examines the extent to which international development agencies have reassessed their views on the role of law in development. The preliminary document review conducted for the project suggests that international development organizations largely sidelined the Chinese development experience until the 2010s. The project reviews legal development policies of prominent international development organizations and selected national development agencies. The project reviews also reviews China’s role in the use of quantitative measures in the design of rule of law policies in development organizations. These include the use of indices, such as the Rule of Law Index referenced by the World Bank. Second, the project seeks to understand the specific role of the Chinese development experience in the reassessment of legal development policies in international development organizations. The project includes an interview study of development experts in the World Bank, the UNDP and ADB. The aim of these interviews is to understand what role the Chinese development experience played in the reassessment of the rule of law consensus within these organizations. No such narrative is currently available in scholarship on law and development. Third, the project provides a comprehensive account of the processes through which information about the Chinese development experience is received, and may be better received, in international development organizations. The project examines: (i) institutional linkages between development organizations and Chinese development experts; (ii) Chinese and foreign vocabularies (or “speech”) on legal development policies; and (iii) the receptiveness of various audiences to information about the Chinese development experience. The results of the project will allow scholars and development experts to better understand China’s role in global development policy making. N/A

titleEnglish: Does Need for Autonomy Prevent Single Individuals from Pursuing Romantic Relationships? Insights from a Multiple-Goal Perspective
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Miss He, Yingyi Prof Hui, Pui Hung Prof Jiang, Wen
abstract: The number of single individuals has been growing globally. One major focus of research on singles has been to understand why individuals remain single. People may be single by choice or due to external circumstances. Those who choose to be single frequently cite a desire for autonomy—the freedom to pursue one's own interests and values—as a primary reason. This reason implies a conflict between the pursuits of autonomy and romantic relationships. However, it remains unclear whether this conflict is commonly perceived among singles and how such perceptions influence their preferences and decisions regarding romantic pursuits.
