According to the multiple-goal perspective, the pursuit of a particular goal is based not solely on its desirability and feasibility, but also on its goal relations with other goals and needs. Consistent with this perspective, the pursuit of a romantic relationship can depend on whether it is perceived to facilitate or undermine autonomy. Our conceptual analysis and pilot studies suggest that autonomy and romance are not inherently incompatible—more than 35% of singles actually view romantic relationships as beneficial to autonomy. Importantly, because singles differ substantially in their perception of the autonomy-relationship conflict, this perceived conflict can (a) generally undermine romantic pursuits and (b) motivate individuals who prioritize autonomy to disengage from romantic pursuits. While our pilot studies provide initial support for these hypotheses, we aim to establish causal evidence. To that end, we have designed two experimental studies testing (a) whether perceived goal conflict between autonomy and relationships can causally increase preference for being single and reduce romantic pursuit, and (b) whether prioritization of autonomy need, both chronic and temporary, increases preference for singlehood and reduces romantic pursuits only when goal conflict is perceived. A six-month longitudinal study will extend these findings by testing whether (c) naturally occurring autonomy need and perceived goal conflict jointly predict romantic pursuit effort and relationship formation. We aim to demonstrate that (a) while not all singles perceive a conflict between autonomy and relationships, the perception of such conflict can undermine romantic pursuit, and that (b) singles who chronically or temporarily prioritize autonomy do not universally reduce their romantic pursuits—it depends critically on whether they perceive a goal conflict. Because both autonomy prioritization and perceived goal conflict are changeable, interventions can help romantically interested singles by either deprioritizing autonomy need (by satiating it) or reducing autonomy-relationship conflict (e.g., through flexible work arrangements). N/A

titleEnglish: Harmonization of Choice of Law in China
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Dornis, Tim Willy Peter Prof Du, Tao Prof Liang, Jieying Prof Peari, Sagi Prof Tu, Guangjian
abstract: China is a rare country that has multiple legal systems (including Chinese Mainland, Macau, and Hong Kong) but lacks harmonized choice of law rules. This creates uncertainty regarding the governing law in cross-border contracts and the potential for forum shopping. The success of the European Union in harmonizing choice of law rules among its member states suggests that such harmonization can reduce transaction costs and promote economic development. This project aims to explore the current problem and methods to harmonize the choice of law rules in contractual obligations among the three legal systems. Harmonizing private international law rules has long been on the agenda of these three legal systems, which have concluded multiple arrangements since the turnovers of Hong Kong and Macau. To date, these arrangements have primarily focused on judgment enforcement, with the most recent being implemented through statute in Hong Kong in October 2022. This ordinance has substantially expanded the scope of civil and commercial judgments that are reciprocally enforceable between Hong Kong and Chinese Mainland. With the new judgment enforcement regime largely established, the natural sequel is to harmonize choice of law rules to resolve conflicts, and efforts have already been made to address these conflict issues in China. Following Mainland's lead, Hong Kong adopted the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods ("CISG") in September 2021. It is expected that a further arrangement will be concluded regarding the application of CISG between the two regions. While this represents an effort to resolve conflicts through the harmonization of substantive laws, the CISG only applies within a limited scope. It is now time to harmonize choice of law rules to resolve the divergence issue beyond international sales of goods. Through comparative and empirical research, this project seeks to answer the following research question: How significant is the current divergence in choice of law rules among Chinese Mainland, Hong Kong, and Macau, and what are the most effective approaches to harmonizing these rules to enhance legal predictability and economic integration? The answer to this question will have a significant impact on governments, practitioners, the business community and academia. Harmonization of choice of law rules has always been one of the most important questions in private international law. A workable proposal to harmonize the contractual choice of law rules in China will serve as a blueprint for Chinese Mainland, Hong Kong, and Macau in future legislation. N/A

titleEnglish: Cross-Platform Discursive Trajectories and Digital Misogyny: The Case of Mainland Chinese Drifter Women in Hong Kong
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Hong Kong is experiencing significant demographic change, with mainland Chinese immigration increasing by about 300% between 2020 and 2024. This influx has created a distinctive community of “Hong Kong drifters” (港漂), young Chinese mainland immigrants comprising primarily university students, professionals, and skilled workers. Over half of these new arrivals are women, many of whom actively document their Hong Kong integration experiences on Xiaohongshu (小紅書/RedNote), China’s dominant social media platform. These posts frequently become major sources for Hong Kong news media reports, where these women’s personal narratives are reframed to generate debates around Hong Kong-mainland conflicts. These news stories are subsequently shared on LIHKG, Hong Kong’s popular forum, where users collectively mobilize misogynistic discourses to delegitimize these drifter women. This project investigates how misogynistic discourses about drifter women are constructed, reframed, and sustained through a triadic discursive trajectory: Xiaohongshu posts > Hong Kong news media recontextualization > LIHKG intertextual reappropriation. The study examines the linguistic and discursive strategies that recontextualize drifter women’s narratives and their potential impacts on the women’s identity construction and social integration. This analysis employs Critical Discourse-Centred Online-Offline Nexus Ethnography (CD-OONE), an integrated multimethod approach drawing on critical discourse analysis, digital ethnography, and online-offline nexus theory. The research design involves three interconnected phases: Phase 1 provides systematic observation, identification, and documentation of triadic discursive trajectories through cross-platform recontextualization analysis; Phase 2 involves digital ethnographic observation of LIHKG discourse practices using netnographic methods and critical discourse analysis; and Phase 3 conducts in-depth interviews with drifter women, analyzed through (feminist) critical discourse analysis and nexus analysis to document how digital misogyny shapes online and offline identity constructions and counter-discourse strategies. This interdisciplinary study is the first to systematically examine cross-platform misogyny in Hong Kong’s immigration context by operationalizing online-offline nexus theory. It addresses unexplored areas in understanding cross-platform discourse processes that potentially impact migrants' social integration, while extending and advancing theoretical frameworks of digital communication. The CD-OONE methodology offers an integrated framework for further research on mediated discourse across platforms and communication spaces. Findings will potentially inform platform governance policies and community support programmes for migrant women facing digital harassment. N/A

titleEnglish: Deploying Real-time Tracking and Geospatial Artificial Intelligence to Advance Research on the Environmental Determinants of Children’s Myopia
department: Institute of Space and Earth Information Science
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Yam, Cheuk Sing Jason
abstract: Myopia (also known as short-sightedness) among children has become a significant global health concern. According to one estimate, the worldwide prevalence of myopia will increase significantly in the coming decades and will affect 50% of the world’s population by 2050. Hong Kong is no exception to this trend: a study found that there is a strikingly high prevalence of myopia among 6–8-year-old children in Hong Kong. Thus, there is a huge potential for reducing the public health burden of myopia by identifying modifiable lifestyle, behavioral, and environmental factors that have significant impacts on children’s myopia. However, past studies have serious limitations that may hinder efforts to identify or develop effective measures for reducing children’s myopia risk (e.g., ignoring environmental features at eye level and the dynamic nature of children’s daily movements). The proposed study seeks to address these limitations and obtain reliable results for effectively reducing children’s myopia risk. (1) The proposed study will adopt an eye-level perspective that takes into account the visual pathway. (2) It will use street view images (SVIs) and geospatial artificial intelligence (GeoAI) methods to derive the environmental features that children see in their daily lives. (3) The study adopts a dynamic conceptualization of exposure and uses real-time GPS tracking and sensing to take into account children’s daily mobility, activity-travel patterns, and dynamic exposure to various indoor and outdoor environments. (4) It will use mobility-based measures of exposure to environmental features to reduce the measurement errors of residence-based measures. (5) The proposed study will examine the direct and indirect effects of various environmental features on children’s myopia, taking into account the complex interactions, mediating pathways, co-exposure patterns, and nonlinear relationships among these features using structural equation and machine learning models. (6) The project will use interpretable machine learning models to enhance the interpretability of model results. (7) It will generate reliable scientific findings to inform more targeted public health strategies, urban planning policies, and behavioural interventions to mitigate the rising burden of myopia in Hong Kong and worldwide. N/A

titleEnglish: English-Medium Instruction and Doctoral Education: Exploring Motivation, Investment, and Identity in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr CURLE, Samantha Prof Lasagabaster, David
abstract: This study investigates the role of English-medium instruction (EMI) in shaping the experiences of doctoral students in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area (GBA). While EMI has become a cornerstone of international higher education, most existing research focuses on undergraduate learners, leaving the linguistic, motivational, and identity trajectories of doctoral students largely unexplored. Given Hong Kong’s ambition to strengthen its global position as a centre of advanced education, understanding how doctoral students navigate EMI environments is both timely and necessary. Drawing on Dörnyei’s L2 Motivational Self System and Darvin and Norton’s Investment Model, the project examines how international and Mainland Chinese doctoral students (n=100) construct their motivation, invest in language and academic practices, and negotiate academic and social identities in EMI contexts. It will adopt a mixed-methods, longitudinal design combining surveys, interviews, and document analysis across EMI universities (n=2) in Hong Kong and their cross-border branch campuses. This approach will generate nuanced insights into how linguistic, cultural, and institutional factors intersect to shape doctoral trajectories. The study addresses several key questions: (1) How is doctoral students’ language learning motivation manifested during their transition into EMI universities? (2) To what extent do they invest in English as part of their academic and professional development? (3) How do they negotiate identities within multilingual and cross-cultural environments? (4) How do these processes compare across Hong Kong and GBA campuses? By focusing on doctoral education, the project makes three contributions. First, it advances theoretical understanding of motivation, investment, and identity in under-researched EMI populations. Second, it provides empirical evidence on the challenges and affordances of EMI at the doctoral level, highlighting implications for pedagogy, supervision, and institutional support. Third, it situates Hong Kong and the GBA within global debates on EMI and doctoral education, offering comparative insights that can inform both local practice and international scholarship. Overall, the project seeks to illuminate how EMI shapes doctoral education in one of the world’s most dynamic academic regions. In doing so, it contributes to applied linguistics, higher education studies, and the broader understanding of how multilingual contexts influence advanced research training and the development of future academic and professional leaders. N/A

titleEnglish: Improving Quality of Informal Care for Persons with Schizophrenia: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Multicomponent Dyadic Intervention
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Celia Hoi Yan Dr LEE, Lok Chun Prof Leung, Kiu Yi Dara Prof Ran, Maosheng
abstract: Background: Schizophrenia imposes a significant burden on families and societies. In rural China, persons with schizophrenia (PwS) often rely on unpaid informal caregivers (ICGs), typically family members, to provide care and extensive support after treatment. Enhancing the quality of informal care is therefore vital for the patient’s recovery and their ICGs' well-being. Objective: This study aims to develop a holistic intervention program called Dyadic Multicomponent Intervention (DMI) to enhance the quality of informal care and health-related outcomes among PwS and their ICGs. Method: We will recruit 228 participants (i.e., 114 pairs of PwS and their ICGs) in Xinjin County, Chengdu, Sichuan Province, China. Participants will be invited to attend an 8-week intervention program. This program will include a three-armed Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs). All participants will be randomly assigned to two intervention groups and one control group: (1) a DMI group (involving psychoeducation, physical, and spiritual activities), (2) a Dyadic Benefit-finding Intervention (DBFI) group (involving only psychoeducation activity), and (3) a control group (receiving only routine care). We will utilize a mixed-method design, combining quantitative and qualitative methods, to assess the effectiveness of interventions. First, quantitative evaluation will involve in-person questionnaires completed by all PwS and ICG participants and clinical professionals. The questionnaire surveys will be conducted before, immediately after, and six months after the interventions. Second, qualitative evaluations will include non-participant observations during interventions, semi-structured interviews with participants (20 pairs of PwS and their ICGs, n = 40), and focus group interviews with intervention implementers (n = 6). Qualitative findings will be used to analyze individual variations, intervention effects, and intervention fidelity. Finally, the quantitative findings will be synthesized with qualitative analysis to empirically investigate the effectiveness and the specific mechanisms of how the theory-based DMI affects the quality of care and health outcomes in realistic intervention practices. Impacts: This proposed study will develop an innovative DMI program for PwS and their ICGs and generate new knowledge on improving the quality of informal care for schizophrenia. It will emphasize the “PwS–ICG dyad” concept and expand our understanding by redefining the quality of informal care and filling in the gaps regarding the lack of relevant measurement tools. The findings will provide valuable insights into empowering the ICGs to better care for PwS and facilitate their recovery. The study will also offer empirical support and protocols for clinical practitioners to implement practical interventions in communities. N/A

titleEnglish: Esperanto Resistance Literature in Wartime Hong Kong (1937-1941): Transnational and Transcultural Perspectives
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Esperanto, the constructed auxiliary language created by L. L. Zamenhof in 1887, entered China at the turn of the twentieth-century. Despite differing historical and cultural contexts between China and Europe, Chinese intellectuals embraced Esperanto as a vehicle for modernization and cosmopolitan exchange. Previous studies have primarily focused on the promotion of Esperanto as a radical linguistic-political reform and on its role in disseminating progressive anarchist ideologies in mainland China. Only since the 1990s have researchers examined the revival of the Esperanto movement during the Second Sino-Japanese War. However, these discussions have largely centred on mainland antiwar magazines and their propaganda strategies. A substantial yet overlooked corpus of Esperanto resistance literature, translations, and criticism produced in wartime Hong Kong, encompassing contributions by more than 300 Chinese and international authors and translators from eight countries across East Asia, Europe, and North America, represents a significant gap in the study of modern Hong Kong literature and Chinese anti-Japanese war literature. This project aims at investigating Esperanto resistance writings and translations of wartime Hong Kong from transnational and transcultural perspectives. It focuses on three intersecting Esperanto intellectual-editorial groups led by (i) the Hungarian Esperantist Ferenc Braun; (ii) the Chinese Esperantists Fang Shanjing and Fu Bicheng; and (iii) the Hong Kong Esperanto and Sin Wenz associations. They issued antiwar periodicals solely in Esperanto (e.g. Orienta Kuriero and Voĉoj el Oriento), alongside Chinese-Esperanto bilingual periodicals (e.g. Tagiĝo and the Bulletin of Hong Kong Sin Wenz Association), as well as Esperanto translations of modern Chinese literature. The project examines how Hong Kong’s Esperanto publishing produced a multidimensional landscape of wartime narratives, heterogeneous yet locally rooted and internationally connected. It compares these periodicals with mainland counterparts and examines their reframing of wartime politics and imaginaries within global internationalist and Esperanto networks. It further explores how Hong Kong-based Esperantists and their collaborators cultivated a transcultural resistance literature that crossed ethnic boundaries and negotiated the tension between Esperanto’s cosmopolitanism and its nationalist instrumentalization. By recovering this long-neglected chapter, the project will contribute to scholarship on Chinese anti-Japanese war literature, repositioning it in relation to international leftist ideologies and literary currents. It will expand the scope of modern Hong Kong literary studies by emphasizing the roles of Chinese and international Esperantists and Esperanto advocates, and it will further deepen our understanding of the transcultural and hybrid articulations of Hong Kong literature in wartime. N/A

titleEnglish: Explaining the changing industrial organization of semiconductor global production networks in the post-pandemic Northeast and Southeast Asia
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Since the early 2020s, semiconductors or “chips” have become extremely important in both economic and geopolitical terms that provide the real-world context for this proposed research. The global market for semiconductors is huge, exceeding US$627 billion in 2024, and is expected to reach over US$1 trillion by 2030. Hong Kong is the world’s largest exporter of semiconductors, at about US$230 billion in value in 2023. Semiconductors are the “brains” that drive technological innovation and performance capabilities in many industries, such as AI, data centres, platform economy, etc.. But who makes chips and where are chip factories (“fabs”) located? East Asia is now the world’s dominant centre of chip production that is organized through complex production networks spanning different geographical locations and involving many different firms. Some 80% of the world’s chips are now manufactured in East Asia and over 90% of the most advanced chips are fabricated in only a handful of locations in East Asia (notably Taiwan and South Korea). In their reshoring or nearshoring drives towards “more domestic chips production”, big powers such as the United States and the European Union have enacted since 2023 over US$100 billion worth of subsidies, grants, loans, and tax incentives through targeted industrial policy. The imposition of severe restrictions on technology exports to China by the US government since 2019 has further escalated the role of geopolitics and national security considerations in shaping global semiconductor manufacturing and market development. This research proposes to explain the continual role and likely dominance of East Asia in semiconductor production networks that will have profound implications for future academic research and public policy and practice. It builds on the PI’s two decade-long research into electronics global production networks and the political economy of state-led development in East Asia. Through qualitative interviews with at least 50 firms and government institutions and collation of secondary data in different East Asian locations, this research aims to provide new knowledge on the geographical reconfigurations of semiconductor production facilities and the changing industrial organization of semiconductor production ecosystems in East Asia from 2027. It explains how these key changes come about and why they matter for economic development in Hong Kong and Greater China. Beyond academic impact, these research findings will benefit government institutions, business firms, and industry associations in East Asia. N/A

titleEnglish: Phantoms and Anchors: A Strategic Dynamic State-Firm Nexus Approach to Spatial Outcomes in China's High-Tech Zones
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chung, Calvin King Lam Dr Zhang, Mengzhu
abstract: Goal: For this qualitative study, supplemented by quantitative analysis, we propose a new conceptual framework, the Strategic Dynamic State-Firm Nexus (SDSF-Nexus), to explain a critical spatial paradox: the proliferation of ‘phantom’ businesses in the Qianhai Cooperation Zone versus the concentration of ‘anchored’ firms in the Hetao Cooperation Zone, two key high-tech zones in Shenzhen, China’s national innovation hub. Despite sharing similar institutional mandates for innovation, these two zones exhibit starkly divergent outcomes. Instead of viewing locational outcomes as unilateral firm decisions (e.g., regulatory arbitrage) or simple products of state policy (e.g., state entrepreneurialism), we hypothesise that they are co-produced through a dynamic and contested process of state-firm interaction. We will critically analyze policy documents and reconstruct the specific processes of negotiation and governance through interviews with key state and firm actors to verify our hypothesis. Significance: China’s national strategy increasingly relies on innovation zones to drive high-tech growth and regional integration, particularly within the Greater Bay Area. However, the phenomenon of ‘phantom’ businesses, where firms register for benefits without establishing a physical presence, fundamentally challenges the effectiveness of these multi-billion-dollar place-based policies. It raises the risk that these zones become symbolic spaces of registration rather than substantive hubs of industrial development, undermining their intended economic role. The findings are crucial for informing more effective governance in Shenzhen and Hong Kong, ensuring cross-border initiatives like Hetao deliver genuine economic value and industrial clustering. Researching how these divergent outcomes are produced is vital for developing theoretical insights into state-firm dynamics and the unintended consequences of incentive-driven urban development. Novelty: Previous studies on firm location have been largely bifurcated. On one side, firm-centric explanations (e.g., strategic coupling, regulatory arbitrage) often downplay the proactive and selective role of the state, treating it as a passive institutional backdrop. On the other, state-centric approaches (e.g., state entrepreneurialism) tend to overlook the strategic agency of firms, viewing them as mere recipients of policy. Both perspectives offer insightful, yet partial, explanations of spatial economic outcomes. Building on these studies, we propose the SDSF-Nexus to examine such outcomes as a process involving concurrent struggles over state’s governance logic and firm’ strategic responses. This framework treats state and firm actors as equally strategic agents whose dialectical relationship co-determines the alignment or separation of corporate registration and physical operation. N/A

titleEnglish: Who is ‘Us’ in Crisis? Moral Registers and Boundary Making in Pandemic Politics in Korea and the United States
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Graham, Jesse Prof LIANG, Hai
abstract: When crises strike, whether pandemics, disasters, or wars, societies do more than respond to immediate threats. They also renegotiate boundaries of solidarity and exclusion, deciding who is embraced within the community and who is left outside it. These processes vary across political cultures: some emphasize collective duty and authority, others liberty and individual choice. Why, then, do societies facing the same crisis respond in such different ways of defining belonging and obligation? This project examines how political leaders, media, and citizens use moral language to draw boundaries of solidarity and exclusion during the COVID-19 pandemic, a global emergency that revealed how contrasting cultural orientations shape crisis response. Korea and the United States provide a revealing comparison: both are advanced democracies, yet their responses reflected divergent emphases on collectivism and individualism. Building on Moral Foundations Theory, we advance the concept of “moral registers,” recurring patterns of moral justification through which culturally distinct moral foundations are translated into boundary‑drawing practices. We employ a dual methodological strategy. First, large-scale computational text analysis of political speeches and media coverage traces how moral registers and boundary-drawing language emerged and shifted over the course of the pandemic. Second, conjoint experiments test their causal influence by varying the justifications attached to crisis policies such as vaccine allocation and mobility controls. We hypothesize that South Korean discourse leaned more heavily on authority and collective duty, while U.S. discourse featured stronger liberty registers and sharper partisan divides. Universalist concern appeared in both but was linked to more inclusive or exclusive boundaries depending on context. In Korea, authority registers are expected to increase support for citizen-first and restrictive policies. In the U.S., liberty registers are expected to boost preferences for less coercive rules and broader mobility, sometimes at the expense of out‑group inclusion. Across both contexts, universalist registers should consistently expand support for inclusive policies, showing how political cultures condition the moral language that shapes solidarity and exclusion. This work will show that moral registers are the missing link between broad cultural orientations and the concrete practices of boundary-making in crisis. More broadly, it provides the first integrated cross-cultural analysis of moral language in both elite discourse and public opinion, and delivers comparative datasets, methodological tools, and practical insights for policymakers and communicators seeking to foster solidarity and resilience in democratic societies. N/A

titleEnglish: The Development of Green Fintech and Regulatory Responses: Chinese Experiences and International Implications
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Arner, Douglas Wayne Prof HO, Virginia HARPER
abstract: This project aims to comprehensively examine China's regulatory development for Green Fintech. Green Fintech is a new term coined as a combination of green finance and financial technology (Fintech) to highlight the implementation of environmental sustainability objectives through the medium of Fintech. While Green Fintech has become an increasingly topical issue with a growing body of international and comparative literature, the case of China has remained under-studied, while it is of unique value given China's leading role in Fintech, strong emphasis on green finance and significant international impact. Despite some studies on the development of Green Fintech in China, more is clearly needed. First, the existing studies are mostly conducted from the economic and technological perspectives, and legal research has not received the level of attention it deserves. Second, while most existing legal studies are either focused on green finance or Fintech, there is a dearth of research on the emerging regulatory issues brought by Green Fintech as a new business model. Last, due to the newness of Green Fintech, extant research on its regulation is mostly out of date, and most of them are doctrinal in nature, with inadequate attention having been paid to enforcement issues. This proposed project will fill the research gap, undertaking a comprehensive, contextualized and empirical study of the development and regulation of Green Fintech in China. Besides examining the key elements of the regulatory regime, the project will also propose a grounded theory to understand the dynamics of the making and enforcement of the regulatory regime. Further, it will critically evaluate the extent to which China has effectively responded to the regulatory challenges posed by Green Fintech, and based on such examination, put forward suggestions for improvement and draw relevant implications. The results of this project will be of both theoretical and practical significance. Theoretically, by examining the Chinese experience, it will make an important contribution to the international discourse and debate on the development and regulation of Green Fintech. On the practical side, it can assist the international community, including private parties, market institutions and policymakers, to better understand China's approach to developing and regulating Green Fintech. It has particular relevance to Hong Kong which aspires to become a Green Fintech hub and has increasing market integration and economic ties with Mainland China. N/A

titleEnglish: Communicating Like a Machine? How Intelligent Agents Influence Our Communication
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr CHEN, Anfan Prof MOU, Yi
abstract: Recent advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), particularly through large language models (LLMs), have allowed intelligent agents (IAs) such as text-based conversational bots to communicate in human-like ways. However, significant differences exist between human and machine communication, with research indicating that IAs often exhibit less variability and creativity in their communication styles (biased toward the average). This raises the concern that humans could adjust their writing to align closely with IAs, posing risks to diversity, innovation, and even human civilization itself. To explore this issue, the proposed project introduces a machine-likeness hypothesis, suggesting that humans may adopt the writing styles of LLM-based chatbots, which could lead to a more machine-like communication style in both human-to-IA communication (HIAC) and human-to-human communication (HHC). The project aims to test this hypothesis through both computational and experimental studies. The computational part will analyze large-scale, real-world conversations with ChatGPT sourced from two independent datasets. We argue that perplexity (PPL), a measure of text predictability, can be used to assess the writing styles of both humans and IAs. A convergence in PPL scores over time would indicate the emergence of machine-likeness. Three online experiments will be conducted with representative samples of the population communicating with ChatGPT or DeepSeek to further validate our computational findings. In addition to estimating changes in PPL, these experiments will investigate the mechanisms underlying machine-like behavior. Specifically, the first experiment will manipulate communication contexts to investigate whether the effects stem from low-level priming, social accommodation, perceptions, or any combination thereof. The second experiment will change how participants view their conversational partners to examine whether outcomes shift when IAs are perceived as human. The third experiment will have participants communicate first with IAs and then with humans to test whether the influence from the initial communications persists. This study has the potential to make several important contributions. Methodologically, by formalizing how we measure communication differences through PPL, it enhances our understanding of HHC compared to HIAC. Theoretically, it connects micro-level communication with IAs to macro-level social consequences, suggesting that our linguistic evolution may be trending toward a machine-like style, which could negatively impact human civilization. In addition, the project will explore the underlying mechanisms, such as priming, social accommodation, and perceptions of conversation partners from various theoretical perspectives. N/A

titleEnglish: Effects of acceptance-based self-care intervention for older adults with multimorbidity: a randomized controlled trial
department: The Nethersole School of Nursing, Faculty of Medicine
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Helen Yue-lai Dr Cheng, Calvin Pak-Wing Dr Choi, Kai-chow Prof Lee, Kam-pui
abstract: The world’s aging population faces a growing challenge as older adults increasingly live with multimorbidity, defined as more than two chronic diseases. Multimorbidity affects more than half of community-dwelling older adults and places significant burdens on both individuals and healthcare systems. However, current disease management fails to address the unique challenges older adults face in multimorbidity, including complex treatment regimens and the overwhelming task of coordinating different healthcare requirements. A particularly concerning but often overlooked aspect in the self-care of multimorbidity management is psychological distress. Older adults with multimorbidity commonly experience psychological distress, such as depressive symptoms that fall below clinical thresholds, and significantly impact their ability to manage health conditions. Previous work showed that acceptance-based intervention increased psychological flexibility and exercise adherence among older adults with chronic disease, helping them cope with psychological distress and focus on valued action despite health limitations. The proposed 27-month three-arm parallel, repeated measure, randomized controlled trial aims to evaluate an acceptance-based self-care intervention for community-dwelling older adults with multimorbidity. A total of 306 participants aged ≥ 60 years and with multimorbidity will be recruited from elderly centers across Hong Kong, and randomly assigned to the acceptance-based self-care group (AG), self-care only group (SG), or control group. The control group will receive usual care only, whereas a 12-week program comprising four home visits and five telephone calls will be delivered to the SG and AG. Specially, acceptance-based interventions (e.g., mindfulness and value clarification) will be integrated into self-care activities (e.g., medication use and lifestyle modifications) for the AG. Primary outcome is self-care activities, measured by Self-rated Abilities for Health Practices. Secondary outcomes include perceived treatment burden, physical functioning, psychological wellbeing, quality of life, and healthcare use. Data will be collected at baseline and at 3, 6, and 9 months post-allocation to assess both the immediate and sustained effects. A descriptive qualitative study with a subsample of 20 AG participants will explore how the intervention influenced outcomes. This study will address a critical gap in multimorbidity management by targeting psychological distress that hinder self-care. The findings will contribute to developing an acceptance-based self-care program that community service providers can adopt to improve holistic support for multimorbidity. This program may also reduce avoidable healthcare use, such as emergency department visits and admissions. The proposed program has the potential for implementation in any country facing multimorbidity in an aging population. N/A

titleEnglish: Cross-linguistic investigation of co-speech gestures and prosody in Chinese languages
department: Dept of Linguistics and Modern Languages
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof So, Catherine Wing-chee Prof Wang, Feifan
abstract: Human communication is inherently multimodal with various types of gesture often accompanying speech naturally in different languages, e.g. manual and facial gestures. Previous studies based mostly on stress languages like English have shown that the speech-gesture alignment is not random but is constrained by the prosodic structure of a language: gestural prominence (e.g., the apex of a movement) often aligns with prosodic prominence in terms of fundamental frequency (F0, e.g., the peak of the stressed syllable). Two accounts were proposed to explain such alignment with F0: linguistic vs. biomechanical. However, a few studies on tone languages illustrated that the alignment is more on the syllable or vowel onsets, as F0 functions differently in tone languages, casting doubts on the universal validity of the two accounts. In order to better-understand the underlying mechanisms of speech-gesture alignment cross-linguistically, this project examines three representative Chinese tone languages with different prosodic patterns (Cantonese, Mandarin, Shanghainese), allowing more precise manipulation of various prosodic factors (e.g., stress, tone sandhi, focus prominence, tone reduction) on alignment patterns in order to isolate their effects individually. Both controlled elicitation and semi-spontaneous tasks will be incorporated for data collection. The data also allow exploration of larger issues in speech-gesture alignments, e.g., how manual and non-manual gestures may differ in and the effects of gesture types on their alignment patterns with speech. The findings not only can elucidate how Chinese tone languages diverge from stress languages in speech-gesture alignment, but can also illustrate how tone languages may differ from one another. This can widen the research perspectives on speech-gesture alignment with rich novel data. In addition to theoretical contributions, understanding how prosody shapes the organization of gesture in Chinese languages also addresses urgent real-world needs. As artificial intelligence and humanoid robots continue to evolve, enabling machines to engage in natural, human-like multimodal communication has become a critical frontier. Although impressive speech synthesis technologies can now make robots talk like human, the generation of natural, synchronized co-speech gestures still remains underdeveloped. Existing research was mostly based on non-tone languages like English. The present project is to address such a research gap with three Chinese languages having diverse prosodic patterns and with many speakers. The findings of this project will have direct relevance for the next technological leap in multimodal human-machine communications as they can be readily applied to improve the naturalness and effectiveness of such communications. N/A

titleEnglish: Modelling Climate Gentrification: A GeoAI-ABM Framework for the Greater Bay Area
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Global climate change is increasingly recognised as a paramount threat to the low-lying coastal cities, which are home to more than half the world’s population and concentrated economic and infrastructural assets. The Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area (GBA), a world-class urban cluster and a powerhouse of technological and economic innovation, is particularly vulnerable to the intensifying climate-related hazards, including sea-level rise, recurrent flooding and storm surges, and extreme heatwaves. This vulnerability, compounded by the region’s exceptionally high population density, diverse demographic composition and socio-cultural communities, is anticipated to profoundly impact the urban built environment, property markets, and residential mobility. Consequently, these pressures threaten to alter the fundamental characteristics of neighbourhoods, potentially displacing vulnerable populations and instigating a new wave of gentrification termed climate gentrification. Existing scholarly literature has extensively documented the corrosive societal impacts of traditional gentrification, including the loss of affordable housing, forced displacement of low-income and marginalized residents, exacerbated economic and social inequalities, the erosion of cultural identity, and the fostering of community tensions. Climate gentrification presents a new, urgent variant of this injustice. If left unaddressed, this phenomenon could generate significant global repercussions, creating feedback loops that cascade with and compound the biophysical hazards of climate change, thereby deepening socio-ecological divides. To address this emerging and complex challenge, this project aims to develop a comprehensive, tri-dimensional analytical framework—encompassing natural-physical, urban-built, and social-behavioural environments—to holistically understand the primary drivers of potential climate gentrification. To operationalize this framework, we will create a spatially explicit model that integrates Machine Learning (ML) and Geographic Artificial Intelligence (GeoAI) techniques with Agent-Based Modelling (ABM). This integrated computational approach will be uniquely capable of simulating complex human-environment interactions and predicting the probability of climate gentrification under various future climate change and urban development scenarios. Furthermore, a dynamic, user-friendly dashboard application platform will be developed to serve as a digital policy sandbox, allowing stakeholders to test and visualize the potential effectiveness of various interventions across the GBA's unique multi-jurisdictional settings. Project outputs will include: 1) A comprehensive geospatial data inventory profiling key climate-related hazards (sea-level rise, flooding, storm surges, and extreme heatwaves) and human demographic/behavioural factors in the GBA; 2) A novel GeoAI-ABM framework to identify and predict potential gentrification hotspots and tipping points; and 3) A policy-intervention dashboard to empower local governments and planning authorities with evidence-based tools for coordinated, equitable, and resilient decision-making. N/A

titleEnglish: Recovery from/in sexual orientation and gender identity and expression change efforts: A survivor-led investigation of meaning-making, posttraumatic growth, and healing journeys
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Huang, Yu Te Prof Mak, Winnie Wing-Sze
abstract: Sexual orientation and gender identity and expression change efforts (SOGIECE) have been widely condemned for their harmful psychological impacts on lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, and other gender and sexually diverse (LGBTQ+) individuals. While existing literature has documented the trauma associated with these practices, there remains a critical gap in understanding how survivors of SOGIECE conceptualize recovery, make sense of their traumatic experiences, and navigate retraumatization and healing within sociocultural contexts that may continue to perpetuate invalidation. This project adopts a survivor-centered, exploratory sequential mixed-methods design to investigate the recovery experiences and trajectories of survivors of SOGIECE in Hong Kong. Study 1 will involve semi-structured interviews with 25 survivors to explore their perceptions of trauma, the re-experiencing of harm through interactions with non-affirming systems, and their definitions of recovery. Additionally, it will examine how survivors engage in narrative reconstruction, reclaim their identities, and make meaning of their experiences to foster resilience and healing. These qualitative insights will lead to the development of a survivor-informed conceptual framework of recovery and guide the survey design in Study 2. This second phase will employ a longitudinal survey across three waves (baseline, 12 months, and 24 months) to quantitatively assess trauma responses, exposure to retraumatization, and posttraumatic growth. A sample of 270 survivors will be drawn from an estimated pool of 1,233 LGBTQ+ individuals. Statistical analyses will track changes in recovery trajectories over time and identify factors that promote posttraumatic growth and recovery. Theoretically, the study represents a paradigm shift by centering a survivor-led approach to conceptualizing recovery from/in SOGIECE. Rather than imposing predefined frameworks, it foregrounds the lived experiences and meaning-making processes of survivors. By differentiating between “recovery from” and “recovery in” SOGIECE, this study theorizes how healing may occur within, rather than outside of, oppressive systems, especially in collectivist societies where separation from family or community is neither feasible nor culturally acceptable. This perspective embraces recovery as a non-linear, culturally embedded, and relational journey. Practically, the findings will inform clinical practices, community-based support strategies, and policy development to improve survivor well-being. The study will provide empirical evidence to support trauma-informed care models that validate survivor experiences and address retraumatization in post-SOGIECE environments. By situating recovery within the cultural realities of Hong Kong and broader Asian contexts, this research offers critical insights for developing culturally responsive recovery interventions and advocacy efforts to regulate and ultimately eliminate conversion practices. N/A

titleEnglish: Fostering Cross-Cultural Understanding: A Linguistic Comparison of Everyday Chinese Life in Chinese and English Autobiographies
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Highly successful autobiographies by Chinese migrant writers—notably Jung Chang’s Wild Swans—have significantly influenced international views of China. While previous scholarship has examined the political nature of these works, there has been no study of how they depict everyday Chinese life to different linguistic audiences, in both the original texts and translations. This project explores the representation of daily life as a key means of creating empathy, the capacity to both feel and understand another person’s emotional experience, arguing that cross-cultural memoir is the ideal site for investigating these empathic strategies. By analysing, at the linguistic level, how these texts attempt to foster empathy across cultures, the study contributes to broader discussions of how intercultural social cohesion and harmony is fostered, in line with the aims of UN Sustainable Development Goals. To achieve this, the project introduces an innovative methodology, centred on linguistic analysis of an eight-million-word corpus of contemporary memoirs about China, with three text groups: nine by Chinese migrants in English, eight in Chinese, and nine by Anglophone travel writers, all with translations into English or Chinese. This dataset makes it possible to separately analyze three key factors in cultural mediation: the audience (domestic vs. international), the author's position (cultural insider vs. outsider), and who is mediating the culture (the author or a translator). This corpus will be analysed through combining Corpus Linguistics methods for large-scale pattern discovery and Systemic Functional Linguistics for principled manual analysis to reveal cultural variations. The project's primary theoretical contribution is the development of a “grammar of empathy”: a framework of the specific linguistic strategies authors use to bridge cultural and affective distances between English and Chinese. The project will also enrich corpus linguistics with manual analytical power and cross-linguistic insight. It will move Translation Studies beyond its traditional source-to-target paradigm by conceptualising migrant writing in English as translation of Chinese culture, and subsequently translated into the language of the source culture. The project should be of interest to anyone who wants to better understand the socio-therapeutic potential of literature towards a sustainable future for all. The grammar of empathy framework will be operationalised as a toolkit for public engagement through workshops and podcasts, equipping audiences including the general public and publishers with tools to foster deeper cross-cultural understanding. These impact activities aim to contribute toward the promotion of peaceful and inclusive societies. N/A

titleEnglish: Leveraging Large Language Models for Personalized Learning in the Acquisition of Japanese Compound Verbs
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Pun, Man-On
abstract: The primary aim of this project is to explore the application of large language models (LLMs), such as GPT, to support the acquisition of Japanese Compound Verbs (CVs) through personalized learning tailored to individual needs. Additionally, the project seeks to identify the benefits, outcomes, and challenges associated with using GPT as a personal tutor in next-generation Japanese language teaching and learning. Mastering Japanese CVs is a persistent challenge for many learners. Traditionally, classroom-based methods have dominated language acquisition. However, since the introduction of GPT in late 2022, there has been growing interest in its potential for enhancing language learning. While extensive research exists on the linguistics and acquisition of CVs, there remains no consensus on best practices for teaching these structures. Moreover, the growing application of GPT in language education is both inevitable and transformative. This project seeks to leverage GPT’s advanced analytical capabilities to address a significant gap in Japanese language pedagogy: providing personalized, effective support for CV acquisition—an area not well-served by traditional teaching methods. The project will be carried out in three phases. In the first phase (months 1–2), the research team will classify essential CVs into categories and prepare for the experimental phase. The second phase (months 3–13) will involve testing intermediate learners' acquisition of these CVs. In the third phase (months 14–24), the experiment will continue with advanced learners. Ultimately, the project will offer a comprehensive view of how artificial general intelligence (AGI) can be applied to CV acquisition for Japanese language learners. This pioneering study aims to analyze the application of GPT in supporting advanced learners in Hong Kong, drawing on expertise from linguistics and machine learning. The project is expected to provide valuable insights into vocabulary acquisition, one of the key aspects of language learning. The findings will be crucial for informing future CV teaching methodologies, benefiting both teachers and learners of Japanese. N/A

titleEnglish: Declining Patrilocality and Persisting Patriarchy? Village Membership Rules under Family Changes and Land Development in Rural China
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Patrilocality expects married couples to live in the husband’s village in rural China, leading to village membership rules that link the official household registration (hukou) system with membership in the family lineage. Patrilocal traditions assume men as rightful inheritors and women as transient members, excluding tens of millions of women’s names from village land contracts. Previous studies see such local customs as difficult to rectify in conservative communities, but in the Yangtze River Delta, weak lineage culture, economic growth, and land development have encouraged women to stay in their prosperous home villages, with a rise of non-patrilocal families. It is to be examined whether the decline of patrilocality has undermined the patriarchal membership rules at the collective level. Village membership is key to accessing rural resources (access to farmland, homestead, and collective benefits), especially when they become more valuable in land development. ¬Village membership rules have been studied under the state-society, majority-minority, insider-outsider dichotomies that highlight grassroots alliances surrounding indigenous rights. Under this framework, many rural collectives deny villagers’ rights of married-out women as a “domestic” issue for a “minority” group. This project challenges this argument by illustrating how land development has reinforced the public nature of the “domestic” problem, and how family changes have pushed more people to join the “minority” via non-patrilocal practices. These two trends call for a systematic study of the “partial-to-full” continuum instead of the binary membership status. This project will focus on a village in the Yangtze River Delta that is representative of family changes and land development in the region. The project will collect survey data and match villagers’ relational positions (“staying”, “married-in”, and “married-out” men and women) with their varying membership status (access to farmland, homestead, and collective benefits) based on fsQCA (Fuzzy-set Qualitative Comparative Analysis). The condition-outcome patterns will be enriched by in-depth analysis of how membership rules are negotiated at the intersection of family relations, communal dynamics, and government policies, based on interviews with villagers, village leaders, and local officials. This project will speak to family changes in patriarchal societies, local governance in post-socialist countries, and rural transformations in developing economies. The findings will contribute to suggestions and actions toward social stability, gender equality, inclusive citizenship, and sustainable development. N/A

titleEnglish: A Motivational Interviewing-based Chatbot to Improve Sleep in Adolescents with Inadequate Sleep: A Randomized Controlled Trial
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Li, Man-ho Dr Li, Shirley Xin Dr Tse, Ka Wo Prof Wing, Yun Kwok Prof Yang, Xue
abstract: Sleep deprivation is a prevalent issue among adolescents, with local data suggested over 30% sleep less than 7h on school nights, which is far less than the recommended duration (8h). This sleep problem has intensified as our data showed that adolescents slept 0.36h less compared to a decade ago. This widespread phenomenon is often normalized as typical adolescent practice. However, numerous data suggested sleep deprivation closely link with multiple adverse consequences in academic performance, mental health, physical health and social relationship, which is increasingly recognized as one of the leading modifiable causes of chronic diseases. Addressing modifiable factors that contribute to sleep deprivation is crucial for enhancing sleep in adolescents. Strategies such as delaying school start time has been identified as a proven effective strategy for improving sleep duration, yet systemic barriers, particularly within Asian cultures that prioritize academic achievement impede their implementation. Alternative strategies, such as advancing bedtime, have shown promise in extending sleep duration, particularly when adolescents' motivation is enhanced. For example, our research group has demonstrated that group-based Motivational Interviewing (MI) intervention can successfully facilitate earlier bedtimes and increase sleep duration in sleep deprived adolescents. However, the scarcity of trained MI therapists constrains the wider dissemination of this already proven intervention, highlighting the urgent need for alternative dissemination methods to increase the accessibility and benefit more adolescents. The development of chatbot technology presents a viable framework for disseminating motivational interviewing intervention. This approach leverages the responsive nature of conversational agents, allowing for dynamic interactions that mimic therapist-client exchanges. While several reviews highlight the positive impact of chatbot-based intervention in improving physical activity, dietary habits and tobacco cessation, particularly when delivered in a MI style. Surprisingly, there is very limited trial that has focused on sleep issue, which emphasize the pressing need for developing highly accessible, feasible and practical intervention. Given the very limited data on sleep health related chatbot, this study aims to conduct a randomized controlled trial to evaluate the efficacy of different chatbot types including a motivational interviewing-oriented chatbot and neutral educational chatbot in promoting healthy sleep behaviors among sleep deprived adolescents, relative to a health education control group. The findings of this project will provide critical insights and optimizing chatbot for improving sleep health, potentially informing future public health strategies. N/A

titleEnglish: Multidimensional Handwriting Assessment Using a Digital Tablet: Empirical Evaluations in Children with Typical and Atypical Writing Development
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Li, Feng Prof Ohyanagi, Toshio Prof Ye, Yanyan
abstract: Handwriting is an essential skill and a fundamental part of a child’s education. Children who exhibit significant difficulties with handwriting are referred to as having developmental dysgraphia, with a prevalence estimated to be as high as 15% among school-age children. Considering the high graphic complexity of the Chinese script, the prevalence is expected to be even higher in Chinese societies. Despite this serious concern, traditional paper-based handwriting assessments have proven ineffective at identifying the risk of dysgraphia early on, pinpointing areas of difficulty, and determining the appropriate support measures. Therefore, early risk identification of dysgraphia using a more effective handwriting assessment tool is an urgent priority, especially in Chinese societies. An effective solution to this issue is to digitize handwriting assessment using technology. In the proposed project, we will develop a tablet-based handwriting assessment application for Simplified Chinese characters, which we refer to as OAHaS-SC (Online Assessment of Handwriting and Spelling – Simplified Chinese). Importantly, our assessment application automatically records multidimensional metrics of the handwriting process, including writing accuracy, latency, duration, and stroke order. The application incorporates automatic scoring functionality, powered by a deep learning algorithm called Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models. The CNN-powered scoring functionality provides rapid, automatic, and unbiased judgments. Furthermore, the web-based assessment enables the simultaneous evaluation of large groups of children using tablets and provides immediate, detailed feedback, making it suitable for a wide range of settings, including classrooms and clinics. This project aims to achieve the following three objectives. First, we will develop an automated, multidimensional handwriting assessment application with CNN-powered scoring and confirm its psychometric properties (reliability and validity). Second, we will demonstrate the usefulness of various handwriting metrics (writing accuracy, latency, duration, and stroke order) by testing a theory-driven model for the relationship between visual-motor and cognitive-linguistic skills, handwriting metrics, and higher-order writing skills. Third, we will further demonstrate the practical utility of multidimensional handwriting metrics by testing their performance in identifying and characterizing different subtypes of children’s handwriting difficulties. From a theoretical perspective, the proposed study will advance the field by shedding light on the dynamics of handwriting processes, such as writing fluency and stroke order, as well as the factors that influence them. From a practical standpoint, our assessment application will be a valuable tool that allows teachers and other professionals to evaluate children’s handwriting development and difficulties more quickly, accurately, and thoroughly. N/A

titleEnglish: Shaping the AI Race: The Role of Legal Infrastructure in the United States and China
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This proposed research undertakes a thorough investigation and comparative analysis of the legal frameworks underpinning artificial intelligence development and deployment within the United States and China, set against the backdrop of the intensifying rivalry for AI supremacy between these two global powers. As AI continues to transform the world economy and recalibrate international relations, the study seeks to elucidate how both countries strategically marshal their legal architectures in pursuit of technological pre-eminence within the AI domain. In the United States, AI innovation flourishes primarily through a fragmented and multifaceted regulatory landscape, characterised by the interplay of numerous federal and state agencies and an agile, highly competitive private sector. Conversely, China employs a hybrid approach, wherein centralised policy direction is allied with decentralised local implementation, underpinned by a set of comprehensive, sector-specific regulations. Central legal aspects examined include data protection and privacy, intellectual property (IP) rights, and export controls—each playing a pivotal role in shaping the contours of national AI ecosystems. The research further engages with recent major technological advances, such as China’s DeepSeek and prominent American-developed large language models including GPT-4, Gemini, LLaMA, and xAI, in addition to analysing governmental strategic initiatives that exert significant influence on the evolution of AI policy. Ultimately, the study aims to shed light on the ramifications of these divergent legal infrastructures for the future trajectory of AI competition and global technological leadership, proffering substantive insights for the refinement of AI governance so as to foster enduring progress and innovation. N/A

titleEnglish: Profile-to-Action Roadmap for Healthy Ageing: Mapping Neurocognitive Profiles to 12-Month Health Risks in Cognitively Unimpaired Older Adults in Hong Kong
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Fong, Daniel Yee-Tak Prof Chan, Helen Yue-lai Prof Cheung, Carol Yim-lui Prof Lam, Thomas Yuen-tung Prof Mok, Vincent Chung-tong Prof Qi, Xiang Prof Woo, Jean
abstract: Hong Kong’s rapid population ageing demands proactive brain health strategies. Current prevention, targeting Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI), is often too late: irreversible neuropathology is already present, and 38% progress to dementia. This “wait-until-impaired” model is insufficient. Our prior United States research provides a solution, proving that “cognitively unimpaired” older adults display heterogeneous patterns in terms of strengthens and weakness across cognition domains. We identified distinct latent profiles (from 4 cognitive domains) that linked with cognitive determinants (such as age, income, and race) and also strongly predict 1-year risks (such as falls and functional decline). For example, a global-vulnerability profile had higher odds of multiple falls (4.66-fold) and depression (2.69-fold), while an orientation-weakened profile predicted shopping (1.79-fold) and banking (2.84-fold) difficulties. However, earlier studies omitted the language and social cognition domains; no study has yet mapped all six domains and comprehensively examined profile determinants and predictive value for future health risks in unimpaired adults. We will establish a 12-month cohort of 1,031 “cognitively unimpaired” Hong Kong residents (≥65). Participants will complete a culturally-adapted battery covering all six cognitive domains. We will comprehensively map determinants (spanning sociodemographics, built environment, cardiometabolic and other conditions, sensory status, lifestyle/behaviour, and psychological status) and track 12-month outcomes (including innovative retinal imaging biomarkers, neurocognitive performance, geriatric syndromes and function, mental/social/sleep health, health-related quality of life, and healthcare utilisation), adjusting for baseline. This project has four interconnected aims. We will: (1) Derive and validate the first-ever six-domain neurocognitive profiles, creating the first Hong Kong-specific map of cognitive heterogeneity. (2) Identify the comprehensive determinants for each profile, revealing targets for screening and prevention. (3) Link each profile to its unique 12-month risk horizon (e.g., falls, retinal biomarkers). (4) Translate this evidence-based engine into the project’s key deliverable: the “Cognitive Profile Calculator.” This clinic-ready, web-based tool will instantly convert a patient's scores into an actionable report, operationalising a proactive “screen → profile → targeted support → re-assess” cycle. This shifts prevention from “wait-until-impaired” to precise, profile-based interception, helping to curb future MCI and dementia burdens and adverse ageing outcomes. N/A

titleEnglish: Monitoring the antiviral effectiveness against acute severity and post-COVID sequelae following SARS-CoV-2 infection in a community outpatient setting
department: The Jockey Club School of Public Health and Primary Care
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Hui, Shu Cheong, David Prof Wei, Yuchen Prof Wong, King Ho Carlos Prof Yang, Aimin Prof Yeoh, Eng Kiong
abstract: While the COVID-19 antivirals, nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and molnupiravir, have generally been showed to be effective, these antivirals have been remarkably underused. Similar to influenza antivirals, COVID-19 antivirals may be less effective against new viral strains, especially given that SARS-CoV-2 antiviral resistance has been suggested. Given that a greater benefit of the antivirals among vaccinated individuals was previously observed, it is expected that the antiviral effectiveness may decrease seasonally considering the waning immunity over time, especially since stringent vaccination policies have been lifted in most countries after the COVID-19 pandemic. Based on these reasons, we hypothesize that the effectiveness of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and molnupiravir against acute severity and post-COVID sequelae in outpatient population is likely to decrease seasonally. We will carry out multiple target trial emulations in each of the four seasons of COVID-19 epidemics (2022/23, 2023/24, 2024/25, and 2025/26) in a community outpatient setting in Hong Kong. Population-based anonymized electronic health records will be obtained from the Hong Kong Hospital Authority and the Department of Health. We will include adult patients (aged 18 years or older) who have visited any of the 75 General Out-patient Clinics in Hong Kong for their COVID-19 with a documented COVID-19 diagnosis within the study period. The primary outcomes are acute and post-acute mortality. Secondary acute outcomes include all-cause hospitalization, ICU admission, and use of ventilatory support, while secondary post-acute outcomes include all-cause hospitalization and post-COVID sequelae. Study covariates will include demographics, chronic conditions, medication history, vaccination status, health services seeking behaviour, etc. Two sets of multiple target trials will be emulated to evaluate the effectiveness of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Set 1) and molnupiravir (Set 2) in each of the four seasons, respectively. For each trial in a season, we will estimate the effectiveness of initiating the antiviral of interest within 5 days of the index date and apply the cloning-censoring-weighting approach to address immortal time bias introduced by the treatment’s 5-day grace period. Cause-specific Cox proportional hazards models will be used to evaluate the antiviral effectiveness, with 95% bootstrapping confidence intervals in each of the seasons. To address the study hypothesis, meta-regression models will be used to assess the association between seasons and effectiveness of the antiviral of interest. We expect that our findings will support timely updates to treatment guidelines, enabling public health officials to access the most current information on antiviral effectiveness and ensuring that healthcare systems are prepared for outbreaks. N/A

titleEnglish: Images of Spatial and Environmental Inequality in Southeast Asian Cinema
department: Dept of Cultural and Religious Studies
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Economists and sociologists have highlighted the worsening inequality across the planet since the late 20th century. As Asian economies have experienced rapid growth, they have become spaces for intensifying income and wealth disparities. Having long been faced with uneven development, Southeast Asian nations have benefitted from the new ‘China Plus One’ investment strategy. Situated along the coast in the Pacific Ring of Fire in the face of rising sea levels, many of them nonetheless remain vulnerable to perennial natural calamities. Whereas writings about inequality conventionally focus on economic and sociological statistics, this research project aims to explore its cultural dimensions. Cinema studies have long been concerned with issues of inequality with their attention to dominant structures of gender, racial, and epistemological hierarchies in the film and entertainment industries. Less works have examined the spatial and environmental conditions of inequality. Most scholarship about Southeast Asian film tends to focus on individual national cinemas. Applying comparative, Inter-Asian, and South-South methods, this project will examine how representations of spatial and environmental inequality in understudied works from Thailand, Indonesia, Vietnam, Malaysia, and the Philippines could be comparatively categorized and analyzed as two recurrent types: 1) images of economic ruin, and 2) images of ecological disaster. First, ‘images of economic ruin’ refer to representations of abandoned spaces, buildings, and infrastructures, which signify the departure of industrial and financial capital. Second, ‘images of ecological disaster’ visualize extreme weather and environmental degradation through narratives about super typhoons, sinking cities, and barren rivers. As Southeast Asia has become a target of development and investment, the Hong Kong SAR government has strived to cultivate economic and cultural linkages with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). Despite the recent critical and commercial successes of Southeast Asian cinema in Hong Kong and around the world, the Hong Kong International and Asian Film Festivals tend to feature works from Southeast Asia less than those from East Asia. Global media representations of Southeast Asia in Hollywood and Hong Kong cinemas typically characterize it as poor, disorderly, sordid, violent, and inert. This project will examine how the common negative perception of Southeast Asia has been mediated and shaped by images of spatial and environmental inequality. Its objectives align with the UNDP’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of alleviating poverty and inequality by uncovering how their recurrent representations are reproduced through cinema. N/A

titleEnglish: Digital Inequalities from the Other Side? Testing an Alternative Framework Using the Case of Potentially Harmful Applications
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Michael Che-ming
abstract: Given the central role of digital technologies in people’s daily lives, digital inequalities—gaps in access (first level), usage and skills (second level), and benefits from technology use (third level) across the population—have become pressing issues that are profoundly shaping both academic research and public policy. Previous studies have typically viewed exclusion from technology as a problem, often assuming that access and use are inherently beneficial. However, as digital technologies grow more complex, many associated risks—such as privacy breaches, data misuse, and digital scams—have emerged. This project introduces a new framework for understanding digital inequality by considering “exclusionary advantages”—the notion that exclusion from certain digital technologies and specific use cases may confer advantages to some segments of the population while disadvantaging others, resulting in an understudied dimension of digital inequalities. Mirroring the classic three levels of inequality identified in previous studies, our alternative framework examines (1) access to harmful technologies, (2) usage patterns and skills for avoiding harmful technologies and related harms, and (3) the negative consequences of risky digital engagement. In this new approach, individuals who lack engagement with harmful digital systems may actually be better off because they avoid possible harms. As an initial empirical test, this project investigates potentially harmful applications (PHAs), which are applications that can potentially cause harms such as fraud or privacy invasions. As awareness of these risks grows, governments in China, the European Union, and elsewhere have begun to regulate PHAs to better protect digital citizens. Our study will conduct large-scale surveys incorporating data donation methods and computational analysis in major Chinese cities, allowing us to analyze actual PHA access and usage. By testing how an array of socio-digital factors predicts variables pertinent to the three levels in our framework, we aim to investigate how groups along these proposed dimensions suffer unequally from technological harms. The findings will have significant theoretical and practical implications. They will deepen our knowledge of digital inequalities by uncovering novel sites of inequality, offer new data on how harmful technologies can reproduce and exacerbate social disparities, as well as encourage policy and educational strategies that focus not only on expanding access but also on safeguarding users from digital harms. N/A

titleEnglish: All Quiet on the Korean Front? The Hidden Endgame of the Imjin War (1592-1598)
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: In both Ming official rhetoric and modern Chinese historiography, the Korean War of 1592-1598 (Imjin War) is remembered as a glorious military victory of the Ming-Korean alliance against Japanese invasion. This research project, to be materialized as a book, uncovers the inconvenient truth about how the War was ended: It was the front-line commanders of the Ming and Japanese forces who initiated the truce, and it was the Ming court which ultimately chose to turn a blind eye. Lurking behind that ostensibly heroic narrative of the War were intriguing stalemate, secret truces, negotiations, deceit, denial, and political maneuvering. By 1598, exhaustion on all sides made peace inevitable. Ming army and Korean navy suffered setbacks, while Japanese troops under Toyotomi Hideyoshi managed to hold their ground without hope of further advance. Following Hideyoshi’s death in September, the caretaker government under Tokugawa Ieyasu ordered a full withdrawal from Korea. Ming front-line commanders took the initiative to secretly negotiate with their Japanese rivals for truce. Xing Jie, the Ming Commissioner for the Korean Campaign, and his deputy Wan Shide, quietly authorized the negotiation. Consequently, messengers were sent, meetings convened, hostages traded, and temporary ceasefires arranged. Yet even as these covert dealings unfolded, renewed clashes culminated in the Battle of Noryang Straits on December 15, 1598, after which Xing Jie declared “great victories on four fronts.” Despite opposition from the Korean court and the impeachment of Ding Yingtai the Ming Censor, Xing’s adroit political manipulation successfully obscured the genuine endgame of the War. Ultimately, Ming Emperor Shenzong and his senior ministers decided that some stones were best left unturned. They endorsed Xing Jie’s heroic-epic trope of victory over Japan. However successful Xing Jie’s political spin was, as this project shows, the inconvenient truth of the endgame of the War could still be reconstructed through careful cross-examination of archives from the three belligerents. This project will show that early modern East Asian international order was characterized by high extent of information-manipulation by all parties to an extent that is unthinkable to modern mind. This project will also emphasize the autonomy and agency of individuals “on the ground”: front-line commanders, soldiers, hostages, etc. In short, this project will shed new light on the genuine operation of war and peace in East Asia within the framework of Ming tributary system. N/A

titleEnglish: Humanising Algorithmic Curation? “Creator operatives” as cultural intermediaries in Chinese video-sharing platforms
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Dong, Chenyu Prof Fung, Anthony Ying-him
abstract: This project investigates the under-researched but influential workforce of creator operatives employed by Chinese video‑sharing platforms. Creator operatives are a distinct group of human intermediaries between platform algorithms and online creator influencers. They advise, manage, promote, and sometimes negotiate on behalf of creators, shaping what content becomes visible and how creators earn money. The prevalence of these professionals in video-sharing platforms further demonstrates that a systematic organisation and exploitation of human labour remain essential to the operation of global tech/platform capitalism.
