Understanding these actors is an urgent matter because they mediate algorithmic power, professionalise parts of the creator economy, and raise questions about labour, governance, and accountability in platformised cultural ecosystems. This research addresses three main challenges. First, creator operatives are often prevalent yet hidden within platform ecosystems, making them difficult to locate and study. Second, their work straddles technical, commercial, and cultural domains and thus requires methods that can capture both knowledge (how operatives “read” and interpret algorithms) and practices (how they coordinate between platforms and creators). Third, the Chinese context adds complexity because state regulation, platform policies, and the unique creator economy shape creator operatives’ strategies in ways that differ from Western contexts. To meet these challenges, we use an interdisciplinary and primarily qualitative approach. Fieldwork combines archival research, semi‑structured interviews with operatives and creators, and autoethnographic observation of operative workflows. The study maps the roles and organisation of Chinese creator operatives, documents the knowledge and expertise they develop to influence creators’ visibility and monetisation, and analyses interactions between human intermediaries and platform algorithmic systems. Ethical safeguards—confidentiality, anonymisation, and careful handling of sensitive material—are integral throughout the research design. The project will produce three types of deliverables. Empirically, it will provide the first comprehensive account of creator operative practices in Chinese video platforms, filling a gap in scholarship on the cultural and labour dimensions of platform curation and creator governance. Conceptually, it will advance theories of cultural intermediaries and platform governance by showing how human labour both complements and contests algorithmic systems. Practically, the findings will inform platform designers, policy-makers, and creator communities about the implications of such opaque human mediation on the formation and curation of online creator cultures. By centring the human agents who work alongside algorithms, this research reframes debates about automation, accountability, and cultural production in the platform age. N/A

titleEnglish: Law in Byzantium and China (10th-13th Centuries): Comparative Perspectives on the Legal Culture of Medieval Bureaucratic Empires
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof CHEUNG, Hiu Yu
abstract: This project will be the first-ever comparative study of the legal cultures of the Byzantine and Chinese empires in the Middle Ages (c. 500–1500), focusing on the 10th to 13th centuries. It is an exploratory effort that will contribute to the growing field of global medieval studies. It will also expand upon previous comparative research on China and the West in the pre-modern era, which has predominantly focused on the ancient Roman and Han Empires (c. 2nd century B.C. –3rd century A.D.). This is a collaborative, interdisciplinary project conducted by two historians of The Chinese University of Hong Kong: a PI (James MORTON) with expertise in Byzantine legal history and a Co-I (CHEUNG Hiu Yu) who specialises in the Chinese Sung Dynasty. Despite differences in their size, geography, and culture, medieval Byzantium and China shared a number of structural similarities. Most notably, they were both examples of pre-modern bureaucratic empires; that is, they were large, multi-ethnic states ruled by a monarch who oversaw a centralized administrative government. Moreover, both had relatively meritocratic educational systems and positioned themselves ideologically as successors of ancient predecessors (Romans; Qin/Han/Tang). The reason for focusing on the 10th to 13th centuries is that the two empires underwent remarkably similar historical processes in this period that affected their laws and legal culture in comparable ways. ‘Legal culture’ describes the relationship between law as an abstract system and the historical social, cultural, and practical contexts in which it operated. In other words, how did members of those societies think about and use the law? We divide the topic into five main areas (ideology; systematisation; jurisprudence; application; book culture), which we shall approach through methods of comparative textual and material analysis. Our goal is not just to highlight similarities and differences between the two states, but to identify important variables in each case that help to explain why they developed in their own distinctive ways. In other words, comparing Byzantium and China can also improve our understanding of both states on their own terms. The main outputs of this project will be three peer-reviewed journal articles and three presentations at international conferences. Our intention is to use these outputs as the basis for future comparative research projects that will build upon their findings by pursuing new topics and engaging more scholars in related fields by, for example, organising an international conference and edited volume. N/A

titleEnglish: Embodied Knowledge, Measured Performance: Singularities in Bamboo Weaving
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Sasatani, Masamichi
abstract: Interest in and demand for sustainable materials is growing. Bamboo’s unique combination of flexibility, strength, and bending resistance has made it a cornerstone of traditional craftsmanship, particularly in woven forms, such as baskets. In traditional bamboo weaving, skilled craftspeople rely on tacit knowledge to introduce singularities – points of changing curvature at which bending stresses are concentrated and therefore at which cracking and failure are likely to occur. However, such craftspeople are vanishing, and nuanced ways of negotiating singularities are being lost, leaving technical and cultural gaps. This loss of knowledge will restrict innovation and impede the use of bamboo weaving in contemporary design applications, such as in architectural use. The proposed study will therefore systematically analyse bamboo’s elastoplastic behaviour, along with its curvature and structural characteristics at singularities in woven architectural structures, to harness its potential for use in structurally reliable and innovative architectural applications. Through experimental testing focusing on bamboo’s bending and elastoplastic behaviour under controlled loading conditions, this study will develop a methodology that bridges traditional craftsmanship and modern engineering to generate foundational knowledge of bamboo’s mechanical properties and structural behaviour. The study will characterise the material properties of bamboo, conduct physical experiments and digital simulations to analyse stress distribution, displacement, and bending limits, and construct prototype structures and mock-ups to validate the experimental findings. It will also develop computational design tools to model the relationship between form and performance, particularly at singularities. This work will both preserve intangible cultural heritage and promote sustainable architectural practices. By systematically testing and analysing the structural behaviour of woven bamboo at singularities, this study will develop evidence-based design principles and reliable guidelines for stable, scalable, and sustainable architectural applications. By transforming tacit knowledge of bamboo crafting into explicit, shareable methods, the project will help preserve traditional bamboo weaving techniques while making them accessible to modern architectural designers and manufacturers for the construction of woven bamboo structures. It will also promote bamboo culture in the Chinese cultural sphere and facilitate the transmission of craft knowledge. These outcomes will align with broader cultural preservation goals, such as those outlined in the Hong Kong government’s cultural policy. The project will also support the development of a sustainable built environment by leveraging bamboo’s exceptional properties as a renewable material, thus contributing to the achievement of global goals for decarbonisation and sustainable development. N/A

titleEnglish: Sustaining the Capital: An Archaeological Study of Bronze Mirror Production for Han-dynasty Chang’an
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chen, Jian Li Dr Qiang, Wu Wei
abstract: The aim of this project is to investigate how Chang’an, the capital of the Han dynasty, was materially sustained, using bronze mirrors—an “affordable luxury” widely used across social ranks—as a tracer of production and circulation. Drawing on newly discovered bronze-manufacturing remains found at Ducheng, a major site complex near Chang’an, this study will analyze how mirrors and other associated bronze products were produced and supplied to sustain the capital’s economies. Bronze mirrors were a hallmark of Han material culture, and Chang’an has yielded the largest published assemblage. Earlier scholarship identified the capital region as a key supply centre. However, no direct workshop evidence has been securely tied to mirror production. This absence leaves essential questions unresolved: which mirrors were locally made, how far the capital’s products circulated, and how local and imported products were integrated. Similarities—typological and compositional—between mirrors from Chang’an and other centres, such as Linzi in Shandong, further complicate the assumptions about local manufacture. While isotopic studies of finished mirrors provide useful clues, they cannot fully distinguish local from imported products. A systematic analysis of remains across the operation chain is urgently required. The project proposes an integrated approach to fill the research gap. Main methodologies include: I. metallurgical analysis of bronze-making remains; II. geochemical analysis to trace and compare ore source between waste and finished products; III. 3D scanning of moulds and final mirrors. Although the Guanzhong region was a major production core, the link between workshop remains and finished products has not been systematically explored. With the new data, this study will ask: (1) what are the “fingerprints” of Ducheng’s workshop waste and cemetery mirrors; (2) do these fingerprints align, demonstrating local provisioning, or diverge, suggesting multiple production units or imports; (3) how does Ducheng’s profile compare with legacy datasets from Chang’an and other centres. The outputs of this project will illuminate several broader issues. First, it will provide first-hand data to identify local products in the Chang’an region. Second, it will establish a framework for improving our understanding of how goods were transported to provision the capital. Ultimately, this archaeological study of mirrors will enhance research on the local production, imports, and the ways such interactions underpinned the functioning of the Han state. N/A

titleEnglish: Testing the compensation account in mitigating dyslexia and predicting Chinese-English word reading profile: A longitudinal study from primary to secondary school years in Hong Kong
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof MAURER, Urs Dr Ning, Xiaoran Prof Torppa, Minna Dr Wang, Li-Chih
abstract: Developmental dyslexia (below dyslexia), characterized by unexpected difficulties in word reading, has affected 9~13% of the population. Despite the uniform symptom at diagnosis, longitudinal studies have revealed great individual variation in developmental trajectories of reading in children with dyslexia. While some children show persistently low performance across time, others show an improving trajectory. The explanation of this heterogeneity remains unclear. One account is compensation; the symptoms lessen by cognitive strengths in other domains and engagement of neural networks that are less involved in typical-developing individuals, despite persisting core deficits. Long-term longitudinal studies are needed to reveal parallel development of the brain, cognitive-linguistic strategies, and word reading to test this account. Furthermore, Hong Kong's bilingual context (Chinese and English) provides a unique opportunity to investigate whether compensation mechanisms observed in Chinese dyslexia transfer to English reading. We propose a multiple-cohort long-term longitudinal study, which will be built on our existing representative sample of 258 grade 2-5 children with clinically diagnosed dyslexia recruited from three cohorts (2018-2019, 2020-2021, and 2022-2023), among which 96 children have participated in EEG recordings. We will follow up on this sample when they are in secondary school in 2027-2029. Study one will test the compensation account by examining the development of neural specialization to print and its connections to Chinese reading gain. The participants will be recorded EEG again in secondary schools while performing the same character decision task as primary school. Data analysis will test the moderating role of reading gain on the development of N1 print tuning (print-non print), which we have found with typical developing children. The neural compensation account predicts that more reading gain is linked to higher level and more longitudinal increase in right lateralization of N1 print tuning, while normalization account predicts so in left-lateralization. Study 2 is to confirm the compensation mechanisms identified in study 1 in an independent sample and explore the cross-linguistic generalizability of it. We propose a cross-sectional study of adolescents with Chinese dyslexia to identify common neural activation patterns associated with both English and Chinese word reading ability. Finally, we will build machine learning models predicting dyslexia children’ s secondary school Chinese and English word reading profiles from primary school cognitive-linguistic and neural markers. The final model will indicate the most important primary school risk factors that predict long-term bilingual word reading difficulties. The results in turn suggest possible mitigating pathways. N/A

titleEnglish: Diverse Deliberations, Convergent Paths: Rethinking the Categorization of Masters Texts in the Early and Middle Western Han Dynasty
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This research project aims to systematically re-examine the classification and integration of various pre-Qin philosophical schools during the early and middle Western Han period. Traditional academic history has largely relied on the categorical framework of the "Bibliographic Treatise" in the Hanshu, assigning figures and texts to specific philosophical schools. However, this approach often overlooks that the classification system itself was a historical product emerging from the mid-to-late Western Han. These thinkers shared common concerns and were not originally confined by school boundaries. The project will focus on the following aspects: 1. Utilizing both transmitted and newly excavated texts—such as Xinyu by Lu Jia, Xinshu by Jia Yi, Huainanzi, Chunqiu Fanlu, and manuscripts from Yinque Shan and Peking University—to analyze the intellectual content of early and middle Western Han thinkers. Each text contains rich and diverse ideas that resist simple categorization under a single school. 2. Examining key cases—such as the formation of "Daoism," the rise of "Eclecticism," and the blending of Confucian and Legalist ideas—to reveal the complex process of selection, interpretation, and attribution behind the Hanshu classification. Many texts exhibit characteristics of multiple schools, reflecting a tendency toward synthesis rather than distinction. 3. Exploring shared intellectual concerns among Western Han thinkers that transcend school-based analysis. Their philosophies were oriented toward contemporary political and social issues, reflecting the real preoccupations of Han society rather than abstract doctrinal disputes. 4. Re-evaluating prior studies on the classification of Han thinkers, including academic histories and research on the "Masters" traditions, to refine scholarly understanding of school attribution. By integrating newly excavated materials—such as the Warring States bamboo slips housed at Anhui University—and adopting interdisciplinary perspectives from intellectual history, philology, and the sociology of knowledge, this study seeks to:  Analyze the complexity of thought within individual texts;  Identify common ground among different schools;  Examine the relationship between intellectual discourse and the sociopolitical context;  Investigate the historical process of school formation and classification. This research will not only deepen understanding of early Chinese intellectual formation but also offer interdisciplinary insights into the interaction between knowledge classification and political order. N/A

titleEnglish: Administrative reclassification, policy treatments, and township urbanization
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: For over four decades of economic reforms, the Chinese administrative state has actively intervened in the classification and definition of local government units, while currently incentivizing local officials through numerous policy schemes to accelerate urbanization in their localities. While many units have been formally reclassified as urban districts (市区), county-level cities (县级市), sub-districts (街道), and urban communities (居委会), the long-run consequences of such reclassification have not been evaluated systematically due to the paucity of cumulative data. We do so both qualitatively and quantitatively, in the context of townships and villages in Shandong province. We model the cumulative and multilevel nature of policy shocks affecting townships over time in the event-history framework of local administrative reconfiguration, combining rich administrative data on township organization with information about policy schemes deployed across Shandong province. We collect indicators of socio-economic development from municipalities down to townships and build a corresponding multilevel longitudinal database. We aim to disentangle the relationship between administrative reclassification and urban development, subject to the shocks of state policy ‘treatments’ aimed at (re)shaping and accelerating local urbanization. We argue that two interrelated pathways can accelerate local urbanization: (1) ex ante urban growth motivates officials to seek upgrading the administrative status of their locality to an urban unit as a validation of successful socio-economic transformation. Once administrative reclassification occurs, local urbanization may accelerate further. (2) the state also leverages policy initiatives to spur growth. By reclassifying units as urban regardless of their actual level of urbanization, the state targets policy support to localities chosen for future urbanization. This complex relationship suggests that state intervention is both a cause and an outcome that co-evolves along with local developmental conditions and should therefore be evaluated jointly and over time. We test the microfoundations of our theory using information about the long-term restructuring process among township-units and villages in the cases of Lanshan district and Linshu county in Linyi municipality, where authorities have been implementing multiple rounds of village mergers. We are particularly interested in the way administrative reorganizations have been sequenced and how varying temporalities have influenced ultimate outcomes on the ground. Specifically, we seek to explain why, following efforts to restructure villages in both cases, urbanization in Lanshan accelerated, but not in Linshu and theorize about how long-held urban biases and varying socio-economic conditions mediate this outcome. N/A

titleEnglish: Balancing growth and well-being in EMI higher education: A dual processing investigation of AI-mediated self-regulated learning
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Lai, Chun Prof Liyanage, Indika Prof ZOU, Di
abstract: English-medium instruction (EMI) is a global phenomenon in higher education. However, since EMI often operates in non-English-speaking contexts, it imposes heavy academic difficulties and evokes strong emotional reactions from students who learn complex subject knowledge through a foreign language. Advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) may help students address these challenges. Students use AI to overcome language barriers, manage course workloads, and reduce anxiety to protect their emotional well-being. Yet, misusing AI might deprive students of opportunities for deep learning and hinder long-term academic development. Thus, students urgently need to become self-regulated learners who strategically use AI to support academic growth in EMI contexts. The study will investigate how students in EMI higher education regulate their learning when using AI, and how such self-regulated learning (SRL) affects their academic success. Guided by Boekaerts and Niemivirta’s (2000) dual processing SRL model, the study will unpack how students navigate the affordances provided by AI in EMI course tasks, balancing two SRL pathways: one oriented toward cognitive growth and the other toward emotional well-being. By integrating the important yet often-neglected affective dimension, the study will add a unique perspective to SRL research in EMI contexts. The study will also provide the first systematic account of AI-mediated learning and its impact on EMI academic outcomes. The study will adopt a two-stage, mixed-methods design that triangulates quantitative and qualitative findings from diverse sources of data. Stage 1 will explore students’ use of AI and associated SRL strategies. Data generated by semi-structured interviews and learner-AI interaction logs with 48 purposively selected students will be used to map how students employ AI in EMI learning and to develop a novel AI-mediated SRL strategy questionnaire. Stage 2 will validate the questionnaire with two cohorts of students (N = 400, N = 1,600) selected via stratified random sampling. Using structural equation modelling, this stage will also examine key learner factors that predict SRL strategy use, and the effect of strategy use on EMI academic success. Finally, complementary task-specific stimulated recall interviews with 24 purposively selected students will provide in-depth insights into the situated, complex decision-making behind students’ strategy use. Together, the findings will extend theory on SRL, yield new research instruments, and generate evidence to design training programs for strategic AI use. Ultimately, the study will contribute to the ‘bigger picture’ of cultivating students’ AI literacy and SRL capacity in EMI higher education. N/A

titleEnglish: Electronic Music as Cultural Diplomacy: Jean-Michel Jarre and Sino-French Relations from the Post-Mao Era to the New Millennium
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This project investigates the complex interplay between electronic music and Sino-French cultural diplomacy through Jean-Michel Jarre’s landmark concerts in the People’s Republic of China in 1981 and 2004. I explore the rationale behind China’s collaboration with a Western pop music icon, hypothesizing that Jarre’s performances were strategically leveraged to project an image of openness and modernity in the wake of China’s 1978 reform and opening-up policy. The 1981 concerts, staged with spectacular multimedia displays in Beijing and Shanghai, and broadcast to an alleged audience of over 500 million listeners and 30 million viewers, were followed by global releases of live recordings and a documentary film. While these events are often credited with catalysing the emergence of electronic music in Chinese conservatories, I contend that the influence was more technological than curricular, given the absence of popular music genres in academic institutions at the time. Through a critical analysis of Jarre’s biography, autobiography, and Andrew Piddington’s documentary, I uncover ideological messaging embedded in the 1981 concerts and reveal covert political maneuvers that shaped their reception. Notably, Jarre’s pre-concert visit to China aboard the same flight as François Mitterrand, soon to become France’s first Socialist president, suggests deeper political alignments than registered in dominant historical accounts. I also interrogate the marginalization of other forms of electronic music—such as ethnic synthesizers, rock, and film scoring—and the contributions of individuals and industrial sectors excluded from the official narrative. The 2004 concert, held at the Forbidden City and Tiananmen Square as part of the Year of France, represents a more cautious and ideologically managed form of cultural diplomacy. I analyse Jarre’s evolving role as a “cultural ambassador” in light of his subtle political gestures, including his public dedication to the values of “liberty, equality, and fraternity.” By foregrounding marginalized narratives and reassessing the ideological underpinnings of cultural exchange, I offer a more comprehensive understanding of electronic music’s emergence in the PRC and the broader geopolitical dynamics of the Cold War and beyond. This project ultimately argues that Jarre’s concerts were not merely artistic spectacles but carefully orchestrated diplomatic interventions revealing a nuanced negotiation of identity, ideology, and international visibility. N/A

titleEnglish: Bimetallic Weaponry in Early China and Its Eurasian Borders: Art and Technology
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This project provides the first systematic and interdisciplinary investigation of iron-blade bimetallic weaponry in the Chinese Central Plains and its Eurasian borderlands from their origins (c. 14th century BCE) until 220 CE. It directly challenges the dominant scholarly paradigm that treats bronze and iron technologies as separate, sequential stages of development. Instead, this research reframes these sophisticated composites as active sites of technological and cultural negotiation, probing why they emerged and persisted for over a millennium within a mature bronze-casting tradition. First appearing in the northeastern sector of Tong Enzheng’s “Crescent-shaped Cultural-Communication Belt,” these weapons were not mere technological precursors but were highly valued items consumed by both early Chinese and Eurasian frontier societies, embodying a complex interplay of established techniques and novel materials. The project’s analysis centers on the material and formal properties of these objects—their specific combinations of metals, methods of joinery, and ornamental and morphological features. It investigates how these visible and tangible characteristics functioned as markers of status, identity, and technical prowess within specific social and geographical contexts. To decode this material syntax, the research employs an integrated methodology, combining formal analysis with advanced non-invasive techniques, including 3D scanning and portable X-ray fluorescence, applied to a targeted corpus from key sites. This generates high-resolution data on form, surface treatment, structural integrity, and elemental composition, all systematically integrated with archaeological context within a unified relational database. The investigation is guided by five core objectives: to establish a verified typology and chronology; to classify distinct manufacturing protocols; to correlate technological choices with specific socio-geographic contexts; to trace the provenance of iron components (distinguishing meteoritic from smelted iron); and to develop a new model of technological transmission for the region. By reconstructing the decision-making of early metallurgists, the project aims to reveal a dynamic landscape of interaction. The research aims to explore how technological knowledge across the Eurasian frontier may have been transmitted, reinterpreted, and reconfigured rather than merely passed along through unidirectional diffusion. In pursuing this line of inquiry, the project seeks to situate early China as an active and dynamic participant within broader networks of Eurasian technological exchange, proposing a refined framework for understanding the interconnected art and archaeology of its frontiers. N/A

titleEnglish: Wild dreams contested: Unpacking the politics of urban rewilding through a planning-financing-sensing nexus perspective
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Wang, Zheng Prof Xu, Jiang
abstract: Imagine cities where wildflowers reclaim concrete lots, birds nest in abandoned buildings, and native species flourish without human control – this is the vision of urban rewilding, an emerging approach to ecological restoration in urban settings. Unlike conventional greening efforts, urban rewilding aims to reintroduce self-sustaining ecosystems into the urban fabric. It is gaining momentum as a practical nature-based solution to climate change and food insecurity, and as an ethical commitment to restoring space for biodiversity. Yet, despite its growing appeal, urban rewilding continues to raise pressing practical and theoretical questions. They concern not only how rewilding, originally developed as a rural conservation strategy, adapts to the fragmented ecologies of urban environments, but also how it becomes entangled in the political-economic complexities of urban governance. It is this latter dimension that remains poorly understood and forms this project's central concern. Addressing this lacuna requires developing a new conceptual lens that integrates three influential strands of critical urban scholarship – sustainable urbanism, urban financialisation, and smart urbanism – to examine urban rewilding as a politically contested process. Sustainable urbanism highlights the role of spatial planning in advancing urban sustainability, as well as the discursive and institutional struggles over how environmental concerns are addressed through planning. Urban financialisation reveals the growing influence of financial actors and instruments, including green finance, over the trajectories of urban environmental governance. Smart urbanism underscores the increasing centrality of data and environmental sensing technologies in both spatial planning and the financial valuation of nature. These literatures, though insightful, have largely evolved in isolation. Our project breaks down these silos by proposing the perspective of planning-financing-sensing nexus, which captures the entangled processes through which urban rewilding is contested and assembled. This lens enables us to trace how spatial planning, financial imperatives, and environmental sensing co-produce urban nature in complex and often contradictory ways. Through a comparative analysis of London and Shenzhen – two global frontrunners in urban rewilding situated within contrasting contexts – this project will illuminate the varied pathways through which urban rewilding becomes possible. By examining how spatial, financial, and technological dynamics intersect to shape urban rewilding in each city, we uncover the complex barriers to implementing self-sustaining ecological restoration and the levers available to promote it. In doing so, the project contributes to a deeper understanding of how cities can embrace the ‘wild’ as a viable and transformative response to contemporary ecological challenges. N/A

titleEnglish: Enduring Parental Responsibilities and Their Link to Low Fertility in China
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Gan, Yiqing Prof Wolbring, Tobias
abstract: Aims: We propose a nuanced conceptualization of enduring parenthood responsibilities (EPR) to understand shifts in intergenerational family dynamics and their potential influence on China’s extremely low fertility. By EPR, we mean parents’ lifelong, child-centered, competitive, and high-standard duties toward their children. We hypothesize that parenting responsibilities have become increasingly intensive and prolonged, which may partly contribute to young adults forgoing childbearing in contemporary China. In the proposed study, we will first examine what constitutes EPR and how young adults perceive and understand it. We will then analyze the relationship between EPR and fertility using both Factorial Survey Experimental (FSE) data and longitudinal observation data from the China Family Panel Studies (CFPS). Finally, we will conduct further moderation analyses by considering cultural norms, socioeconomic resources, and the situation of parent-young adult intergenerational interdependence. Significance: Low fertility is one of China’s most contentious demographic issues, with far-reaching implications for aging, the labor market, and related domains. Existing theories, largely developed in Western contexts, are insufficient to explain China’s situation, where strong traditional family values and a pro-natal culture have long prevailed. Historically, strong intergenerational support and familism facilitated childbearing among young adults. In recent years, however, the responsibilities between parents and children have become increasingly imbalanced, resulting in intensive and prolonged transfers from parents to children, rather than the other way around. Parents are now expected to shoulder substantial, lifelong responsibilities under elevated standards. This intensification and prolongation of parenthood, combined with rising individualism among younger generations, may have partially backfired against the pro-natal culture in China. Novelty: First, EPR is a new concept we will develop to capture nuances in the intergenerational dynamics among Chinese families, which is beyond the recent development of intensive parenthood for childhood. Second, mixed methods—including qualitative research, online surveys for developing EPR scales, and Factorial Survey Experiments (FSE) for understanding the relative importance of different factors on EPR and its linkage with fertility, as well as longitudinal data analysis from the CFPS, will complement one another to provide a deeper and more nuanced understanding of EPR and its relationship to fertility. Third, the approach to China’s low-fertility context extends beyond the short-term effects of having children on young adults, prompting a long-term consideration of intergenerational relationships and fertility. Our findings will help clarify paradoxical fertility patterns and strengthen evidence-based policymaking in the Chinese context. N/A

titleEnglish: Precarious Working Lives and Projected Futures: Biographical Narratives of Delivery Riders, Care Workers, and Social Media Content Creators in Hong Kong
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: It is estimated that over 150 million individuals are involved in platform-mediated gig work, while over 50 million individuals work as social media content creators globally. Despite differences in the nature of their work and levels of privilege, there is a growing consensus among scholars and policy makers that the lived realities of workers are characterized by algorithmic management, precarious working conditions, and limited social protections, contradicting the flexible and entrepreneurial narratives promoted by labor platforms. While these discussions offer invaluable insights into platform governance, we know relatively little about the longer-term social impacts of precarious platform work on workers’ lives beyond the workplace and their capacity to make future plans. Building on the Principal Investigator’s years of qualitative research on the struggles of platform-mediated workers in various geographical contexts, this 30-month qualitative project examines (1) why individuals – despite being aware of and critical towards labor exploitation and precarity – continue to work on platforms, (2) how prolonged precarious platform work experiences extend into other domains of social life and shape workers’ future projections, and (3) how workers’ narratives of precarity vary across different sectors and life stages in Hong Kong. Theoretically, the project sits at the intersection of platform labor studies and sociological research on precarity and future-making. Platform workers often experience not only insecurity at work but also difficulty in envisioning long-term life trajectories. As many enter platform work at different stages of their lives, these experiences can act as “turning points” that prompt workers to reassess their biographies and aspirations. The project also explores how workers’ future projections are shaped by their dynamic life trajectories and unevenly shaped across social groups, potentially reinforcing or reshaping existing patterns of social inequality. Empirically, the project adopts a biographical approach to examine the cultural narratives and future imaginaries of three types of platform-mediated workers in Hong Kong: delivery workers, care workers, and content creators. This project contributes to scholarly debates on the platformization of service, care, and creative labor by theorizing the temporal dimensions of worker agency, precariousness, and cultural narratives. By offering a culturally grounded analysis of how prolonged precarious platform work experiences shape other domains of workers’ lives in Hong Kong, it has the potential to inform policy interventions, particularly around labor protections and future planning support for platform workers. N/A

titleEnglish: Using Positive Friction to Reclaim Human Agency in the AI News Environment: A Two-Phase, Mixed-Methods Study
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof McDonald, Thomas Niall Prof Zhang, Jingwen
abstract: Generative AI (GenAI) is rapidly changing how we access and process information. While the convenience of getting instant answers and summaries is immense, this very efficiency carries a significant risk. The seamless, “frictionless” experience encourages users to passively accept AI-generated content without critical thought. This is especially concerning for news consumption, where it can erode critical evaluation skills, diminish our sense of control (human agency), and increase vulnerability to sophisticated misinformation. This project moves beyond simply debating whether to use AI, and instead asks: how can we interact with it to become smarter and more autonomous news consumers and thinkers? Our research confronts the frictionless design ideal by introducing and testing the concept of “positive friction.” These are small, deliberately designed moments of pause or challenge that interrupt mindless consumption and prompt users to engage more deeply with AI-generated news content. The goal is to transform the user from a passive recipient into an active, critical partner in the information process. Our approach is a two-phase, mixed-methods study. First, a foundational qualitative study will explore how young adults currently integrate GenAI into their daily news routines, providing a rich understanding of their real-world behaviors and perceptions. The insights from this phase will directly inform the design of Phase 2: a longitudinal field experiment. Here, we will develop and empirically test our positive friction interventions over six months to measure their effectiveness in fostering more critical engagement and enhancing human agency. This project will deliver new knowledge on how to design more responsible human-AI interactions. Crucially, we will translate our findings into actionable, evidence-based guidelines for educators teaching news literacy in the AI era, for designers building more human-centric interfaces, and for the general public on how to use these powerful tools wisely. The ultimate aim is to foster a more thoughtful and empowered relationship with AI, ensuring it enhances, rather than erodes, human intellect. N/A

titleEnglish: Keywords in Motion: Analyzing the Translation and Circulation of Concepts about Contemporary China, 2001–2025
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This research project explores the evolving landscape of key concepts that are perceived as defining contemporary Chinese culture and society, examining their translation, evolution, and cultural significance. These key concepts will be identified through a search in a large corpus of texts related to China that will identify words that occur significantly more frequently than in a comparable reference corpus of unrelated terms. The study is grounded in the historical use of key concepts as tools for understanding cultural nuances, seeking to move beyond the two dominant perspectives: traditionalists, who reference the canonical texts of Confucianism, and modernists, who advocate for new terms reflecting current realities, believing that there has been a rupture with the past. The project aims to tackle several key questions: How does translation affect the interpretation of key concepts? How have the terms used to reference these concepts evolved over time in both Chinese and English contexts? What differences exist in how these concepts are framed in media from the two languages? This project will first compile two large corpora of texts: one in Chinese and one in English, spanning the years 2001 to 2025. These corpora will be compared against reference corpora in English and Chinese to identify keywords. Then keywords will be subjected to a variety of advanced digital humanities methods—such as data mining, sentiment analysis, and network visualization—to uncover hitherto patterns and connections on both the macro and the micro level. At the macro level, changes in usage over time and across geographic or cultural distance will be investigated to reveal how public perceptions shift over time, influenced by political and social changes. At the micro level, the project will uncover the complexities of individual terms like “China Dream” (zhongguo meng) and “copycat” (shanzhai), which may serve as case studies illustrating the cultural and contextual differences between languages. Ultimately, this research aims to illuminate not just the keywords themselves but the underlying concepts and cultural narratives they represent. By examining these concepts, the project aspires to expose existing stereotypes to foster greater cross-cultural understanding between China and the global community, revealing how language and translation shape our perception of a rapidly changing world. The findings will contribute to ongoing discussions in translation studies, cultural discourse, and digital humanities, offering fresh insights into the dynamics of language and culture in contemporary China. N/A

titleEnglish: Layers of Need Fulfillment: Understanding Student Well-being in AI-Mediated Learning Contexts
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Background The rapid integration of AI into higher education is reshaping how students learn, communicate, and prepare for future careers. While much scholarship has emphasized risks such as AI-related anxiety and stress, less attention has been paid to how engagement with AI influences students’ broader well-being. Drawing on Self-Determination Theory (SDT), this project argues that the psychological needs of competence, autonomy, and relatedness form a foundation for flourishing in AI-mediated environments. These needs can be satisfied at two levels: a provision-based level, supported by external access, resources, and training provisions, and a self-integrative level, reflecting personal internalization of skills and agency, such as critical evaluation and purposeful engagement for social good. We propose that these layers of need fulfillment differentially associate with well-being, with higher-level satisfaction more strongly linked to eudaimonic outcomes such as purpose, beyond momentary hedonic enjoyment. Purpose The project aims to investigate unique well-being experiences of students in the face of AI, and how psychological need satisfaction shapes students’ hedonic and eudaimonic well-being, and in turn, how well-being influences their engagement with AI. Guided by SDT, the project focuses on the three basic needs and introduces the concept of layered need satisfaction to distinguish between resource-based fulfillment and deeper, self-integrative fulfillment. Methodology The study adopts a mixed-method longitudinal design. Quantitative surveys across three time points will track changes in psychological need satisfaction, AI-related behaviors, and well-being indicators. Complementary qualitative interviews will capture how students experience well-being in AI environments, including the tensions, uncertainties, and opportunities they encounter; and the adaptive strategies they develop for wellbeing. This combination will illuminate both the mechanisms and the lived meanings of flourishing with AI. Contributions Theoretically, the project will advance SDT by introducing layered need satisfaction in the AI era, showing why resource-based level is insufficient and why deeper internalization of competence, autonomy, and relatedness is critical for long-term flourishing. Empirically, it is among the first longitudinal, mixed-method studies to trace how AI engagement shapes, and is shaped by both hedonic and eudaimonic well-being, shifting the focus from short-term risks toward the developmental trajectories of purpose and growth. Practically, the project will provide evidence-based guidance for higher education, moving beyond teaching AI as a technical skill toward creating environments that cultivate flourishing. By centering students’ voices, it will inform policies and pedagogies that prepare students not just to cope with AI, but to thrive with it. N/A

titleEnglish: Causal Modelling, Reasoning, and Discovery in the Presence of Non-causal Dependencies
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Zhang, Kun
abstract: Causal modelling has proved useful in several research fields, including philosophy and artificial intelligence. In philosophy, it serves as a formal tool for studying important concepts such as causation and explanation and for examining logical and epistemological foundations of causal reasoning. In artificial intelligence, it is applied to automate the discovery of causal relationships from data and to design machine learning models that leverage causal information. The standard framework of causal modelling focuses exclusively on causal dependencies and assumes that variables of interest do not share intimate non-causal relations, such as conceptual, compositional, or constitutive dependencies. While this restriction simplifies many problems and enables elegant theorization, it limits the framework's ability to represent complex systems that involve both causal and non-causal dependencies. In earlier work, the PI proposed a way to generalize Judea Pearl’s structural causal models to incorporate both causal and non-causal dependencies. While still preliminary, this proposal shows promise for being sufficiently flexible to accommodate a variety of philosophical theories and sufficiently expressive to support counterfactual reasoning. Moreover, its use of directed graphs to represent dependency structures makes it plausible to adapt existing causal discovery algorithms to this more general framework. This project aims to systematically develop the proposal to substantiate these merits. The work is divided into three parts. The first part examines the philosophical foundations and interpretations of the formalism and evaluates the compatibility of various philosophical theories with the basic formal setup. The second part studies interventional and counterfactual reasoning within the generalized models and investigates the distinctive features introduced by the new framework. The third part explores methods for inferring dependency structures—both causal and non-causal—from data, guided by several prominent approaches in the causal discovery literature. We adopt a mixed methodology in this project, which integrates philosophical analysis, mathematical investigation, and computational experimentation. Philosophical interpretation and argumentation will be employed to clarify conceptual foundations and examine how the proposed formal setup aligns with or challenges significant theses in metaphysics and the philosophy of science. Mathematical derivation and demonstration will be used to explore the consequences of alternative formal definitions and establish theoretical results about interventional and counterfactual reasoning, as well as algorithms for structural discovery. Finally, experiments on synthetic and real data will be conducted to test and illustrate the behavior of the proposed methods and evaluate their feasibility and reliability. N/A

titleEnglish: Cold War Angels: The Visual Politics of Girlhood, Diplomacy, and Soft Power in South Korea's Little Angels Art Troupe
department: Dept of Cultural and Religious Studies
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This research project investigates South Korea’s Little Angels Children’s Folk Ballet as a pivotal case study in the gendered cultural diplomacy of the Cold War. Established in 1962 under the patronage of the Unification Church and the Park Chung-hee regime, the troupe was deployed internationally to project an image of South Korea as a modern, yet tradition-bound, non-threatening ally. This study argues that the Little Angels’ performances constituted a form of jaerong (winsomeness) diplomacy—a distinct mode of soft power that mobilized the affective labor of pre-adolescent girls to aestheticize authoritarian development and facilitate the US-ROK alliance. Through a critical analysis of state-produced newsreels (Daehan News), media archives, and contemporary artistic reinterpretations, the project dissects the visual and performative construction of this “angelic” girlhood. It demonstrates how carefully curated elements—hybridized choreography, traditional costumes, and narratives of purity—served to erase the trauma of war and colonialization, presenting a sanitized national identity for Western consumption. A comparative analysis with the jazz singer Yoon Bok-hee illuminates the state’s preference for a controllable, “traditional” femininity over transgressive modernity. By theorizing the “girled body” as a biopolitical instrument, the research reveals how gendered performance was central to Cold War nation-building. It ultimately traces the discursive legacy of this state-sponsored diplomacy to the contemporary Korean Wave, challenging market-centric narratives of Hallyu by highlighting its roots in a state-initiated tradition of deploying disciplined youth for ideological ends. The project contributes to Cold War studies, visual culture, and gender studies by uncovering the intimate linkages between cultural production, gendered politics, and neocolonial power. N/A

titleEnglish: From Security Threats to Security Guards: Assuming Black Masculinity in Asia
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Ho , Lawrence
abstract: This research asks how the same black African bodies could be constructed paraodoxically as security threats and security agents in HKSAR. It goes beyond any easy diagnosis of “anti-blackness” to examine how meanings of blackness are constituted, negotiated, and remade in the Asian context. An increasing number of ex-asylum-seekers from sub-Saharan Africa in Hong Kong – labelled by the HKSAR government as “security threats” – have worked as security guards, body guards, and bouncers. What structures of difference enabled this specific binary of “threats” and “guards” to be projected onto black African bodies, and in turn, how has this paradoxical positioning shaped black Africans’ subjectivities, experiences, and aspirations? With the expansion of Africa/China and Africa/Asia exchanges, and the increase of African migrants in Asia, this research contributes to the emergence of Asia as a key site in the global debate about blackness. The interrogation of race and racialization processes beyond the Euro-American contexts would further animate the theorization of race in and through Asia. The primary focus of the research will be on the men’s experiences across the two sides of the security threshold (from “threats” to “guards”), as well as how they navigate their racialized identities as black male security personnel. How do they manage their identities on the job as agents of security and order, while negotiating with popular image of black men as physical and sexual dangers? A second dimension of the research is to understand the security industry’s deployment of black masculinity in the expansion of private security operations both in Hong Kong and in the region. The growing significance of private policing has implications on issues of accountability and basic rights protection, as well as surveillance ethics, the privatization of public space, and labor rights. The increasing number of black African security personnel reflects the multiple ways blackness and security intersect that could be profitable but also render these men exploitable. The third dimension of the project aims to contribute to the theorization of race and gender – in particular black masculinity – from an Asian perspective and understand the polyvalence of blackness in the 21st century. N/A

titleEnglish: Singing Without Scores: Notations, Soundscape, and Aria Culture in Ming China
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This project examines the notation of qu-arias and the vibrant singing culture in Ming China (1368–1644). Professionally trained singers, such as courtesans and music teachers, infused urban centers with singing music throughout the entire Ming dynasty. They were often joined by well-educated aria aficionados, ranging from retired officials to well-off young men. Those enthusiasts dedicated themselves to collecting, performing, and composing new arias, fostering a rich soundscape and a flourishing market for printed singable texts. Yet, despite the presence of various musical notating systems and the documented enthusiasm for aria music, not a single musical note is transcribed in any dramatic manuscripts or imprints from this period. Instead, these texts are marked by indicators of tone, pronunciation, and rhythm, suggesting the music’s performative rather than notational character. I argue that this apparent absence of musical notation should not be seen as a primitive form but rather as a reflection of an alternative historical conception of music distinct from modern understandings (Sheng 2024). To probe further into the Ming discourse of aria music and expand the scope of research, I combine technical analyses of the aria notation in dramatic manuscripts and imprints with cultural research into aria performance during the Ming dynasty. While those dramatic books have often been attributed to literati, I rather decenter the literati in my studies by synthesizing approaches from literature studies, music studies, book history, and performance studies to examine a non-literary component in those books. In so doing, I illuminate a singing culture that is both regulated and chaotic, shaped by participants from all walks of life, and challenges modern presumptions about music as well as the evolution of musical notation. The project will yield several conference presentations and two journal articles submitted to top-ranked, peer-reviewed journals. N/A

titleEnglish: Tool or Transgression? Understanding Students’ Ethical Judgments of AI Use in Academic Work
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chai, Ching Sing Prof Stephens, Jason
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) tools like ChatGPT are rapidly transforming how students complete academic work. This technology can support creativity, efficiency, and confidence, but also raises pressing questions about integrity, fairness, and learning. Surveys show that while about half of students consider AI use to be “cheating,” many of them also use it regularly. They are divided on whether “gray area” uses (e.g., paraphrasing, brainstorming, seeking feedback) are ethical, and many worry that AI may undermine critical skills such as creativity and critical thinking. Meanwhile, universities struggle to keep pace, potentially issuing policies that are unclear or inconsistent and leaving students without firm guidance. This project investigates how students make sense of these dilemmas, focusing on early undergraduates at a formative stage of academic development. We examine how motivations (such as efficiency or stress relief) and ethical reasoning might jointly guide AI use, changes in beliefs and behaviors over time, and why students might sometimes knowingly use AI in unethical ways. We introduce an innovative framework combining two established perspectives. Uses and Gratifications Theory explains why students turn to AI to meet cognitive, practical, or emotional needs. Social Domain Theory explains students’ ethical reasoning processes and how they weigh considerations of fairness (moral), rule-following (conventional), skill development (prudential), and personal choice. A third concept, Moral Disengagement, links the two by explaining how students rationalize questionable behaviors (“everyone does it,” “it doesn’t really hurt anyone”) to pursue immediate benefits. To capture these processes, we will follow 700 first- and second-year undergraduates across a full academic year. Students will complete four in-depth surveys on trait-level ethical attitudes, motivations, and behaviors, alongside bi-weekly assessments of recent, state-level experiences. This design can distinguish between-student differences (e.g., those who are generally more cautious or permissive than their peers) from within-student fluctuations (e.g., a particular student turning to AI more often during stressful weeks). Linking shorter-term changes with longer-term patterns can reveal how motivations, ethical views, and AI habits crystallize or drift in the crucial early years of university. This project comes at a critical juncture, as students’ adoption of AI is advancing far faster than the ethical norms and policies guiding it. Identifying how and when students balance benefits and integrity will provide urgently needed evidence to inform fair, sustainable educational policies and support students in developing responsible habits for the future. N/A

titleEnglish: Effects of Pre-Session Role Transition Intervention for Beginning Counselors
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chong, Eddie Siu Kwan Dr Luk, Sau Ha
abstract: Counselors’ pre-session emotional states have been investigated in several studies, but their effects on counseling process and outcomes remain inconclusive. Research on pre-session exercises targeting counselors’ mood and emotion regulation also shows mixed results. Counselors’ mental preparation likely involves not only emotions but also counseling-related cognitions and behaviors. According to boundary transition theory, counselors need to exit other life roles and enter the counselor role successfully before the thoughts, feelings, and behaviors associated with the counselor role are activated. Inadequate role transition may result in spillover effects where behavioral tendencies, such as communication styles, related to other roles interfere with the execution of the desired role. In addition, external and explicit cues may facilitate role transition and role adoption, especially for individuals who are new to a role. Based on these premises, a pre-session role transition intervention will be developed and tested in this study. The pre-session intervention lasts 10 minutes and includes activities designed specifically to aid in the role transition process, as supported by the empirical and theoretical literature. A pilot study involving 26 counseling trainees showed preliminary evidence that the new intervention increased counselor role adoption, reduced trainees’ pre-session anxiety, and enhanced the working alliance with clients. Using a quasi-experimental design, trainees will be assigned to either the intervention (A) or control (B) condition according to their supervision group (random assignment is avoided to prevent intervention contamination when trainees in the same supervision group talk). Trainees in Group A will receive the pre-session role transition intervention before Sessions 2-5 with one client, and trainees in Group B will receive the intervention before Sessions 6-9 (i.e., delayed intervention). It is hypothesized that counselors’ pre-session anxiety will be lower, and their counselor role adoption and counseling self-efficacy will be higher, when counselors receive the pre-session intervention (i.e., comparing Sessions 2-5 between Group A and Group B, and comparing Sessions 2-5 and Sessions 6-9 within Group B). Ratings of counselor presence, session quality, and working alliance will also be higher when counselors have the intervention. The maintenance effect of the intervention will be examined by assessing the outcome variables in Group A after the intervention is withdrawn starting at Session 6. In addition, counselors will be invited to participate in a brief post-practicum interview to discuss their experience with the pre-session intervention, its impact on their counselor role adoption, and recommendations for improving the intervention. N/A

titleEnglish: Equitable Access to High-Quality Kindergartens in Hong Kong: A Geospatial Analysis of Supply, Quality, and Educational Orientations
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof GONG, Jing
abstract: Access to high-quality early childhood education (ECE) is widely recognized as critical for children’s development and for reducing long-term inequality. Since the launch of the Kindergarten Education Scheme in 2017, Hong Kong has achieved near-universal ECE by subsidizing tuition and regulating quality. Yet questions remain about whether families in all neighborhoods have access to high-quality kindergartens. Addressing this issue is vital because ECE supports child development, prepares children for primary school, and contributes to positive long-term social and economic outcomes. The proposed project will investigate family-level inequality in Hong Kong’s kindergarten sector across three dimensions of ECE: the availability of kindergarten places, the quality of educational services, and the orientations emphasized by schools. The proposed study will integrate census data, administrative records, classroom observations, and text-based analysis to capture how the landscape of kindergarten supply is experienced by families across different neighborhoods. First, geospatial measures will be developed to map kindergarten availability. Second, schools’ structural quality will be measured through administrative indicators, while process quality will be assessed through direct classroom observations in 60 classrooms across 30 kindergartens. Third, educational orientations will be derived from the Education Bureau’s Quality Review reports using structural topic modeling. The proposed project will contribute to both academic and policy discussions. It will identify any city-wide spatial inequities in Hong Kong’s kindergarten sector, extending international research on quality disparities to a high-density Asian context. Methodologically, it will offer a novel contribution by developing an integrated, multi-method approach that combines geospatial analysis, classroom observations, and large-scale text mining. The findings will help policymakers refine the Kindergarten Education Scheme, moving beyond affordability to emphasize accessibility, quality, and diversity of educational goals. Ultimately, the proposed project will provide evidence-based recommendations for promoting equitable ECE opportunities, with implications for Hong Kong as well as other global cities facing similar challenges. N/A

titleEnglish: Academic Returns to Early Childhood Investment: How Home-Visiting and Center-Based Programs Shape Rural Chinese Students' Path to College
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Mr Lu, Mai Prof Snow, Catherine E. Prof XIE, Jian
abstract: This study addresses a critical gap in early childhood education research: understanding whether affordable programs targeting China's poorest rural families produce lasting educational benefits measurable through high school and college entrance examinations. While research demonstrates short-term gains from parenting support (ages 0-3) and preschool education (ages 3-6), evidence is scarce on whether these investments persist through adolescence in low-income contexts, and whether combining both programs produces amplified effects. This question has direct policy urgency as China confronts plummeting birth rates (1.09 births per woman in 2022) while debating how to allocate constrained resources between cash subsidies and educational services for its shrinking rural child population. We leverage a natural experiment in County G, Hunan Province—a nationally-designated poverty county (annual rural per capita income: $1,707) where two flagship programs were introduced six years apart. One Village One Preschool (OVOP), launched in 2012, provides center-based education for ages 3-6 across village classrooms, serving approximately 300,000 children across 1,800 villages since 2009. China REACH, introduced in 2018, delivers weekly home-visiting with parenting guidance and learning materials for ages 0-3, serving 62,000 children across 1,092 villages since 2015. This staggered rollout creates variation in program exposure: children born 2009-2015 could access only preschool; those born after 2015 received both home-visiting followed by preschool; comparison children in neighboring counties received neither program. Using administrative records from County G's centralized examination system, we track approximately 10,000 students across cohorts through three assessment points: school readiness tests at elementary school entry (ages 6-7), high school entrance examinations determining academic versus vocational tracks (age 15), and college entrance examinations (Gao Kao, age 18). County-wide standardized tests administered twice annually provide measures of literacy and mathematics development, while high-stakes examinations offer objective outcomes determining students' educational and economic futures. Approximately 2,400 children in subsample counties completed detailed cognitive assessments (Woodcock-Johnson IV) between ages 6-13, enabling analysis of specific skill trajectories underlying examination performance. Findings will provide the first rigorous evidence on whether sequential early childhood investments costing $400-600 per child annually produce benefits detectable in high-stakes academic outcomes, whether starting earlier produces better results than quality preschool alone, which children benefit most, and when effects persist versus fade. Results directly inform investment strategies for China's declining child population and early childhood policy globally as nations confront falling birth rates while seeking cost-effective interventions reducing educational inequality. N/A

titleEnglish: Grotesque: How Gun Rights Advocates Distort History and Facilitate Oppression
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Grotesque: How Gun Rights Advocates Distort History and Facilitate Oppression argues that contemporary interpretations of the Second Amendment to the United States Constitution have strayed far from its original intent. The amendment grew out of the founding generation's fear of military oppression and the need for civil control over armed forces. Contrary to current narratives that frame the Second Amendment as a guarantee of individual gun rights, it was designed to prevent the emergence of a standing army that could threaten a free society. The project pursues three main objectives: to dismantle prevalent misconceptions surrounding the Second Amendment’s history, to provide an accurate account of its inclusion in the Bill of Rights, and to emphasize its relevance today amidst concerns of a politicized military. Using a combination of intellectual history and cultural history, Grotesque also traces the evolution of the Second Amendment’s interpretation since it was written. It will especially criticize the Supreme Court's 2008 DC vs. Heller ruling, which mischaracterized the amendment as solely protecting individual gun ownership. The project also explores how gun rights advocates have successfully reframed the debate, transforming the Second Amendment into a "guns amendment," while obscuring its original civil-military relations context. That civil-military context is what the United States needs today, given that it threatened by the prospect of an increasingly politicized military acting within the United States, and especially in American cities. By reintegrating eighteenth-century debates on civil control over the military into contemporary discussions, Grotesque will highlight amendment's original goals, which remain critical in the face of rising authoritarianism and the potential for military overreach in modern America. N/A

titleEnglish: Data-Informed English Supports for DHH Readers: Evidence from a Chinese–English Eye‑Movement Corpus
department: Dept of Linguistics and Modern Languages
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof HWANG, Haerim
abstract: This project seeks to reduce long-standing obstacles to higher education faced by deaf and hard-of-hearing (DHH) students in Hong Kong, where limited English proficiency often restricts opportunities despite sign bilingual education. Grounded in Hong Kong’s biliterate–trilingual context and the Sign Bilingualism and Co-enrollment (SLCO) model, we examine how DHH and hearing readers process English and Traditional Chinese texts in real time. Using large-scale, naturalistic eye-tracking, we will build a bilingual passage-reading corpus from DHH Hong Kong Sign Language (HKSL) signers and their hearing peers. This corpus will enable modelling of word-, phrase-, and clause-level processing and the identification of linguistic features that increase difficulty, such as low-salience morphology, connectives, and clause boundaries. These insights will inform minimally intrusive, text-embedded supports (e.g., explicit chunking, connective highlighting), which will be tested in an intervention to improve English reading efficiency and comprehension for DHH learners. Deliverables will include an open-access bilingual reading corpus, analysis tools, peer-reviewed studies, and practical resources for teachers to support inclusive literacy instruction. By developing the first bilingual eye-movement corpus with DHH readers that spans both English and Traditional Chinese scripts—and by applying these insights to targeted interventions—this project delivers a first-of-its-kind, large-scale, naturalistic account of cross-script reading processes. N/A

titleEnglish: Are we on the same page? A mixed-methods investigation of (in)congruent appraisal of symptom severity and care preferences in dementia care dyads
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Helen Yue-lai Prof Fung, Helene Hoi-lam Prof Irani, Elliane Prof Kwok, Timothy Prof LI, Xiaomin Prof Lou, Vivian Weiqun
abstract: Dementia, the eighth leading cause of death in Hong Kong, is projected to affect over 332,700 older adults by 2039. Person-centered care (PCC), the essence of which is providing care tailored to the needs, preferences, and abilities of persons with dementia (PWDs), is the guiding principle in dementia care internationally. Ensuring that PWDs and family care partners (FCPs) are on the same page is critical for shared care planning, which is core in achieving PCC. However, FCPs’ voices are often prioritized over those of PWDs in clinical practice. This violates PCC and can cause a series of harms to individuals, families, and care outcomes. To promote PCC, this proposed project adopts a dyadic, longitudinal design to examine appraisal congruence (defined as the alignment between PWDs and FCPs in perceptions of symptom severity and care preferences/values) and associated dyadic health outcomes (individual and relational well-being) among care dyads living with early-stage dementia. Potential similarities and differences between spousal and parent–child dyads will be examined to capture diverse dementia care configurations, knowledge that is particularly salient in Chinese societies but remains understudied. The moderating role of Chinese cultural values (i.e., family harmony and interdependence orientation for all dyads and filial piety for parent–child dyads only) will also be tested. This sequential explanatory mixed-methods study has two phases. In Phase 1, we will use validated instruments to collect survey data from 320 dementia care dyads (640 respondents) to assess appraisal (in)congruence, individual and relational well-being, and Chinese cultural values at three time points: baseline [T1], 6 months [T2], and 12 months [T3]. Advanced dyadic analytic techniques, including actor–partner interdependence cross-lagged panel models, latent growth curve modeling, and actor–partner interdependence moderation analysis, will quantify reciprocal relationships between changes in appraisal congruence and individual and relational well-being over time. Phase 2 will involve in-depth qualitative interviews with a purposive subsample of 30 dyads (60 respondents) with varied directions and magnitudes of (in)congruence to gain deeper insights into their experiences. The narrative data will be analyzed using thematic analysis. Theoretically, this proposed study extends Western-dominated dyadic health theories by developing an extended sociocultural dyadic health framework. Practically, the results will inform the key ingredients of targeted dyadic interventions to promote congruence within care dyads. This research also responds to the global call to preserve personhood in dementia care by promoting shared decision-making within family care dyads. N/A

titleEnglish: Everyday Ethics of Gene Therapy: Bridging Lived Experience and Policy Through Multi-sited Ethnography and Policy Lab
department: The Jockey Club School of Public Health and Primary Care
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Gu, Shen Prof Zhu, Jianfeng Prof Zhu, Wei
abstract: Gene therapy (GT) represents one of the most transformative frontiers of modern medicine, offering potential cures for diseases once considered untreatable. In China, the field has expanded at an extraordinary pace, driven by strong policy incentives, industrial investment, and scientific ambition. Yet this rapid growth has outpaced the development of coherent ethical and regulatory frameworks, creating uncertainty about what constitutes responsible and equitable practice. While patients and families are increasingly central to GT, participating not only in clinical trials but also in research design, funding, and advocacy, little is known about how ethical norms are formed and enacted in these everyday interactions. This project investigates how ethics is lived and practiced across China’s evolving gene therapy ecosystem. Using the theoretical framework of everyday ethics, it examines the relational and moral dilemmas that arise as GT moves from promise to practice. The study focuses on how patients, families, clinicians, scientists, developers, and regulators negotiate trust, responsibility, autonomy, and care in ordinary spaces such as clinics, laboratories, start-ups, homes, and online communities. By situating ethics in these everyday settings, the project aims to move beyond abstract principles to understand how ethical decisions are made, challenged, and sustained in practice. Methodologically, the project combines multi-sited ethnography with community-based participatory action research (CBPAR) and a two-round Policy Lab to generate empirically grounded and practice-ready ethical guidance. The ethnographic phase will map stakeholder relationships, identify everyday ethical challenges, and analyze how dilemmas are navigated in real-life settings through participant observation, interviews, and digital ethnography. Building on these findings, the Policy Lab will bring together patients, families, clinicians, researchers, industry representatives, and policymakers to co-produce context-specific ethical frameworks and practical tools for governance and implementation. The project’s main outputs will include an ethics consensus statement, role-specific codes of conduct, and a patient-facing decision-support toolkit. These deliverables will translate lived experience into actionable ethical guidance that strengthens governance, enhances transparency, and promotes a more patient-centered and socially accountable model of gene therapy in China. Beyond academia, the project will inform national policy, empower patient communities, and establish a transferable framework for ethical governance in other emerging biomedical fields. By bridging lived experience and policy formation, it aims to ensure that the future of gene therapy in China advances with both scientific integrity and moral responsibility N/A

titleEnglish: Effectiveness of the Chit-Chat educational intervention (CC-ed) for enhancing engagement in advance care planning among family caregivers of older adults with multimorbidity: A randomised controlled trial
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Helen Yue-lai Dr Chen, Wai Tsan Dr Chen, Weilin Ms Chui, Ling Yi Prof Leung, Doris Yin Ping Prof MAK, Yim Wah Dr Ng, Lai Han Betty Prof WANG, shanshan
abstract: Advance care planning (ACP) empowers medically vulnerable, yet mentally capable older adults with multimorbidity to make proactive end-of-life (EOL) decisions. Despite its importance, ACP engagement remains low, especially in Chinese communities, where cultural taboos surrounding death often hinder open discussion. Currently, most ACP interventions concentrate solely on the proactive communication regarding EOL care with patients but lack sufficient engagement of family caregivers. Family caregivers play a pivotal role in the ACP process but often lack relevant information and frequently feel ill-equipped to initiate these conversations during the time triggering the ACP discussion for older adults with multimorbidity, resulting in potential conflict and delayed decision-making. This study evaluates the effectiveness of the Chit-Chat -Educational (CC-ed) intervention- a novel, culturally sensitive program designed from the perspective of Chinese family caregivers. The CC-ed comprises three core components: 1) education on chronic disease progression, 2) insights into EOL care realities, and 3) introduction to ACP and training in practical communication skills for ACP discussions.
