In a two-arm randomised controlled trial, 504 family caregivers of community-dwelling older adults with multimorbidity will be assigned to either the CC-ed intervention group or an active placebo-control group. The intervention group will attend two 2.5-hour, face-to-face CC-ed sessions spaced one month apart. The control group will receive general geriatric care education of equivalent duration and an ACP information leaflet. The primary outcome is the change in family caregivers’ ACP engagement, assess using the ACP Engagement Survey. Secondary outcomes include improvement in ACP knowledge and caregiver- reported ACP communication. Data will be collected at baseline (T0), immediately post-intervention (T1), and at 6-month (T2) and 12-month (T3) follow-ups. A mixed-methods process evaluation, including semi-structured interviews with a purposive sample of 20 participants, will explore the intervention’s mechanisms of impact. This study is innovative in its focus on empowering family caregivers on ACP engagement within a Chinese cultural context. Findings will offer robust evidence on the sustainability and clinical utility of the CC-ed intervention, presenting a -scalable, community-based model to enhance ACP engagement, reduce caregiver burden, and improve EOL care outcomes. N/A

titleEnglish: Effects of an AI-empowered scenario-based point-of-care hand hygiene training and auditing platform (AI-HAND-TA) in nursing assistants of residential care homes: a mixed-methods cluster-randomized controlled trial
coInvestigators: Dr Bonnici, Ermira Tartari Prof Cao, Jiannong Dr Hewitt, Jayne Prof Li, Chen Dr Liao, Qiuyan Prof LIU, Yat Wa Justina Mr Ng, Ngai Ming Anthony
abstract: Residential care homes (RCHs) are hotspots for healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) and antimicrobial resistance (AMR). Recent studies show hand hygiene compliance rates among nursing assistants (NAs) providing direct care in community RCHs are only one-third of those observed among nurses in hospital settings. There is a need for targeted interventions that not only reinforce the “5 Moments for Hand Hygiene” (know when) but also integrate the “7 Steps of Hand Hygiene” (know how), together with continuous auditing using wearable devices, to enhance the quality and thoroughness of hand washing among NAs. This project aims to address this pressing issue by developing an innovative AI-empowered training and auditing platform, named AI-HAND-TA, designed for NAs in RCHs. Our approach involves enhancing an existing app for the “7 Steps of Hand Hygiene” techniques only, HAND-HEART (HAND HygiEne Augmented Reality Tool), by integrating two key features: a chatbot that offers scenario-based instructions on when and how to perform hand hygiene, and an automatic monitoring system that tracks compliance using low-energy Bluetooth technology. This dual approach not only provides immediate guidance but also continuously assesses adherence to hand hygiene practices. To evaluate the effectiveness of the AI-HAND-TA platform, we will conduct a cluster randomized controlled trial involving 200 NAs across four RCHs in Hong Kong. We will measure changes in knowledge and hand hygiene performance at three intervals: before training, immediately after, and three months later. Additionally, we will assess the usability and satisfaction of the platform through surveys and focus interviews. By integrating quantitative outcome measures with qualitative feedback, our mixed-methods approach provides a more comprehensive understanding of the intervention’s impact, feasibility, and real-world implementation. The anticipated outcomes of this project include improved hand hygiene compliance among NAs, leading to a significant reduction in HAIs within RCHs. By establishing a sustainable infection prevention and control (IPC) infrastructure, this platform not only benefits NAs and residents but also serves as a model for other healthcare disciplines. In fact, this platform can be easily adapted to different clinical training scenarios for the purpose of suiting healthcare workers in different settings, by creating relevant clinical scenarios and corresponding training instructions. Ultimately, our research will contribute valuable insights into effective hand hygiene training and compliance monitoring methods and the integration of AI in IPC practices, enhancing safer care environments for vulnerable populations of RCH residents. N/A

titleEnglish: Physics-Guided AI Dual-Driven model for Decadal Aerosol Composition Retrieval
department: Department of Land Surveying and Geo-Informatics
coInvestigators: Prof Wong, Charles Man-sing Prof Yuan, Qiangqiang
abstract: Atmospheric aerosols exert critical impacts on the Earth’s radiative balance by absorbing and scattering solar radiation, which are also essential for environmental and climate-related studies. Thus, how to realize aerosol parameter estimation accurately and reliably at finer spatio-temporal resolution has attracted extensive attention. Numerous studies have been conducted for satellite-based aerosol optical depth (AOD) retrieval, which is a key parameter to describe the effect of aerosol on light attenuation. However, other aerosol parameters (e.g., Single Scattering Albedo (SSA), Fine Mode Fraction (FMF), aerosol types) are generally neglected, rendering it difficult to comprehensively characterize the aerosol properties, thereby introducing substantial uncertainties in aerosol-climate interaction models. The research team proposes to develop a novel physics-guided artificial intelligence (AI) dual-driven model to achieve aerosol composition retrieval at finer spatio-temporal resolution based on Fengyun-4 (FY-4) and Advanced Himawari Imager (AHI) observations. The proposed project is structured by three inter-related work programs: (i) a decadal Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF)-corrected land surface reflectance (LSR) dataset will be constructed by synergizing multi-angle and multi-spectral satellite data; (ii) a pioneering physics-guided AI dual-driven model will be developed by introducing physical constraints of Radiative Transfer Model (RTM) to an Attention-augmented Random Forest Transfer Learning (ARFTL) architecture for multiple aerosol parameter estimations; (iii) a Multi-Dimensional Aerosol Fingerprint Classification (MDAFC) method will be developed to enable dynamic aerosol type identification at pixel level by leveraging the derived multi-dimensional aerosol fingerprint database. This project will fill the research gap of lacking a methodology to realize multiple aerosol parameter retrievals at finer spatio-temporal resolution and the classification approach for pixel-level aerosol type identification. Deliverables from this project, especially for the proposed several novel algorithms, a long-term BRDF-corrected LSR dataset, a comprehensive aerosol fingerprint database and decadal dynamic pixel-level aerosol type maps, are expected to significantly advance satellite-based aerosol retrieval techniques. They will provide vital data for air quality monitoring, climate model calibration, and informed policy-making, supporting Hong Kong and the global community in achieving environmental sustainability and climate resilience. N/A

titleEnglish: Nurturing Minds, Not Lazy Clicks: A Design-based Inquiry into Pedagogically Responsible AI Learning Tools for Children
abstract: AI-based learning tools are rapidly becoming part of children's education, offering personalized feedback and adaptive support, yet many are designed without sufficient consideration of how children learn effectively or of their developmental needs. As a result, many tools may risk promoting cognitive laziness, weakening learning quality, and discouraging active thinking. The proposed project aims to address this learning gap from a design perspective by investigating interaction mechanisms in AI learning tools that scaffold children’s minds-on learning, defined as active cognitive engagement in reasoning, problem solving, and meaning making rather than mere taps, swipes, or answer-seeking. The research will unfold in three stages: first, contextual inquiry with children will document how they interact with and make sense of AI learning tools in everyday settings; second, participatory design workshops with children, parents, and educators will surface values and co-create developmentally appropriate, pedagogically grounded interaction mechanisms for children's minds-on engagement; and third, toolkit development and evaluation will involve usability testing and structured feedback to ensure the guidelines explicitly support cognitive engagement over stimulus-response clicking. Together, these efforts will generate theoretical insights and a practical toolkit for designers, developers, and educators, grounding design in the realities of children's learning and the values of key stakeholders to provide actionable guidance for creating AI learning tools that nurture young minds rather than promote passive clicks. N/A

titleEnglish: Scaffolding the forethought phase of self-regulated learning of English-speaking with AI
coInvestigators: Prof Hwang, Gwo-Jen Dr Kohnke, Lucas Dr Pan, Xie
abstract: English as a foreign language learners often struggle with limited oral proficiency. As classroom time for speaking practice is typically insufficient, effective speaking development relies heavily on self-regulated learning (SRL) beyond the classroom. Among the three SRL phases: forethought, performance, and reflection, the forethought phase is particularly important, as it involves goal setting, strategic planning, and motivational regulation before learning begins, shaping the quality of subsequent learning and performance. However, many learners lack the skills to engage in effective forethought independently. Artificial intelligence (AI) offers adaptive, real-time scaffolding to guide this process, while mind mapping enables learners to organize and visualize their thinking by transforming abstract plans into coherent, structured representations. The integration of AI and mind mapping therefore holds strong potential to support learners in planning and preparing effectively for speaking tasks. This project proposes a Self-regulated AI-supported Forethought for English-speaking (SAFE) model, which positions mind mapping at the center to connect the four SRL dimensions (i.e., cognitive, metacognitive, social-behavioral, and motivational regulation) into a coherent and accessible process. To investigate different balances between learner autonomy and external support, the project further proposes three mind-mapping scaffolding conditions: self-constructed, prompt-guided, and template-driven. It will evaluate SAFE’s effectiveness in enhancing speaking performance, motivation, self-efficacy, and willingness to communicate (WTC) while reducing speaking anxiety, and examine how different scaffolding conditions, mediating and moderating mechanisms, and learner factors influence its overall impact. The project adopts a mixed-method design. Stage 1 involves multiple case studies with 20 students. Data from forethought artifacts and interviews will be thematically analyzed to develop a taxonomy of SAFE strategies. Stage 2 follows an iterative design-based research process to refine and validate the SAFE model. Expert reviews and pilot classroom implementations will guide successive revisions to ensure pedagogical soundness, feasibility, and sustainability. Stage 3 employs a quasi-experimental design with 12 first-year university English classes (approximately 240 students) to compare the SAFE approach with business-as-usual forethought across the three mind-mapping scaffolding conditions. Pre- and post-tests of speaking performance and questionnaires on AI literacy, motivation, self-efficacy, anxiety, and WTC will be administered, complemented by learner artifacts and interviews. Quantitative analyses will evaluate SAFE’s effectiveness and identify mediating and moderating mechanisms, while qualitative data will undergo thematic analysis to reveal patterns in forethought strategies and perceptions of AI scaffolding. Synthesizing both strands, the study will suggest evidence-based guidelines for integrating AI into SRL and speaking instruction. N/A

titleEnglish: An Advancement in Retrieval of Nighttime Aerosol Optical Thickness
department: Department of Land Surveying and Geo-Informatics
coInvestigators: Prof Lee, Kwon-Ho Prof Qin, Kai
abstract: Aerosols have a significant impact on air quality, ecosystem, the Earth’s energy balance, and our climate. Extensive studies have been devoted to studying aerosol optical thickness (AOT) under daytime condition using solar irradiance. However, nighttime AOT retrieval remains poorly developed. Existing studies mainly use Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) Day/Night Band (DNB) on satellites for nighttime AOT retrieval due to its broad spatial coverage. However, current retrieval algorithms have several uncertainties on: (i) background nighttime light (NTL), which is the dominant factor and is strongly affected by viewing geometry and seasonality; (ii) surface reflectance, which is limited by insufficient and uneven satellite observations; (iii) aerosol models, which are generally simplified into only one type, leading to error in complex environments, e.g. urban areas; (iv) the validation dataset, i.e. AERONET lunar AOT is only available during periods of sufficiently strong moonlight, resulting in gaps from waning to waxing gibbous phases. To address these challenges, this study proposes a new framework to reduce uncertainties in all four aspects. Nighttime AOT will be retrieved from VIIRS DNB calibrated radiance at a spatial resolution of 750 m. Firstly, a seasonal geometry-discrete maximum nighttime light method is proposed to derive the background NTL. Secondly, a hyperspectral camera mounted on Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV), with ancillary data of land cover types and three-dimensional (3D) building models, will be utilized to model the Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF) and rectify the surface reflectance. Thirdly, nighttime aerosol models will be established by considering the daytime aerosol properties, air quality, and meteorological data. The Mie model coupled with a core-shell structure will be utilized to construct the nighttime aerosol models. Fourthly, a nighttime reference dataset will be assembled by combining AERONET lunar AOT, Lidar-derived AOT, and AOT derived from ground NTL using integrated spheres, for validation. The proposed methodology will be validated across three representative regions in East Asia with diverse climates and aerosol regimes: Hong Kong (China), Xuzhou (China), and Gangneung (South Korea). These advancements are expected to yield a set of more accurate nighttime AOT products, which could strengthen climate modelling by improving estimates of diurnal radiative forcing. Besides, assimilation of nighttime AOT will enhance predictions of particulate matters in areas with limited ground-based air quality stations. Given the availability of the data in the retrieval process and the release of our models, this method can be transferable to other regions worldwide. N/A

titleEnglish: A cluster-randomised controlled trial of a slow-jogging intervention for improving the executive function and physiological regulation of adolescents with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Dorothy Fung-Ying Prof Chan , Oi-wan, Karly Dr Chung, Raymond Chi Keung Dr Mok, Kam-Ming Dr WONG, Simpson, Wai-lap
abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) typically degrades high-level executive functions, such as planning, attention, flexibility, and impulse control, and is exhibited by approximately 5.6% of adolescents worldwide. ADHD treatments commonly include medication and psychosocial therapy, but these can be costly and time-consuming, respectively, and are not effective for all patients. Accordingly, this proposed project will investigate the efficacy of slow jogging, a moderate-intensity exercise, as a novel and accessible means of enhancing executive functioning and emotional regulation in adolescents with ADHD. Specifically, over 10 weeks, participants will be divided into three groups. One group will receive a slow-jogging intervention, a second group will receive a high-intensity interval training (HIIT) intervention, which involves alternating between short bursts of high-intensity running and periods of recovery walking, and a third group will serve as a waitlist control group. Slow jogging is performed at a comfortable pace known as the “niko niko” (smiling) pace and emphasises rhythm, mindfulness, and enjoyment. It is hypothesised to promote a balanced physiological state that supports cognitive control and emotional stability. In contrast, HIIT involves short bursts of intense running and may be more stimulating but less sustainable for adolescents with ADHD. The proposed project will be based on a psychophysiological model linking executive function deficits in adolescents with ADHD to autonomic dysregulation—impaired regulation of arousal and emotional responses by the autonomic nervous system—which in turn disrupts prefrontal cortex functioning, the brain region responsible for high-level executive functions. One hundred and eighty adolescents aged 12 to 17 with a clinical diagnosis of ADHD will be recruited from secondary schools and randomly assigned to a slow-jogging group, an HIIT group, or a waitlist control group. Assessments will be conducted at baseline, post-intervention, and three months following intervention completion. We hypothesise that compared with the HIIT and waitlist control groups, the slow-jogging group will exhibit greater improvements in high-level executive function, particularly planning (primary outcome), cognitive flexibility, inhibition, sustained attention, and reaction time variability. We further hypothesise that these gains will be mediated by increased heart-rate variability, highlighting psychophysiological regulation as a key mechanism whereby slow jogging alleviates the symptoms of ADHD in adolescents. Overall, the project may demonstrate that our slow-jogging intervention is a practical tool that can be used in familial and educational settings by adolescents with ADHD to improve their daily functioning, emotional well-being, and academic performance. N/A

titleEnglish: Trajectories of Family Victimization and Polyvictimization in Hong Kong: A Population-Based Longitudinal Study
coInvestigators: Prof CHEN, Qiqi Dr Ho, Frederick Ka Wing Prof Ip, Patrick
abstract: Family violence is a pervasive public health issue with profound consequences for individuals and society. Traditional research has often examined specific forms of violence—such as child abuse and neglect, intimate partner violence, or elder abuse and neglect—in isolation, overlooking the complex interplay of multiple victimizations within the same family. The concept of Family Polyvictimization (FPV), developed by the principal investigator, addresses this gap by focusing on the co-occurrence of three or more victims within a family, with perpetrators being family members. Our previous cross-sectional studies have established the prevalence and risk factors of FPV, but the developmental trajectories and long-term impacts remain poorly understood due to a lack of longitudinal research. Objectives: This study aims to (1) investigate the trajectories of family victimization (FV) and polyvictimization over three years, categorizing families into sustained/increased FV/FPV, decreased FV/FPV, or non-FV groups; (2) examine family-level risk and protective factors associated with these trajectories; and (3) assess health impacts of different victimization trajectories on all family members, including adults, children, and the elderly. Method: A population-based longitudinal panel study will be conducted, following up 1,400 families previously recruited in a baseline survey in Hong Kong. The sample included families with at least one child under 18 years of age, representing diverse socioeconomic backgrounds. Data will be collected using the validated Family Polyvictimization Screen (FPS), which enables multiple family members to report violence against adults, children, and the elderly within a family. Health-related quality of life and mental health outcomes will be assessed using standardized instruments. Family-level risk and protective factors will be measured through validated scales on family hierarchy, socioeconomic status, family stress, father and grandparent involvement in childcare, social support, and coping strategies. Quantitative data will be analyzed using multinomial logistic regression and multiple regression models, while qualitative in-depth interviews with selected family members will provide contextual insights into the dynamics and service needs related to FPV. Contribution: This study is the first to longitudinally examine the trajectories of family polyvictimization in a representative population sample in Hong Kong. By identifying risk and protective factors and assessing the health impacts of different victimization patterns, the research will advance theoretical understanding of FPV and inform the development of targeted prevention and intervention strategies. The findings will provide evidence for policymakers, practitioners, and service providers to enhance early identification, multidisciplinary collaboration, and integrated approaches to family violence prevention. N/A

titleEnglish: AI-Enhanced, Game-Based Cyberbullying Bystander Intervention for Preadolescents, with Parent and Teacher Modules: App-Based versus Face-to-Face Delivery in a Three-Arm Randomized Controlled Trial
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Edward Ko Ling Prof Li, Chen Prof ZHU, Yuhong
abstract: Cyberbullying presents significant risks to young people worldwide, and bystanders are considered crucial to its prevention. Many existing bystander interventions focus on individual skills without fully integrating parents, teachers, and students, limiting coordinated school-home approaches. Evaluations often lack evidence from randomized controlled trials (RCTs), with outcomes typically assessed only in the short-term and concentrated in high-income countries. These gaps highlight the need for scalable and culturally adaptable solutions. GISCC2.0 (Gamified Intervention for School and Cyberbullying in Children) is an AI-enhanced, game-based bystander intervention grounded in the Theory of Planned Behavior and Self-Determination Theory. It builds on the success of GISCC1.0, which was developed and evaluated by the team in China. The primary objective of GISCC2.0 is to improve Behavioral Application of Learned Intervention Strategies. Secondary objectives include enhancing Attitude toward Intervening in Cyberbullying, Self-Efficacy to Support Victims, Ability to Overcome Social Pressure, and Motivation to Intervene in Favor of Victims. The intervention is delivered through app-based and face-to-face formats with shared core scenarios, decision trees, and feedback rubrics, and role-specific adaptations for preadolescents, parents, and teachers. Individual learning incorporates scenario role-play and gamified progression, group learning uses collaborative tasks and structured interactions, and system-level levers include class-wide norm-setting contracts and teacher response protocols. AI components personalize learning experience by providing adaptive feedback while maintaining privacy safeguards and expert oversight. The effectiveness of GISCC2.0 will be evaluated through a three-arm, mixed-methods RCT, with 667 students (Grades 6-7), 667 parents, and 67 teachers across Hong Kong, Qingdao, and Inner Mongolia. Using class-level randomization (1:1:1 blocked by grade) to minimize spillover and align with teaching practices, participants will be assigned to app-based, face-to-face, or waitlist control group. Contamination risks will be mitigated through arm-specific teacher assignments and exposure monitoring. Assessments will occur at baseline, post-intervention, and at three- and six-month follow-ups, with latent growth models used to evaluate the outcomes over time. Data will be analysed using intention-to-treat multilevel models accounting for clustering, mediation tests of theory-based pathways, and qualitative focus groups and interviews to capture user experiences and context. We hypothesize that both active formats will outperform the waitlist control group, and app-based delivery will perform comparably to face-to-face delivery on outcomes. GISCC 2.0 will provide schools with a scalable, cost-efficient, and AI-enhanced program to combat cyberbullying. The program will deliver culturally relevant, policy-informed solutions for education and health systems and offer a replicable model for cross‑sector school-family partnerships. N/A

titleEnglish: Uncovering Real-Time Brain Mechanisms of Word Composition by Bridging Neuroimaging and Multimodal Models
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Prof Pylkkanen, Liina Prof Siok, Wai Ting
abstract: Humans effortlessly understand novel expressions by combining the meanings of familiar words. We can grasp "smash a cake" without prior encounter because we know what it means to smash something and what a cake is. This ability, known as semantic composition, to generate limitless new meanings from a finite set of elements, is a cornerstone of language. Therefore, understanding the neural basis of this compositional process is an essential step toward explaining the mechanics of human language comprehension. Although cognitive neuroscience has begun to reveal the neural basis of semantic composition, most studies overlook the multifaceted nature of meaning itself. Consequently, how different meaning types—such as perceptual/sensory versus symbolic/linguistic—shape compositional processes remains unclear. This study investigates the spatiotemporal dynamics of these processes, asking: (1) Is there a general-purpose neural circuit for semantic composition, or are distinct circuits recruited for different meaning types? (2) What computational operations underlie this process—is meaning integration achieved through simple addition, or through more flexible, interactive blending, and under what conditions? A key innovation of our work is using a timely solution to a major challenge: accurately quantifying perceptual and symbolic meaning. Previous studies relied on manually defined features and human ratings, which are difficult to scale and lack precision. Here, we leverage a powerful new tool—multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Recent breakthroughs in AI now allow us to automatically and precisely extract two distinct forms of meaning: perceptual representations from video encoders that capture sensory, experiential knowledge, and symbolic representations from text encoders that reflect abstract, linguistic knowledge. Eighty adults will read two-word phrases and short stories while their brain activity is recorded using electroencephalography (EEG). For each target word, perceptual representations will be derived from AI-generated videos, and symbolic representations from the corresponding word forms via a text encoder. Two compositional models—linear addition and nonlinear weighted transformation—will combine single-word meanings into phrase-level representations. We will then apply regression analyses to link these model-derived features with neural responses, revealing how meanings are dynamically combined over time across the different forms of meaning. This project will provide the first detailed account of how real-time semantic composition operates in both the perceptual and symbolic systems. The findings will advance our understanding of how the brain constructs meaning and guide the design of next-generation AI systems that integrate knowledge in a more human-like way. N/A

titleEnglish: The Scripture of the Jade Sovereign: A Textual Study
department: Department of Chinese History and Culture
abstract: The Scripture of the Jade Sovereign (Gaoshang Yuhuang benxing jijing 高上玉皇本行集經; hereafter YHJ) stands as one of the most significant and influential scriptures to emerge from the Southern Song period. As one of the most widely circulated Daoist scriptures—both within and beyond Daoist circles—the YHJ played a crucial role in establishing the Jade Sovereign (Yuhuang 玉皇) as the head of the Chinese pantheon. Furthermore, it inspired a vast array of liturgies and new scriptures in his honor. Given its profound influence, a textual study of the YHJ is an urgent scholarly priority. This project presents the first complete English translation and comprehensive study of the YHJ. To accurately decipher this technically precise work, the translation is grounded in a rigorous scholarly methodology centered on textual analysis. This analysis necessitates an exhaustive comparative and inter-textual approach, placing each passage of the YHJ in dialogue with a vast array of other Daoist scriptures and, where appropriate and necessary, related non-Daoist texts from other Chinese traditions, especially Esoteric Buddhism. This meticulous cross- referencing is crucial because the scripture was composed in a highly specialized language. Its terminology makes specific, technical references to precise liturgical actions. The key word in this project is intertextuality. Undertaking deep philological investigation, this study demonstrates the crucial fact that the very composition and redaction of the YHJ witness the integration of a vast spectrum of Daoist scriptures, which are interwoven into a new, coherent text. The project makes a dual contribution to the fields of Daoist studies. Firstly, it provides, for the first time, a meticulously researched but accessible English translation of a key text, making it readily accessible to students and scholars alike. Secondly, using a new interpretive framework based on intertextuality, the original translation highlights ritual performance as the central key to understanding the text’s devotional function as well as its cultural impact. This study traces the history of the YHJ and reveals how it came to be located in the late imperial and modern corpus of Daoist literature and how it engendered the production of new scriptures devoted to the Jade Sovereign. N/A

titleEnglish: Navigating Emotions in the Age of GenAI: English Language Teachers’ Emotional Trajectories and Practices in Hong Kong K-12 Schools
coInvestigators: Prof Hu, Guangwei Prof ZOU, Di
abstract: This research investigates the complex interplay between English language teachers’ emotions and practice as they integrate Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) into K-12 education. Emotions are recognized as central to teachers’ identities and pedagogical approaches, influencing their agency, professional development, and adaptation to educational innovations. The rapid integration of GenAI, strongly promoted by Hong Kong’s education authorities, has created a dynamic and sometimes volatile environment, making it crucial to understand how teachers emotionally navigate this technological shift. Relatedly, understanding teachers’ emotions regarding GenAI and impact of teachers’ emotions on their practice is essential for maintaining a resilient workforce. This study adopts an interactional approach to emotions, which considers both individual and social factors, and is informed by Complex Dynamic Systems Theory. This theoretical lens allows for the examination of emotions as dynamic, context-dependent, and evolving systems, rather than static states. While previous research has explored teacher emotions and AI integration, significant gaps remain: few studies track emotional trajectories over time, focus on the Hong Kong context, or examine K-12 language teachers using a mixed-methods approach. To address these gaps, the research employs a convergent mixed-methods design. The quantitative component utilizes Q methodology to identify distinct emotion profiles among English teachers, capturing subjective experiences and shared viewpoints regarding GenAI integration. 64 teachers from 16 Hong Kong schools (primary and secondary) will sort statements about their emotions towards AI, with data analyzed using by-person factor analysis to reveal core emotion profiles. The qualitative component comprises the analysis of post-sorting interviews, classroom observations, and teaching artifacts (i.e., activity plans and teacher-GenAI interaction records). While post-sorting interviews probe teachers’ emotional responses, observations and artifacts illustrate how emotions are manifested in practice. Thematic analysis is adopted to explore the entanglement of emotion and practice, tracking changes in emotion profiles and teaching approaches over three semesters. The findings will provide insights for policymakers, teacher educators, and developers, informing emotionally responsive interventions and professional development. This research will help move AI adoption in Hong Kong education from the potential “trough of disillusionment” (i.e., declining excitement over the technology due to challenges in sustainable adoption) to the “plateau of productivity” (i.e., achieving sustainable use of the technology since its affordances are broadly demonstrated) according to the Gartner Hype Cycle. Ultimately, the study seeks to facilitate the productive integration of GenAI in education by understanding teachers’ emotional trajectories. N/A

titleEnglish: Life-making on the land: Gender and generational dynamics in China’s agrarian transformation
abstract: Despite their significant contributions both in agricultural production, as farmers, workers, retailers, and entrepreneurs, and in family reproduction, through taking up the bulk of domestic chores and performing care work for children and the elderly, the role of rural women’s productive and reproductive labour in sustaining global agri-food systems has constantly been overlooked and under-valued. Rural women’s circumstances are affected not only by what goes on within households, but also by the broader political economic currents of agrarian transformation that directly shape their livelihoods. In many parts of the developing world, agricultural commercialization and intensification has unfolded as a deeply gendered process, reconfiguring farm labor, land use, resource access, and distribution of responsibilities in ways that are distinctly uneven for men and women. This project proposes to investigate the gender and generational dynamics of agricultural transformation in China today, adopting a multiple-case research design to compare how different trajectories of agricultural restructuring have co-evolved with changing strategies of family reproduction. In existing studies of agricultural modernization in China, predominant focus has been given to structural changes in production, regimes of land and labour, and dynamics of class differentiation, with scant attention being devoted to the gendered and generational aspects of agrarian transition. The gradual replacement of smallholder farming and household-based production with a diversity of new operators from agribusinesses to cooperatives has nonetheless reconfigured gender and family dynamics in varied ways. Rather than presenting a uniform narrative, this project will use the comparative method to probe the gendered implications of differentiated pathways of transition in Heilongjiang, Shandong and Guangdong. Using in-depth ethnographic research and postulating a framework that examines interactions between agricultural restructuring, family reproduction, and women’s agency, this project’s findings will afford crucial insights into the social dynamics of varied trajectories of agrarian transition in China. Going beyond the academia, our aim is for this project to generate policy and educational impact by de-invisibilising processes of life-making on and off the farm and centre-staging women’s agency in the co-evolution of agricultural transformation and family reproduction. The United Nations General Assembly has declared 2026 the International Year of the Woman Farmer, providing a timely opportunity for engaging policymakers, non-profit organizations, and the general public. Through firsthand empirical research and public engagement initiatives, this project will contribute to pluralizing global knowledge on women and rural development in the agrarian Global South. N/A

titleEnglish: Enhancing gesture, speech prosody, and their integration in autistic children through humanoid-robot-assisted training
department: Dept of Language Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Dr Bosker, Hans Prof Chan, Angel Prof Chen, Zhuoming Dr Cheung, Chung Wai Prof Sheng, Li Dr Tang, Tempo Po-yi Prof WEN, Chunyi
abstract: Autistic individuals are known to show deficits in speech prosody, gesture and their integration. However, the current intervention plans have not targeted at the integration of speech prosody and gesture and the use of gesture production to improve speech prosody. It is thus high time that we study 1) the training effects in improving the temporal alignment between the speech prosody and gesture. Weak Central Coherence theory may explain autistic children’s difficulties in the temporal alignment between the speech prosody and gesture as they may focus on uni-sensory information instead of multisensory processing. Training programs targeting at multisensory processing may thus lead to improvement in the integration of speech prosody and gesture. 2) the training effects of using gesture to boost the use of speech prosodic cues. Specifically, it is hypothesized that gesture may boost prosodic prominence in focus marking as predicted by cumulative-cue hypothesis, which proposes a positive correlation between acoustic and kinematic correlates of prominence. Ninety autistic children (30 in the training group with gestures, 30 in the training group without gestures and 30 in the control group), 30 typical developing children aged from 10 to 12 will complete pre- and post-training production experiments as well as training. We will analyze acoustic cues using Praat and gesture using ELAN. Statistical modelling will be further applied to all the extracted data to compare pre- and post-training performance. We will program a humanoid robot to make co-speech gestures targeting at improving the temporal alignment and the improvement of speech prosody through gesture training. This project will be among the first to investigate the training effects in improving the deficits in the integration of the visual and auditory modalities in focus marking among autistic children. It also enhances our understanding on the relationship between gesture and speech prosody and the benefit of using gesture to boost the use of prosodic cues. Therefore, the project may provide potentially valuable information for the diagnosis of autism and the design of intervention plans. The intervention plan will be among the first to be comprehensive in addressing the temporal alignment of two modes and to use gesture to improve acoustic realization of focus marking in speech. N/A

titleEnglish: Rethinking “Bad” Films: Chinese Videotape Cinema and Its Legacies (1986-1993)
department: Department of Chinese History and Culture
abstract: This research project is an in-depth study of an underexplored popular artistic commodity/product, namely, Chinese videotape films. The term "Chinese videotape film" refers to direct-to-video fictional films made in Video Home System (VHS) format, which were originally written and produced by small to medium-scale commercial production teams in Mainland China during the 1980s and 1990s. The guiding hypothesis is that these demotic artistic products constituted a noteworthily distinct alternative cinematic history, alongside the mainstream national one. The research first examines the genres, themes, and social imaginaries of an array of videotape films. Second, it investigates the production mechanisms employed by these films, which were created outside the purview of state-owned studios and prominent commercial film production companies. Third, the research explores the consumption infrastructures, screening spaces, and distribution channels associated with these films. The research seeks to unravel and understand the diverse ways in which the producers, video hall owners, and audiences together engendered and participated in an alternative cinematic landscape. The significance of this research is manifold. First, it represents the first concerted and comprehensive endeavour to unearth Chinese films that existed outside of the official archival systems. It furnishes, as a result, an inventory of primary materials—a visual bibliography—for future studies in Chinese film and media. Second, it expands the domain of Chinese film studies by drawing attention, for the first time, to the heretofore neglected staple of Chinese videotape films, the investigation of which encourages a fresh understanding of the interplay between VHS media and the diverse visual productions and circulation channels prevalent in the period in question. Third, it brings to light the experimental, cult, and genre films that fall outside the conventional framework of Chinese-language cinema scholarship, showing how they displayed a different cinematic vocabulary and aesthetics. Fourth, these films are revealed to be informative windows to the larger world, that is, a China that was navigating the fraught processes of the transition from a planned economy to a market economy, where artistic creativity and its consumption became an integral of the popular cultural fabric. As an interdisciplinary effort that carves out a novel space within visual studies, the project pursues a multidimensional study of media materiality, cultural production, urban culture, and popular literature, shedding light on a drastically transforming China in the midst of fomenting social changes of the 1980s and 1990s. N/A

titleEnglish: Co-regulated task-based language teaching with peer and AI partners: Effects on ESL learners’ speaking, self-regulation, and engagement
coInvestigators: Dr Fung, King Tat Daniel Prof LI, Shaofeng Prof ZOU, Di
abstract: Year-1 undergraduate students in Hong Kong, most of whom are English-as-a-second-language learners, face considerable challenges in academic communication and regulating their own language learning process as they transition from secondary to tertiary education. Although English is the medium of instruction in universities, many students come from Chinese-medium instruction secondary schools and have had limited exposure to English academic discourse. As a result, they often lack the proficiency which are required for complex communicative tasks. These students must adapt from a teacher-directed, exam-oriented learning environment to a more autonomous, self-regulated learning (SRL) approach–a transition that typically requires structured support. Task-based language teaching (TBLT) presents a promising approach to address these dual challenges. By engaging students in authentic academic tasks through a structured cycle–pre-task planning, task performance, and post-task analysis–TBLT fosters both academic communication skills and opportunities for learners to plan, monitor, and evaluate their own learning. However, research has produced mixed results regarding TBLT’s effectiveness in developing learners’ SRL. This inconsistency may be due to many learners lacking sufficient knowledge of SRL and struggling to implement them independently within the task cycle. As such, learners often require co-regulation–support from peers or others–to develop and internalise effective SRL skills. To bridge this gap, this project integrates co-regulated learning (CoRL)–where learners collaborate to regulate their learning–into TBLT, enabling them to practise academic communication through pragmatic oral tasks while receiving targeted scaffolding to develop SRL abilities. Employing a mixed-methods quasi-experimental design, this project will examine how peers and AI agents serve as co-regulators and conversational partners for Hong Kong Year-1 ESL learners. It will compare the impact of peer and AI support on learners’ speaking proficiency and SRL development (outcome), as well as on their task performance and engagement (process). Learners’ perceptions of CoRL-TBLT, experiences with peer and AI partners, and the challenges encountered will also be explored. The findings will provide evidence on the effectiveness of integrating co-regulation into TBLT for L2 learning, offering insights into learning processes and outcomes. The project will yield practical recommendations for teachers on designing tasks that address linguistic and pragmatic demands, and on leveraging peer and AI-based scaffolding. Ultimately, the results will inform best practices for English learners, guiding them in the effective use of generative AI and peer collaboration to enhance both speaking proficiency and SRL skills. N/A

titleEnglish: Structural Barriers, Life Events, and the Everyday Life: An Ethnographic Study on Older Offenders’ Crime Trajectories in Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Cheung, Nicole Wai-ting Prof Chui, Eric Wing Hong Prof Laidler, Karen Ann Prof Yan, Elsie Chau Wai
abstract: As the global population ages, criminal behavior among older adults has increased worldwide. In Hong Kong, over 28% of offenders are aged 50 and above, predominantly engaged in survival-driven street crimes—such as shop theft, street deception, illegal gambling, and employment in the criminal market—often committed to meet basic needs such as food or shelter. Our previous study indicates that older street offenders, primarily men, commonly face challenges at various levels—including inadequate welfare provisions, an ageist employment market, declining health, repeated incarcerations, fractured family relationships, homelessness, and drug addiction (Cheng, 2024; Cheng & Lapto, 2023a, 2023b). Consequently, their crime trajectories are shaped not simply by individual choice, but by the cumulative impact of these structural, gendered, life events, and everyday factors. However, existing studies and interventions have focused on the situational factors faced by younger offenders, overlooking the unique structural challenges faced by older adults and limiting the development of effective support for their desistance. This exploratory study addresses the research gap by combining a critical criminological lens that situates crime within the context of structural inequalities, with insights from desistance studies. It examines the unique crime trajectories of older survival-driven offenders by investigating (1) how macro factors such as structural inequalities, cultural and gender norms, and policy context shape their survival need and everyday experiences; (2) how later-life events (e.g., bereavement and retirement), mediated by these structural factors, affect their everyday experiences and their pathways into or out of crime; and (3) how they interpret and navigate the intersecting pressures of aging, adverse life events, and structural disadvantages, shaping their persistence or desistance. Ethnographic methods, combining 480 hours of observation over 24 months in locations where older offenders spend their daily routines and in-depth interviews, will be used to compare the lived experiences of 20 desisters (crime-free for at least seven years), 20 deescalators (whose crime severity has reduced), and 20 persisters (whose crime severity remains the same or accelerates), including both women and men, in Hong Kong. This study contributes to crime trajectory studies by extending its theoretical framework to encompass later-life offending and desistance beyond Western contexts, and by deepening insight into the relationship between aging, crime, and social inequality. It also provides an empirical base for developing targeted, culturally responsive frontline support strategies and formulating social policies aimed at facilitating desistance among Hong Kong’s aging offender population. N/A

titleEnglish: Integrating Generative-AI and Mixed Reality to Support Older Adults’ Spatial Experience and Social Participation in High-Density Cities
coInvestigators: Dr Jian, Yi Izzy Prof ZHOU, Zhibin
abstract: Population ageing is one of the most pressing challenges of our time, particularly in high-density cities such as Hong Kong, where older adults often reside in small apartments or shared homes, often with restricted privacy and limited opportunities for social participation. These spatial and social constraints heighten risks of loneliness, reduced autonomy, and declining well-being. Although smartphones and online platforms expand communication opportunities, many older adults struggle with small screens, complex steps, and unfamiliar interfaces, leaving them excluded from technologies meant to reduce isolation. To address these limitations, this study will explore how the combination of Mixed Reality (MR) and generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) can offer more accessible and engaging alternatives. MR extends living spaces by overlaying digital layers and allowing direct, gesture-based interaction, which makes environments more engaging while also making them easier to share. Additionally, GenAI transforms simple voice or sketch inputs into personalised content, making it easier for older adults to express themselves, understand others, and therefore build social connections. Together, these technologies can turn older adults' everyday environments into spaces for creativity and communication, rather than being limited to medical or therapeutic use. The recent emergence of lightweight AI-enabled glasses further lowers barriers to use, making such systems both more feasible and more relevant for ageing societies. This study will engage with older adults through a multi-stage design-based approach to create and evaluate a GenAI-based MR prototype that enhances everyday spaces, creative expression, and social participation. First, more than 60 participants aged 65–85 will be recruited from three housing types, and their spatial and social needs will be identified through interviews and guided practice. Second, a prototype will be designed with simplified interaction, personalised content creation, and shared collaborative spaces, and then developed using modular MR and AI toolkits. Third, the prototype will be tested and iteratively refined in collaboration with older adults, using usability scales, spatial and creative tasks, and social participation measures. A six-month follow-up evaluation will ensure its usability, relevance, and inclusivity. The study will provide a deeper understanding of how older adults can utilise technologies to enhance their daily environments and interactions, while also contributing new theoretical insights into how MR and GenAI influence spatial experience and social participation in high-density cities. This research aims to advance the field of healthy ageing, guiding the development of age-friendly policies and practices that support the creation of more supportive and inclusive societies. N/A

titleEnglish: Robots in Public Places: The Emergence of a New Interaction Order
coInvestigators: Prof Reeves, Stuart Prof Smith, Robin
abstract: Robots, often seen as embodied artificial intelligence, have started to appear in public places in the form of robotaxis, delivery vehicles, or cleaning robots. This is happening worldwide, but perhaps nowhere more so than in China where robots have become part of everyday life. This has led to new kinds of interaction: spontaneous encounters between human and robots. Currently, robots in public places are still relatively rare, but this is likely to change in the future. What would happen if we had thousands of these robots roaming our streets? While there are many studies that have focussed on the technical challenges of such deployments, this project will the first to explore the social consequences for people: What challenges do robots pose for people in public places? Starting with Goffman there has been much work that has explored the interaction order between human participants. People are constantly monitoring each other not just to avoid accidents, but also to maintain the civility of public places (e.g., respecting each other’s personal space or avoiding starring at other people). Research has thus established that this public order is a moral order with consequences for those that break that order. This project therefore asks how robots are understood by humans and when and how robots are seen as breaking the rules of the interaction order. We will conduct an innovative qualitative video-based study of spontaneous encounters between humans and robots in public places. On the basis of a pilot study, we have identified six different kinds of public places (from squares to restaurants to university campuses), where different kinds of robots (cleaning, delivery, security, selling) interact with different user modalities (pedestrians, cyclists, mopeds, cars). We will adopt a following-robot recording method, where researchers follow the robots. These recordings will be supplemented by social media analysis of reports of extraordinary encounters of people with robots. This project will, first, identify instances where robots are seen to disrupt the interaction order by exhibiting everyday incivility. It will, second, explore how humans understand these robots and what kind of status they ascribe to them. Third, it will investigate what kind of accommodation work is required from people to welcome these new agents in public places, from helping them to dealing with their unexpected behaviour. Finally, the project will draw out implications for the regulation and design of robots in public places. N/A

titleEnglish: Dyadic-based physical activity intervention for children who are undergoing cancer treatment: A randomized controlled trial
coInvestigators: Dr Cheng , Wai Tsoi Frankie Prof HO, Ka Yan Dr Tyrovolas, Stefanos Ms Wang, Hai Xia Prof Wong, King Ho Carlos Mr Wu, Stanley Prof Yu, Clare Chung-wah
abstract: Background Early interventions since cancer diagnoses to promote physical activity(PA) are of paramount importance. However, most PA interventions for children undergoing cancer treatment reveal no significant beneficial effects on PA levels. Our team conducted a randomised controlled trial (RCT) to promote PA in children undergoing cancer treatment. Although the results showed significant differences in PA level among the intervention and control groups, more than two-fifth of children maintained low PA self-efficacy (PASE). Bandura’s Social Cognitive Theory and previous literature show that parental involvement may have an influential effect on children’s PASE and PA levels particularly during cancer treatments. Thus, parent–child dyadic interventions may be a potential strategy to promote PA self-efficacy and hence, the PA levels of these children. A literature review found that a well-designed dyadic PA intervention to promote children’s PASE is lacking. Therefore, developing and testing a parent–child dyadic intervention on children undergoing cancer treatment to promote PASE and PA levels remains an important gap to be addressed. Objectives To examine the effectiveness of a parent–child dyadic intervention in promoting PASE and PA levels in children undergoing leukaemia maintenance therapies and to explore its cost-effectiveness and how dyadic interventions influence children’s PA levels. Study design A sequential mixed-methods design with a three-arm RCT(dyadic vs. individual vs. control) will be conducted in the paediatric oncology wards of the Hong Kong and Greater Bay areas. A total of 99 parent–child dyads with children aged 9–18 years old who are undergoing leukaemia maintenance therapies will be recruited. Over 3 months, dyads in the dyadic group (n = 33) will receive dyadic intervention with 12 visits. Meanwhile, dyads in the individual group (n = 33) will receive individual intervention with 12 visits. The control group (n = 33) will receive no intervention. At the baseline, 3-, 6-, 9-, and 12-month follow-ups, all children will fill in the questionnaires measuring their PA levels, PASE, and quality of life. Children’s PA levels will also be measured using accelerometers. 20 dyads in the dyadic group will be purposively invited to participate in one-on-one semi-structured interviews in 3-month follow-up and 20 in 12-month follow-up. Implications If found effective, parent–child dyadic intervention is anticipated to be integrated into practice in children undergoing cancer treatment, reducing the consequences of physical inactivity and related healthcare costs. N/A

titleEnglish: Effectiveness of a multi-component self-management intervention for anxiety and respiratory-related symptoms in patients with lung cancer: a 3-arm randomized controlled trial
coInvestigators: Prof Currow, David Christopher Prof HO, Ka Yan Prof Lam, Wendy Wing Tak Prof LI, Yan Prof Loong, Herbert Prof Takemura, Naomi Prof Wong, King Ho Carlos Prof WONG, Samuel Yeung-shan Prof Yeung, Wing-Fai
abstract: Abstract Background: Breathlessness, cough and fatigue are common in patients with lung cancer. Respiratory distress symptom intervention (RDSI) was found to be an effective to manage these three symptoms in our previous randomized controlled trial, but the intervention effectiveness was greatly compromised in those with high levels of anxiety, a prevalent symptom in patients with lung cancer. Mindfulness based on mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) has shown to be an effective component to reduce anxiety which is feasible to be added into the RDSI to form mindfulness-RDSI (M-RDSI) to enhance the intervention effectiveness. Objective: The primary objective of our study is to examine the effectiveness of M-RDSI in Hong Kong Chinese patients with lung cancer. Study design: We will adopt a three-arm mixed-methods randomized controlled trial over 3 years. A total of 189 patients with lung cancer will be recruited from the Department of Clinical Oncology, Prince of Wales Hospital. Subjects will be randomized into one of the three groups according to the 1:1:1 ratio: (1) RDSI group (n=63): receiving three 60-minute face-to-face training sessions (weeks 1, 2, and 4) by a trained nurse, covering controlled diaphragmatic breathing exercises, calming techniques, cough easing techniques, acupressure, and tips for increasing activity levels, plus one telephone follow-up at week 6; (2) M-RDSI group (n=63): receiving the same intervention schedule as the RDSI group for the three face-to-face sessions, with an additional 30 minutes per session to practice core mindfulness exercises from MBCT, plus one telephone follow-up at week 6; (3) Control group (n=63): receiving routine follow-ups with a health educational booklet on symptom management. All subjects will complete a set of validated questionnaires to assess anxiety as the primary outcome, breathlessness, cough, fatigue, health-related quality of life and mindfulness as the secondary outcomes. Data collection will be done at baseline, week 6, and week 12. The generalized estimating equations model will be used to assess changes in primary and secondary outcomes over time among different groups. Contributions of the study: Symptom management is crucial for patients with cancer, including those having lung cancer, especially they often experience compromised daily function and health related quality of life due to breathlessness, cough and fatigue. If M-RDSI is proven effective, it has a great potential to be integrated in clinical settings to improve the symptom management for lung cancer patients and be extended to other patients with respiratory diseases. N/A

titleEnglish: Understanding and overcoming colorectal cancer screening disparity in Hong Kong: A qualitative investigation of the voices of the socially disadvantaged for an equitable healthcare.
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Engle Angela Dr Fong, Yuk Fai Prof LAI, Wing-Leung Daniel
abstract: Colorectal cancer ranks as the third most common cancer and the second leading cause of cancer death in Hong Kong in 2022. In response to this, the Hong Kong government has implemented a Colorectal Cancer Screening Programme (CRCSP) for those aged 50 to 75 years since 2016. Unfortunately, only around 510,000 eligible persons have participated in the CRCSP since the programme launch as of the end of 2024. The CRCSP screening rate of 18% since the programme launch is far lower than the target screening percentage of 68.3% as suggested by the Healthy People 2030 goal for colorectal cancer screening among age-eligible adults, and far lower than those observed in other Asian countries and in the United States. In particular, past literature shows that cancer screening disparity is notable worldwide. People who are the socially disadvantaged groups such as the poor are less likely to receive cancer screening. According to literature, the socially disadvantaged groups are in an unfavourable position in enjoying screening because of the social and structural barriers. Therefore, diagnosis at a late stage is not uncommon among the socially disadvantaged groups. Building on the advocacy of the critical medical anthropology (CMA) framework which proposes health disparities are determined by social structure and institutions with a particular interest in prioritising a joint action of building a just and equitable healthcare, this study is proposed to tackle colorectal screening inequity encountered by the socially disadvantaged groups in Hong Kong, with the aims to: (1) identify the barriers preventing eligible adults aged 50 to 75 years, who are the socially disadvantaged, from participating in the CRCSP from a CMA perspective, (2) identify the incentives that can assist the socially disadvantaged to participate in CRCSP and thus overcome screening inequity, and (3) explore the perspectives of primary care doctors on their experiences in CRCSP and in communicating with the socially disadvantaged about the screening. The proposed study is a qualitative and participatory action research with two study stages. Stage 1 will conduct semi-structured in-depth interviews with the socially disadvantaged and with primary care doctors. Stage 2 will involve a participatory action research following the Three Co’s Framework which will involve the service users (the socially disadvantaged) and the service providers (primary care doctors) in the co-creation of a solution manual that can guide the future practice of CRCSP to become more friendly and actionable to the socially disadvantaged populations. N/A

titleEnglish: Neural substrates of handwriting deficits in children with developmental dyslexia: Testing the Procedural circuit Deficit Hypothesis
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Prof Bi, Hongyan Prof Lau, Kai Yan Prof Tao, Sha Prof Ullman, Michael T. Dr Yang, Yang
abstract: This project seeks to understand handwriting difficulties in children with developmental dyslexia, particularly in relation to domain-general procedural memory. Children with dyslexia often struggle not only with reading but also with fluent writing, which can manifest as poor spelling and pauses during handwriting. These challenges adversely affect their academic performance, as handwriting is a routine task in paper-based exams and classroom activities. Despite the prevalence, handwriting difficulties have received insufficient attention in research. A better understanding of these challenges is crucial for helping dyslexic children achieve their full potential in school and life. The central idea of this project is that handwriting challenges in Chinese children with dyslexia may be linked to issues in the procedural memory brain system—the type of memory we use for implicit learning of sequences through practice, like handwriting. This theory is known as the Procedural circuit Deficit Hypothesis (PDH). It suggests that problems in the procedural memory system can explain various symptoms of dyslexia. While the PDH mainly focuses on the cortico-basal ganglia circuits, we will also explore the cerebellum’s role in explaining these difficulties. To test this hypothesis, we will investigate how procedural memory, including its behavioral manifestations, neural substrates, and overnight consolidation, contributes to handwriting and reading skills in Chinese children with and without dyslexia, via a novel two-day design. Fifty children with dyslexia and 50 age-matched controls will perform two fMRI tasks inside the scanner—a classical procedural memory task and a handwriting task (day 1); they will repeat these tasks after 24 hours, to assess overnight consolidation (day 2). We predict that children with dyslexia will exhibit overlapping abnormalities in the procedural memory system across both tasks. Furthermore, we hypothesize that individual differences in procedural memory—reflected in behavioral and neural measures collected over both days—will significantly explain the children’s handwriting and, to some extent, reading abilities. As a secondary objective, we will explore the potential distinction between the cortico-basal ganglia and cortico-cerebellar circuits, specifically examining their contributions to central processes (e.g., retrieving functional orthographic units from memory) and peripheral processes (e.g., motor execution) involved in handwriting. The findings from this research should contribute to the theoretical understanding of dyslexia, particularly regarding its connection to procedural memory. Ultimately, these findings may lead to better educational strategies and therapies for improving handwriting skills in dyslexic children, helping them achieve better academic outcomes. N/A

titleEnglish: Design of an Innovative Size-free Co-knit Weaving System for Sustainable Production
abstract: While woven fabrics dominate textile production, their creation relies on essential but non-value-adding sizing and desizing processes, which are auxiliary stages that reinforce yarns for weaving but require subsequent removal. This dependence leads to significant environmental problems (chemicals and high effluent loads), extended production chains, and substantial resource consumption (raw materials, water, energy, and labour), resulting in increased costs and reduced productivity. Researchers and industries have explored various strategies to alleviate these environmental and economic challenges; however, they are hindered by concerns regarding effectiveness, scalability, yarn compatibility, costs, and other factors. This study thus aims to design and develop size-free co-knit weaving apparatus and systems that incorporate chain-stitch knitting into the loom to enhance warp yarns’ resilience against the intense stresses and strains encountered during weaving. Instead of depending on chemical coatings, warp stability is attained through loops that incorporate flexibility, redistribute stress, and form a novel hybridized textile structure. The method is conceived as a sustainable intervention and an innovative enhancement of the designer’s toolkit. The methodologies are threefold: (1) creation and modeling of a size-free co-knit weaving system with an integrated apparatus capable of synchronized chain-stitch knitting and weaving; (2) optimization of knit module parameters and development of a feedback matrix with geometric analysis to achieve accurate localized elasticity, structural cohesion, and stress redistribution, resulting in effective size-free warp yarn configurations; and (3) evaluation and validation of the properties to compare textile performance under co-knit conditions with traditional sizing methods. This also identifies any potential functional and aesthetic attributes resulting from the co-knit technique. This suggested innovation hopes to enhance textile processes by eliminating chemical sizing, leading to reduced environmental impact and increased fabric versatility. Key features include improved warp yarn resilience through knitting, streamlined production chain control over elasticity, and application to all yarn types. The method modifies the preparation phase without additional chemicals, addressing a gap in size-free weaving research and providing predictive insights that guide parameter optimization within the knit-woven interface. Additionally, the integration of a pre-knit chain stitch into woven fabrics enhances the co-knit weaving system, enabling the emergence of new design possibilities for functional and aesthetic textiles, distinguished by elasticity, comfort, and expressive material attributes. By emphasizing structural hybridity as a creative approach, co-knit weaving situates itself at the convergence of sustainable production, material innovation, and design methodology. N/A

titleEnglish: Capital Pursuer or Peasant Protector? A Study on Local Agricultural State-owned Enterprises in Western China
coInvestigators: Prof Chen, Yiyuan Dr Lan, Xi Prof Yan, Hairong
abstract: China’s pathway to agricultural modernization remains contested. Since the state actively promoted new agriculture operators to boost agriculture modernization, the capital’s encroachment on smallholders have drawn a central concern of Chinese rural studies. However, current scholarship obscures a significant hybrid actor: local agricultural state-owned enterprises (LASOEs). As Chinese agriculture is ladened with the dual tasks of production and the well-being of rural communities, local governments are increasingly turning to LASOEs as a comprehensive strategy to address this tension. Unlike central state-owned enterprises (SOEs) and SOEs in pillar sectors that are expected to play roles in market, agricultural SOEs are often identified as functional enterprises, prioritizing social service instead of economic objectives. This distinction obliges us to ask, whether would the social responsibilities of LASOEs provide new possibilities for agrarian justice? and whether and to what extent would LASOEs align with the process and outcome of capitalist accumulation typically associated with private capital? By shedding light on the dynamics of the economic-social dual objectives of LASOEs, this research attempt to interrogate how LASOEs as a new agriculture operator to face the tension of agriculture modernization and protection of peasants. This project proposes employing a political economy framework to examine the logics and mechanisms of LASOEs. Our study will focus on four key themes: 1) the means of production, namely the ownership of materials; 2) the social division of labor, namely the ways of labor arrangement in agricultural productive activities; 3) the distribution of income, with a focus on the relationship between the distribution mechanisms and local village communities; 4) the social relations of consumption, reproduction, and accumulation, including the utilization of the labor surplus. As LASOEs root in local society, we will also integrate social-economic embeddedness perspectives in this political economic inquiry. Delving into these processes through ethnographical study in three counties in Western China, we aim to provide insights into LASOEs’ role and influences in rural development and explicate the complexities of China’s agrarian transformation. This study will provide a novel perspective to understand rural China by bridging SOEs studies and critical agrarian studies. Our findings will contribute to understandings on whether and how public institutions such as state-owned enterprises can mitigate the social harms associated with agriculture capitalization. Empirically, the findings of this study will offer valuable reference for China’s rural subsidiary policies and innovative strategies for the rural revitalization. N/A

titleEnglish: A randomized controlled trial on the effects of a mindfulness-based professional training programme on improving occupational health and professional competence
coInvestigators: Dr KWOK, Jojo Yan Yan Dr LAU, Elsa Ngar Sze Prof Mak, Winnie Wing Sze Prof Wong, Daniel Fu Keung Prof Wong, Samuel Yeung-shan Dr Zhang, Zoe Jiwen
abstract: This project is about sustainability in mental health and social care. Mindfulness-based interventions have received growing attention in view of their wide application to a variety of target populations that can enhance individual’s mental health and positive coping, including enhancing occupational health. Mental health and social care professionals (schoolteachers, social workers, counsellors, nurses and allied health professionals) have encountered significant stress and elevated risk of burnout during and following COVID pandemic. There is an increasing demand from the mental health and social care professionals due to the alarming prevalence of mental health issues and other social problems such as professional manpower shortage following COVID. As they play key people to translate the mindfulness approach into different real-world settings, recent initiatives and studies explored how to support these professionals to improve their occupational health and strengthen their professional competence through time-limited training. Personal mindfulness practice has been viewed as an important domain in professional competence in delivering mindfulness-based intervention, which is unique to mindfulness approach. In this study, the effects of a 6-day professional training programme which integrates 4-day training in teaching mindfulness with 2-day mindfulness retreat will be investigated. A three-arm randomized controlled trial is designed to compare the 6-day programme with two comparators, a 4-day teaching mindfulness training, or participants receiving reading materials only. A total of 220 helping professionals will be recruited and randomly allocated to three arms. The primary outcome of the programme will be empathy, and other secondary outcomes on personal self (including trait mindfulness, burnout, depression, anxiety, and self-compassion), and professional self (work engagement, and competence in applying mindfulness) will be evaluated. The data collection will be enhanced by using Ecological Momentary Assessment, enabling the project team to collect additional data on key measures including mindfulness, peace of mind, burnout, self-compassion, and empathy in close intervals for investigating the changes of these variables over time, reducing bias in reporting, for participants in all arms. This study can enhance the knowledge and skills in delivering mindfulness-based intervention. Findings can shed light on the effects of incorporating mindfulness practice in the development of professional competence, suggesting directions to strengthen mental health and social care during this critical time in Hong Kong. N/A

titleEnglish: Climate change threatens my culture: Investigating the influences of perceived symbolic threats of climate change via the threat response x construal level perspectives
coInvestigators: Prof Clayton, Susan Prof Tam, Kim-Pong
abstract: Climate change has dreadful impacts on cultures and cultural heritage. It can damage historical buildings and natural habitats that are icons of cultural identities, disrupt ways of living that are intertwined with natural environments and weather conditions, and ultimately erode people’s sense of place and identity by forcing them to relocate. However, the study of how individuals respond to climate change threats has predominantly focused on its impacts on safety, health, and the economy (i.e., its realistic threats), overlooking the symbolic threats it poses. To fill this gap, we propose a threat response x construal level model to investigate how perceived symbolic threats of climate change (PSTCC) influence individuals’ climate actions, cultural defense, and mental health, and under what conditions such influences are more pronounced. We posit that PSTCC would motivate people to engage in direct resolution (i.e., climate actions) when collective efficacy beliefs are high, but to adopt palliative responses (i.e., cultural defense) when such beliefs are low, as well as triggering stronger impairment associated with climate change anxiety. Our model further integrates construal level theory to consider when perceived symbolic (versus realistic) threats are more relevant in guiding people’s responses, providing nuanced insight into the differences between the two threats. Our model enables us to derive novel hypotheses for testing how the effects of PSTCC vary across countries by considering socio-ecological features that influence collective efficacy beliefs and the mental proximity of climate risks. As the symbolic threats of climate change are relevant to most countries, it is crucial to investigate how people across different countries perceive such threats and whether such threats have similar or different influences. To test these ideas, we propose conducting four studies with multiple methods. Studies 1 and 2 will employ an experimental design to examine the causal effect of PSTCC and the moderating role of collective efficacy beliefs and psychological distance. Study 3 will be a cross-national survey study examining the differences in the levels and effects of PSTCC across countries and testing the moderating roles of socio-ecological factors. Study 4 will employ a three-wave longitudinal design to examine the longer-term effects of PSTCC on the three outcomes over time. Positive results from this project will broaden and deepen our understanding of various climate change threats, open new research directions, and offer practical insights into climate change communications and promoting climate actions and cultural protection. N/A

titleEnglish: Wiring the Hearts: A Sentimental Media History of the Telephone in China, 1877-1990s
department: Department of Chinese History and Culture
abstract: This project not only seeks to construct the first media history of telephony in modern China by utilizing diverse materials but also aims to elaborate on the concept of "telephonic sentiments"—romantic love, conjugal intimacy, and revolutionary passion as expressed through the landline. It aims to illuminate how this infrastructurally underdeveloped yet publicly pervasive and affectively potent medium reconfigured everyday life and epistemic frameworks, particularly in the affective domain. The telephone functioned as an affective media infrastructure, intertwining technological mediation with sentimental connectivity across transnational, national, and local scales. My tentative argument is that the telephone in modern China embodied a liminal and convergent medium, serving as a dynamic nexus of activities, functionalities, and possibilities within the media ecosystem, operating in an intermediary position among contemporaneous media technologies. The project unfolds across five chapters: Chapter 1 examines writings on the telephone during the late Qing and early Republican periods. Telephonic-form writings not only depicted poignant reunions of separated lovers but also offered a space where traditional Chinese motifs merged with Western industrial capitalism.
