Chapter 2 explores the 1930s advertising campaigns of the Shanghai Telephone Company, which framed telephones as tools of efficiency, while urban fiction and films dramatized them as instruments of instant erotic expression. Chapter 3 investigates the revolutionary passion expressed through the telephone during the 1950s–1970s films, when telephony prioritized collective enterprise over private intimacy. Chapter 4 analyzes Reform-era public telephones as sites of involuntary public intimacy and emotional hotlines, which merged telephony with print, radio, or broadcasting to create confessional spaces. Chapter 5 examines infrastructural failures—delays, interruptions, and disconnections—in 1990s international long-distance telephony, framing them as constitutive elements of media infrastructure that revealed the political-economic chasms between China and developed countries. This project makes a critical intervention in current scholarship, which often prioritizes mobile telephony and subaltern agency, by tracing the longue durée of telephonic culture before the mobile revolution. This project integrates a diverse range of materials, including institutional records, personal testimonies, cultural imaginaries, techno-public discourses, as well as insights from interviews. Bringing together affective histories, media/technology studies, and cultural studies, it positions the telephone as affective infrastructure mediating intimacy, power, and technological embodiment in modern China. Moving beyond historiographies that frame telecommunications primarily through national sovereignty or technological/economic development, this project reconceptualizes Chinese telephony in sentimental and cultural terms, situating it within the global telecommunications network. N/A

titleEnglish: Youth Mental Health Service Delivery in China: Navigating Organizational Complexity in Social Work Practice
coInvestigators: Prof Ku, Hok Bun Dr LI, Bo Prof MCBEATH, Bowen Dr SO, Suk Ching
abstract: China faces a serious youth mental health crisis. Social workers are central to the response, yet they confront multilayered organizational complexities that impede effective service delivery. These complexities originate from: (1) frontline case-level challenges, where practitioners make decisions under conditions of bounded rationality, incomplete information, and high uncertainty; (2) managerial and administrative struggles to coordinate care within volatile, uncertain, complex, and ambiguous (VUCA) environments; and (3) systemic organizational barriers, including institutional fragmentation, misaligned policies, and resource constraints. These interconnected problems highlight organizational, rather than solely clinical, deficiencies at the core of the service delivery system. This study will investigate these organizational complexities by adopting a systems-oriented framework conceptualizing challenges across three interrelated levels of social work practice: (1) micro-level case decision-making, where frontline practitioners navigate clinical complexity under conditions of bounded rationality; (2) meso-level coordination within teams and agencies operating in VUCA environments; and (3) macro-level policy-practice gaps, where street-level bureaucrats negotiate system-level constraints and resources. Guided by this framework, the study aims to: (1) identify and analyze key organizational factors, such as resource allocation patterns, interprofessional collaboration barriers, and training gaps, that generate complexity across micro, meso, and macro levels of practice; (2) examine how these multilevel factors manifest within and are navigated across urban and rural organizational contexts; and (3) co-develop organizational strategies with frontline and managerial stakeholders to mitigate these identified complexities. Using a co-production approach, the project will engage stakeholders across organizational roles (frontline social workers, supervisors, and administrators) from partner agencies in urban (Guangzhou Poai) and rural (Wuding Zhengxin) settings throughout the research process. Data collection will proceed in three stages: (1) participatory focus groups to map shared challenges across organizational roles; (2) semi-structured interviews capturing situated experiences and discretion at different organizational levels; and (3) mixed-role workshops to co-interpret findings and refine strategies. The project will deliver context-sensitive interventions tailored to resource-diverse settings, including decision-support tools for frontline practitioners, supervisory protocols for middle managers, and interagency coordination guidelines for administrators. Theoretically, the study will advance an integrative, multilevel model of organizational complexity through mapping how micro-level discretion, meso-level coordination, and macro-level policy constraints interact within everyday practice. Although centering the expertise of Chinese social workers, the research will also offer transferable insights for addressing systemic barriers, such as resource constraints, skill gaps, and coordination failures, in mental health and social service systems across the Global South. N/A

titleEnglish: Unpacking parent-child dynamics in gaming disorder: A dyadic ecological momentary assessment study
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Edward Ko Ling Dr Ho, Frederick Ka Wing Prof Ip, Patrick
abstract: Background Gaming disorder is a public health concern affecting adolescents and their families. Theoretical frameworks for gaming disorder lack focus on the crucial role of parents. Empirical research on parent–child dynamics is limited by an over-reliance on adolescents’ retrospective reporting and cross-sectional data. We need innovative data collection approaches that capture the impact of the evolving nature of parental influences on problem gaming. This study will generate nuanced insights into the dynamic interplay between adolescents and their parents in the context of gaming disorder. The findings will drive the needed development of research-based, family-focused interventions to treat and prevent the disorder. Objectives This longitudinal study will use an app-based ecological momentary assessment approach with parent–adolescent dyads (dyadic EMA). Grounded in attachment theory, we will: (1) examine the reciprocal relationship between parent–adolescent attachment and adolescent–avatar attachment, and their joint impact on gaming engagement and gaming disorder symptoms over time; (2) investigate the reciprocal relationships between parental stress/negative emotions and adolescents’ gaming engagement; (3) determine the mediating roles of parental mentalizing and parenting behaviors in these relationships. Study design A total of 223 parent–adolescent dyads will be recruited via secondary schools in Hong Kong. Adolescents meeting the cut-off score on a gaming disorder scale will be recruited. The research team has conducted a feasibility study with 65 parent–child dyads. In the proposed study, both parent and adolescent participants will participate in a pre-EMA survey, a 14-day EMA, a post-EMA, and a three-month follow-up survey. During the 14-day EMA period, participants will respond to EMA surveys via an app four times per day. Each EMA survey will ask parents to report their stress and negative emotions, mentalizing capacity, parenting behaviors, and their physical and social contexts. Adolescents will report on their game engagement, parent–adolescent attachment, adolescent–avatar attachment, and gaming disorder symptoms, and their physical and social contexts. Contributions This study will use a relational lens to examine gaming disorder. The study will provide detailed accounts of how parents influence adolescent problem gaming, and how gaming impacts the parent–child relationship. The evidence will improve the current conceptualization of gaming disorder, and this enhanced conceptualization will drive the development of effective family-based interventions and engagement strategies. The dyadic EMA approach will advance research on social interactions and dynamics in gaming disorder and broader family studies. N/A

titleEnglish: Gradient 3D Knitting of Stretchable Cushion for Knee Protection of the Elderly
coInvestigators: Prof Leong, Hio Teng Dr Ng, Sun Pui Prof Yick, Kit-lun
abstract: Fear of injury is one of the main reasons that the elderly avoid physical exercises or sports. While they could wear protective gear, the currently available rigid and hard cushioning on wearable protective gear resist joint movement and cause discomfort. A knee protector which is specifically designed for the elderly based on their knee geometry and joint support needs as well as injury prevention is not available. The main reasons for the lack of such a knee protector are due to unknown properties of the knees of the elderly, absence of stretchable cushioning material that conforms to the body contours and dynamic movement, and the lack of biomechanical studies on the effect of the cushioning and knee protector on human kinetic and physiological responses. To address the knowledge gap, this study characterises the properties of the knees of the elderly and quantifies knee deformation during dynamic movement to design cushioning for knee protectors. A shaping workflow that does not use a mould but instead 3D-to-2D flattening with knit-in slit will provide accurate representation of the contours to minimise displacement during movement. A multi-component gradient knitted cushion which is composed of a force-diffusing outer shell, energy-absorbing middle layer, soft inner face and stretchable areas will be created to optimise the protection and fit for improved dynamic movement and wear comfort. The one-step production of the knitted cushion directly from yarn streamlines the manufacturing process and provides a greener option for cushion manufacturing. Knee protector prototypes will be developed for evaluation through a biomechanical wear trial to validate the design. A biomechanical model can predict the mechanical changes of the cushion and displacement during dynamic movement further supports the design process. The expected outcomes include a geometric-based design framework for elderly-specific protective wear, a novel knitted structure that provides a better fit and cushioning of the joints, an industry-ready knee protector prototype and a biomechanical model for enhancing the development of knee protectors. This study not only provides new insights and scientific guidelines for the designing and engineering of personal protective products but also transforms knee protectors from barriers to enablers of physical activity, thus potentially increasing the adherence of the elderly to physical exercise and enhancing their health and quality of life. N/A

titleEnglish: Revisiting de Tournon’s Mission to Qing China: A Chinese Perspective of a Pivotal Sino-Western Encounter
department: Department of Chinese History and Culture
abstract: The Chinese Rites Controversy was a significant event in the history of Sino-Western relations and ranks among the most bitter and long-lasting religious conflicts in history. The papal legate Carlo Tommaso Maillard de Tournon’s (1668-1710) mission to China gravely exacerbated the controversy and exerted a lasting impact on the development of the relations between China and the West in the subsequent centuries. Over the past few decades, scholars have done numerous studies on this historical event. However, so far, no comprehensive research from a Chinese perspective has been conducted. There remains a lack of detailed examination of the Chinese reception of, and response to, the mission, purposefully using Chinese documents preserved in China and Europe. Since 1993, I have visited about ten European archives and reviewed over 100 volumes of archival materials. Altogether more than 120,000 Chinese characters of documentation related to Tournon’s mission have been collected, including letters between Chinese Christians and missionaries, reports of Chinese catechists, decrees by the Kangxi Emperor and his eldest son, as well as memorials from imperial and local officials that are no longer available in China. These materials significantly supplement the Western sources. By carefully analyzing these archival documents and comparing them with Kilian Stumpf’s Acta Pekinensia, as well as other European documents, this project aims to provide a more comprehensive and balanced portrayal of Tournon's legation in China. The time frame spans from his arrival in Beijing in 1705 to 1716, when the imperial court issued the Red Manifesto. Unlike the Qing official court documents, these Chinese records reveal the voices of local officials and Christians, providing insights into the activities of believers from the lower social classes. The interactions between the converts and missionaries offer another angle from which to view the Controversy. However, the views of the Chinese Christians and local officials have been largely overlooked in earlier scholarship. To fully understand the history and dynamics of the Rites Controversy, the responses of Chinese Christians, together with the various roles of the emperor, princes, Imperial Household officials, provincial governors, prefects, and county magistrates, must be examined. This effort includes an in-depth study of the Kangxi Emperor’s final Southern Tour in 1707. The goal is to provide a well-rounded understanding of the history of the Papal legation in China and help to situate the Rites Controversy within a broader socio-political context. N/A

titleEnglish: Investigating the effects of human chemosensory signals on prosocial learning
coInvestigators: Dr Ye, Yuting
abstract: It is well recognized that mammalians transmit various social information, such as social dominance and reproductive state, through body odor and urine. With the development of social neuroendocrinology, there is increasing interest in understanding the effects of these chemosensory signals on human social cognition and behavior. This proposal investigates the effects of the chemosensory substance androstadienone, an odorous compound found in sweat and semen, on human decision-making. Following on from our own previous finding that androstadienone increases aggression in women but decreases it in men, the proposal asks whether androstadienone similarly affects prosocial decision-making. In a double-blind, placebo-controlled, between-participants design, male and female participants will receive either androstadienone or a control carrier. Given the mixed evidence regarding androstadienone’s effects on prosocial behavior, we will assess its potential dose–response effect by administering three different concentrations or a control (placebo). This allows us to detect inverted-U dose-response relations, which would explain why previous findings were mixed. Prosocial behavior will be measured with a prosocial learning task in which self- and other-regarding interests can change independently of each other and androstadienone-induced changes in prosocial decision-making can therefore be interpreted more clearly. We hypothesize that the effects of androstadienone on human prosocial decision-making will be sex-specific, leaving the direction of the effects open because of the mixed findings of previous research. By combining approaches from neuroendocrinology and decision neuroscience, the proposed project will help us understand the relationship between chemosensory signals and human prosocial decision-making. More generally, the project will shed new light on the multidimensional nature of human social interactions. N/A

titleEnglish: Identity Reshaping, Psychological Wellbeing and Future Life Planning of Children with Cross-border Migration Experiences in Hong Kong: A Multisystemic Resilience Framework
coInvestigators: Prof Lu, Shuang Prof Ungar, Michael Prof Yu, Lu Prof Zhong, Hua
abstract: Children with cross-border migration experiences (CCBM), a mobile population emerging from the unique geo-political context of Hong Kong, are composed of three different groups with varying migration experiences and family configuration, including children of family reunification (CFR), cross-border children (CBC) and children of non-local talents (CNLT). Living in and frequently shifting between two distinct sociocultural systems, these children are faced with significant challenges, often leading to confusion and ambivalence in cultural identity. This study aims to investigate how the varying experiences of migration and family configuration and how micro-, meso-, and macro-level factors in a multisystemic resilience framework contribute to the cultural identity, psychological wellbeing and future life planning of children with cross-border migration experiences independently, jointly, and through complex interplays. It also seeks to understand how children with cross-border migration experiences exercise their own personal agency to mobilize resilience resources to navigate cultural identities, promote wellbeing and plan for future life paths. The study will employ a mixed methods research design, following the QUAN-qual paradigm. Phase I involves a three-wave longitudinal survey with 1,440 children of 7th-9th grade with various cross-border migration experiences, including CFR, CBC, and CNLT, to examine the complex associations among cross-border migration experiences, resilience, cultural identity, psychological wellbeing and future life planning, as well as to track the overtime changes in all these components and their relationship mechanisms. Phase II involves in-depth interviews with 45 children with cross-border migration experiences, 15 from each group, to complement the results of longitudinal data and to better understand how CCBM proactively mobilize resources in navigating cultural identities and promoting psychological wellbeing and future life planning. This research will have significant long-term impacts in theoretical, practical and policy terms. First, the study will advance the resilience theory by proposing and applying a Multisystemic Resilience Framework that emphasizes the interplay of multiple systems and highlights the importance of CCBM’s personal agency. Second, findings of this research will be informative for families and schools to develop effective strategies to facilitate the resilience building of CCBM and will contribute to enhanced public awareness on the importance of an inclusive and supportive society for children from diverse backgrounds. Third, this study will also inform policymakers of the need to support CCBM for their identity navigation, wellbeing promotion and future life planning, which could prove to be a valuable investment in the human capital growth of the Hong Kong Society. N/A

titleEnglish: Moderated Mediation vs. Mediated Moderation: Resolving the Confusion with the Two-Level Regression Approach
coInvestigators: Prof KWAN, Lok Yin Joyce Prof LIU, Hongyun
abstract: To approximate complex relationships among variables, integrating mediation and moderation analyses has been a central focus in applied research. Two models of integration exist and address two distinct questions: (1) moderated mediation (moME), which tests whether mediation effects vary across contexts, and (2) mediated moderation (meMO), which explains how moderation effects operate through certain mechanisms. In applied research, the use of these two models has grown rapidly over time. For instance, the number of moME studies rose from 11 in 2005-06 to 398 in 2015-16, and reached 1,459 in 2023-25. Despite this growth, a critical problem persists in that moME and meMO share identical statistical models, which makes them analytically indistinguishable. This "flip sides of the same coin" problem forces researchers to rely solely on theoretical preference rather than empirical evidence in model selection. Simply put, given the same dataset, there is no way to falsify whether the process is indeed moME or meMO, leaving conclusions potentially arbitrary and scientifically fragile. Different attempts have been made to resolve this moME-meMO confusion, yet none have fully succeeded in differentiating between them. This project proposes to resolve the confusion through the two-level moderated multiple regression approach (2MMR). This approach formulates moderation hierarchically and captures heteroscedasticity patterns that reveal which variable truly serves as independent or moderating variables. This capacity represents a critical step toward resolving the moME-meMO confusion and moving beyond the long-standing view that moME and meMO models are merely the "flip sides of the same coin". While theoretically sound, the empirical performance of proposed approach remains uncertain, and even if the method proves effective, encouraging applied researchers to adopt it widely requires practical tools and demonstrations. As such, this project aims to both evaluate and translate the method into practice through two distinct phases. Phase 1 will conduct a simulation study to assess the empirical performance of 2MMR in differentiating moME and meMO under varying data conditions (e.g., sample size and effect size). This phase will identify optimal analytic conditions under which 2MMR can reliably distinguish between the two models. Phase 2 will enhance translational impact by (1) developing two statistical tools (a syntax-based R package and a graphics-based Shiny web app) to facilitate easier implementation by applied researchers, and (2) reanalyzing real-world datasets from existing studies to showcase how the method can be applied in real practice and how more valid conclusions about moME and meMO can be drawn. N/A

titleEnglish: Integrating GenAI and Corpora in Translation Pedagogy: A Mixed Methods Study of Learner Engagement, Self-Efficacy, and Performance
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Dr Wang, Xing
abstract: Corpus-assisted translation teaching has shown clear benefits, particularly in helping students analyze authentic language data and develop data-driven translation strategies. However, traditional corpus tools also pose practical challenges. Although technically advanced, they often overwhelm learners with vast datasets and technical demands, making them less accessible to novices. In addition, many corpus platforms are relatively static, which limits interactivity and diminishes the overall learning experience. Generative AI (GenAI) can help address some of these issues by offering real-time feedback and adaptive learning features that make translation tasks more approachable. Yet, GenAI also has notable limitations, including occasional inaccuracies and unreliable handling of context-specific translations. As a result, relying solely on either corpora or GenAI fails to fully meet students’ learning needs. To tackle these challenges, we propose developing an AI-powered, corpus-integrated platform that combines the strengths of both GenAI and corpus technologies. Building on the existing Translator Training Corpus created by the PI, the platform will offer key features such as corpus-based pattern summarization, AI-generated translation suggestions, real-time feedback, and personalized AI–human interaction. Importantly, the platform will be designed to integrate a range of mainstream large language models, including the ChatGPT series, Claude, DeepSeek, and Qwen through API connections. This interoperability will ensure flexibility, enhance translation quality, and reduce the risk of over-reliance on a single model. The project will be evaluated through a mixed-methods design involving 80 master's students in translation at a Hong Kong higher education institution. Participants will be divided into two groups: a control group using an AI-only platform and an experimental group using the integrated AI-corpus platform. Over a nine-month period, the study will assess the impact on self-efficacy, engagement, and translation performance using multiple data sources, including translation logs, surveys, interviews, and reflective journals. This mixed-methods approach will allow for longitudinal tracking and triangulation of data, ensuring both the reliability of quantitative findings and the richness of qualitative insights. In sum, this research addresses current gaps in translation pedagogy by exploring the underexamined role of AI in translator training. While AI is increasingly adopted in other educational domains, its application in translation remains limited. This study seeks to provide empirical insights into how AI-enhanced corpus platforms can support translation teaching in multilingual contexts such as Hong Kong. The findings will inform pedagogical practice and offer practical guidance for integrating AI and corpora into translator training. N/A

titleEnglish: The Impact of Family Involvement in Exercise-Based Rehabilitation for Chronic Low Back Pain in the Elderly
coInvestigators: Prof Chui, Wing Hong Prof Ferreira, Paulo Prof NG, Chi Kit Dr Shea, Graham Ka Hon Prof TSANG, Man Ha Sharon
abstract: Background: Low back pain (LBP) affects about 40% of older adults, reducing quality of life and independence. Exercise can alleviate symptoms, yet maintaining long-term adherence remains a major challenge due to limited motivation, uncertainty, and lack of support. Family involvement has improved outcomes in other chronic conditions, but its role in exercise programs for older adults with LBP remains underexplored. Objectives: This study will (1) determine whether a family-assisted Pilates program reduces functional decline compared with a standard program without family involvement; (2) examine interdependent relationships between patient outcomes (pain, disability, mobility, adherence) and family members’ skills, confidence, and attendance; and (3) explore participants’ experiences to identify barriers, facilitators, and strategies for sustaining family-supported exercise. Methods: A two-arm randomized controlled trial will recruit 156 older adults aged 60–80 years with chronic nonspecific LBP and a family member willing to participate. Participants will be randomly assigned to a 7-week Pilates-based program either with or without family involvement. Family members in the intervention group will be trained to act as exercise facilitators. Outcomes—including disability, pain, physical function, social participation, and quality of life—will be assessed at baseline, post-intervention, 6 months, and 12 months. Statistical models will examine associations between family-related factors and outcomes, while semi-structured interviews with a purposive sample from both groups will provide qualitative insights to explain quantitative findings. Hypothesis: We hypothesize that older adults exercising with family support will show less functional decline and greater improvements in disability, pain, mobility, and quality of life compared with controls at 12 months. Greater family attendance and competency are expected to predict higher adherence and better outcomes. Conclusion and Impact: This randomized controlled trial will determine whether a family-assisted Pilates program can mitigate disability progression in older adults with chronic low back pain compared with a standard program. We will test interdependent actor–partner effects between patients and family caregivers over one year to identify mechanisms driving sustained adherence and improved outcomes. The findings will provide evidence to guide family-centered community rehabilitation models that enhance healthy ageing and reduce healthcare burden in Hong Kong. N/A

titleEnglish: Elucidating the Causal Role of Cortico-Subcortical Circuits in Syntactic Processing via a Transcranial Ultrasound Stimulation Approach
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Prof CHEN, Robert Prof FRIEDERICI, Angela D. Prof HARTWIGSEN, Gesa Prof LI, Yuanning Prof WANG, Shaonan
abstract: A milestone of human language is our ability to combine words into hierarchical structures (rather than simple linear lists) to share and comprehend thoughts. Such “syntactic processing” works across all languages. Studying and modulating the brain systems backing up this ability could deepen our understanding of how language works in the brain. It might also help build promising brain-behavior prediction models for future translational research. However, prominent language processing models mainly focus on the left fronto-temporal (i.e., a cortico-cortical) network, including the left inferior frontal gyrus (LIFG) and the left posterior temporal lobe (LpTL) for syntactic processing. Emerging data show that subcortical structures, such as the left caudate nucleus (LCN) within the basal ganglia (BG), might also play a role in human syntax. Nevertheless, the specific functions of and interactions between the cortico-subcortical regions for syntactic processing are largely unclear. Previous neuroimaging studies have demonstrated the brain-behavior correlations, providing a firm basis for subsequently scrutinizing their causal relationships. Thus, the causal relevance of cortico-subcortical circuits for hierarchical syntactic processing awaits urgent investigation. Leveraging the low-frequency focused transcranial ultrasound stimulation (TUS), a cutting-edge non-invasive cortical and deep brain stimulation technique to safely modulate the neural activities directly, this project pioneers the application of TUS in language (especially syntactic) processes. We aim to combine TUS with functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to explore whether and how the cortico-subcortical circuits may drive syntactic processing. Study 1 (encompassing 60 healthy adult native Chinese speakers) will utilize fMRI during a sentence-reading task to identify syntactic neural correlates and personalized TUS coordinates by comparing complex sentences with simple ones. Study 2 will combine TUS and fMRI, and the same participants will undergo 4 TUS sessions (LIFG, LCN, LpTL, and vertex [the control site] TUS): They will receive TUS first, followed by the fMRI scan. Both behavioral and imaging data will be compared to the control to reveal the causal mechanisms. In Study 3, a “perturbation mapping model” incorporating the personalized “virtual brain twins” will be constructed by integrating multimodal data to enable brain-behavior prediction for syntactic processing. In summary, this project initially integrates neuromodulation and neuroimaging techniques to elucidate the causal role of the cortico-subcortical circuits in syntactic processing. Its findings will not only update the cortices-centered language processing models, but also provide society with efficient and novel TUS methodological references and valuable brain-behavior models for future practical translations. N/A

titleEnglish: Investigating Large Language Models’ Conveyance of Speaker Emotions in Machine Interpreting. Toward a Multimodal Computational Linguistic Approach
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Prof Liu, Kanglong Prof Peng, Gang Dr Yu, Jianfei
abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has significantly shaped the interpreting profession in recent years. With their strong functions in semantic understanding and multilingual processing, LLMs have greatly contributed to the advancement of machine interpreting, which is the automatic translation of spoken language from one language to another in real-time communication. The past few years have seen a growing adoption of machine interpreting in the industry due to its consistent performance, scalability, and cost-efficiency. However, despite its wide application, limited empirical studies exist to investigate the accuracy level of LLM-generated output in machine interpreting, particularly regarding their fidelity to critical aspects of communication, such as the speaker’s emotions. Specifically, the extent to which LLMs differ from professional interpreters in capturing and conveying emotions remains largely unexplored. Building on this research gap, the proposed project aims to systematically examine how LLMs convey the speaker’s emotions in machine interpreting, as compared to professional interpreters. Adopting a multimodal computational linguistic approach, this study employs a set of multimodal indicators to model the transmission of emotions across languages at both textual and prosodic levels. Data will be obtained from the United Nations Simultaneous Interpreting Corpus (UNSIC, existing), which includes professional interpreters’ performance, and the United Nations Simultaneous Machine Interpreting Corpus (UNSMIC, to be compiled), which features LLM-generated comparable renditions. This study attempts to address: (1) how LLMs vary from professional interpreters in conveying emotion-related textual content, (2) how LLMs differ from professional interpreters in their emotion-related prosodic delivery, and 3) what factors contribute to the observed variations, if any. The proposed project is among the first to systematically examine the conveyance of emotions in machine interpreting. Theoretically, it advances existing understanding of the difference between LLMs and human interpreters in emotion transmission and evaluates the effectiveness of the multimodal computational linguistic approach for measuring accuracy in Translation and Interpreting Studies. Practically, the findings will inform the development of more reliable LLM-based machine interpreting systems, contributing to the growing integration of new technologies in cross-lingual communication. N/A

titleEnglish: The Local and Global: The Transformation of the Overseas Chinese State Farms in China since 1985
department: Department of Chinese History and Culture
abstract: Many overseas Chinese returned to China after the founding of the People’s Republic of China, where many of them were resettled in the overseas Chinese state farms, which were segregated rural farming communities directly administered by the state. The earliest overseas Chinese state farms were established in the early 1950s, and by the 1980s, there were over 80 overseas Chinese state farms in the country, most of which were located in southern China. The huge gap in economic-technological development and living standards between China and the more advanced regions of the world and between the cities and villages within China were among the main reasons for the creation of the overseas Chinese state farms, and to settle the returnees on the state farms was a compromise between relocating them to the cities and sending them to the villages, and the returnees in the state farms enjoyed some privileges and benefits that were denied to other rural residents. Although the state farms were expected to become models for the surrounding villages, most overseas Chinese state farms failed to attain financial independence and sustainability as fast as the PRC government had anticipated, and by the mid-1980s, they had become a burden for the state, which made it imperative for the government to reform the overseas Chinese state farm system. In 1985, the central government decided that the state farm system should be integrated into the local society partly because many villages around the state farms had by then become more prosperous than the state farms as a result of the rural reform. What is interesting and significant is that localization not only made the state farms local but also made some of them global in the sense that the reform paved the way for them to rebuild their connections with the outside world. The local governments turned many farms into special economic zones in part to make efficient use of the free land as well as the international connections of the residents, and efforts have also been made to transform many overseas Chinese state farms into tourist sites where visitors can enjoy exotic cultural practices of foreign countries. This project will provide a comprehensive analysis of the causes, processes, and consequences of the recent reform of the overseas Chinese state farms, focusing on the role of international connections and influence in the local integration of the overseas Chinese state farms. N/A

titleEnglish: Promoting AI Literacy through Positive Youth Development: A Longitudinal Mixed-Methods Investigation among Social Science Undergraduates in Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Datu, Jesus Alfonso Daep Prof LEUNG, Janet Tsin Yee Prof Yu, Lu
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming education, work, and society. University students – Hong Kong’s future professionals, citizens, and leaders – must be ready to use AI critically, effectively, and ethically. However, global surveys reveal that one in two university students feel unprepared in using AI technologies, and nearly half of young people lack confidence in critically evaluating AI outputs. This challenge is especially pressing for students from non-computer science programs such as the social sciences, who often receive little formal training in AI, yet heavily rely on it for research, learning, and professional preparation. Without adequate attention to AI literacy, Hong Kong risks producing graduates underprepared to navigate the opportunities and ethical challenges of an AI-driven society. To date, most efforts to improve AI literacy have focused on technical and cognitive skills, often overlooking the crucial role of non-cognitive psychosocial factors. Positive Youth Development (PYD) – a framework emphasizing the 5Cs of competence, confidence, connection, character, and compassion – highlights the psychosocial strengths that can help students thrive academically, socially, and ethically. These resources may shape not only what students know about AI but also how responsibly they use it and how they apply it to benefit society. This two-year project adopts a longitudinal mixed-methods design to investigate how PYD contributes to AI literacy among Hong Kong social science undergraduates as they complete their final year projects (FYPs) throughout three semesters. Specifically, the project aims to: (1) examine the longitudinal associations between PYD characteristics and AI literacy dimensions during students’ FYPs; (2) identify distinct profiles of students based on their PYD characteristics and track their AI literacy trajectories over time; and (3) explore students’ attitudes, conceptualizations, and personal experiences regarding AI use across different PYD profiles. By integrating both quantitative and qualitative findings, this project aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of how non-cognitive psychosocial strengths shape AI literacy in Hong Kong’s higher education context. Beyond theoretical contributions, the findings will inform practical strategies for educators and policymakers to strengthen responsible AI readiness, ensuring that graduates are equipped not only for academic success but also for meaningful participation in an AI-driven workforce and society. Ultimately, this work aims to support Hong Kong’s higher educational sector in preparing graduates who are not only technologically competent but are also ethically grounded, ready to thrive in a rapidly evolving, AI-driven society. N/A

titleEnglish: From Self-reported Symptoms to Diagnoses in Traditional Chinese Medicine: A Language Data Analytical Approach to the Identification of Psychological and Physiological Information from Patients’ Chief Complaints
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Prof Chen, Xiuhua Prof Huang, Chu-Ren Prof Li, Shoushan
abstract: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) has a long history and is widely practiced globally as an alternative or complement to Western medicine. Despite this, the lack of standardized methods for identifying, classifying, and documenting medical symptoms—particularly in the context of patient-reported chief complaints—has hindered the broader adoption of TCM as a reputable approach within modern medical frameworks. This project aims to address this gap by developing a comprehensive, standardized labeling and classification framework for medical symptomatic information, specifically focusing on patients suffering from emotion-related disorders. Emotional disorders, such as anxiety and depression, are commonly treated in TCM, but the subjective nature of symptoms and the holistic approach to diagnosis make it difficult to align with standardized medical practices. To achieve this, we will construct a corpus of TCM medical records, concentrating on chief complaints related to emotional disorders. These records will be annotated with both psychological and physiological information, reflecting the TCM diagnostic process, which considers the interrelationship between mind and body. Our research will investigate the correlation between psychological and physiological symptoms as well as the linguistic features in chief complaints, exploring how these factors influence TCM diagnoses and the resulting treatments. In doing so, we aim to develop computational models capable of extracting relevant information from chief complaint data, which will facilitate more systematic and data-driven analyses of TCM medical records. The expected outcomes of this research are multifaceted. First, the creation of this annotated corpus will provide a valuable resource for researchers, allowing for comprehensive analysis of the relationship between symptomatology and TCM diagnoses. Second, TCM practitioners will benefit from enhanced access to well-annotated medical records, enabling more informed and effective diagnoses and treatments. Finally, this project will contribute to the advancement of AI-driven TCM auxiliary diagnosis systems, improving diagnostic accuracy and supporting the integration of TCM within global healthcare systems. Ultimately, this research seeks to elevate the practice of TCM by providing a more structured, standardized, and technologically advanced approach to the diagnosis and treatment of emotion-related disorders. N/A

titleEnglish: Enhancing Self-care in Individuals with Cardiometabolic Multimorbidity Using a Blended Behavioral Intervention: a Sequential, Multiple Assignment, Randomized Trial
coInvestigators: Prof Chen, Shanquan Prof Griva, Konstantina Dr Leung, Kwan Wa Maria Prof Mo, Phoenix Kit-han
abstract: Cardiometabolic multimorbidity, the co-occurrence of ≥2 cardiometabolic diseases (e.g., hypertension, diabetes, and stroke), places significant burdens on individuals, society, and healthcare systems. Yet, effective and cost-effective interventions for multimorbidity remain scarce. In response to WHO’s call to strengthen patient self-care to complement healthcare systems and improve prognosis, this project aims to introduce and rigorously evaluate a blended, adaptive intervention to enhance self-care among individuals with cardiometabolic multimorbidity, focusing on intervention efficacy, cost-effectiveness, and underlying mechanisms. The intervention will utilize a stepped-care model, beginning with a low-burden digital program (“SA-II2”) that integrates self-affirmation, health information, and implementation intention to promote physical activity, healthy diet, and medication adherence. SA-II2 is an optimized version of our previous digital SA-II trial, which demonstrated feasibility and effectiveness in improving hypertensive medication adherence. Participants with suboptimal responses to SA-II2 will receive additional human-delivered health coaching, providing personalized self-care support. This adaptive design could improve cost-effectiveness and sustainability by allocating human resources to those most in need, while fostering autonomous motivation and patient-centered care. We will employ a sequential multiple assignment randomized trial design. A total of 549 adults with cardiometabolic multimorbidity and self-care risks will be randomized in a 2:1 ratio to either the SA-II2 intervention or an information-only control group. After six weeks, non-responders in SA-II2 arm will be re-randomized to receive either stepped-up health coaching (SA-II2+coaching) or continued observation (SA-II2). The primary outcome is a composite Z-score reflecting changes in physical activity, diet, and medication adherence, assessed at baseline, 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months post-intervention. Non-responders will be identified by failure to achieve minimal clinically important differences in the primary outcome or loss to follow-up. Analyses will follow the intention-to-treat principle, using generalized estimating equations to compare the relative efficacy of SA-II2, SA-II2+adaptive health coaching, and control conditions. Inverse probability weighting will address over or under-representation of the outcomes due to randomization scheme, and observations will be replicated when included in multiple comparisons. Cost-effectiveness will be evaluated using gained quality-adjusted life years as the measure of health effects from a societal perspective to ensure comprehensive inclusion of all relevant costs. Incremental cost-effectiveness ratios will be calculated based on differences in mean costs divided by differences in mean quality-adjusted life years between intervention and control arms. Structural equation modeling will examine whether autonomous motivation, perceived competence, and perceived support mediate intervention effects, based on self-determination theory. N/A

titleEnglish: Beyond Speech: Musical Contexts and Training Effects on Lexical Tone Perception
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
abstract: Speech perception in tonal languages like Cantonese requires listeners to resolve substantial acoustic variability across speakers while maintaining stable lexical representations. This perceptual constancy is achieved through talker normalization, whereby listeners exploit contextual pitch information to adjust categorical boundaries for lexical tones. While research demonstrates that speech contexts elicit robust normalization effects whereas nonspeech music contexts do not, critical questions remain regarding how varying degrees of musicality in speech-like contexts—spanning from natural speech through rhythmic and melodic rap to song—modulate normalization processes, and whether musical training enhances these mechanisms. This research investigates three interrelated questions: (1) How does contextual musicality modulate lexical tone normalization in native Cantonese speakers? (2) Does musicianship differentially affect how listeners exploit contexts varying in musical properties? (3) What neural mechanisms underlie musicianship-related differences in context-dependent normalization? Our pilot study with Mandarin speakers revealed that rhythmic rap contexts elicited significant boundary shifts similar to natural speech, whereas melodic rap and song contexts did not differ from nonspeech cello baselines, suggesting that additional musicality may hinder listeners' ability to adopt pitch information for normalization. Two experiments will address these questions using complementary behavioral and neurophysiological approaches. Experiment 1 employs a word identification paradigm with 40 native Cantonese speakers testing five context types (speech, rhythmic rap, melodic rap, song, and nonspeech cello) manipulated across three pitch levels (raised, unshifted, and lowered). Experiment 2 uses electroencephalography to compare cortical processing across three groups (nonmusicians, amateur musicians with 4-5 years training, and professional vocalists) during normalization tasks, examining event-related potential components (N1, P2, N400) and their neural sources. Expected outcomes include behavioral evidence that rhythmic speech-like contexts facilitate normalization whereas highly melodic contexts do not, and neural evidence that amateur training enhances early cortical responses to pitch contrasts without fundamentally altering phonological processing. These findings will advance theoretical understanding of the music-language interface and have practical implications for language-education pedagogy and clinical interventions targeting speech perception deficits. N/A

titleEnglish: Generative AI in doctoral research and writing in Hong Kong: Ethical considerations, emotional reactions, and identity ramifications
coInvestigators: Prof Hu, Guangwei Dr MOORHOUSE, Benjamin Luke
abstract: The rapid spread of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) tools is significantly changing research and writing practices in higher education worldwide. In Hong Kong, where doctoral students face intense pressures to publish and excel in a highly competitive academic environment, GenAI has become an integral part of scholarly workflows, which brings unique opportunities and complex challenges. While GenAI tools promise increased support and productivity, their integration into doctoral research and writing raises critical ethical, emotional, and identity-related questions that remain underexplored. This mixed-methods study seeks to systematically investigate the adoption, use, and impact of GenAI tools among doctoral students across four academic disciplines in Hong Kong. Specifically, the project aims to: (1) identify the specific GenAI uses by doctoral students in research, writing, and scholarly practices; (2) examine the factors influencing their decisions to use or refrain from using GenAI, including disciplinary norms and existing policies; (3) explore the ethical and emotional reactions associated with GenAI use; and (4) study how GenAI use and its ethical and emotional ramifications shape the academic identity construction of doctoral students. The study will be conducted in two phases. Phase 1 will involve the development and administration of a validated survey to 220 doctoral students from four disciplines representing hard-pure, hard-applied, soft-pure, and soft-applied fields. The survey will collect quantitative data on GenAI usage patterns, influencing factors, ethical concerns, emotional responses, and perceived impacts on academic identity. Phase 2 will involve narrative frames collected from at least 30 students per discipline, followed by in-depth semi-structured interviews with 10 students from each discipline. These qualitative approaches will explore how doctoral students interpret their interactions with GenAI, articulate ethical concerns, explain emotional experiences, and negotiate their academic identities. By focusing on the Hong Kong context, characterized by competitive higher education and high rates of GenAI adoption, this project aims to enhance understanding of how doctoral students use GenAI tools in their research and writing practices. It will shed light on the associated ethical and emotional complexities and their implications for identity development. The findings will inform the development of responsible, inclusive, and supportive policies and practices for GenAI use in doctoral education. Insights from this study will be valuable for university administrators, doctoral supervisors, and policymakers seeking to foster responsible and efficient AI use, ethical awareness, emotional well-being, and agency among doctoral students in the GenAI era. N/A

titleEnglish: A Longitudinal Investigation of GenAI-Assisted Differentiated Instruction in L1 Chinese Integrated Writing: Effects on Secondary Students’ Performance, Self-Efficacy, and Engagement
department: Department of Language Science and Technology
abstract: Integrated writing (IW), a critical skill for academic and real-world success, requires students to extract, synthesize, transform information from multimodal source materials and to draw on prior experiences when appropriate to construct coherent texts. The cognitive and linguistic complexity of IW often reduces secondary students’ writing performance and lowers their self-efficacy and engagement. Differentiated instruction (DI), which tailors learning content, process, and assessment to learners’ readiness, has proven effective in addressing learner diversity but remains underutilized in IW instruction due to its time- and resource-intensive nature. This gap between students’ needs and teachers’ capacity highlights the potential for innovative, generative AI (GenAI)-assisted solutions to support DI in IW instruction. This longitudinal project investigates the impact of GenAI-assisted DI on students’ IW performance, self-efficacy, and engagement. It also examines how self-efficacy and engagement influence IW development and identifies factors affecting the successful implementation and sustainability of this approach. With 24 Grade 10 teachers from eight secondary schools (three teachers per school), the study adopts a two-part research design. In Part 1, a professional development program will be conducted. Teachers will be randomly assigned to three groups: Experimental Group 1 (EG1), trained in DI-based task sequencing and feedback; Experimental Group 2 (EG2), receiving the same training as EG1 with an additional focus on integrating GenAI tools; and the Control Group (CG), continuing with conventional IW instruction. In Part 2, these instructional approaches will be implemented with approximately 600 Grade 10 students (25 students × 3 groups × 8 schools). CG teachers will conduct regular extracurricular IW instruction, while EG1 and EG2 teachers will implement DI involving flexible student grouping, adaptive task sequencing, and differentiated feedback. The project employs rigorous quantitative and qualitative methods. A linear mixed model will analyze changes in students’ IW performance, self-efficacy, and engagement across pre-test, post-test, and delayed test phases. A latent growth curve model will further explore how self-efficacy and engagement shape IW development over time. Student interviews will triangulate quantitative findings, while teacher interviews will identify contextual and practical factors influencing the implementation and sustainability of GenAI-assisted DI. As the first longitudinal investigation of GenAI-assisted DI in first-language (L1) Chinese IW education, this project deepens theoretical understanding of DI in secondary contexts and informs instructional transformation through emerging technologies. Its findings offer actionable insights for improving student outcomes and addressing persistent challenges in IW pedagogy. N/A

titleEnglish: PREP-ME: PREParing Pre-retirees for Retirement and Later Life with Motivational Interviewing-Enhanced Education—A Three-Arm Cluster Randomised Controlled Trial
coInvestigators: Dr Hu, Bo Prof Lai, Daniel W. L. Prof Tang, Yong Prof YAN, Elsie Dr ZHANG, Youjuan Dr Zhou, Shuai
abstract: As increased longevity extends retirement, comprehensive, proactive planning is crucial for smooth transitions and sustained well-being in later life. However, the PI’s 2024–2025 Panel Study of Active Ageing and Society (n =5,000) found that fewer than 15% of ageing adults in Hong Kong have concrete later-life plans. Her GRF project (2023–2025) further revealed that nearly half of late middle-aged adults (aged 50–64) are “sandwiched,” supporting multiple generations while struggling to balance family responsibilities with their own ageing challenges and later-life goals. These findings highlight the urgent need for targeted support to improve retirement and later-life preparation. Our systematic review and meta-analysis reveal that most interventions focus narrowly on health and financial planning. Educational interventions yield only short-term benefits, lacking motivational reinforcement. Pilot data from the PI’s GRF project (2024–2025) confirms that motivational interviewing (MI) produces more lasting effects than education alone, highlighting the need for an MI-enhanced educational approach. This study will integrate proactive coping theory, resource-based dynamic model, preparation for future care needs model, transtheoretical model, social cognitive theory, and learning theory to develop a three-week multidimensional intervention for retirement and later-life preparation—including financial, health, family life, social participation, and long-term care domains. Leveraging online accessibility and flexibility, we will develop a MOOC-based, MI-enhanced approach and compare its effectiveness to face-to-face MI-enhanced education and an active control in a three-arm randomised controlled trial. Pre-retirees (N = 339, aged 50–64) will be recruited from 18 organisations selected from the Hong Kong Institute of Human Resource Management and the Research Centre for Gerontology and Family Studies’ extensive network. Participants will be randomized into one of three intervention conditions: face-to-face, MOOC-based, or active control. Participants will be assessed for retirement preparedness, attitude toward ageing and retirement, self-efficacy, life satisfaction, and workplace age-friendliness, at pre-intervention, immediately post-intervention, and four-week follow-up. Quantitative data will be analysed using the intention-to-treatment principle and multilevel linear models, while qualitative data will be analysed using thematic and framework analyses. This project brings together experts in gerontology, social work, social policy, sociology, psychology, and health and lifestyle. It will validate a scalable, MI-enhanced education model and reusable MOOC assets for multidimensional retirement and later-life preparation. The findings will inform policy and service development to enhance retirement preparation and workplace age-friendliness, ultimately boosting silver productivity and Hong Kong’s economy. N/A

titleEnglish: Confucian Intellectual Virtue and Exemplary Rulership
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
abstract: A central tenet of early Confucianism is that rulers must be morally and intellectually virtuous to govern well. Early Confucian philosophers disagree, however, on what qualities count as intellectual virtues, how many there are, and to what extent rulers must exhibit them in governing. The aim of this project is, first, to offer a systematic analysis of early Confucian views on intellectual virtue and its relation to good governance and, second, to critically evaluate these views by bringing them into dialogue with contemporary epistemologists. Research on early Confucianism has largely focused on its moral philosophy, leaving the distinctively epistemological aspects that inform early Confucian thought underexplored. Although Confucians emphasise the importance of having intellectual qualities such as wisdom (智), lucidity (明), and various capacities for knowing (知), there exists no comprehensive and systematic study of these concepts and their relation to the question of what knowledge is and what role it plays in exemplary rulership. One of my aims is to fill this research gap by providing a comprehensive analysis of the distinctively intellectual qualities that the early Confucians take to be essential to being an exemplary ruler. By focusing on exemplary rulership, this project aims to highlight the practical context in which the early Confucians were doing epistemology, and it demonstrates the contemporary relevance of what Confucian texts have to offer on intellectual virtue. An essential part of this project’s argument is the thesis that, although there is a great variety of views on intellectual virtue expressed by different early Confucian texts, they broadly agree on a general conception of intellectual virtue as (what I call) epistemic skill. More specifically, they agree that intellectual virtue is a skill for acquiring and putting relevant knowledge into practice, that it can be developed with adequate training, and that it is essential for governing and acting morally well. Some of this project’s research will be published in a monograph, currently under contract with Cambridge University Press, provisionally titled “Early Confucian Ethics”. The aim of the monograph is to provide an integrated and unified account of early Confucian ethical thought by highlighting the importance of both the moral and intellectual Confucian virtues and to defend it as a viable way of thinking about the role of virtue in practical reasoning. N/A

titleEnglish: Using compression algorithms to map the mental lexicon and improve AI alignment
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Antoni B. Prof Hsiao, Janet Hui-wen
abstract: In visual word recognition, people decompose words into constituents, and words that share constituents are linked in the mental lexicon. Some evidence suggests that people decompose words to the same constituents as a compression algorithm called byte-pair encoding (BPE), which would imply that visual word recognition approaches optimal efficiency and could improve models of the mental lexicon. Furthermore, most large language models (LLMs) use BPE to segment text into tokens, and if tokens map onto psychologically real constituents, it could facilitate concept alignment. That is to say, BPE could help LLMs interpret words like people do. However, supplying LLMs with human-like constituents is not enough; concept alignment also requires that LLMs use those constituents in a human-like manner. People use orthographic constituents selectively, with stronger formal associations between words with fewer semantic associates, which averts failures in word meaning access. So far, LLMs seem to lack this tendency, suggesting that adaptive strategies which people use to compensate for underdetermined meanings are not recovered through statistical learning, so LLMs miss this opportunity for concept alignment. To test these possibilities, we will conduct three sets of experiments. Study 1 will use masked priming in lexical decisions to provide evidence that BPE identifies psychologically real orthographic constituents in English. For example, according to one promising BPE model, people should recognize “allure” faster following “pasture”, which shares the final constituent “ure”, than following “stature”, which has the final constituent “ature”. Studies 2 and 3 will provide evidence that BPE constituents correspond to links in the mental lexicon and that people use those links to supplement representations of vague or unfamiliar words (e.g., “gypsum”) with orthographically related words (e.g., “gypsy”). To investigate these links in the mental lexicon, we will construct multiplex networks, which simultaneously consider orthographic and semantic connections between words, and we will evaluate their performance based on how well they model word associations in Dutch, English, and Spanish, generated by people and LLMs. We hypothesize that people internalize complex orthographic patterns, which influences how people retrieve and represent word meanings, and that although LLMs have direct access to those patterns, they lack adaptive strategies that are essential to human word processing. These findings would support theories of language evolution, improve models of the mental lexicon, and identify potential sources of AI misalignment. N/A

titleEnglish: Translocal belonging and practices of care among people from Indian and Filipino backgrounds in Hong Kong
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Dr Gube, Jan Christian C Dr KHALIKOVA, Venera
abstract: This transdisciplinary project investigates how people from Filipino and Indian backgrounds in Hong Kong experience, understand and express their sense of belonging across multiple locations, and asks how these translocal connections are influenced by care relationships. People of South and Southeast Asian heritage in Hong Kong encounter systemic marginalization, which calls into question conventional notions of belonging based on cultural and linguistic homogeneity. Furthermore, a sense of belonging that transcends local boundaries is usually linked to care needs and responsibilities, including personal, domestic and health care as well as the flow of resources such as money, gifts, news, and support. These critical aspects of belonging, in the context of a sometimes challenging local environment, are not well understood, highlighting a gap in scholarly knowledge and effective policy measures. This matters because the lack of a nuanced conception of belonging may undermine Hong Kong’s efforts to be an inclusive talent-nurturing society. The project involves collaboration between academics at three Hong Kong universities (in linguistics, anthropology and education), support NGOs, and trainee teachers. Embedding care in questions of what it means to belong, we will conduct a contextualising survey, and – with four family groups – will engage in innovative linguistic and arts-based ethnography. Central to each family network is a Key Participant (KP), an adult in Hong Kong with south-south translocal connections and care relationships in either the Philippines or India, country backgrounds with significance for South and Southeast Asian people in Hong Kong. We will explore how our participants experience these relationships through interaction, narrative and the creative expression of their translocal belonging; consider, through our exploration, the challenges that people with links in Hong Kong and elsewhere experience when developing a sense of belonging; and understand how belonging for people with translocal networks is mutually shaped by care relationships. To do this, our work will generate and analyse narratives of belonging and care in localities in Hong Kong and abroad that are meaningful for the participants, combining these with creative outputs on the project’s theme of translocal belonging and care, developed through the KPs’ own photography. Impact will be achieved by developing training and educational materials for schools and for health and social workers, and through an exhibition for a general audience. A long-term goal is to establish the topic of belonging among minoritized people as an area of research strength in Hong Kong. N/A

titleEnglish: Cultural Borderlands and Transnational Negotiations: Russian Émigré Literature in the Far East During the Early Twentieth Century
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
abstract: This research project aims to explore the rich landscape of Russian émigré literature in the Far East, set against the intricate backdrop of East Asian literature and culture in Manchuria. The tumultuous events of the twentieth century-specifically the First World War, the 1917 Russian Revolution, and the ensuing civil war-forced many Russian citizens to flee the Russian Empire, with a significant number seeking refuge in the Far East. While substantial scholarship has focused on Russian émigré literature in Western contexts, the literary contributions of émigrés in the Far East remain largely underexplored, warranting closer examination. Historically, Russian émigré literature in the Far East has been viewed as secondary to the modernist movements in Russia. However, it not only serves as a compelling counterpart to Russian émigré narratives in Europe, shaped by distinct cultural environments, but also enriches the literary landscape of Manchuria, which is predominantly influenced by East Asian discourses. By bringing together the study of the Russian diaspora with the literature of Manchuria-both of which have gained increasing attention in recent English-language scholarship-this project endeavors to illuminate the significance of Russian émigré literature in the Far East, offering fresh insights into themes of exile, belonging, displacement, cultural hybridity, memory, and nostalgia. This study will focus on trans/spatial narratives to investigate how literature became a vital means for émigrés to navigate their experiences of uncertainty and dislocation in transcultural contexts. It seeks to address critical questions: How did the experiences of Russian émigrés in the Far East differ from those of their counterparts in Europe? In a region like Manchuria, characterized by the complex interplay of Chinese, Russian, Japanese, and Korean cultures, how did these émigrés engage with their host societies? Moreover, how did these transcultural interactions manifest in their literary works? Utilizing the concepts of homeland/borderland and secular/natural spaces, this research will leverage recent scholarly insights in spatial humanities to analyze the works of notable Russian émigré writers, including Valerii Pereleshin, Nikolai Baikov, and Nataliia Il'ina, alongside East Asian figures such as Xiao Hong and Ushijima Haruko. Additionally, an examination of early twentieth-century Russian periodicals will help reconstruct the community's habitus. By exploring how Russian émigrés in Manchuria expressed their displacement in the early twentieth century, this project makes visible the pivotal role of literature in enriching Russian émigré discourse in unfamiliar terrain, emphasizing its significance for understanding diaspora, border-crossing, and transnational identities. N/A

titleEnglish: Can Innovation Under Intensive Governance Sustain Frontier Competitiveness? Unpacking the Paradox and Sustainability of China’s Generative AI Development
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Prof Yang, Guobin Prof Yarime, Masaru
abstract: The rapid rise of Chinese generative AI firms—achieving frontier-level capabilities within only five years—presents a paradox for innovation theory. It has unfolded under a coordination-intensive governance regime emphasizing safety and control, seemingly at odds with the conventional expectation that stringent regulation suppresses technological breakthroughs. Whether such governance-embedded innovation represents sustainable adaptation or short-term mobilization that risks institutional lock-in remains unresolved—a question with implications extending beyond China to global debates on governing transformative technologies. This project investigates how Chinese AI developers navigate regulatory imperatives through three organizational mechanisms: anticipatory compliance, efficiency optimization, and institutional entrepreneurship. By examining these mechanisms, the study assesses under what conditions regulatory coordination may function as a catalyst rather than merely a constraint on innovation. To capture this dynamic, it advances a Governance-Innovation Trade-off Model that analyzes how governance enhances technical performance while constraining exploratory capacity, identifying boundary conditions that separate adaptive coordination from institutional rigidity. Employing an integrated multi-method design, the project triangulates evidence across organizational, technical, and institutional levels. Case studies of leading Chinese AI firms use process tracing and elite interviews to reveal how regulatory signals translate into design architectures and management strategies. Computational benchmarking and value auditing of frontier models assess governance-performance trade-offs in safety, accuracy, and adaptability. Publication, patent, and policy analysis trace how governance principles diffuse into standards and market practices, while comparison with EU and U.S. frameworks situates the Chinese case in global perspective. These form a unified analytical pipeline linking governance architectures, organizational behavior, and innovation outcomes. Three questions guide the research: (1) How do firms internalize and operationalize governance requirements in design architectures and strategic decisions? (2) How do these governance-mediated strategies influence innovation direction—channeling development toward incremental optimization or enabling paradigm-shifting breakthroughs? (3) How are governance principles codified into standards and market practices that institutionalize legitimacy and shape long-term innovation pathways? Theoretically, the study conceptualizes regulation as an ambivalent force enabling innovation through coordination while constraining it through conformity pressures. Empirically, it establishes the first systematic, multi-dimensional evidence base examining how coordination-intensive governance shapes innovation outcomes. Rather than prescribing models, it develops assessment frameworks allowing policymakers to evaluate trade-offs between short-term performance and long-term sustainability. By producing open-access comparative datasets and analytical protocols, the project builds transferable methodological infrastructure for examining governance-innovation dynamics across sectors and jurisdictions, advancing scholarly understanding and policy capacity to align governance with sustainable innovation. N/A

titleEnglish: The Function of History and Philosophy of Science in Modern Scientific Societies
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
abstract: In the 20th century the significance of scientific inquiry intensified across all levels of social, political, and intellectual life. After the Second World War especially, governments across the globe drastically augmented their financial support for scientific research. This increased the need to reflect on science as an intellectual and social force in modern society. One important field of inquiry, which arose in the Western world out of this, is History and Philosophy of Science (HPS) - a field aimed at integrating insights from history and philosophy to improve our understanding of what science is and how its results should be implemented. Until now, scholars in HPS have conceived the emergence of their field as a purely intellectual response to the tradition of logical empiricism. Logical empiricists used formal logic to model scientific language which resulted in an understanding of scientific inquiry abstracted from actual experimental and theoretical practice. In the 1960s, scholars like Norwood Hanson and Thomas Kuhn advocated that the history of science would prove the best resource to provide interesting cases to understand science as it is actually performed. Traditionally, their work is understood as the main inspiration for the rise of HPS departments, programs and journals ever since then. The traditional narrative, however, has a problem of scope: it focuses only on the 1960s, on a limited set of actors, and is centered around the American context. This project intends to broaden the historical narrative by looking at both intellectual and institutional developments in the early 1940s and 1950s in the USA and Europe which led to the emergence of HPS as a field of study. The project will also expand available oral recollection about the emergence of HPS by interviewing important scholars from these various regions about their early training and work in this field. This enriched narrative will not only be significant for the history of philosophy, but also aims to refocus attention to the explicitly social motivations, of administrators, philosophers and scientists, to improve how scientific knowledge is received and used in our modern societies. Contemporary work in HPS is faced with challenges to reach beyond its disciplinary confines and have a significance on education and broader culture. This project will reflect on those challenges by recovering past problems earlier scholars and administrators faced and by extending these problems to our contemporary situation. N/A

titleEnglish: Dreamscape: A New Perspective on Narrating History and Reality
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
abstract: This project centers on dreamscape—a core component of surrealist writing—in 21st-century Chinese novels, examining its narrative construction and ideological role in key works: Yan Lianke’s The Lingering Soul, Su Tong’s Good Weather, Mo Yin’s Ritual Paper Figure, Du Li’s Ripple, Hon Lai-chu’s Empty Face. While scholars like Robert Ford Campany (Campany 2020) have conducted systematic studies on dreams and dreaming in early medieval Chinese literature, contemporary Chinese literary research has largely overlooked this vital representational approach. This gap is closely tied to Max Weber’s concept of “disenchantment”: modernity’s emphasis on secularization and rationalization has sidelined dream-focused inquiry, reducing surreal dream worlds to dismissible superstition rather than recognizing their literary value. Dream research itself presents unique challenges, as no single, universally applicable theory exists to unpack its complexity—even Buddhist, Daoist, or Confucian frameworks alone fail to fully explain the intricate relationship between dreams and literature. Yet dreamscape has long been a defining motif: it anchors classical Chinese masterpieces (e.g., Dream of the Red Chamber, The Peony Pavilion, Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio) and modern works like Lu Xun’s Wild Grass, while Western authors such as Jorge Luis Borges, Roberto Bolaño (2666) and Ismail Kadare (The Palace of Dreams) also deploy dreams extensively. This study poses critical questions: How does the dreamscape in 21st-century Chinese novels differ from those of Eastern and Western traditions? Do contemporary writers develop innovative approaches to depicting dreams, and does the inherent unverifiability of dreams grant them a distinct form of “alternative truth”? Furthermore, this project will take into account the representation of the dreamscape in cultural production such as films, web-novels, TV series, and other media. Focusing on dreamscape’s concrete manifestations—including immersive dream realms and spirit journeys shaped by dream logic—the project argues this element engages with, intervenes in, and even rewrites historical narratives and lived reality more powerfully than realist techniques. Methodologically, the study combines close textual analysis of dreamscape representations with cultural studies inquiries into the social-political contexts shaping these narratives. It positions dreamscape as a vital aesthetic innovation for 21st-century Chinese surrealist fiction and media. Research outputs will include peer-reviewed journal articles, presentations at international conferences, and a potential monograph exploring supernatural literary representations. N/A

titleEnglish: Empowering Artistic Creativity: AI-Assisted Creativity Support for Art-Tech Ideation
department: Division of Arts and Machine Creativity
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
coInvestigators: Dr Lam, Miu Ling
abstract: Art-tech, the convergence of art and technology, harnesses technological advancements to create, enhance, and inspire artistic expression. Recognizing its potential, Hong Kong's 2020 Policy Address identified art-tech as a strategic priority for driving creative innovation. Yet artists often face significant obstacles when engaging with emerging technologies, due to technical complexity, fragmented resources, and communication gaps between artistic and scientific communities. Although generative artificial intelligence (AI), particularly large language models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, holds great promise for enhancing artistic ideation, its creative potential and cultural implications within art-tech practice remain largely unexamined.
