To address the existing gaps in knowledge, this project will conduct case-based analyses of IP screen fictions such as Andor, Doctor Who: 60th Anniversary Specials, and Alien: Earth, which have not been officially released in China. The study investigates how audience transcreators on Bilibili retell IP screen fictions through remix and danmu and explores the theoretical implications of such user-driven transcreation for AVT research. Methodologically, the project will involve collecting video and textual data and interviewing Bilibili’s users and content creators for computational cultural analytics on sustained contextual engagement. Dissemination through scholarly publications and conference presentations will highlight the significance of remix+danmu as emergent transcreative media in Chinese digital production, offering new insights into what constitutes audiovisual translation in a networked franchise era. N/A

titleEnglish: Ann Hui at the End of Hong Kong Cinema
abstract: Ann Hui’s career spans the last four decades of Hong Kong cinema. She entered filmmaking in 1979 as part of a young generation of directors who came to be collectively known as the Hong Kong New Wave. They ushered in an extraordinary chapter in Hong Kong film culture, one in which commercial and critical success went hand in hand, and experimental approaches meshed fluidly with popular appeal. Of the very small number of women who have made their names as directors in Hong Kong’s mainstream film industry, Hui has achieved the most enduring success, with over thirty films to date. These have earned plaudits at prestigious international film festivals as well as praise at home, leading one critic to describe her as “the most Hong Kong of Hong Kong directors.” But despite her status and significant body of work, there is a dearth of scholarship on Hui. Not a single book-length study has been published on her in English (with the exception of one monograph focusing on her film Song of the Exile). I seek support for the completion of the first English-language academic monograph dedicated to Ann Hui. I plan to publish this project as part of the Contemporary Film Directors series from University of Illinois Press, a book series that is unique in its long-running commitment to the format of the single-director study, while also prioritizing inventive methods that challenge conventional ideas of authorship. The need for critical reflection on the construct of authorship is especially pressing in Hui’s case, given her rejection of the label of auteur and its implication of a singular distinct style. “I have not been innovative in terms of film language or technique,” she insists, and “I adopt different styles for different films.” Nonetheless, to look closely at Hui’s body of work is to discover a deep and abiding fascination with authorship – with literary sources, as in her adaptations of stories by the modern fiction writer Eileen Chang; with the lives of writers, which have inspired her documentaries and fictional biopics; or with subjective point-of-view as a starting point for creative approaches to film form. Thus my book engages with authorship as a conceptual lens into Hui’s filmography. N/A

titleEnglish: Innovate to collaborate: A design-based research to develop a GenAI-enabled interprofessional education programme for future healthcare professionals
department: Bau Institute of Medical and Health Sciences Education
coInvestigators: Prof Gašević, Dragan Prof King, Ronnel Bornasal Dr Ng, Davy Tsz Kit Prof Orlu, Mine Prof Whitehead, Cynthia Dr Xyrichis, Andreas
abstract: Communication and collaboration failures contribute to 60-70% of preventable adverse events in healthcare globally, with Hong Kong's Hospital Authority documenting similar patterns linking communication deficits to sentinel events. While interprofessional education (IPE) has demonstrated effectiveness in developing collaborative skills, the healthcare sector’s accelerating integration of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) introduces unprecedented educational challenges. Existing GenAI applications in healthcare education emphasize individualized learning rather than team-based collaborative competencies, creating a disconnect between traditional IPE training and the AI-mediated teamwork environments students will encounter in clinical practice. Addressing this disconnect, the sequential two-phased project pursues four aims: (1) iteratively develop a GenAI-enabled IPE model, (2) implement its optimized version, (3) examine underlying mechanisms in Hong Kong and UK contexts, and (4) examine generalizability across educational contexts. Phase 1 implements design-based research incorporating quasi-experimental comparisons across three iterations using distinct clinical scenarios (Dementia, Depression, Cancer). Each iteration employs an independent cohort of 160 healthcare students from five disciplines, forming 10 interprofessional teams, randomly assigned to conditions comparing near-peer facilitation enhanced with ChatGPT (8 teams) against near-peer facilitation alone (8 teams; N=480 total across Phase 1). Mixed-methods evidence—combining pre-post competency measurements with stakeholder focus groups and interviews—drives systematic refinements between cycles, generating empirically-grounded design principles for GenAI integration. Phase 2 employs structural equation modeling with independent Hong Kong and UK cohorts (n=430 per location; 860 total) experiencing the refined intervention. Mediation analyses investigate whether AI tool usability, behavioral engagement, and emotional engagement transmit intervention effects on collaborative competencies. Moderation analyses examine AI literacy as a contextual factor influencing student responsiveness. Conducting parallel analyses across both sites examines whether findings generalize across different educational contexts. The research yields implementable frameworks for integrating GenAI into interprofessional training, simultaneously strengthening current team-based competencies while developing AI collaboration capabilities essential for technology-integrated healthcare environments. Beyond healthcare education, these design principles inform human-AI collaboration training in high-stakes team environments where effective coordination between professionals and AI systems is increasingly critical. N/A

titleEnglish: Can Artificial Intelligence Help Debt Mediation? A Field Experiment
abstract: Can artificial intelligence (AI) improve legal practice? AI has already generated significant research interest in the field of law. However, few studies have examined whether AI assistance actually impacts legal practice in real-world settings, or through what mechanisms such impact occurs. This project addresses these questions in the context of debt mediation, using an experimental design embedded in actual practice. Specifically, I will partner with a mediation agency in China to develop and deploy a machine learning algorithm that predicts the likelihood of successful settlement for each mediation case. In half of the cases, randomly assigned, mediators will receive the AI-predicted probability of mediation success. To prepare for this project, I have already built an algorithm using real data from the agency, and it has demonstrated strong predictive power. This study aims to answer two main questions: 1. Does AI assistance improve mediation outcomes? Specifically, do cases assisted by AI achieve higher settlement rates and recovery amounts compared to cases without AI input? 2. What mechanisms drive any observed improvements? Predictive AI could influence mediators in several ways. I will test and measure the relative importance of the following potential mechanisms: i) Effect on Mediation Strategy and Efficiency: With AI predictions, mediators are better informed about each case's prospects and can tailor their strategies accordingly. Efficiency of mediation may also be improved. I will examine mediation strategies and efficiency at scale using novel measurements enabled by advanced language models. ii) Nudging Effect: AI predictions may prompt mediators to increase their efforts. iii) Better Time Allocation: With AI input, mediators may allocate their time more strategically, focusing more on cases with higher predicted success. This will be measured by assessing whether the impact of AI on call frequency and communication time varies with the AI-predicted probabilities. By way of background, debt collection litigation places a tremendous strain on judicial systems worldwide. Over 25% of civil lawsuits in U.S. state courts involve consumer debt collection, and in China, small loan disputes account for 20–25% of all civil cases. This overwhelming caseload limits judges’ capacity to address each case thoroughly, making alternative dispute resolution methods—such as debt mediation—an essential strategy for judicial relief. Understanding the real-world effects of AI assistance in debt mediation will offer valuable insights for both behavioral theories and legal practice, with broad implications for the adoption of AI in justice systems globally. N/A

titleEnglish: Supporting Academic Vocabulary Outcomes for Students in Hong Kong: An Intervention Study combining Corpus-informed Wordlists, AI-generated Text Sets, and Professional Development on Vocabulary Research, Resources and Pedagogy
coInvestigators: Prof Cheung, Alan C.K.
abstract: Academic vocabulary is fundamental for K-12 students and academic success (Coxhead, 2000; Hiebert, 2020). The HK EdB emphasizes Academic English as critical for across the curriculum; Yet locally and internationally, there is limited research to guide teachers, policymakers, and training on effective pedagogies, especially in multilingual contexts like Hong Kong. While high-quality corpus-derived wordlists exist, few studies have taken them into classrooms, integrated them into research-based pedagogies, and measured student outcomes (Dang & Webb, 2025). Although interventions are not the only form of educational evidence, they are increasingly valued in policy and teacher-training debates, with (quasi-)experimental data often prioritized (Hattie, 2023; Shanahan, 2020). This proposed 24‑month, quasi-experimental project targets Grades 6–8 in five schools, led by an international team. It will deliver a scalable academic vocabulary intervention, trialing AI’s generative capacity to produce texts contextualizing vocabulary from corpus-informed wordlists. A persistent challenge in translating corpus wordlists into practice is the risk of decontextualized instruction (Beck et al., 2013). Embedding target words in authentic contexts is difficult for teachers (Dang & Webb, 2025). This study will address that by generating texts that contextualize vocabulary and build curriculum content knowledge, while teaching core elements such as morphology (Lawrence et al., 2017). The PI’s prior GRF project developed a Hong Kong-specific vocabulary database with rich morphological data, but its classroom impact remains untested, and the current proposal further maximizes that funding's impact. The intervention -GAIN (Gen‑AI for Academic vocabulary Instruction)- comprises four 20‑minute sessions per cycle, 22 cycles over a year (McKeown et al., 2018). Each cycle targets five academic words, embedded in AI‑generated texts to: (1) contextualize learning; (2) develop morphological awareness; (3) build lexical connections; and (4) support fluency. Drawing on current, research-informed pedagogical frameworks and theory, GAIN aims to build students’ ability to acquire words through understanding word parts, connections, context, and background knowledge. Outcomes will be measured using the Vocabulary Knowledge Scale, Academic Vocabulary Test, Woodcock Passage Comprehension, and Metacognitive Vocabulary Learning Questionnaires, with pre‑, post‑, and delayed post‑tests analyzed via latent growth modeling. Qualitative data from interviews and lesson artifacts will undergo content analysis. By applying corpus-based lists through AI‑generated contextualization, this project addresses a critical gap and, if funded, will yield a scalable, low‑cost pedagogy for Hong Kong and internationally. N/A

titleEnglish: The Humility/Confidence Paradox and Education
abstract: Humility and self-confidence seem to have a paradoxical relationship. Humility and self-confidence both appear vital to constructive engagement with others, yet they also seem to be directly opposed. This issue, which I refer to as the ‘humility/confidence paradox’, raises the question of why and how to value humility and/or confidence in ourselves or in others in schools and other settings. Despite a bourgeoning global literature on humility and self-confidence in philosophy, psychology, and education, little agreement is found within or across fields about what they are: Are they virtues, dispositions, attitudes, or beliefs? Many accounts focus on either humility or self-confidence but do not consider how they relate to each other. In addition, many contend that the value of humility and self-confidence is context-dependent. That is, they argue that their appropriateness should be judged based on the context: for example, one’s social role and status, cultural considerations, potential consequences, the extent of one’s knowledge, or one’s capacity for self-evaluation. However, considering such factors makes it difficult, if not impossible, to generalize about what makes these traits good versus when they go too far. These academic disputes ultimately give us no guidance on why or how we should cultivate or promote humility or self-confidence in education and society. This project will develop and defend a novel, practice-oriented account of humility and self-confidence and their value in education and other social settings. Through examining relevant real-world examples, I will argue for a context-independent (or inclusive) conception of humility and confidence after contending with major objections. I further articulate humility and confidence as intertwined dispositions toward personal and social practices rather than as virtues involving belief or approaches to belief. This practice-oriented account uncovers a positive educational and social role for some postures that are usually problematized in Western thought, such as imposter syndrome, false modesty, and strategic ignorance. It also provides constructive responses to cross-cultural dilemmas related to confidence and humility as well as concerns about virtue signalling and grandstanding. The project will result in a scholarly book on the humility/confidence paradox and its significance in schools and other settings. N/A

titleEnglish: Improving fine-resolution impervious surface mapping in high-rise compact coastal cities using multimodal remote sensing and foundation models
coInvestigators: Prof Gamba, Paolo Prof Zhang, Yongjun
abstract: Accurate mapping of fine-resolution urban impervious surfaces (UIS), such as roads, buildings, and port facilities, is critical in environmental monitoring and disaster management for the resilient and sustainable urban development. As one of the world’s most dynamic coastal megacities, the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area (GBA) houses over 86 million people, hosting three of the world’s top ten container ports, and contributes more than one-third of China’s exports. However, mapping reliable UIS in this region remains highly challenging due to its subtropical climate, dense and vertical urban forms, and complex coastal settings. Persistent cloud cover limits optical satellite observations, while shadows from skyscrapers, ports, and compact city centers create spectral confusions from optical data and scattering confusions from synthetic aperture radar (SAR), where similar materials such as concrete, asphalt, and container surfaces exhibit confusing signals. These issues lead to significant uncertainties in UIS estimation using single-source remote sensing technologies. To overcome these limitations, the project aims to develop a novel fine-resolution UIS estimation framework by synergistically integrating multimodal optical and SAR satellite data based on the remote sensing foundation model in coastal cities in GBA. First, we will systematically analyze the spectral confusion mechanisms from optical data and scattering confusion mechanisms from SAR data of typical land covers in high-rise compact coastal cities. Second, we will develop a novel framework that synergistically integrates optical and SAR data by a multimodal foundation model with a complex-valued Transformer network. This framework is designed to exploit the complementary strengths of spectral and scattering features to overcome urban land cover confusions in high-rise compact coastal cities. The expected outcomes of this research include: 1) A formalized spectral-scattering mechanism of comprehensive coastal land cover confusions and its scale effects for UIS mapping in high-rise compact coastal cities; 2) An accurate fine-resolution UIS products in the GBA using innovative and scalable multimodal foundation model. The fine-resolution UIS dataset can further support coastal infrastructure management and sustainable urban planning in the coastal cities of GBA. Beyond the GBA, the advanced foundation model can be transferred to UIS mapping in other coastal cities facing similar challenges. N/A

titleEnglish: A Typology of the Last-Resort Safety Net: A Comparison of Minimum Income Protection between East Asian and Western Societies
department: Department of Social Work and Social Administration
coInvestigators: Prof Aya , Abe Dr Chang, Yu-Ling Dr Kang, Ji Young Prof Ku, Inhoe Dr Marchal , Sarah Dr Natili , Marcello Prof Ng, Irene Y.H. Dr Wang, Julia Shu-Huah Dr Zhao, Xi
abstract: Minimum income protection (MIP) schemes provide the last resort for the poor, serving as a cornerstone of the welfare state. Societies exhibit similarities and differences in their approaches to delivering MIP. Emerging comparative studies classify different types of MIP based on their characteristics to grasp a comprehensive picture of the last-resort safety net. Much of the existing literature is focused on Western countries, leaving the rest of the world largely unexplored and limiting our understanding of diversity in MIP. This project aims to expand the MIP comparison by incorporating East Asian societies. East Asia’s welfare model possesses two unique aspects that can enrich the current comparative study of MIP. Influenced by Confucian values, East Asian societies culturally tend to emphasize family as a source of help. Another feature is its ‘productivism,’ subordinating social concerns to economic considerations when formulating social policies. In the context of MIP, the stringency of familial obligations influences the extent to which family duties should be fulfilled before individuals living in poverty can seek government support. Preference towards productive elements can be demonstrated through the features of Active Labor Market Policies (ALMP). These policies aim to incentivize able-bodied MIP beneficiaries to integrate into the workforce. It is intriguing to explore whether and how the inclusion of East Asia might reshape the typology of MIP. This project aims to answer two research questions: 1) What are the typologies of MIP in East Asian and Western societies? 2) Is there a unique East Asian MIP model that is distinct from Western countries? For example, do East Asian societies tend to be more familistic or adopt a specific type of ALMP? We will use fuzzy set ideal type analysis to compare four dimensions of MIP—coverage, generosity, family obligation, and ALMP—in eleven societies, six from East Asia (Chinese Mainland, Hong Kong SAR, Japan, Singapore, South Korea, and Taiwan) and five Western countries (Denmark, Germany, Italy, the UK, and the US). The four dimensions lead to sixteen combinations of ideal types, which are further clustered into eight theoretically relevant types of MIP. This project advances the conceptualization and development of comparative MIP studies by expanding the scope (e.g., including East Asian societies), dimensions (e.g., family obligation), and timeframe of comparison (e.g., data in 2026). An enhanced typology study lays the foundation for future studies to examine MIP’s shaping forces and effects. N/A

titleEnglish: Circadian Rhythms in Human Milk: Pathways Linking Maternal Wellbeing and Infant Sleep
coInvestigators: Dr Hui, Amelia P.W. Ms Lin, Yan Prof Liu, Jianghong Prof Lok, Kris Yuet Wan
abstract: Sleep problems affect 20%–30% of infants worldwide and often persist into later childhood, contributing to emotional, behavioral, and cognitive difficulties. Maternal mental health and sleep quality are key determinants of infant sleep, yet the biological mechanisms underlying these associations remain unclear. This study investigates human milk melatonin as a potential biological pathway linking maternal wellbeing to infant sleep regulation. Human milk contains hormones that follow daily rhythms, with melatonin peaking at night and absent during the day. Because infants cannot produce melatonin in early life, they rely on external cues to support circadian development and sleep consolidation. Emerging evidence suggests that maternal stress and sleep disruption can alter hormonal profiles, raising the possibility that maternal distress disrupts milk melatonin rhythms. Critically, approximately 17% of mothers experience postpartum depressive symptoms, and 35%–75% report poor sleep. Given the co-occurrence of maternal mental health problems and poor sleep—both of which can alter milk composition—downstream effects on infant sleep are plausible. Modern feeding practices, such as expressing milk during the day and feeding it at night, may compound these disruptions by misaligning milk intake with infants' circadian needs. Our preliminary data indicate that maternal mental health and sleep problems predict subsequent child sleep difficulties, but whether disrupted milk melatonin rhythms mediate this relationship remains unknown. We propose a longitudinal study of 360 mother–infant dyads from late pregnancy through three months postpartum to examine the interplay among maternal depressive symptoms, maternal sleep quality, milk melatonin rhythmicity, and infant sleep outcomes. Using advanced longitudinal modeling, we will explore infant sleep development trajectory, test whether human milk melatonin alteration mediate the relationship between maternal wellbeing (depression and poor sleep) and infant sleep problems. Reciprocal effects of infant sleep disturbances on maternal wellbeing will also be examined to clarify bidirectional processes. This project innovatively integrates psychological, behavioral, and biological factors within a unified framework, emphasizing chrono-nutrition in breastfeeding. Findings will provide the first comprehensive evidence on biological pathways linking maternal mental health, milk composition, and infant sleep. Results will inform evidence-based guidelines for milk expression and feeding timing and support early identification of high-risk dyads. By shifting from symptom management to pathway-based prevention, this research has potential to enhance maternal recovery, infant development, and family wellbeing while informing public health strategies. N/A

titleEnglish: The interplay between teacher input and student listening strategies: An exploratory study of implicit strategy instruction in EMI science classrooms
coInvestigators: Dr Chung, Hiu Yui Edsoulla Prof Lo, Yuen Yi Prof Macaro, Ernesto
abstract: In English Medium Instruction (EMI) programmes, students learn content subjects (e.g. science) through English as their second or foreign language. Previous studies have found teacher talk to dominate in EMI classrooms, with students experiencing difficulties in comprehending teacher input. It is therefore essential to investigate how students can learn to listen using a range of listening strategies. For example, students can be taught how to infer the meaning of unfamiliar words, recall their prior knowledge, and monitor their understanding. However, EMI teachers are usually content subject experts, not language experts. It is thus unreasonable to assume that they would know how to explicitly teach students strategies. Situated in the state-of-the-art theory that students’ strategy use can be regulated or prompted by the teacher, this study proposes a novel perspective that teacher input can be regarded as implicit strategy instruction. For example, a teacher can break down a complicated word (e.g. biodiversity = bio+diversity) to prompt students in making inferences. The study will be divided into three phases. Phase I will involve the development of an intervention programme and a pilot study on two experienced EMI teachers and their students. Phase II will be a quasi-experimental study in two schools. In each school, following lesson observations, stimulated recall interviews with selected students, a pre-intervention questionnaire and a science quiz to establish a baseline, four classes of students (n=30 each) will participate in the programme. For Class 1, the PI will engage the school teachers in dialogic reflection and co-planning of a second unit of lessons, highlighting how the teacher can provide implicit strategy instruction through strategy-prompting input; Class 2 will receive explicit strategy instruction and take the second unit of lessons; Class 3 will receive both explicit and implicit strategy instruction; and Class 4 will serve as a control group, taking the unit of lessons in the usual way without any strategy instruction. All classes will complete a post-intervention strategy questionnaire and science quiz, and four students in each class will take part in stimulated recall interviews to measure the effectiveness of strategy instruction. Lesson videos will also be analysed to examine how teachers have changed their input provision. In Phase III, the findings will be evaluated to theorise the usefulness of implicit strategy instruction. The study will have important pedagogical implications regarding how EMI teachers can provide implicit strategy instruction, thereby guiding students to become more strategic learners. N/A

titleEnglish: Let’s Reverse the Expertise: Towards a Bottom-Up Approach to Designing a Program Evaluation Tool of Technology Ethics Education
coInvestigators: Dr Datu, Jesus Alfonso Daep Prof HUANG, LINUS Prof Jackson, Liz
abstract: How Computer Science (CS) professionals design technologies can gravely impact our individual and collective well-being. Algorithmic bias in hiring exacerbates employment inequities, while AI chatbots without fact-checking measures can lead to “AI hallucinations.” In response to these “tech-ethics” problems, academics and industry professionals call for teaching ethics to undergraduate and graduate students in Computer Science programs (e.g., Master of Science in Artificial Intelligence) so that they can integrate ethical considerations into technology design. Tech-ethics education, however, is complicated. Scholars disagree on its goals, content (what should be taught), and how to evaluate its effectiveness. Importantly, the goals, content, and evaluation should be interlinked, as evaluation instruments, such as the Socially Responsible Computing Readiness (SRCR) Scale, must be designed and aligned with the goals of tech-ethics education. The present project contributes to this scholarship by examining the SRCR Scale, a program evaluation instrument (in progress), drawing insights from individuals with industry experience. The project’s core innovation lies in expertise reversal: While the scholarship positions tech-ethics philosophers and researchers as educational experts (“top-down” approach), the present project challenges this assumption and treats those with industry experiences as experts of tech-ethics education (“bottom-up” approach). The project asks: What capacities – knowledge, skills, and dispositions – are needed to navigate tech-ethics problems in practice? What are the real-world ethical dilemmas that students need to learn about before entering the industry? If tech-ethics education is about preparing CS professionals who can engage in ethical technology design in the real world, tech-ethics education and its evaluation tool must be informed by such “on-the-ground” perspectives. The study is as follows: First, the PI plans to interview individuals with industry experience on what it takes to address ethical problems that arise from developing technologies. The PI will utilize the interview data to revise the SRCR Scale. Second, the project will disseminate a survey to examine the content validity of the revised SRCR Scale. Third, leveraging the interview data, the PI plans to design pilot Normative Case Studies (NCS), which present fictional stories that mirror real-world ethical problems faced by technology professionals, to produce a scenario-based tech-ethics reasoning skills test. These pilot NCS would lay the groundwork to examine the convergent validity of the SRCR Scale. N/A

titleEnglish: New Beginnings: Gay Men, Fatherhood, and Doing Family in Hong Kong
abstract: This research examines pathways and barriers to parenthood for gay fathers in Hong Kong. There has been a generational shift from gay men becoming parents through heterosexual marriages to gay parenthood through adoption, foster care, co-parenting with (often) lesbians, or surrogacy (altruistic/commercial; traditional/gestational). With access to adoption and foster care difficult for gay men, and co-parenting or altruistic surrogacy often complicated by custody issues, commercial gestational surrogacy has become the predominant pathway to fatherhood, despite costs and varying legality. Studies of pathways to parenthood for lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, and other sexual minorities (LGBTQ+) have primarily focused on lesbian mothers, though literature on gay fathers is growing. Despite increases in lesbians and gay men seeking alternative family formations in Hong Kong, few studies explore these journeys. This research aims to address this gap as one of the first sociological studies of gay fathers’ pathways to parenthood in Hong Kong, examining the process from initial planning to childcare, focusing on (1) the ‘becoming father’ transformative process by which gay men develop ‘procreative consciousnesses’ and actualise fatherhood; (2) family dynamics between gay co-fathers and natal family members, as well as interactions with wider social circles; and, when applicable, (3) the process of commercial surrogacy, particularly the labour process between gay fathers and female labourers (e.g., egg donors, surrogates). Situated in the sexual citizenship and doing family literatures and the queer reproductive justice framework, this research uses in-depth interviews with gay fathers (n=30) and other stakeholders (n=40) to engage with three debates: (1) the extent to which gay fatherhood represents a new definition of sexual citizenship and challenges traditional associations between homosexuality/childlessness, masculinity/femininity (the identity/citizenship debate); (2) the extent to which gay men’s pursuit of parenthood challenges or reinforces heteronormativity and patriarchy, as many practices are modelled on heterosexist nuclear family, contradicting queer ideals that disconnect intimacy from blood ties or reject reproduction (the queer family debate); (3) the extent to which pathways to gay fatherhood adhere to economic justice principles, particularly in commercial surrogacy where relationships may be equal or exploitative, given its class-specific and transnational nature (the labour debate). Contributing to the sociology of gender/sexuality, family, and labour, this research offers social and policy implications regarding sexual diversity, family equality, and social inclusion, with beneficiaries including LGBTQ+ individuals, families, and communities; the social welfare and education sectors; government; and the general public. N/A

titleEnglish: Post‑traumatic stress disorder across the lifespan in Hong Kong: incidence, multimorbidity, service utilisation, and mortality in a 24‑year population‑wide electronic health records cohort
coInvestigators: Dr Chan, Kwun Nam Joe Prof Chang, Wing Chung Dr Ni, Michael Yuxuan Prof Sham, Pak-Chung
abstract: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a mental disorder that can arise after experiencing or witnessing traumatic events and is associated with substantial distress, poorer physical health, and reduced life expectancy. In Hong Kong, repeated population-level stressors—such as social unrest and epidemics—suggest a greater and more complex PTSD burden than previously recognised. Yet most existing research focuses on specific groups, provides only snapshots in time, or uses designs poorly suited to tracing long-term trajectories. There is limited information on age- and period-specific incidence, on when and how physical and mental disorders co-occur, and on how PTSD shapes healthcare use and the risk of serious outcomes such as self-harm, suicide, and death. This study addresses these gaps by analysing territory-wide, real-time electronic health records spanning 24 years (2002–2025). We will estimate the incidence of newly diagnosed PTSD overall, by age group, and across periods, profile clinically defined multimorbidity of physical and mental disorders across the lifespan, and examine diagnostic conversion from incident PTSD to severe mental disorders such as schizophrenia or bipolar disorder. We will also evaluate mental health service utilisation (out-patient clinic visits, emergency attendances, hospital admissions, and use of allied health services), risks of adverse events (self-harm and suicide attempts), and short- and long-term mortality (all-cause and cause-specific). All individuals aged 16 years or older who attended specialist out-patient clinics, general out-patient clinics, accident and emergency departments, or were admitted to hospitals between 1 January 2002 and 31 December 2025 will be eligible. PTSD, mental, and physical disorders will be identified using International Classification of Diseases codes. The first recorded PTSD code will define the person’s first-ever (incident) diagnosis. To clarify PTSD-specific risks, we will use a matched, population-based cohort design with two comparators: age- and sex-matched individuals from the general population without recorded mental disorders, and people with first-diagnosed anxiety disorders other than PTSD. The study will deliver the first territory-wide estimates of PTSD incidence in Hong Kong over 24 years, including age- and period-specific trends during social unrest and epidemics, and a comprehensive profile of physical–mental multimorbidity, service utilisation, adverse outcomes, and mortality. The findings will provide a real-world view of clinically diagnosed PTSD, identify high-risk periods and subgroups across the lifespan, and inform targeted prevention, integrated care pathways, surge planning, and resource allocation to improve continuity of care, thereby strengthening population mental health in Hong Kong. N/A

titleEnglish: Balancing Geopolitics and Economic Development: How are U.S. states adjusting their China policy in the age of great power rivalry and what are the implications for Hong Kong S.A.R.?
abstract: The proposed study seeks to enrich the existing literature on sub-national diplomacy, generally referred to in academic literature as “the involvement of the constituent units (regions) of national states in international affairs”, and to provide practical advice for the Hong Kong S.A.R. government in promoting regional and national interest. Studying sub-national policies targeting China in the state governments of the U.S. over the past decade, this research project compares sub-national diplomacy in the age of globalization and in the present era of geopolitical rivalry, revealing the evolution of policy motivations, actors, and mechanisms. The findings will also offer policy-relevant guidance for the Hong Kong S.A.R.’s external engagement in an era of heightened geopolitical complexity. According to existing literature, sub-national diplomacy emerged alongside the development of globalization. Domestic decentralization and international liberalization of economies around the turn of the 21st century led to the emergence of sub-national actors in international affair. In today’s world, geopolitical tensions have reshaped the global order and the pretext for the previous pattern of sub-national diplomacy has significantly subsided. Provinces, regions, and cities increasingly find themselves drawn into the “whole government approach” in great power competition. While previous studies have documented the U.S. and European countries’ policy shifts towards China on the sub-national level, the policy mechanisms of how these shifts took place remains to be explored. Without a clear understanding of the key actors in the political systems and how they transpired to push for policy change, the policy shifts can easily be attributed to overall worsening of bilateral relationships between China and the West with little insight. Furthermore, in a federalist political system such as the U.S., studying the interplay between state governments and the federal government on foreign relations could reveal novel understanding of the policy diffusion dynamics. The intergovernmental policy diffusion framework could also be applied to other important fields such as AI regulation or climate governance, contributing to the understanding of American public policy process in today’s polarized political climate. N/A

titleEnglish: Effects of exercise snacks on improving cognitive performance in older adults with mild cognitive impairment: A pragmatic randomized controlled trial
coInvestigators: Dr Cheng, Calvin Pak-Wing Dr Fong, Daniel Yee-Tak Prof Gibala, Martin Prof Sit, Cindy Hui-ping
abstract: Cognitive decline is a growing public health concern. Mild cognitive impairment (MCI) is the pre-clinical stage preceding early dementia, characterized by deficits in one or multiple cognitive domains beyond normal aging. MCI is the critical time frame for early intervention to prevent deterioration into dementia. Exercise are widely endorsed to promote healthy aging and reduce cognitive decline risk, yet specific exercise recommendations for MCI remain undefined. Recent epidemiological studies reveal a dose–response benefit for vigorous-intensity, but not moderate-intensity, physical activity, linking it to reduced Alzheimer’s disease-related mortality, particularly when undertaken in short bursts of 1–2 minutes. These studies also suggest optimal risk reduction at the amounts of vigorous physical activity recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO). The concept of exercise snacks—brief, purposeful bouts of vigorous exercise spread across the day—is emerging as a promising way to deliver the WHO physical activity recommendations, because it offers flexibility, which mitigates time and logistical barriers, and enhances convenience and adherence by embedding exercise into everyday settings. Nonetheless, the effectiveness of exercise snacks for combating cognitive decline is unexplored, especially in real-world settings. The proposed project will directly compare exercise snacks and community-based exercise to address the question: Do exercise snacks induce equivalent cognitive benefits relative to community-based exercise at a matched weekly exercise duration, and can both interventions improve cognitive performance compared to usual care in adults with MCI? We will conduct a robust three-group pragmatic randomized controlled trial with a pretest–posttest design. Older adults with MCI will be randomly allocated to exercise snacks group, community-based exercise group, or usual care control group. Each intervention group will receive a 12-month exercise intervention accumulating 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity exercise weekly, either via exercise snacks (five bouts of ~2 minutes daily) or supervised group exercise (three 25-minute sessions weekly), whereas the usual care group will receive a health education session. Outcome measures, including cognitive performance measured using the Montreal Cognitive Assessment, mental health, cardiorespiratory fitness, intervention adherence, health-related quality of life, medication usage, adverse events, and habitual physical activity, will be examined by assessors blinded to the group allocation at baseline, 12 months (postintervention), and 24 months (12-month postintervention follow-up). Data will be analyzed using generalized estimating equations. Our findings are expected to refine the exercise guidelines for older adults with cognitive impairment and inform the optimal exercise approach for MCI management. N/A

titleEnglish: Regulating Donation-Based Crowdfunding Platforms in China: Challenges and Future Directions
abstract: Donation-based crowdfunding platforms have become a major force in Chinese philanthropy, raising over RMB100 billion (approximately USD14 billion) and benefitting millions since 2014. These platforms fall into two categories: charitable donation-based crowdfunding platforms (CDCPs), which support public interest projects, and non-charitable donation-based crowdfunding platforms (NDCPs), which provide assistance to individuals facing financial hardship. However, the rapid growth of the platforms has outpaced regulatory development, resulting in operational inefficiencies, fund mismanagement, and declining public trust. This research will examine how China’s unique social, economic, and political context shapes the regulation of CDCPs and NDCPs across three dimensions: legal, administrative, and contractual. The legal dimension investigates the legislative framework, particularly the Charity Law (2016, amended 2023), to assess how these platforms are defined and regulated, the allocation of responsibilities among operators, donors, and beneficiaries, and how key issues such as transparency, accountability, and liability are addressed. The administrative dimension focuses on the role of regulators, analysing how laws are interpreted and enforced, the balance between fostering innovation and ensuring oversight, and the influence of non-legal factors such as political priorities and public opinion on regulatory practice. The contractual dimension explores agreements between donors, platform operators, and beneficiaries, with particular attention to risk allocation, dispute resolution, and the use of standardised contracts to protect stakeholders and promote fairness. To address the three dimensions, the project will adopt a mixed-methods approach, combining doctrinal legal analysis, comparative study, and semi-structured qualitative interviews. Doctrinal analysis will map the current legal landscape and identify regulatory gaps, while the comparative study will examine regulatory practices in the United States, Hong Kong, Australia, and England to identify lessons and best practices for China. Interviews with platform operators, regulators, donors, and beneficiaries will provide empirical insights on operational challenges and opportunities. The proposed research promises both theoretical and practical contributions. Theoretically, it will contribute to the discourse on the role of law in governing digital philanthropy, offering a nuanced understanding of how legal principles such as transparency and accountability apply to online crowdfunding platforms. Practically, it will deliver evidence-based recommendations to strengthen regulatory effectiveness, public trust, and the protection of all stakeholders in China’s evolving crowdfunding sector. N/A

titleEnglish: The 'unfinished housing' crisis in China: Development failures, homebuyer navigation and state responses
department: Department of Real Estate and Construction
coInvestigators: Prof Chen, Jie Dr Waldron, Richard
abstract: China's unfinished residential development (URD) crisis represents a fundamental breakdown in state-led urbanisation, with 231 million square metres of stalled construction affecting 7.85 million households since 2021. Comparable Western housing crises have been arguably spurred by state-encouraged property development via financial deregulation and speculative incentives. Conversely, China's unprecedented URD crisis emerged from contradictions within its state-led development model, where growth-oriented planning strategies, land revenue dependence and pre-sale systems have created conditions for systematic overbuilding, predominantly in non-first-tier cities. The crisis reveals how state institutions can actively foster – rather than merely regulate – development failures and risks, with state-led strategies simultaneously pursuing housing development and creating the conditions for crisis. Under China's institutional model, local governments are incentivised to pursue aggressive land development, while developers adopt highly-leveraged practices that transfer construction risks to homebuyers via pre-sales. When official debt-control policies triggered widespread developer defaults in 2020-2021, accumulated risks materialised as URDs, exposing millions of homebuyers to housing precarity – where individuals service mortgages on uninhabitable and unsalable properties while securing alternative housing, prompting homebuyer and state responses to manage the crisis. This project examines China's URD crisis through three theoretical lenses within the political economy of housing and urban statecraft: institutional facilitation of capital accumulation and crisis generation; homebuyer navigation of compounding housing precarity; and state recalibration of governance frameworks in response to crisis conditions. Three research questions guide this inquiry: 1) How have state-led urbanisation strategies and institutional arrangements facilitated excessive housing development and generated URDs? 2) How do affected homebuyers navigate and respond to extreme housing precarity? 3) How have state actors responded to the URD crisis through their evolving roles, strategies and governance approaches? Using a mixed-methods approach across three case study cities, the study combines document and policy analysis, stakeholder interviews, focus groups, homebuyer interviews with photo elicitation and social media content analysis. Collaborating with three local planning institutes, it will identify common structural dynamics underlying URD formation, reveal how crisis manifestations vary across different urban contexts and examine homebuyer and state responses to URDs. The study makes three theoretical contributions: extending URD studies beyond post-2008 Western contexts to state-led development models; conceptualising compounding housing precarity as the intersection of financial burden, uninhabitability and livelihood disruption; and theorising statecraft adaptation under crisis conditions. Findings will inform institutional innovations in sustainable housing production and homebuyer protection, with transferable insights for other state-led urbanisation contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Cardinality versus ordinality: exploring their relations and their unique contributions to arithmetic through a randomized controlled trial
abstract: Children’s mathematical competence predicts important life outcomes, it is thus important to identify the cognitive factors that underlies successful mathematical problem-solving. Cardinality and ordinality are the two major themes in recent decades. While the former is about the understanding about the relations between number symbols and their underlying quantity representation (e.g., 5 = ooooo), the latter is about the understanding of the relations among number symbols (e.g., 5 goes between 4 and 6). These two aspects of numerical processing are usually assessed using number comparison (NC, which involves deciding which of the two numbers is larger) and number ordering (NO, which involves deciding if triplets of numbers are in ascending order) tasks respectively. While performance in NC has been shown to be robustly linked to mathematics achievement (Schneider et al., 2017), recent studies suggest that NO performance may be even more strongly related to children’s mathematics achievement, accounting almost half of the variance in arithmetic (Lyons & Beilock, 2011). However, the NO task can be solved by multiple strategies, such as reciting the count list or comparing the numbers. There have been conflicting findings on which aspects of NO performance drives its relation with arithmetic competence (e.g., Goffin & Ansari, 2016; Orrantia et al., 2019). The current proposed study aims to address this issue using a randomized controlled trial. First to third graders (n = 300) will be randomly assigned to one of the three experimental conditions: the NC condition, the NO condition, and a literacy condition as an active control. Participants in each condition will engage in an educational intervention targeting the relevant construct, and their improvement in numerical skills after the intervention will be assessed. By examining whether the intervention effects are generalized to other numerical domains (e.g., NO intervention results in improvement in NC and arithmetic), it is possible to examine if there are shared mechanisms underlying ordinal versus cardinal processing and to identify the active ingredient of the intervention that brings about improvement in arithmetic performance. The findings have huge implications on the mathematical cognition literature, which has been focusing on developing cardinality interventions. If ordinal processing is indeed more causally relevant to mathematical competence, which makes theoretical sense given that advanced mathematics is increasingly reliant on the understanding of the interrelations among numbers, the findings will drive a shift in the focus on mathematical cognition literature from cardinality to ordinality. N/A

titleEnglish: Free or Fee? Understanding Public Preferences for Municipal Solid Waste Policy in Hong Kong
department: Department of Politics and Public Administration
abstract: Hong Kong faces a mounting municipal solid waste crisis: waste loads remain high, recycling rates low, and landfills are nearing capacity. The Government has set ambitious targets and introduced the Municipal Solid Waste Charging Scheme as a central strategy. Yet, the scheme was suspended in 2024 following poor public acceptance during pilot trials. This setback highlights a fundamental challenge: effective waste management policies cannot succeed without broad public support. Existing research shows that Hong Kong residents generally support waste reduction in principle, but often resist policies once personal costs become clear. Studies have identified factors such as fairness and effectiveness as important, yet most focus on single instruments in isolation and rely on correlational evidence. Little is known about how citizens evaluate policy packages—combinations of charging, recycling, producer responsibility, infrastructure, and education—or how specific design features (e.g., charge levels and revenue use) shape perceptions and acceptance. Nor has prior work systematically tested whether targeted information can shift public reasoning and support. This project addresses these gaps through a large-scale survey experiment with 4,000 Hong Kong residents, complemented by focus group interviews. The core method is a conjoint experiment, which presents respondents with paired, randomly generated policy packages that vary across eight attributes. Respondents will choose between packages, rate overall support, and evaluate each package on effectiveness, fairness, personal costs, and business impacts. This design allows causal identification of how specific policy design features influence both perceptions and support. In addition, prior to the conjoint tasks, respondents will be randomly assigned to receive information about (1) the severity of Hong Kong’s waste problem, (2) how different policies work, (3) both, or (4) neither. This enables rigorous assessment of how information changes perceptions and acceptance. The study makes three contributions. First, it advances knowledge by analyzing policy interdependence: how acceptance of one instrument depends on the presence of others. Second, it links perceptions to preferences, showing the mechanisms through which design features gain or lose support. Third, it provides causal evidence on how policy design and communication strategies can be directly leveraged to improve acceptance. The findings will generate actionable insights for policymakers designing Hong Kong’s waste policy and NGOs conducting advocacy and outreach. More broadly, this project contributes to international debates on how to design politically feasible and socially legitimate environmental policies in contexts where public resistance has stalled reform. N/A

titleEnglish: Post-discharge cessation support for smoking patients using a large language model (LLM)-based counselling chatbot: a randomised controlled trial
coInvestigators: Dr Chan, Nyai Yin Dr Cheung, Yee Tak Derek Mrs KWOK, Sheung Yee Prof Lam, David Chi Leung Dr Tseng, Cee Zhung Steven Miss Wu, Yilan Mr Xie, Xiaoyun Dr Zeng, Yingpei
abstract: Hospitalisation provides a teachable moment for smoking cessation (SC), yet post-discharge relapse rates remain high despite clinical guidelines recommending cessation support. To address this challenge, we propose enhancing standard brief face-to-face counselling with a large language model (LLM)-based chatbot delivered via mobile health instant messaging to provide scalable, personalised behavioural support. While our previous randomised controlled trial showed that nurse-led mHealth support significantly improved cessation outcomes, such human-dependent interventions face scalability constraints. High-quality evidence on advanced LLM-based chatbots for post-discharge patients remains limited. This study builds upon our successful nurse-led mHealth trial for this population and our ongoing studies of LLM-based SC chatbots for youth smokers and community smokers. Smoking patients (N = 790) will be recruited from inpatient and follow-up services and individually randomised into Intervention (n = 395) or Control group (n = 395). Both groups will receive the same baseline face-to-face interventions: brief 5As/5Rs advice, a health-warning leaflet, a SC services referral card and a self-help e-booklet. The intervention group will additionally receive 12 weeks of personalised support via an LLM-powered chatbot, grounded in the Capability-Opportunity-Motivation Behaviour (COM-B) model and employing evidence-based Behaviour Change Techniques (BCTs). The chatbot will deliver tailored support based on smoking patterns and health conditions, using 5As dialogues for quit-ready users and 5Rs with Motivational Interviewing (MI) for ambivalent users, delivered through a Love and Care communication approach. Both groups, if they opt in, will be referred to SC services at 3- and 6-month follow-ups. The control group will receive regular messages for follow-up reminders for attention control. The primary outcome is biochemically validated tobacco abstinence at 12 months. Secondary outcomes at 6 months include biochemically validated abstinence, self-reported 7-day point prevalence abstinence, smoking reduction (≥50% from baseline), quit attempts (≥24 hours of abstinence), and cessation service use. We will conduct qualitative interviews, compliance analysis (complier-average causal effect), mediation analysis, and economic evaluations to comprehensively assess the efficacy, mechanisms, and cost-effectiveness of the intervention. Conversation logs will be analysed to identify engagement trajectories and active components with the BCTs taxonomy, and the effects on outcomes. Outcome assessors and data analysts will be blinded to group allocation, and the trial will adhere to CONSORT-EHEALTH guidelines. This study will establish an evidence-based, scalable mHealth behavioural change model and guide the implementation of AI-driven remote cessation support, directly addressing the cost and manpower limitations of human-led interventions while maintaining intervention efficacy. N/A

titleEnglish: Human-AI Relationships: combined cross-sectional and prospective longitudinal examination of the affective and neural effects of AI companionship
coInvestigators: Dr Kong, Lingpeng Prof Potenza, Marc Prof YU, Rongjun
abstract: Over 700 million people now interact weekly with artificial intelligence chatbots, and nearly 40% of young Chinese adults use AI companions daily for emotional support and companionship. The industry develops AI companions that are increasingly personalized and empathetic, designed to recognize and respond to human emotions. While this offers tremendous opportunities for mental health support, debates about problematic attachment, emotional dependence, and adverse outcomes - including documented suicide cases - are increasing. Despite widespread adoption, we lack fundamental understanding of how AI companions affect the human brain and behavior. Decades of research have established the evolutionary behavioral and neural processes underlying human social interaction, including affiliation formation, problematic dependence, and social support's positive effects on mental health and emotion regulation. However, it remains completely unclear whether AI companions engage or even alter these same processes. This research will conduct the first systematic examination of AI companion use on behavioral and neural processes underlying attachment/emotional dependence and social support-facilitated emotion regulation. We will compare long-term daily users of personalized AI companions with AI-naive individuals, and next examine changes in naive users before and after six weeks of daily AI companion interaction. Participants will undergo advanced brain imaging using functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) during two conditions: (1) natural interaction with their AI companion during positive exchanges, and (2) receiving real-time social support from the AI companion while regulating negative emotions. Together with the implementation of advanced machine learning based brain analyses and careful control conditions, this will enable first "near-real world" examination of brain circuits involved in affiliative human-AI communication and social support, while determining long- and short-term changes in brain systems mediating attachment and dependence and social support and emotion regulation. Daily-life effects including attachment, emotional dependence, social support, and stress will be assessed using AI-adapted validated questionnaires. The design will explicitly explore both negative and positive impacts, informing evidence-based safety guidelines for AI companion design and regulations to safeguard public health while promoting therapeutic benefits. The project will establish Hong Kong as a leader in the neuroscientific foundation of human-AI relationships and position Hong Kong prominently in responsible AI research within the Greater Bay Area's development ecosystem. Results will guide safe AI companion deployment for millions worldwide, potentially transforming digital mental health care while preventing psychological harm from unregulated deployment. N/A

titleEnglish: Getting Early Modern Asia Right: The Chinese Historical International Relations (CHIR) Dataset
department: Department of Politics and Public Administration
abstract: What factors can decrease the likelihood that governments engage in costly conflicts? Can trade between states, as well as economic and political pressures within them, reduce violent international conflict, and if yes, through what processes? These questions, long fundamental to the study of international relations, loom large today. In particular, the reemergence of China as a major power, and the rise of emerging economies in Asia more generally, have spurred debates about the benefits and risks of economic engagement, economic and political globalization backlash, and the spectre of China-US conflict. Concurrently, over the past two decades an exciting research agenda has asserted that many analysts are “getting Asia wrong” by neglecting its historical international relations and relying on Eurocentric theories to understand Asia’s rise. Researchers view early modern Asia–including the Ming and Qing dynasties–as a valuable setting given China’s powerful regional position. Scholars point to five centuries of relative peace as a counterpoint to claims that China’s rise will be violent and destabilizing. However, despite immense academic and policy interest, “getting Asia right” remains a work in progress. First, disproportionate focus on “tributary diplomacy” has neglected whether and how other external and internal factors–such as trade between states or economic and political pressures within them–affect conflict. Second, existing research almost exclusively examines East and Southeast Asia while ignoring other regions. Finally, and undergirding these limitations, international relations research on early modern Asia severely lacks rigorous, comprehensive data sources. This research will address these omissions and make two primary contributions. First, it will develop testable hypotheses about how trade and domestic pressures mitigated conflict throughout early modern Asia. Second, it will provide breakthrough evidence for studying early modern Asian international relations, including the above hypotheses, by introducing the Chinese Historical International Relations (CHIR) Dataset. CHIR will be a public research good that can help expand and diversify the field of historical international political economy. This research will expand and deepen the PI’s research agenda on Chinese historical foreign relations. It will produce three peer-reviewed articles in top political science or international relations journals (4*/3*), with research presented at major conferences. I have a track record of producing rigorous research and major datasets on China’s international relations and am confident that I will complete the research as scheduled. N/A

titleEnglish: Common Law in Common Hands: The ‘Registrum Brevium’ Manuscript in 13th–16th Century England
abstract: This project advances the study of English manuscript culture and the history of the English common law (c.1227–1531) by undertaking a new analysis of 'Registrum Brevium' manuscripts: legal books containing registers of writs. Examining these collections reveals the nature of public engagement with the common law during formative centuries of case-law development, and demonstrates the importance of this popular manuscript type to English written culture. Current estimates suggest that over 270 'Registrum Brevium' manuscripts survive; my recent research on the related genre of ‘ancient statutes’ manuscripts indicates that the true number may be significantly higher. These manuscripts, produced commercially in England for English users, were among the most ubiquitous secular books of the period. 'Registrum Brevium' manuscripts collect formularies for writs (causes of action for lawsuits), but they also aggregate other material: legal treatises, tracts, statutes, and even domestic records and literary texts. They were commissioned and used not only by common lawyers and judges but by landholders, ecclesiastics, merchants, and civic officials: readers and legal subjects whose interactions with the register of writs illuminate how the common law was understood, appropriated, and mobilised beyond the Inns of Court. Yet despite their scale and significance, 'Registrum Brevium' manuscripts have not received comprehensive study. No consolidated handlist exists to show the full extent of the corpus; there is no systematic account of their formats, companion texts, marginal practices, or patterns of ownership; and they are given little place in narratives of English book production. This project addresses that gap through two linked aims: (1) to develop new knowledge on the social history of the common law by mapping for the first time the commissioning, annotation, and use of registers of writs; and (2) to extend and deepen understandings of English book history by analysing how these widely produced legal compilations shaped textual structures, reading apparatus, and commercial bookmaking before print. An analysis of 'Registrum Brevium' as a manuscript corpus (undertaken here for the first time) will clarify how collections of writs, often termed the “seed-bed of the common law,” circulated in common use, and how they functioned – both as a practical and accessible legal technology, and as signifiers of authority and participation in the liberties of English law. N/A

titleEnglish: Investigating the Role of Occupant Behavior Change in Mitigating Building Energy Demand under Climate Change
coInvestigators: Prof Eom, Jiyong Prof Ou, Yang
abstract: Occupant behavior changes, such as adjusting indoor temperature setpoints to reduce building cooling and heating demand, offer a flexible, low-cost demand-side measure with significant potential for climate mitigation through reduced building energy use. Integrating occupant behavioral dynamics into building energy system modeling supports the development of effective mitigation strategies, particularly in energy-import-dependent regions like Hong Kong or during energy crises. However, the lack of region-specific data on behavioral responses to local temperature variations complicates building energy demand modeling and the assessment of behavior-driven mitigation impacts. These challenges are exacerbated by uncertainties in future climate change and socioeconomic development, which further impede the creation of effective mitigation strategies and progress toward sustainable, climate-resilient building energy use. To address these limitations, this project aims to enhance the accuracy of building energy modeling by explicitly integrating occupant behavior into the energy system modeling framework. First, we will develop an advanced machine learning model to quantify base temperatures (Tbase) globally at a regional resolution (comparable to U.S. states or Chinese provinces). These base temperatures define thresholds for heating (below the heating base temperature) or cooling (above the cooling base temperature), capturing regional energy use patterns influenced by local climate and socioeconomic conditions. Using these region-specific Tbase estimates and climate data, we will calculate heating and cooling degree days to evaluate building energy demand uncertainty across various climate models, climate change scenarios, and technology scenarios, identifying the spatiotemporal dynamics of key uncertainty sources. Finally, these region-specific heating and cooling degree days will serve as proxies for occupant behavior changes to assess the impacts of diverse behavioral strategies on building energy demand within the Global Change Analysis Model (GCAM), a widely used integrated assessment model. This analysis will quantify how behavioral adaptations can influence building energy demand under diverse future scenarios. By generating a globally consistent, region-specific Tbase dataset and integrating behavior-driven dynamics into building energy modeling, this project will establish a robust scientific foundation for demand-side policy design. The anticipated outcomes will provide evidence-based insights to reduce energy consumption, enhance building sector sustainability, and contribute to climate mitigation. N/A

titleEnglish: Conceptualizing and Operationalizing Social Network Quality for Social Support Mobilization and Well-Being in Family Caregiving of Older Adults: A Mixed-Methods Study in Hong Kong
department: Department of Social Work and Social Administration
coInvestigators: Dr Choy, Chak Pui Jacky Prof Lou, Vivian Weiqun
abstract: Family caregivers play a vital role in providing long-term care for older adults. Despite existing government support programs, recent tragedies in Hong Kong and elsewhere have exposed major gaps in formal care systems. Social capital offers a promising solution by connecting caregivers with informal networks and community resources, aligning with the Decade of Healthy Aging (2021–2030). However, three key research gaps persist. First, social network quality in caregiving remains poorly conceptualized, with limited understanding of the qualitative attributes of social network that drive support mobilization and caregiver well-being. The Principal Investigator’s prior research identifies three culturally embedded traits in Chinese contexts (e.g., familialization of social relationships, particularized trust, and intermediary efficacy) but further theoretical development is needed. Second, these qualitative attributes of social network have not been operationalized, leading to an overreliance on quantitative metrics like network size and resulting in measurement error and validity concerns. Third, despite Lin’s social capital model emphasizing both networks and support mobilization, few studies examine how support mobilization mediates the link between network characteristics and caregiver outcomes. This proposal aims to develop and validate a new theoretical framework of social capital tailored to caregiving. It will: (1) identify and conceptualize the qualitative attributes of social networks that facilitate support mobilization and enhance caregiver well-being, using inductive and deductive approaches; (2) operationalize key components of social network quality by developing valid and reliable measurement instruments; and (3) empirically test how support mobilization mediates the relationship between quantitative (e.g., size) and qualitative attributes of networks and caregiver well-being. The project will include two studies. Study 1 will involve eight focus group interviews with 48–64 family caregivers to explore the key qualitative attributes of social networks that facilitate support mobilization and to conceptualize network quality (Objective 1). Study 2 will be conducted in two phases: Phase 1 will use the Delphi method with a panel of 20 experts—comprising five academics, ten caregivers, and five policy professionals—to develop instruments for measuring social network quality (Objective 2). Phase 2 will involve a cross-sectional survey of 450 caregivers to validate the measurement tools and evaluate the proposed framework (Objective 3). This proposal moves beyond Western-centric perspectives by introducing a timely and culturally grounded conceptualization of social network quality that drive support mobilization. The findings will inform policies that empower caregivers, enhance well-being, and support sustainable long-term care systems. N/A

titleEnglish: Understanding and modulating decision-making alterations in problematic social media users: Computational mechanisms and effects of abstinence and tDCS
coInvestigators: Prof Cheng, Cecilia Prof Zhang, Lei
abstract: Social media has become increasingly popular worldwide. However, approximately 5% of users have experienced problematic social media use (PSMU), a potential behavioral addiction that poses significant risks to mental health and well-being. Therefore, it is urgent to elucidate the cognitive mechanisms underlying PSMU and to develop tailored interventions. Value-based decision-making deficits are highly relevant to PSMU. However, decision-making involves multiple sub-processes (e.g., risk and ambiguity evaluation) that may not be directly observable. Computational modeling provides a theory-driven approach for precisely characterizing sub-processes involved in a decision-making task. Therefore, this method allows us to accurately assess which sub-processes are alerted in PSMU. Decision-making sub-processes are dynamic and can be influenced by different PSMU stages, such as abstinence-induced withdrawal. Although positive effects of abstinence have been reported in regular users, abstinence is more likely to increase withdrawal levels in individuals with PSMU, which may disrupt some decision-making sub-processes. However, the effects of abstinence on decision-making sub-processes in individuals with PSMU remain largely unexplored, and no studies have examined strategies to complement abstinence to further reduce withdrawal symptoms and improve decision-making sub-processes. This project aims to address these gaps through two complementary studies: Study 1 will first use a combination of three decision-making tasks and computational models to comprehensively examine which sub-processes are altered in the PSMU group compared to the regular social media use (RSMU) group. Additionally, Study 1 will examine how abstinence influences different decision-making sub-processes in both groups. Participants in each group will be randomly assigned to either the abstinence or unconstrained use condition for one week and will then complete a post-assessment. We hypothesize that abstinence will lead to higher withdrawal levels and less improvement (or greater disruption) in some decision-making sub-processes in the PSMU group compared to the RSMU group. Study 2 will investigate whether transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS), a non-invasive neurostimulation, can complement abstinence to improve decision-making in individuals with PSMU. The dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) will be targeted based on previous research. Participants will be randomly assigned to one of four groups based on the combinations of tDCS (active or sham) and abstinence (yes or no) conditions. Decision-making and withdrawal symptoms will be assessed before and after the intervention and at a three-month follow-up. This project will advance our understanding of the cognitive mechanisms underlying decision-making alterations in PSMU and provide important insights for designing more effective interventions. N/A

titleEnglish: Prevalence and multidimensional risk factors of risky loot box use in Hong Kong: Comorbidity with and transition to internet gaming disorder and problem gambling
coInvestigators: Prof TSE, Samson Shu Ki Prof Yip, Paul Siu Fai
abstract: Background: Technological advancements and increased popularity in digital behaviors contribute to Internet Gaming Disorder (IGD) and Gambling Disorder (GD) in young people. Loot box purchase, which refer to buying virtual random items using real money or in-game currency, resembles gambling-like behaviors and are addictive in nature. A better understanding of risky loot box use in terms of prevalence, multidimensional risk factors, and symptom comorbidity with IGD and GD, is needed. According to the I-PACE model, addictive behaviors in loot box use, internet gaming, and gambling result from interactions between triggers, motives, temperamental features (intolerance of uncertainty and impulsivity), affective responses (emotion dysregulation), and cognitive biases (metacognitions). Fear of missing out could be a social risk factor complementing I-PACE model. Existing studies have not empirically examined these risk factors simultaneously, nor their bidirectional relationships with IGD, GD, and risky loot box use in longitudinal samples. Methods: The proposed research aims to conduct a longitudinal, 4-wave online survey to recruit 2000 young gamers aged 16 – 29 in Hong Kong via local universities, youth service operators, and gaming forums. Three follow-up assessments are conducted in 6-month intervals. The participants will complete a self-report questionnaire including gaming and gambling behaviors and validated measures on IGD, GD, RLI, personality traits, gaming motives, metacognitions, and psychosocial risk factors. Study 1 validates the RLI and estimates the prevalence and severity of RLI in Hong Kong gamers. Study 2 evaluates the comorbidity between RLI, IGD, and GD at the symptom level in Wave 1 – 2 data using cross-lagged panel network. Study 3 conducts longitudinal moderated mediation modeling to examine the pathways from Wave 1 intolerance of uncertainty and impulsivity to Wave 3 RLI via cognitive and psychosocial risk factors at Wave 2. Study 4 performs random-intercept cross-lagged panel modeling to examine the within-person bidirectional relationships between cognitive and psychosocial risk factors and addictive behaviors (RLI, IGD, GD) in Wave 1 - 4 data while accounting for the stable between-person variance. Significance: The proposed studies will provide the first results on the prevalence and severity of RLI in Hong Kong young gamers and offer insights into symptom comorbidity with IGD and GD, and their bidirectional relationships with multidimensional risk factors. The findings will advance our understanding of cognitive and psychosocial mechanisms and temporal directionality and have important public health implications in informing targeted clinical interventions and regulation policies related to loot box use among young gamers. N/A

titleEnglish: Who Checks Whom? Mapping Interactions and Inequalities in Global Fact-Checking Networks
abstract: Misinformation increasingly flows across national borders, accelerated by the rise of global social media platforms. While fact-checking has become one of the most prominent tools to counter falsehoods, it has largely been studied within national or regional contexts. As a result, we still know little about the cross-border dynamics of fact-checking: which countries’ claims attract international attention, how fact-checkers engage with each other across borders, and what structural and political conditions shape these interactions. Addressing these questions is both a scholarly and practical challenge, with implications for journalism, media systems, and the fight against misinformation. This project investigates the global dynamics of fact-checking using a comprehensive dataset of over 255K claims verified by 942 fact-checking organizations worldwide over the past 15 years. By analyzing who checks whose claims, the topics of these claims, and the platforms on which they first appear, the study will map the international flows of misinformation and verification. It will also examine how country-level factors, such as digital media infrastructure, internet and social media penetration, and political regimes, influence both the activity of fact-checkers and the direction of cross-border verification. By combining computational content analysis and network analysis, this research will illuminate global patterns of fact-checking, identify structural inequalities, and provide evidence to support more equitable and effective international strategies for combating misinformation. The project contributes new knowledge in three ways. First, it moves beyond national case studies to provide the first systematic mapping of cross-border fact-checking networks. Second, it shows how political and media contexts influence which claims receive attention and which countries play leading roles in the global fact-checking ecosystem.
