Third, it develops a comparative framework for understanding misinformation not as an isolated national problem but as a transnational challenge that requires coordinated responses. The anticipated impact is twofold. For scholarship, the study will expand research on journalism and misinformation by integrating computational analysis with comparative media perspectives. For practice, the findings will inform fact-checkers, media organizations, and policymakers about where global verification gaps exist and how international collaboration can be strengthened. Ultimately, the project will help explain how fact-checking functions in a connected digital environment and provide actionable insights for designing more effective strategies to contain misinformation worldwide. N/A

titleEnglish: Exploring Hong Kong Primary Teachers' Pedagogical Beliefs and Practices in AI-Mediated Computational Thinking Education: A Longitudinal Study on Continuing Professional Development
coInvestigators: Prof Angeli-Valanides, Charoula
abstract: This research project explores how Artificial Intelligence (AI) shapes the teaching and learning of Computational Thinking (CT) in Hong Kong's primary schools. As CT becomes increasingly central to digital literacy, the thoughtful integration of AI technologies is creating new opportunities for classroom practice. In this context, AI-mediated CT education refers to the use of AI tools, including code-generation applications, platforms that scaffold problem-solving, and machine learning demonstrations, to enrich students' experiences with computational problem-solving and to deepen their engagement with CT concepts and practices. The project is grounded in the understanding that the integration of AI in CT education is influenced by how teachers interpret, adapt, and incorporate these tools into their everyday teaching. This study seeks to explore how teachers' beliefs and instructional approaches develop as they engage with AI technologies through a three-year continuing professional development (PD) program. Particular attention is given to how teachers respond to the opportunities and challenges presented by AI, as well as how their professional thinking and classroom practices shift and evolve during this process. A constructivist approach guides the research. The study adopts action research within a multiple-case study framework, focusing on the lived experiences of teachers participating in the PD program. Data will be collected through surveys, interviews, classroom observations, teacher reflections, and the analysis of teaching materials. This approach is intended to capture the processes and perspectives that shape teachers' journeys as they experiment with and reflect on AI-mediated CT instruction. The Interconnected Model of Professional Growth provides the main analytical framework. This model facilitates tracing the connections between teachers' learning experiences with AI, their classroom practices, and their ongoing PD. It also supports an examination of how teachers' direct experiences with AI tools during PD sessions are reflected in their thinking and actions related to CT in the classroom. The aims of the study are to describe how teachers' beliefs and practices develop as they work with AI in CT education, to identify the supports and obstacles they encounter, and to illustrate how these changes manifest in authentic classroom settings. By centering the perspectives and experiences of primary teachers, this project addresses a gap in current research. The findings are intended to inform PD, curriculum, and policy design, offering practical insights for supporting teachers as they integrate AI into CT education in Hong Kong and similar contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Scholars' engagement with generative AI: Evidence from Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Alves Horta, Hugo Duarte Dr DAI, Kun Prof Oleksiyenko, Anatoly V. Prof Thelwall, Mike
abstract: Scientific research is being reshaped by generative AI—tools that can produce text, code, images, and more—bringing major opportunities for speed, creativity, and collaboration alongside serious concerns about bias, accuracy, integrity, and accountability. Policies and practices are evolving quickly, but there is limited, Hong Kong‑specific evidence connecting university rules to what scholars actually do in their day‑to‑day work. This project addresses that gap by mapping institutional policies, documenting real scholarly use, and explaining why researchers adopt or resist generative AI over time within Hong Kong's multilingual, research‑intensive ecosystem. Guided by Bronfenbrenner's PPCT (Person‑Process‑Context‑Time) framework—which links people, interactions, settings, and change over time—this study will map the policies and guidelines on generative AI across Hong Kong universities and disciplines, describe how scholars use these tools throughout the research workflow, and identify the personal factors, interaction patterns, organizational conditions, and time‑based shifts that drive adoption or resistance, explaining the mechanisms behind them. An explanatory mixed‑methods design integrates three components. First, a policy analysis will build a bilingual corpus of university and discipline‑level documents, coding their scope, permitted/prohibited uses, oversight, disclosure, data protection, IP, equity, dual‑use risk, enforcement, support structures, and update cadence. Second, a stratified survey of active scholars across institutions, disciplines, and career stages will quantify use cases, frequency, perceived benefits, and concerns across tasks such as idea generation, literature review, data analysis, and writing. Third, semi‑structured interviews (in English, Cantonese, or Putonghua) will elicit mechanism‑rich accounts that link individual motivations and barriers to institutional environments and policy features, tracing how practices change with new tools and rules. The project will deliver: (a) the first systematic map of Hong Kong universities' research policies on generative AI and how they vary by discipline, (b) robust, comparative evidence on how scholars actually use or avoid these tools, and (c) a multilevel explanation of adoption and resistance mechanisms grounded in the PPCT framework. Together, these outputs will inform actionable recommendations for universities, funders, and journals on governance, training, verification and disclosure standards, infrastructure and equitable access, and support for reproducible, trustworthy research. By aligning policies with real practices, the study will help Hong Kong's world‑class research sector harness generative AI responsibly and effectively. N/A

titleEnglish: Effects of cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) on reward processing in youths with insomnia: A randomised controlled trial
coInvestigators: Dr Chan, Joey Wing-yan Dr Chan, Ngan Yin Prof Hu, Xiaoqing Prof Li, Martin Dr Rosenzweig, Ivana Prof Wing, Yun Kwok Prof Zhang, Jihui
abstract: Adolescence is a sensitive developmental period of brain development, often linked to the emergence of sleep and mental health problems. Insomnia, characterised by difficulty initiating sleep and difficulty maintaining sleep, is highly prevalent in this group, affecting up to 36% of adolescents. Untreated sleep disturbance can lead to significant impairments in daytime functioning and adverse psychosocial outcomes in adolescents. Notably, growing evidence points to an intricate relationship between insomnia and reward processing dysfunction, which is often implicated in mood-related psychopathology. This suggests that targeting sleep disturbances may potentially offer a novel avenue for improving reward processing and mitigating associated negative outcomes in adolescents, a group that is especially vulnerable to reward dysfunction-related psychopathology and consequences. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT-I) is recommended as the first-line treatment for insomnia. Previous research has shown the beneficial effects of CBT-I in not only improving night-time sleep but also daytime functioning in individuals with insomnia, including mood symptoms, but the existing research was mostly conducted in the adult population and mainly relied on self-report measures. Furthermore, the mechanisms through which CBT-I improves mood remain unclear. Emerging, albeit limited, evidence suggests that CBT-I may modulate reward processing, highlighting a potential mechanistic pathway. Nonetheless, there is a need for more rigorous research to investigate how a sleep-focused intervention affects reward processing over time and to incorporate neuroimaging techniques to explore the underlying neural changes associated with the improvement of sleep upon intervention in young people. To address the existing research gaps, we propose to conduct a randomised controlled trial (RCT) to investigate: 1) whether CBT-I is effective in improving reward processing in adolescents with insomnia, 2) whether changes in sleep mediate the alterations in reward processing following CBT-I intervention. A total of 198 eligible participants will be randomised to either a 6-week group-based CBT-I intervention or an active psychoeducation control condition. Assessments including both self-report as well as behavioural and electrophysiological measures of reward processing will be conducted at pre-treatment (week 0), post-treatment (week 7/one-week after the last treatment session), and at a 6-month follow-up. On a conceptual level, this proposed study will allow us to further test the hypothesis of a causal relationship between sleep disruption and reward processing dysfunction, especially in young people. The therapeutic model and protocol established in this study will inform clinical practice and facilitate optimising future clinical management of reward dysfunction-related psychopathology in this vulnerable population. N/A

titleEnglish: From Text to Dialogue: A Generative AI Approach to Fostering Immersive Science Inquiry in the Metaverse
coInvestigators: Dr Chen, Chen Logan Prof Su, Cai Prof Zhen, Yuanyi
abstract: Science education aims to cultivate students’ scientific literacy, moving beyond memorization to foster inquiry and critical thinking. However, learning is often constrained by static science textbooks and teacher-dominated instructional methods, which limit opportunities for dynamic, student-led exploration. While research highlights the profound benefits of dialogic interaction for developing reasoning skills, creating authentic environments for such interaction remains a major challenge. This project addresses this critical gap by proposing an innovative solution that complements traditional textbook-based learning. We will design, develop, and evaluate T2Metaverse, a system that leverages cutting-edge Generative AI to automatically transform frontier scientific findings from leading journals like Nature and Science into immersive, interactive Metaverse learning experiences. This approach moves beyond the static presentation of science as a collection of settled facts to allow students to actively and collaboratively explore the very process of scientific discovery. The project’s feasibility is grounded in the PI’s extensive expertise in using AI for dialogue analysis and enhancement and is supported by a strong interdisciplinary team. A functional prototype of the T2Metaverse workflow developed by the team already demonstrates technical viability. Further, a rigorous human-in-the-loop protocol involving researchers, teachers, and students will ensure all generated content is scientifically accurate, pedagogically sound, and age-appropriate. The project’s effectiveness will be rigorously assessed through a quasi-experimental study involving 20 secondary science teachers and approximately 1,000 of their students in Hong Kong over one semester. Using a pre- and post-test design, we will compare a treatment group using the T2Metaverse environments with a comparison group receiving conventional instruction. To strengthen the design, comparisons will be made between classes taught by the same teacher, providing a robust control for teacher- and school-level confounding variables. A mixed-methods approach will be used to collect and analyze quantitative data (questionnaires, tests, system logs) and qualitative data (interviews, video recordings) to measure the impact on students’ scientific literacy, learning engagement, collaborative learning skills, and problem-solving abilities. The statistical analysis will employ a two-level linear mixed model to account for the nested data structure. The research holds significant potential to innovate technology-supported science education. By demonstrating a scalable method for automatically creating dialogic inquiry-based learning environments, the T2Metaverse system promises to enhance student learning engagement and outcomes. The project will yield deep insights into designing effective learning experiences for students. Findings will inform AI-enhanced education across disciplines and provide evidence-based guidance for educational policy. N/A

titleEnglish: Aspirational Nationalism: Emulation, Enmity, and the Making of Chinese National Identity
department: Department of Politics and Public Administration
coInvestigators: Dr He, Yingtian Prof Peng, Peng
abstract: We develop a new theory of aspirational nationalism to explain how national identity is built not only through resistance to outsiders but also through learning from them. Existing theories of nationalism emphasize war, modernization, or elite manipulation, focusing on the politics of enmity. We argue that national identity also evolves through emulation, a forward-looking process in which elites and publics treat foreign powers as models of reform. Aspirational nationalism, therefore, operates through two pathways: an enmity pathway, grounded in threat and exclusion, and an emulation pathway, grounded in admiration and adaptation. Together, they explain how nationalism can both oppose and imitate its perceived rivals. Our study theorizes these dual pathways and tests them in the context of modern China between 1850 and 1949, a century of imperial decline, foreign invasion, and rapid reform. We ask three questions. First, how do national narratives vary along the three dimensions of pathway, referent, and issue of interest? Second, how do external threats, such as wars and crises, alter the balance between enmity and emulation? Third, how do elites shape the content and trajectory of aspirational nationalism, and which elites have the power to redefine the nation? From these questions, we derive hypotheses linking external shocks, elite experiences, and ideational change. Empirically, we combine large-scale computational text analysis with historical interpretation to examine how Chinese elites reimagined "China" across a century of transformation. Drawing on digitized archives of newspapers, journals, and biographical records, we build the most extensive dataset of elite writings containing the keyword Zhongguo (中国), the linguistic marker of modern nationhood. The corpus includes about 679,000 newspaper titles and 80,000 journal titles, each linked to biographical information about their authors. Using an AI-assisted workflow, we annotate each entry in the rich archive along three theoretical dimensions of aspirational nationalism: pathway, referent, and issue of interest. We then analyze the annotated documents with a mixture of computational and qualitative methods: We use time series analysis to trace how the national narratives evolved over time, network analysis to map how nation-building ideas circulated among elites, and qualitative readings of selected archives to provide interpretive depth and historical grounding. Our study has both theoretical and empirical contributions. Theoretically, it re-conceptualizes nationalism as a politics of aspiration. Empirically, it shows how computational methods can track ideational change at a scale once inaccessible to traditional research methods. N/A

titleEnglish: Bridge or Barrier? The Double Edged Role of AI Self Disclosure in Shaping Help Seeking Pathways Among University Students in Hong Kong.
department: Department of Social Work and Social Administration
coInvestigators: Dr Alan, Dougherty Dr Habibi, Mojtaba Dr Qin, Lili Prof Williams, Jo Dr Yeung, Wing Fai
abstract: In Hong Kong, more than one in four experience depression, yet only a quarter ever seek help. After COVID‑19, social withdrawal has surged. It leaves many to face their struggles alone. At the same time, a new trend has emerged: young people are increasingly talking to generative AI chatbots about their feelings and problems. For some, talking to AI feels safer than opening up to friends or professionals. But does this habit encourage help‑seeking, or could it replace human connection and delay care? Current research overlooks three critical gaps. First, no validated tool measures AI substitution (swapping human chats for AI), unlike social media scales. Second, studies ignore whether AI disclosure boosts or kills help-seeking, skipping why (mechanisms) and relying on weak snapshots. Third, traits like low life meaning (which worsens risks) or self-compassion (which protects) are ignored. This project tackles that urgent question through a new Twin‑Mechanism Model explaining how conversations with AI affect real‑world help‑seeking. It identifies two linked processes. The first, AI‑Perceived Partner Responsiveness (AI‑PPR), is a psychological process in which users feel understood and validated by AI, which may boost confidence and reduce stigma around seeking professional help. The second, the AI Substitution Index (ASI), is a behavioural process that measures how much time and emotional energy shift from people to AI, potentially lowering real‑life help‑seeking. Whether AI acts as a bridge or a barrier depends on how these two forces interact. The project unfolds in two phases. Phase 1 will develop and test short, reliable tools to measure AI‑PPR and ASI through in‑depth interviews with university students. Phase 2 will apply these tools in a three‑wave longitudinal study of 360 students, tracking how self‑disclosure to AI predicts later help‑seeking, connectedness, well‑being, and dependency. Advanced statistical models will identify genuine change, ensuring results are rigorous and replicable. This study is unprecedented in testing the Twin‑Mechanism Model, linking psychological theory to behaviour over time. Its novelty lies in showing how emotional comfort with AI might inspire formal, real-world help‑seeking or, instead, substitute digital talk for human care. This project will help guide Hong Kong and the world toward safer uses of AI for youth mental health. N/A

titleEnglish: Artificially Enhanced Justice: An Exploration of Human-AI Interaction in Legal Processes
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into law promises greater efficiency but also threatens to undermine fundamental values of human justice. Past studies have documented a judicial human-AI fairness gap: people consistently perceive adjudication by algorithms as less fair than adjudication by humans, even when the outcomes are the same. This project investigates how the public perceives and responds to the deployment of AI in the legal field. It builds on past studies by examining assistive rather than adjudicative uses of AI. It also innovates by considering uses of AI inside and, critically, outside the courtroom. This shift in attention is important since most disputes do not proceed to trial. Three axes of inquiry are presented. First, this project examines whether human oversight can eliminate the human-AI fairness gap. It explores whether and why minimal human review might render algorithmic decisions acceptable to the public. Second, it investigates how people process AI-generated legal advice, testing whether uniqueness neglect—the fear that AI cannot appreciate their unique circumstances—causes them to second-guess or reject correct information given by a machine. Third, it analyzes how individuals interpret the probabilistic outputs of AI, e.g., a 70% chance of winning, and how these interpretations shape their legal strategies and expectations. By using experimental methods to explore these questions across different jurisdictions, this project seeks to uncover the normative and psychological mechanisms that influence how we interact with AI technology in law. The findings will provide a crucial evidence base for designing legal systems that are not only efficient and accessible but also perceived as fair and legitimate by the people they serve. N/A

titleEnglish: Cross-border travel and consumption in cross-border regions: Who is left behind and why?
coInvestigators: Prof Yin, Ling Dr Zhao, Ya
abstract: While residents of regions like the Schengen Area (Europe) enjoy seamless travel across internal borders, many elsewhere still face significant barriers, e.g., the obligation to use specific ports of entry, along with the associated costs and bureaucratic formalities. Despite these barriers, the post-COVID era has witnessed a surge in cross-border travel and consumption (CBTC) in many cross-border regions, such as the Hong Kong-Shenzhen cross-border region. Yet, amid this trend, a segment of the population thereof remains within their borders. Who are they? Why do they choose—or are they compelled—to stay? Furthermore, how is their wellbeing affected compared to that of cross-border travelers? Notably in Hong Kong, more and more residents face dwindling local amenities. The rise of CBTC—driven by cheaper and often superior options—is drawing customers away from local businesses, reducing the supply of amenities like restaurants and grocery stores. This research focuses on residents of cross-border regions who seldom or never cross the border over different time intervals, e.g., 2, 4, 6 months. We hypothesize that a segment of these stayers are willing to travel and consume abroad but are deterred by significant perceived barriers; we term this group constrained stayers. Consequently, their wellbeing may suffer compared to their cross-border peers, who benefit from access to cheaper and more diverse goods and services. Utilizing a mixed-method approach and drawing on concepts and theories from disciplines like transport, positive psychology, economics, and philosophy, the proposed research will gather and utilize both primary and secondary data in Hong Kong to address the following research questions: • Whether and how does the profile of constrained stayers vary when they are defined over different time intervals—for example, 2, 4, and 6 months? • What motivates or deters (different) constrained stayers from undertaking CBTC? • How do the negative effects of CBTC influence the wellbeing of constrained stayers? • Based on the answers to the above questions, what theories and policies can be proposed to support constrained stayers? In short, this research will investigate the impacts of CBTC on the constrained stayers’ wellbeing by developing theories, measurement tools, and predictive models. Its ultimate goal is to provide decision-makers with evidence-based recommendations to improve the constrained stayers' wellbeing. N/A

titleEnglish: Writing in Fragments: Eileen Chang in Archives and Manuscripts
abstract: “Writing in Fragments” continues my earlier and ongoing work to situate the study of Eileen Chang (1920-1995) in archives and manuscripts. Given Chang’s steady rise in global recognition in recent decades, I argue for the need to expand what we consider as “texts” to include manuscripts, papers, correspondences, photographs, drawings, and other documents and objects. I have already begun this work and am now seeking support to allow me to broaden the scope of my research to include a greater variety of material housed outside of Hong Kong. My goal is to lift Chang out of a linear Chinese literary historiography, to place her in the larger context of twentieth-century literary and cultural production, and to define her in precise terms as a major figure in world literature. In Anglophone and Francophone literary studies of recent decades, major figures such as Virginia Woolf, James Joyce, and Emily Dickinson have been reexamined in light of a vast amount of papers, manuscripts, and other documents that have newly emerged. Returning canonical texts to their original state as manuscripts becomes imperative in current scholarly discourse. Similarly, taking writings that were previously considered incomplete, informal, and fragmented as texts proper has enriched and complicated our understandings of familiar literary contours. My work responds directly to this “archival turn” in global literary studies. While Chang’s early works have been canonized and thoroughly researched, her later works, particularly those written during the last three decades of her American life, call for more archive-based research. After Chang died in 1995, her manuscripts that were relatively complete were published posthumously, some with substantial reconstruction and editorial input. She also left behind much material that would be deemed unpublishable, as it was too fragmentary. In her later years, Chang was increasingly writing for a future audience, going at her own pace, one fragment at a time. I define Chang’s “late style” as a form of “future writing,” as she had provided many beginnings of what would have been longer narratives. Studying Chang’s later works in their draft form and in a state of perpetual incompleteness becomes important. Significantly broadening the scope of Eileen Chang studies, my new project will paint a thorough and more nuanced portrait of a global writer who persisted with her experiments till her last days. N/A

titleEnglish: Reactivating positive memories during sleep to weaken negative memories: A closed-loop targeted memory reactivation approach
coInvestigators: Prof Li, Shirley X. Dr Ngo-Dehning, Hong-Viet
abstract: Sleep offers a unique window for consolidating daily experiences, shaping what we remember and how we feel. Yet not all memories are equally welcome. When negative memories dominate over positive ones, people may develop pervasive negative schemas that distort attention, memory, and interpretation of events, thereby heightening vulnerability to psychiatric disorders including depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). In contrast, reinforcing positive memories and positive thinking can yield cascading benefits: boosting mood, reducing stress, and ultimately enhancing psychological resilience. However, how to strengthen positive memories and positive thinking during sleep while weakening negative ones remains an open challenge. We propose to address this critical question using targeted memory reactivation (TMR)—a technique that unobtrusively delivers acoustic cues linked to prior daytime learning during sleep to modulate memories. Specifically, we will employ TMR to reactivate and thus strengthen positive memories during sleep while weakening competing negative memories. To enhance efficacy, we will employ closed-loop algorithms to deliver memory cues precisely during the up-state of slow oscillations in non-rapid eye movement (NREM) sleep, a transient window optimal for memory reactivation. Addressing this question is essential for understanding the mechanisms of emotional memory consolidation, and for developing novel sleep interventions to promote mental well-beings. Across three experiments, the current proposal aims to address the following research questions: 1) how pre-TMR retrieval of positive memories would modulate TMR’s impact on strengthening positive memories and weakening negative memories, both immediately and after a delay. 2) whether reactivating positive reappraisals of negative autobiographical memories during sleep can weaken these memories; and 3) whether the sleep-based memory modulation protocol improves emotional well-being among individuals with subthreshold depression in daily life. Our proposal offers unique advantages. First, initiating memory reactivation during sleep will bypass conscious effort. This particularly benefits vulnerable populations (e.g., depression, anxiety) who often show difficulties in retrieving and maintaining positive emotional memories. Second, employing innovative memory modulation techniques such as closed-loop TMR and positive reappraisal to weaken real-world negative autobiographical memories, our proposal bears high ecological validity. Third, by testing the protocol’s effectiveness in memory modulation among individual with subthreshold depression, the proposal will inform scalable, non-invasive, drug-free interventions that complement existing therapies. In sum, by harnessing sleep’s intrinsic processes, this research could pave the way for innovative strategies to promote psychological resilience and reduce the burden of psychiatric disorders at a population level. N/A

titleEnglish: A Tale of Two Systems: Neural and Behavioral Investigations of Pain Empathy in Mother-Child Dyads of Autistic and Neurotypical Children
abstract: Pain empathy, the ability to perceive another’s pain, is a vital skill that fosters mutual understanding and deepens emotional bonds during social interaction. However, due to atypical neural activity patterns, autistic children exhibit significant difficulties in pain empathy. To help them acquire proficient empathy skills, this study will delineate the neural dynamics underlying their empathic dysfunction. Specifically, our three-year longitudinal, electroencephalography (EEG)-behavioural, and mother-child dyadic design will examine (1) the neural mechanisms of empathy to physical and social pain in autistic and neurotypical children, (2) the empathic neural responses to others' pain in mothers of autistic children, and (3) the mother-child empathic links at both neural and behavioural levels. These objectives stem from three identified gaps. Our recent (Wang & Tong, 2025) and other EEG studies (e.g., Liu et al., 2023) have (1) overlooked the multidimensional nature of pain by focusing exclusively on physical pain, but not social pain; (2) neglected the impact of raising an autistic child on the mother’s empathic pain response, and how that potentially altered response may shape the child’s empathic development; and (3) omitted the mother-child connection in empathy development by relying on a cross-sectional design of a specific age group without integrating behavioral measures of empathy. For each of three consecutive years, we will track two mother-child dyadic groups: 87 autistic children aged 6-7 and their 87 neurotypical mothers; and 87 neurotypical peers and their 87 mothers. All will complete a behavioral assessment of cognitive and affective empathy. Mothers will complete questionnaires on their and their children’s anxiety and stress. EEG recordings will be conducted during the first and final year, with mother-child dyads viewing animated videos depicting real-life physical and social pain situations to elicit their empathic responses. Multiple EEG analytical methods, i.e., event-related potentials, functional connectivity, neural oscillations, and aperiodic activity, will characterize neural activities related to pain empathy. Bayesian predictive modeling will examine neural and behavioral dynamics between mothers and children, showing how pain empathy develops over time and links brain activity to behavioral measures. Results will elucidate (1) the neural dynamics underlying impaired pain empathy in autism, (2) the impact of raising an autistic child on the mother’s empathic brain response, and (3) the empathic link between mothers and their children. These findings will clarify the principles and factors necessary for designing interventions for families raising autistic children. N/A

titleEnglish: Archiving an indigenous multi-functional public building type of Hong Kong: the urban infrastructural node of the Municipal Services Building
abstract: Archiving an indigenous multi-functional public building type of Hong Kong: the urban infrastructural node of the Municipal Services Building (MSB) proposes to critically examine a selection of eight of the until-now overlooked public building type, its vertically-stacked multi-functional composition indigenous to Hong Kong’s density and evolution of its institutional frameworks. Conceived in the mid-1970s, following the institutional restructuring that expanded Urban Council (UC)’s remit to culture, the buildings, formerly known as the UC complexes, were built as part of the expanded portfolio of what the UC showcased in the 1986 Building Journal as “urban amenities by the people, for the people and of the people.” Realized alongside visibly-iconic buildings such as the Cultural Centre and Art Museum, these were ordinary architectures for everyday use—with wet markets at the base, libraries in the middle, sports facilities atop, and sometimes theatres and galleries in between. A systematic documentation of their architectural proposals and transformations, drawing on both drawing records and measured surveys, as well as an archival excavation of the UC’s reports and meeting notes, i.e. from the UC’s Planning-Development (1974-1980) then Capital-Works Committee (1981-1999) will firstly contextualize these public architectural productions in the aspirations as well as challenges of the time. The UC’s engagement of Hong Kong’s seminal private architects, including Chung Wah Nan, Dennis Lau, Palmer and Turner, and Wong Ouyang, also showed the importance of these public nodes in the rising skyline of the 1980s-1990s dominated by the commercial towers and shopping malls and manifesting the city’s otherwise laissez faire economic priorities. A second part of the project proposes to collect also artefacts—drawings, photos, ephemera—as well as oral histories from the surviving actors who were involved in the MSB’s design and realizations, archiving the significance of these yet-to-be-recognized late-20th-century buildings. Even though Hong Kong has increasingly recognized post-war spaces as heritage—though the youth of the MSBs preclude their recognition—and the M+ Museum is collecting the works of locally-trained post-war architects, highlighting the value of their designs to architectural discourse in general, there remains until today little formal documentation of the MSBs. With the slated demolition and renovations that have and will change the urban interface of this unique architectural type, archiving both their physical and institutional formations takes on growing urgency. N/A

titleEnglish: Early Postnatal Testosterone during "Mini-Puberty" at 1-3 Months of Age, Parental Input, and Gender-Related Outcomes at 48, 60, and 72 Months of Age
abstract: BACKGROUND Thousands of non-human mammal experiments across numerous species have demonstrated organising (long-term, programming) effects of early exposure to androgenic hormones (e.g., testosterone) on aspects of behavioural development that show average sex differences. In human gender development, the early postnatal testosterone surge during “mini-puberty” at 1–3 months of age has been postulated to be a critical window for testosterone to exert organising effects (Kung, 2022; Kung et al., 2024). Parental influences may also contribute to a range of gender-related outcomes. NOVELTY The current longitudinal study is first-of-its-kind, generating novel insights into the role of early postnatal testosterone, as well as its interaction with parental influences, in shaping gender-related outcomes. METHOD: Prior Time Points & Measures The longitudinal cohort was built and recruited by the PI’s team (Kung et al., 2025). This cohort completed assessment time points at 1–3, 12, & 24 months of age, and the 36-month-old follow-up is currently underway. At 1–3 months of age, 3 weekly saliva samples were collected from each infant for testosterone assays. At ages 1–3, 12, 24, and 36 months, measures assessing parental input and child developmental outcomes have been included. Individual, in-person data collection sessions have been conducted at ages 12, 24, and 36 months. METHOD: New Time Points & Measures This new project will conduct follow-ups at 48, 60, and 72 months of age. Gender-related outcomes will be assessed during individual, in-person sessions. These new follow-ups will continue to assess previously included outcomes such as play preferences, physical aggression, and social communication (eye contact & autistic traits). These new follow-ups will also continue to assess parental input. As children reach age 48 months in the current project, additional gender-related outcomes can be assessed. These additional outcomes include activity level, mental rotation (a visuo-spatial skill), and aspects of language/verbal development. Child-reported, parent-reported, cognitive, observational, and accelerometery measures will be employed. METHOD: Sample Size In total, 472 infants were enrolled at age 1–3 months. The participation rate at follow-ups across ages 12 (completed), 24 (completed), and 36 months (on-going) has been high (~90% at each follow-up). It is expected that around 200 boys and 200 girls (~85% participation rate) will take part in the proposed follow-ups at 48, 60, and 72 months of age. IMPORTANCE This one-of-a-kind longitudinal project will luminate the developmental origins of numerous phenomena in human development. N/A

titleEnglish: Teachers First: Social and Emotional Learning for Well-Being and Equity Through SEL4U and SELbot
department: Department of Social Work and Social Administration
abstract: As the global movement to scale up school-based social and emotional learning (SEL) accelerates, cultivating teachers’ own social and emotional competencies (SEC) has emerged as a cornerstone for effective, sustainable, and equitable SEL implementation. Teacher SEL refers to empowering educators to embody core SEC, to enhance their well-being and SEL practices, thereby fostering caring and inclusive environments that support all students’ growth and well-being. In East Asia, both teachers and students face mounting mental health challenges, yet research on teacher SEL in this region is limited—especially regarding its impact on overall outcomes and disparities therein. The Principal Investigator (PI) has advanced the field by developing and validating a multidimensional measure of teacher SEC, including competencies closely related to promoting equitable learning conditions. The PI’s work has demonstrated links among teacher SEC, teacher well-being, and student SEL outcomes, and explored the impact of universal SEL programs on student disparities. However, experimental evidence on teacher-focused SEL interventions—both in terms of average effects and equity implications—is scarce in East Asia. This project addresses these gaps by leveraging digital technology for remote learning and real-time personalized support through the development and evaluation of SEL4U, an online professional development program, and SELbot, an AI-powered supplementary coaching tool. SEL4U, developed with the Greater Good Science Center at UC Berkeley and culturally adapted with East Asian educators and researchers, integrates asynchronous online modules with synchronous collaborative sessions. SELbot, co-developed with a Hong Kong-based AI company, offers individualized, interactive support. Both interventions emphasize not only general SEC but also equity-promoting competencies tailored to regional sociocultural contexts. The study will employ a three-arm cluster randomized controlled trial (RCT) in 30 Hong Kong public primary schools (approximately 360 teachers and 7,200 students), comparing (1) SEL4U only, (2) SEL4U plus SELbot, and (3) a control group. Leveraging established partnerships with local schools and a NGO, the study will measure outcomes at baseline and post-intervention, including teacher SEC, teacher well-being, SEL implementation, classroom climate, student SEC, and student well-being. The project will assess both direct, indirect, and trickle-down effects of SEL4U, and the incremental value of SELbot, on average and across diverse student subgroups. By rigorously evaluating culturally relevant, technology-supported SEL professional development, this project aims to generate robust evidence on effectiveness, feasibility, and equity impacts, guiding the future scale-up of teacher SEL initiatives in East Asia. N/A

titleEnglish: BiSyntax Builder: Enhancing Bilingual Syntactic Awareness and Reading Comprehension Using a Hybrid Factorial-SMART Design
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chou, KeeLee Prof Deacon, S. Hélène
abstract: Children’s ability to understand written texts sets the foundation for learning at school and success at work. However, reading comprehension worldwide is declining, and educators are struggling to effectively support students’ mastery of reading comprehension, a task even more complex in a Hong Kong bilingual environment, where children must learn two structurally distinct languages, Chinese (L1) and English (L2). Approximately 13% of Hong Kong Chinese-English bilingual children experience reading comprehension difficulties in both languages, most often related to weaknesses in syntactic or morphosyntactic awareness. Our recently published studies suggest that syntactic structure binds lexical and sentence information, then contributes to reading comprehension through two pathways: syntax-to-lexicon and syntax-to-sentence. This project extends our previous investigations by developing the BiSyntax (Bilingual Intervention on Syntax) programs to strengthen these pathways in bilingual children. Employing an adaptive 4-arm randomized controlled trial (RCT) with a 2 × 2 factorial design and a 2-stage sequential multiple assignment randomized trial (SMART) design, we will: (1) develop L1 Chinese and L2 English syntax interventions and evaluate their effectiveness on improving syntactic awareness and reading comprehension in each language; (2) identify optimal pathways (i.e., syntax-to-lexicon, syntax-to-sentence, or combined) for improving bilingual children’s syntactic awareness and reading comprehension; (3) test whether increasing the intensity of one pathway or adding additional booth activities benefits participants who show no improvement from initial training; and (4) assess the crossover effects of Chinese syntactic training to English syntactic awareness and reading comprehension, and vice versa. We will recruit 378 Chinese-English bilingual children (ages 10–11) from eight primary schools. Participants will be randomized into one of three embedded two-stage adaptive interventions, which differ in their pathway sequence: starting with syntax-to-lexicon, starting with syntax-to-sentence, following a combined pathway, or a control group. All children will complete pretest, training, posttest 1, and posttest 2. During the pretest and posttest, participants will complete behavioral assessments of Chinese and English syntactic awareness and reading comprehension, and measures of control variables. By integrating factorial and adaptive methodologies, this project will advance precision education in both theory and practice. Theoretically, it will disentangle syntax-to-lexicon versus syntax-to-sentence pathways; test whether their effects are additive, synergistic, or redundant; and quantify cross-language transfer. Practically, our project will replace static group assignments and one-size-fits-all methods with individualized instruction that dynamically adapts to students’ ongoing responsiveness, and promote continuously refined strategies to improve both teaching effectiveness and academic outcomes. N/A

titleEnglish: A randomised control trial of an individualised and AI-assisted nap-optimisation protocol in emerging adults with subclinical insomnia, depression, and anxiety
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Antoni B. Prof Chan, Christian Shaunlyn Prof Chung, Ka Fai Prof HO, Yan Yee Fiona Prof Hsiao, Janet Hui-wen Miss Kojima, Hinako Dr Lee, Jung Jae Jay Prof Li, Jun Dr SUN, Iris Rong Wei
abstract: Is napping good or bad for our mental health? Existing studies offered piecemeal and contradictory answers. This 'napping paradox' is at least partly a result of the differences in the study populations and the lack of specification of napping features, as well as the cross-sectional correlational design commonly used. Without unequivocal evidence, sleep authorities generally warn the public about the potential detrimental effects of napping on nighttime sleep, particularly for individuals with insomnia. Healthy individuals are loosely recommended to nap as needed or wanted, earlier in the day and for a short duration. The handful of clinical guidelines addressing napping tends to neglect specific factors, e.g. napping frequency, reasons, intentionality, and regularity. Even less has been done in understanding and optimising the effects of napping, and none, to our knowledge, in individuals with subclinical symptoms of insomnia, depression or anxiety, which are highly prevalent in the community, especially in university students. In this randomised control trial, a novel personalised nap-optimization protocol will be conducted, based on individuals’ naturalistic nap-mood pattern (Nap-Op intervention group, n=150), in addition to a general sleep health education (SH) for all participants (SH only control group, n = 100). Over 28 days (and nights), we shall comprehensively monitor the associations of napping behaviours and rest activity rhythms (RAR) with subsequent nighttime sleep quality and self-reported cognitive-affective states, using actigraphy watches, portable electroencephalographic (EEG) headbands, sleep diaries, and ecological momentary assessments (EMA). Utilising the deep learning approach of Hidden Markov Modelling (HMM) to quantify the 24-hour continuous patterns of activity (wake) and rest (daytime napping and nighttime sleep), in conjunction with other napping characteristics gathered through both objective tools and self-reports, we shall conduct the nap-optimization protocol to give napping recommendations on timing, duration, and frequency in planning for naps, based on the identified individualised RAR patterns as predictors of sleep and cognitive-affective states. To evaluate the efficacy of the nap-optimization protocol, in comparison to the general napping recommendations focusing on contraindications and precautions, we shall analyse the within- and between-group differences in mood outcomes, nighttime sleep quality, and also RAR, baseline and post-intervention. Assisted by machine learning, tracking technologies, and sophisticated statistical analyses, this study will help identify the 'who' (baseline individual characteristics as moderators) and the 'how' (behavioural and EEG features of naps) for optimising napping as an individualised evidence-based practice to achieve the goal of precision beyond medicine to community health for all. N/A

titleEnglish: Bilingual Self-Teaching in Chinese Orthographic Learning: Revisiting the Self-Teaching Hypothesis within a Combinatorial Framework among Cantonese–Mandarin Bilingual Children
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Li, Hui Dr Wang, Hua-Chen
abstract: This work seeks to advance scientific understanding of the processes by which bilingual children acquire robust written word knowledge through independent text reading. One of the most influential theories in explaining children’s written word learning is the Self-Teaching Hypothesis (Share, 1995), which emphasizes the centrality of phonological recoding (print-sound translation) in written word learning. This hypothesis has received abundant empirical support across writing systems, including Chinese; however, the original hypothesis did not consider bilingual contexts, and this gap has been mirrored in existing empirical studies. In a multilingual era, more than half of the world’s population is bilingual, and many children face a medium-of-instruction (MoI) mismatch. Such a mismatch often requires young children to pick up a new spoken language for schooling that differs from their mother tongue, thereby introducing obstacles to reading development and beyond. In Hong Kong specifically, the MoI for learning to read Chinese shifts between Cantonese (local dominant language) and Mandarin (national standard). Thus, a re-evaluation and refinement of the Self-Teaching Hypothesis in bilingual written word learning is urgent, with implications for both theory and educational practice. To this aim, this project is among the first to test the hypothesis in bilingual children learning to read the same writing system through two spoken languages. Beyond a MoI mismatch obstacle, Cantonese-Mandarin bilingual children must cope with a spoken-written register gap when the MoI is Cantonese, because Cantonese does not map onto written Chinese as closely as Mandarin. But how do bilingual children pick up written words differently in their mother tongue and MoI early in their lives? Relatedly, how do these children overcome the MoI mismatch obstacle and the spoken-written register gap over their reading development? While the PI’s pilot work on Grade 2 children in Hong Kong supports the feasibility of them reading Chinese words in Cantonese and Mandarin, it is not intuitive to unpack how they self-teach Chinese in two spoken languages. Therefore, three studies will be conducted to clarify children’s bilingual self-teaching development. The overarching aim is to uncover the underlying mechanisms by which Cantonese-Mandarin bilingual children self-teach Chinese in their mother tongue versus MoI (Studies 1 and 2) and how their ability to self-teach Chinese in two spoken languages develops longitudinally (Study 3). The findings will deepen understanding of critical mechanisms underlying literacy development across languages and will inform effective reading instruction in multilingual contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Living Cultural Heritage and Youth Identity: A Sociolinguistic Study of School Mottos and Anthems in Hong Kong, London, Guangzhou, and Singapore
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Hou , Xingquan Prof Lee, John Chi-Kin Prof O’Regan, John Dr Shen, Ruiqing
abstract: School mottos and anthems are far more than slogans or songs; they are powerful symbols that reflect a school’s values and history. Recited or sung daily, they shape students’ sense of identity and belonging. While common themes like “love” or “diligence” often appear, there remains a gap in understanding why certain values emerge in specific cultural settings or how these symbols influence students’ identity and community ties. This project seeks to address this gap by exploring the social and cultural forces behind these traditions and their effects on youth. The study compares schools in four global cities: Hong Kong, London, Guangzhou, and Singapore. Hong Kong, with its unique mix of Eastern and Western influences, is the central focus. It is compared to London (a hub of Western education), Guangzhou (representing Chinese cultural roots without colonial history), and Singapore (a similarly multicultural society). This comparison acts as a “natural experiment” to uncover how factors like colonial history, national policies, and religious beliefs shape school symbols and their meanings. The research adopts an exploratory sequential mixed-methods design. In Phase I, we analyze 100-200 school mottos and anthems per city through archival and textual studies to identify key values and historical trends. Phase II builds on this by conducting fieldwork in selected schools, using a balanced selection strategy. It integrates qualitative methods (participant observation and focus-group interviews) with quantitative surveys of 300 students per city. This approach investigates how these school symbols function as “living heritage” and influence students’ identities and sense of community. The study draws on Critical Discourse Analysis to uncover hidden messages in symbols, the Ethnography of Communication to examine everyday interactions, and Social Identity Theory to assess psychological impacts. This integrated framework bridges broad cultural trends with individual experiences, offering a fresh, comprehensive view of how school traditions pass on cultural heritage. Ultimately, the findings will offer practical insights for educators and policymakers on using school symbols to foster community and enhance educational outcomes, aligning with UNESCO’s Sustainable Development Goals (e.g., SDG 4, Quality Education). By focusing on Hong Kong while contributing to global knowledge, this project aims to make a meaningful impact in sociolinguistics, cultural studies, and education, emphasizing local relevance with international significance. N/A

titleEnglish: Tanka as Linguistic Interface on the Maritime Silk Road of China: Contact, Evolution, and Convergence
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Peyraube, Alain Prof Simmons, Richard Van Ness
abstract: The Maritime Silk Road (MSR), active from the 2nd century BCE to the 19th century CE, was a crucial network of cross-cultural exchange, with multilingual port hubs like Guangzhou facilitating Eurasian trade. While existing research has extensively covered material exchanges and elite actors, it critically overlooks the sociolinguistic processes mediated by subaltern communities (Gap 1). This oversight is compounded by contact linguistics’ limited focus on maritime multilingualism and marginalized agency (Gap 2), as well as fragmented research on the Tanka people, who historically served as crucial linguistic and trade intermediaries in South Chinese ports. Despite their centuries-long role as a maritime linguistic conduit bridging Sinitic, Austronesian, and Eurasian traders, no study systematically examines how the Tanka language functioned as a dynamic linguistic interface between maritime and terrestrial language spheres, nor documents its endangered state in Southeast China (Gap 3). Building on PI’s five grants for Tanka studies (3 RGC and 2 University grants), this project reconceptualizes the Tanka not merely as subjects but as a historically evolving linguistic interface that mediated multilingual encounters along the MSR. Using a combined qualitative and quantitative approach, we employ structured interviews as the primary method to collect extensive linguistic data on Tanka language varieties from key MSR ports: Guangzhou, Quanzhou, Haikou, and Beihai, encompassing six field sites with 12 informants. This includes a substantial corpus of phonetic, lexical, and grammatical features, alongside naturalistic speech samples. Grounded in theories of language contact and convergence, the project traces the historical trajectory from contact-induced change to linguistic evolution and, ultimately, convergence. We employ an integrated three-part analytical framework examining contact (mechanisms of language interaction and power dynamics), evolution (diachronic changes in Tanka language), and convergence (linguistic outcomes in maritime contact zones). The project generates transformative impacts across multiple dimensions. Theoretically, it challenges unidirectional diffusion models by introducing the Port Language Chain Model, which emphasizes Tanka agency in multidirectional transmission. It also proposes the Maritime Convergence Nexus Model, redefining marginalized groups as active architects of trade lingua franca. Methodologically, it establishes protocols for documenting endangered maritime contact languages applicable globally. Practically, the project produces the first Dictionary of Tanka Language, an essential resource for preserving intangible cultural heritage, while providing evidence-based insights for equitable language policies in multilingual port cities. By positioning Tanka at the forefront of revising MSR linguistic history, this project redefines our understanding of language contact, convergence, and subaltern resilience in globalization. N/A

titleEnglish: Toward a peer-support model in reducing loneliness among older adults: Unpacking the mechanism and dyadic process of volunteering using a dual randomized controlled trial
department: Department of Special Education and Counselling
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Choi, Namkee G Prof Chou, Kee Lee Prof Warner, Lisa M. Prof YEUNG, Yuen Lan Dannii
abstract: Globally, three major trends have emerged: 1)rapid population aging; 2)a growing loneliness epidemic, impacting mental and cognitive health of older adults; and 3)despite these challenges, today’s older adults are better educated and possess greater socioemotional resources than previous generations, positioning them as potential active contributors to society. Integrating these trends, an empirically supported strategy to combat loneliness among older adults is to develop a peer-support model through volunteering, whereby older adults deliver non-specialist psychosocial interventions to lonely peers. In a completed project, our team successfully conducted a dual randomized controlled trial (RCT), in which we trained 185(vs.190 active controls) older volunteers feeling lonely to deliver psychosocial interventions to 1,151 vulnerable peers feeling lonely. We found significant mutual benefits: 1)volunteers experienced reduced loneliness and improved mental health at 6- and 12-month follow-ups against active controls; and 2)recipients reported loneliness reduction and mental health improvement at 3-month, 6-month, and 12-month follow-ups against their active controls. However, key questions remain:1)Are the effects of this peer-support model reciprocal at the dyad (volunteer-recipient pair) level? 2)What mechanisms drive these benefits at the individual and interpersonal levels? 3)What dyadic relationship factors predict the effects for both? Theoretically and methodologically, research predominantly focused on either volunteers or recipients, largely overlooking the reciprocal effects and interpersonal dynamics, despite empirical evidence supporting their importance for intervention outcomes. To address these gaps, our proposed dual RCT comprises two interconnected trials. In the Volunteer RCT, 384 lonely older adults will be randomized to either lay counselor volunteer groups to deliver psychosocial interventions or active control groups. In the Recipient RCT, a separate sample of 192 lonely older recipients will be randomly assigned to one of two psychosocial interventions delivered by volunteers. Volunteers and recipients will be randomly matched in a one-to-one dyadic structure. We will examine: 1)the effects of the peer-support model on both volunteers and recipients at individual and dyadic levels; 2)underlying mechanisms; and 3)whether and how interpersonal perception and exchange during intervention delivery predict outcomes. Data will be collected at baseline, during intervention delivery, and at 3- and 6-month follow-ups. The primary outcome is loneliness; secondary outcomes include psychological well-being, perceived stress, depression, anxiety, and cognitive function. Our project will 1)address significant theoretical and methodological gaps; 2)provide comprehensive understanding on why, how, and for whom models reduce loneliness by combining a dual RCT with daily dyadic assessments; and 3)inform development of peer-support models to promote mental health. N/A

titleEnglish: Language to Literacy: Longitudinal Contributions of Prosodic, Syntactic, and Pragmatic Skills to Reading Development in Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing Children
department: Department of Special Education and Counselling
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Chen, Yuan Dr CHUNG, Wei-Lun Dr YUEN, Chi Pun Kevin
abstract: Deaf and hard-of-hearing (DHH) children show significant delays in language and reading development. Prosody (speech rhythm), syntax (structure rules), and pragmatics (social communication) are core language components that develop rapidly during early childhood and are essential for reading development. Despite their importance, the interrelationships among these language domains and their longitudinal impact on reading development in DHH children remain unclear. To address this gap, this three-year longitudinal study aims to (1) investigate the interconnections between these skills in preschool-aged DHH children; (2) examine their co-development across the preschool-to-primary-school transition; and (3) determine their longitudinal contributions to reading achievement. A total of 300 Mandarin-speaking DHH children with bilateral severe-to-profound hearing loss (better-ear pure-tone average > 70 dB HL at 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 kHz) and 300 age-matched typically-developing (TD) children will be assessed annually from kindergarten year 2 (K2) through Grade 1 (G1) on prosodic (lexical tone identification, phrasal boundary, sentential intonation), syntactic (word order, morphosyntax, grammar), and pragmatic (introduction and responsiveness, nonverbal communication, social-emotional attunement, executive function, and negotiation) skills. Reading outcomes (word reading and reading comprehension) will be assessed at G1. Foundational language and cognitive abilities will be measured annually as controls. A Bayesian multigroup confirmatory factor analysis will model the interconnections between prosody, syntax, and pragmatics in K2 DHH and TD children (Objective 1), hypothesizing weaker interconnections among these domains in DHH children. A Bayesian multigroup latent growth model will estimate and compare the developmental trajectories of these domains between groups from K2 to G1 (Objective 2), anticipating slower linear growth in DHH children. A Bayesian multigroup structural equation model will examine how initial levels and growth rates in each domain predict G1 reading outcomes across groups (Objective 3), hypothesizing that predictive weights will be weaker across all domains for DHH children compared to TD peers. Theoretically, this study advances the prosody-syntax interface model and the Reading Systems Framework by clarifying the longitudinal interdependencies between prosodic, syntactic, and pragmatic skills, highlighting their critical influence on reading development under constrained auditory input conditions in DHH children. Practically, our longitudinal data will help educators identify critical developmental stages and delays in prosodic, syntactic, and pragmatic skills among DHH children, guiding targeted support during their preschool-to-primary-school transition. By integrating theoretical insights with practice, our research will inform policymakers on resource allocation for supporting language and literacy development, advocating for equitable systems to enhance DHH children’s long-term academic success. N/A

titleEnglish: Hong Kong as a city of translations: the semiotics of place-making in linguistic and cultural dynamics
department: Department of Linguistics and Modern Language Studies
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Liu, Fung Ming Christy
abstract: For over half a century, Hong Kong’s visual identity has been shaped by the extraordinary density of urban texts, where Chinese and English signs created a distinctive streetscape that symbolized the city’s vitality and cross-cultural character. These texts form a holistic urban discourse that shapes social interactions and cultural meanings. Although recent regulations have led to the removal of many large-scale signs, Hong Kong remains a text-rich environment where multiple languages and scripts continue to shape public spaces, urban discourses, and cultural identity in evolving forms reflecting social and technological change. Previous studies have focused mainly on contemporary practices or isolated periods, often overlooking the evolution of urban textscapes amid shifting sociocultural and geopolitical realities, and the impact of physical carriers (from three-dimensional signage to mobile displays) on the city’s semiotic ecosystem. The interplay among text display, urban density, and architectural styles remains undertheorized, as does the spectrum of translation practices mediating between literal linguistic conversion and broader cultural acculturation. This project examines public signs in Hong Kong as a holistic system of everyday discourse, situating developments from 2015 to 2027 within a broader historical and sociopolitical context. Through fieldwork across diverse neighborhoods, from text-rich vernacular areas to text-poor financial districts, I will investigate the interaction of signage with color schemes, architectural details, and other semiotic resources in constructing urban visual identity. The research theorizes anew China-Western convergence by conceptualizing Hong Kong as a “city of translations”, where language and semiotic affordances mediate cultural interaction across China, the West, and beyond. Archival research and theoretical analysis of collected data will support an innovative framework for urban languages and semiotics, and the role of translation in shaping civic participation. An interactive digital platform will be established to serve as both a research corpus and a public engagement tool, which will enable continuous documentation of Hong Kong’s evolving linguistic landscape and fostering community participation in cultural preservation and creative imagination. This project will advance our understanding of urban spaces as mediators of cultural exchange, providing insights for language policy, heritage conservation, and urban planning. By conceptualizing Hong Kong as a city of translations, it will contribute new theoretical perspectives to sociolinguistics, translation studies, and cultural studies, and innovate our understanding of multilingual urban environments amid rapid technological and social change. N/A

titleEnglish: Does Play Still Matter? Investigating The Roles Of Achievement Goals, Play Beliefs, And Playfulness In Promoting the Well-Being of Senior Primary Children And Their Caretakers
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Fung , Wing Kai Dr Lam, Chun Bun Ian
abstract: A 2023 survey commissioned by the Hong Kong government from the Chinese University of Hong Kong, revealed that 6.9% of local students aged 6 to 17 manifested suicidal behaviours, 6.1% had anxiety and 5.4% suffered depression. Despite continuous government efforts to foster well-being, the mental health situation of primary education stakeholders, among them children, parents, and teachers, and including increases in student suicide and teacher burnout, is worrying. On the one hand, Hong Kong’s highly competitive and result-dependent academic system shapes children’s performance-oriented mindset. On the other, Hong Kong parents highly value academic achievement, investing considerable time and finances to promote their children’s subject knowledge through participating in extra tuition classes or enrichment programmes at the expense of more holistic development. Although people generally recognise that play is essential to facilitate learning and development in early childhood, school-aged children’s need to play tends to be downplayed or disregarded to squeeze extra time for academic activities. This study proposes a framework that acknowledges that children’s achievement goal orientation and playfulness are both important factors in sustaining their well-being through the senior primary years. Other influential factors include the goal orientation and play beliefs of parents and teachers that affect their parenting styles, parent-child conflicts, and teacher-child relatedness, and their own well-being. The potential influences of these factors on children’s academic skills (e.g., reading and mathematics) will also be explored. The study employs a longitudinal design with three data collection points over three years (Time 1 - primary 4, Time 2 – primary 5, Time 3 - primary 6), incorporating a series of survey and direct-assessed measures, to collect data from 300 participating children and their parents and teachers. Multilevel path analysis will be used to estimate the longitudinal predictions among the variables. This study expands and augments current theory by examining the unique roles of achievement goal orientation and playfulness in the well-being of senior primary school children. Practically, it also informs alternative approaches to improving the well-being of parents and teachers in the Hong Kong primary education sector. N/A

titleEnglish: FAST PE: Facilitating Accelerated Subjective Time in Physical Education
department: Department of Health and Physical Education
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Chan, Ching Yat Roy Prof Ha, Sau Ching Amy Prof Hettinga, Florentina Dr Leung, Ka Man Prof Marcora, Samuele Dr Tse, Choi Yeung Andy
abstract: Adolescent physical inactivity is a major public health concern, associated with long-term risks of obesity, cardiovascular disease, and poor mental health into adulthood. In Hong Kong, where academic attainment is strongly prioritised and PE occupies limited curricular space, students’ opportunities to experience activity as enjoyable and meaningful are restricted. Existing interventions have typically addressed structural barriers or used technology as novelty, but these approaches have rarely produced lasting change. This project adopts a psychophysiological approach, focusing on how pupils experience the passage of time during PE and whether this experience is linked to enjoyment, value, and sustained motivation. Time perception in this context refers to how long an activity feels while it is happening and how its duration is remembered. Activities perceived as shorter than chronological time are more likely to be recalled positively, enjoyed, and repeated. Conversely, when time drags, activities are often considered effortful or aversive. The project investigates whether these temporal impressions can serve as an innovative mechanism for improving adolescent engagement in PE and establishing positive, healthy behaviours that can be sustained into adulthood. Approximately 140 students aged 13–15 will participate in a longitudinal repeated-measures study embedded within standard 60-minute lessons. Baseline assessments will capture pupils’ typical experiences of time, enjoyment, and motivation in non-standardised PE sessions, alongside the simple physiological index of heart rate variability (HRV) which provides a practical, non-invasive marker of attentional regulation. Students will complete three structured lessons delivered through an approach-motivation framework designed to be autonomy-supportive, mastery-focused, and consistent across schools. After each session, pupils will report perceived time, enjoyment, task value, behavioural intention, and effort, with effort distinguished as both aversive cost and motivational engagement. HRV will be recorded during the sessions to provide supporting evidence of the psychophysiological processes associated with temporal experience. A six-month follow-up will assess how lessons are recalled and whether experiences of time are associated with longer-term motivation and intention. By reconceptualising PE as both a behavioural and temporal experience, FAST PE provides a theoretically grounded framework for enhancing adolescent activity and guiding future interventions, ensuring that innovations, including technology, are not short-term novelties but aligned with how time is perceived. Findings may inform curriculum design, teacher development, and public health strategy, while shaping how pupils experience, recall, and value activity. The project lays innovative foundations for sustained behavioural practices and investigates how perceptions of time influence engagement across wider contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Contested Educational Futures: A Qualitative Longitudinal Study of Alternative Pathways in Chinese Homeschooling
department: Department of Education Policy and Leadership
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Hsu, Yung-kang Prof Qian, Haiyan Prof Wang, Hui-Lan
abstract: While homeschooling has been growing globally, most research on this topic relies on cross-sectional studies and parental accounts, which have lacked longitudinal evidence on how parents and children navigate challenges and sustain homeschooling during educational transitions. This gap is particularly pronounced in non-Western, high-stakes Chinese contexts where educational credentials are highly valued, and families face unique sociocultural and regulatory tensions. Without longitudinal, child-inclusive insights, policymakers and educators lack the evidence to effectively address educational diversity and emerging educational models in the post-pandemic and AI era. Accordingly, we propose a longitudinal study to investigate the educational trajectories of Chinese homeschooling families while disentangling the processes and outcomes related to child development and transitions. Building on the PI’s foundational study (2021-2023) of homeschooling families in Taipei and Hong Kong—two Chinese cities with shared cultural backgrounds but differing legal approaches to homeschooling—the proposed study tracks the original cohort as their adolescent children face transitions (e.g., university admissions, vocational accreditation). We will also recruit 30-50 new families who began homeschooling recently to capture generational and contextual shifts, such as post-pandemic motivations and digital learning adaptations. The study will employ a qualitative longitudinal research (QLR) design. Semi-structured interviews will be conducted with parents from both the original and new cohorts to explore their views, practices, and challenges. Interviews will also be conducted with children aged 12+ to examine their perspectives and outcomes. Observations on pedagogical interactions, family dynamics, and learning environments will complement the interview narratives—researchers will conduct field visits during typical learning times for a sub-sample of families in both cohorts (approximately 10-15, selected for diversity and willingness). An Iterative Thematic Inquiry (ITI) approach will be adopted to analyze data, along with systematic within-family and cross-cohort comparisons, to identify continuities, changes, factors shaping trajectories, and outcomes. The study’s significance lies in its unique capacity to generate globally relevant knowledge on how homeschooling, as an alternative educational pathway, is sustained, adapted, or discontinued amidst challenging transitions and contexts. By longitudinally tracking families and incorporating children’s voices within the Chinese context, our findings will offer theoretical insights into the negotiation of educational agency and structural constraints, while (re-)imagining educational futures relevant to societies facing the rise of emerging educational models. The findings will also inform policies on educational diversification, offering empirical evidence for designing supportive frameworks for non-traditional learning in competitive ecosystems. N/A

titleEnglish: The Role of Larynx and Tongue Height in Tone Production by L1 and L2 Speakers: An Ultrasound Study
department: Department of Linguistics and Modern Language Studies
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Chen, Shuwen
abstract: This project builds on the applicant’s current GRF study (#18601423) which investigates tone-vowel interaction in Cantonese and Mandarin. This line of work challenges the traditional ‘source and filter’ assumption that tone and segments are independent of each other. In this proposal, we seek to extend our current work on the tongue to another articulator – the larynx, using ultrasound imaging. This used to receive little attention in the past due to the low temporal resolution of ultrasound scanners, but is now emerging in phonetics due to much higher frame rates. To date, there are only a handful of published studies looking at larynx height with ultrasound (notably by Marc Brunelle at U of Ottawa) on Southeast Asian languages, leaving behind a huge gap in the literature on Chinese languages which also have rich tonal inventories. This project seeks to answer the following questions: - How do L1 and L2 speakers differ in tone production (esp. ‘tonal coarticulation’) - How are the movements of the tongue and the larynx temporally coordinated during tone production? - Does larynx height predict individual differences in tone-vowel interaction? We plan to test native Cantonese and Mandarin speakers, who also speak the other language as L2. Twenty participants will be recruited for each L1 group. They will produce Cantonese and Mandarin (either as L1 or L2) disyllables, and have their voice, as well as tongue and larynx movement recorded using ultrasound imaging. Our findings will enhance our understanding of articulatory planning and execution with respect to tone. N/A

titleEnglish: Modeling Heritage Bai Vocabulary Attrition: The Predictive Role of Language Dominance in Bai-Putonghua Bilingual Development
department: Centre for Research on Chinese Language and Education
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Cai, Mingjia Dr Li, Xuan
abstract: Heritage language vocabulary attrition refers to the reduced accessibility or loss of lexical items in a minority language. It has become a vital lens for understanding the intricate interplay between language processing and sociolinguistic environments in bilingual development. While recent research increasingly recognizes language dominance as a dynamic, multidimensional sociopsychological construct influencing this regression, the specific ways in which language dominance affects heritage language attrition remain underexplored. Key unresolved questions include not only whether and how language attrition occurs, but also which facets of language dominance most strongly predict attrition trajectories over time. Building on our previous research with adult Bai-Putonghua bilinguals, this project aims to fill these gaps by adopting a longitudinal, multidimensional approach to measuring language dominance as a predictor of real-time heritage Bai vocabulary attrition. We will follow 300 Bai-Putonghua bilingual children in Dali, Yunnan, China, throughout their first two years of formal Putonghua schooling—a critical period marked by rapid shifts in their language environment. Methodologically, this project addresses limitations in the existing literature, which often treats vocabulary attrition as a unitary process and rarely integrates dynamic assessments of language dominance with detailed longitudinal linguistic data. To overcome these shortcomings, we employ repeated sampling, multifaceted lexical metrics, comprehensive dominance profiling, and advanced statistical modelling.
