Lexical attrition will be measured through a picture description task designed to elicit spontaneous language use while controlling for individual memory capacity. A comprehensive set of lexical indices, including token count, mean length of utterance, moving-average type-token ratio, words per minute, and parts-of-speech distribution, will be analyzed. Language dominance will be assessed using a validated, multidimensional questionnaire capturing four key dimensions: language use, history, attitude, and proficiency. Data on lexical performance and language dominance will be collected at five time points. Analyses will begin with ANOVA and proceed to advanced techniques such as generalized linear mixed models and latent growth curve modelling to examine both cross-sectional and longitudinal effects of language dominance. This study represents one of the first systematic efforts to link dynamic profiles of language dominance with real-time heritage vocabulary attrition in early bilinguals. By integrating sociolinguistic and psycholinguistic perspectives and applying rigorous multidimensional frameworks alongside fine-grained lexical measures, the project contributes to advancing empirical research on the dynamics of heritage language change. The findings will have significant theoretical implications for models of bilingual development and practical applications in heritage language preservation and minority language maintenance. N/A

titleEnglish: Lexical Evidence for the Divergence Time of Liangshan Yi
department: Centre for Research on Chinese Language and Education
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Wang, Feng
abstract: This study employs lexical evidence to investigate three unresolved questions regarding the history of the Liangshan Yi language (Ngwi or Loloish, Tibeto-Burman) and the peopling of the Liangshan Yi speakers. The two linguistic questions are: (1) when the Liangshan Yi language split from other Loloish languages in Yunnan and Guizhou, and (2) when the Liangshan Yi dialects diverged from one another. By establishing these linguistic divergence timelines, this research subsequently addresses a key ethnohistorical question: (3) when the Liangshan Yi people migrated across the Jinsha River from Yunnan into Sichuan. Methodologically, six Liangshan Yi dialects and four Loloish languages from Yunnan and Guizhou were examined. The lexical data collection focuses on terms that provide temporal insights into when these concepts had emerged, including how Chinese loanwords were adapted into the Loloish languages based on Chinese historical phonology. These words can be categorized into three historical layers: Pre-Han concepts (before 220 CE), such as stone, bronze, iron, wheat, barley, and buckwheat; Tang-Song concepts (618 to 1279 CE), such as eggplant, matchmaker, and fertilizer; and Ming-Qing concepts (1368 to 1912 CE), such as maize, tobacco, potato, and peanut. These concepts are either native of the Loloish languages or borrowed from Chinese. Strict sound correspondences across all phonological categories are examined to establish relatedness. If sound correspondence is established across all Loloish varieties under comparison, it indicates no divergence at the time when the concepts emerged. Conversely, a lack of correspondence suggests divergence. To support the strict sound correspondence among the selected lexicon, additional lexical evidence from the same ten Loloish varieties, totaling approximately 25,000 lexical items, is employed to form the empirical foundation. The findings will be used to: 1. To determine whether Liangshan Yi is phylogenetically related to other Loloish languages in Yunnan and Guizhou. While we expect vocabulary expressing early concepts to show regular sound correspondences and later concepts to show divergence, we may observe alternative correspondence patterns. These could indicate that some languages currently classified as "Ngwi" or "Loloish" do not belong to its current (sub)group. 2. To establish the upper and lower temporal limits for the divergence of the Liangshan Yi language from other Loloish languages in Yunnan and Guizhou, and the divergence of the Liangshan Yi dialects from each other. 3. To infer the timing of Liangshan Yi migration into Sichuan. As language divergence primarily results from speaker population separation, estimated divergence times directly inform migration chronology. N/A

titleEnglish: Generative AI and Second Language Writing: Investigating Learner Development, Designing a Pedagogical Framework for Teacher Education, and Validating Practice Across Languages
department: Department of Linguistics and Modern Language Studies
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Lee, Ju Seong Prof Wang, Yijen
abstract: The rapid integration of generative artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT into educational contexts has created what can be termed an “implementation-before-investigation” phenomenon, where pedagogical practice has outpaced empirical research on learning outcomes. This study addresses the fundamental question of whether generative AI supports genuine second language (L2) writing development or merely enhances performance whilst technological support is available. Through a rigorous three-phase mixed-methods design, this project investigates both learner development and teacher preparation across multiple linguistic contexts. Phase 1 employs a quasi-experimental study with 200 L2 English learners using a matrix design that alternates between AI-supported and independent writing tasks. Crucially, pre- and post-benchmark assessments conducted without AI support will measure authentic development using established quantitative metrics (error-free T-units, lexical diversity) and qualitative measures (coherence, cohesion). Systematic interventions will introduce evidence-based strategies including prompt engineering, critical evaluation of AI output, and AI-assisted revision workflows. Phase 2 develops and validates a pedagogical framework through training 300 student teachers across English and modern languages programmes (French, Japanese, Korean). This cross-linguistic approach enables investigation of framework transferability across different orthographic systems and pedagogical traditions. Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modelling will examine relationships between teacher beliefs, confidence, perceived AI usefulness, and pedagogical intentions. Phase 3 validates findings through classroom implementation, following student teachers into practicum and early in-service contexts. Simplified assessment tools from Phase 1 will monitor authentic student development whilst PLS-SEM modelling validates predictive relationships between teacher preparation and observed pedagogical behaviours. This research directly addresses critical gaps in our understanding of the impact of AI on language education by differentiating between AI-enhanced performance and sustained learning development. The validated pedagogical framework will provide scalable, evidence-based guidance for responsible AI integration, with cross-linguistic validation ensuring applicability across Hong Kong’s multilingual educational landscape and beyond. Outcomes will inform teacher education curricula, institutional policies, and assessment practices, contributing to principled approaches to AI integration during this transformative period in language education. N/A

titleEnglish: Arts-Based Performances (ABPs) in Hong Kong Kindergartens: Stakeholders’ Attitudes and Self-Reported Practices
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr KONG, Siu Hang Eric Prof Leung, Suzannie Kit-ying Dr Mendoza, Norman Biliwang Dr Wong, Kit Mei (Betty) Dr Yang, Weipeng
abstract: Evidence from our prior project (#04592) suggests that the practice of offering public arts-based performances (ABPs) in Hong Kong kindergartens (e.g., children’s concerts, theater plays, dances) might have unintended negative consequences. In semi-structured interviews, teachers and principals commented that preparing ABPs is: (a) potentially detrimental to children’s learning, as regular classroom activities are interrupted for rehearsals; (b) not beneficial for the artistic creativity and self-expression of children, as scripts for the performances are generally pre-determined by adults; and (c) often stressful for teachers, leading to increased anxiety and even intentions to leave the teaching profession. No prior study has focused on the topic of ABPs. The aim of our future research program is to systematically examine this commonly taken-for-granted cultural practice, which could be inadvertently impacting on children’s learning and teachers’ wellbeing. This initial project will give voice to the main four stakeholders in kindergarten education: teachers, principals, parents, and children. Adopting a neutral stance and guided by the Theory of Planned Behavior, we will investigate three research questions: RQ1: What are the attitudes toward ABPs of Hong Kong kindergarten teachers, principals, parents, and children (focusing on cognitive, affective, and behavioral factors), and how do these attitudes differ across stakeholder groups? RQ2: What are the self-reported ABP pedagogical practices of teachers and principals, and what factors influence the adoption of these practices? RQ3: How do teachers’ and principals’ attitudes toward ABPs correlate with their self-reported ABP pedagogical practices? We will use a sequential exploratory mixed-methods design (QUAL→QUAN), with semi-structured individual interviews (qualitative) followed by surveys (quantitative). Data collection instruments will be tailored to each stakeholder. Interviewees (n = 80) will be recruited from 20 carefully selected kindergartens, including the principal, one K3 teacher, one K3 child, and his/her parent in each school. Transcripts will be analyzed using open and axial coding. Based on the findings, we will design four principle-based surveys (one per stakeholder). Surveys will include four to six items for each of the three latent factors. A chain-referral sampling method will be used to recruit 60 principals, 100 K3 teachers, 170 parents, and 210 children from kindergartens across Hong Kong. Descriptive, non-parametric, and parametric statistical analyses will be conducted. Findings will lead to subsequent projects, interventions, frameworks, and policies to better foster children’s artistic creativity and self-expression in kindergartens. N/A

titleEnglish: Crafting to Sing Praises: The Making of Parallel-Verse Hagiographies of Seventh-Century Tang China
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: The seventh century witnessed a zenith of hagiographic writing in the form of parallel verse (pianwen) in Tang-dynasty China. In this new development of Confucianism, Daoism, and Buddhism, why was there a surge of exquisite hagiographies and what role did they play? The proposed project studies this vogue of writing parallel-verse hagiographies from new perspectives and with important goals to achieve. This material has long remained understudied mainly because of two biases, namely 1) modern literary studies of medieval China favoring poetry, a term narrowly understood as the equivalent of shi 詩, almost exclusively; 2) the convention is to regard this kind of writing as intellectually shallow and predictable. The PI strives to refute these biased views by restoring the aesthetic value of this material and, in so doing, broadening the coverage of Tang verse as well as providing first-hand materials for how religious and intellectual schools strove to promote themselves. The material under investigation achieved aesthetic appeal, a main aspect for recovering its value. These hagiographists employed florid diction, perfect parallel couplets, and apposite allusions in carefully designed structures in portraying the religious or intellectual apotheosized figures. The project will analyze these rhetorical devices to demonstrate why they achieved such a high place and why writers of the time vied for recognition by means of these kinds of compositions. Given their large number and disparity in literary quality, the texts under investigation, from printed materials, manuscripts, and stele inscriptions, must be selective to highlight their major contribution. The hagiographies selected for our investigation are mainly by renowned writers of the time, such as Wang Bo (650–676), Yang Jiong (b. 650), Lu Zhaolin (d. ca. 689), and Chen Ziang (661–702). In addition, some works of monumental value in the respective religions/schools will also be selected. About ten such pieces of writing have been chosen for intensive investigation; each piece comprises about 3,500 to 4,500 characters. Restoration of the aesthetic appeal of seventh-century hagiographies is of crucial importance in relevant scholarships. It renders due recognition of many understudied literary works and broadens the coverage of Tang verse in the study of Tang literature. It also provides annotated translations for the study of medieval Chinese religions, which has long been based on limited primary sources. These main contributions will apply to the Chinese and Western-language speaking worlds; the project will conduct dialogues between them to enhance interactions. N/A

titleEnglish: Relationship between Self-Efficacy, Source Use, and Source-Based Writing Performance: Considering the Role of Source Modality
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Zhu, Xinhua
abstract: Source-based writing (SBW), defined as the ability to construct an essay for a specific purpose using provided sources, is widely acknowledged as a crucial skill for college students’ academic and career success. However, students often struggle to appropriately integrate sources into their writing. To explain individual differences in writing performance, previous studies have highlighted the importance of source use as a cognitive factor and self-efficacy as a motivational factor. Yet, the mechanism through which these factors interplay to influence writing performance remains unclear. Moreover, most existing research has adopted reading-to-write or reading–listening–writing tasks, leaving a noticeable gap in the exploration of students’ writing when video sources are provided. Given the global shift toward digital and multimodal environments in language education, writing across modalities is rapidly becoming a core literacy skill. Consequently, it becomes very necessary to study the role of source modality in SBW. Focusing on the relationship between source use, self-efficacy and writing performance, the proposed study will involve 300 first- and second-year undergraduates in Hong Kong, who will be randomly assigned to complete a Chinese SBW task. All students will receive the same source content, but the modality will differ: one group will write based on three videos, whereas the other will use written texts transcribed and edited from these videos. Students’ writing self-efficacy, prior knowledge and cognitive load will also be collected. Furthermore, a subsample of 30 students will be invited to attend a post-task stimulated recall interview. All produced texts will undergo detailed textual analysis, beginning with segmentation into T-units to examine source use in terms of idea origin and linguistic transformation. Writing performance will then be obtained through a separate round of scoring. Comparisons will first be made to identify differences between the two groups in source use, self-efficacy, and writing performance. Structural equation modeling will be employed to explore the relationships among these variables in depth. Interview data will be analysed thematically to capture qualitative insights that complement and enrich quantitative findings. This study will not only clarify how source use and self-efficacy interact to influence writing performance, but also deepen our understanding of their characteristics across different source modalities. The findings are also expected to offer practical implications for curriculum design and instructional strategies. Ultimately, we hope better prepare our students for the complex literacy demands of real-world academic and professional contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Assessing the Cross-cultural Effectiveness of Simulation-based Learning for School Leaders in the Asia Pacific Region
department: Department of Education Policy and Leadership
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Banoglu, Koksal Prof Gurr, David Prof Hallinger, Philip Dr Kulophas, Dhirapat Dr Liu, Sheng-nan Dr Thien, Lei Mee
abstract: School leader learning has long been criticized for the slow pace of adopting new learning methods, especially those using learning technologies. Despite early innovators' success in experimenting with simulation-based learning (SBL) in the 20th century, the adoption of SBL remains limited in educational leadership and management (EDLM). The use of simulations is particularly scarce in EDLM programs in the Asia Pacific region. This project responds to several gaps in research and practice. First, there is a need for relevant, high-impact instructional content that leverages learning technologies in preparing school leaders to implement educational reforms. Second, there is a need to validate the effectiveness of active learning methods that address global social and environmental sustainability challenges. Finally, single-society or single-university studies dominate the multi-disciplinary literature on simulation-based learning. Thus, there is a need for comparative studies of simulation effectiveness, especially cross-cultural research. This project leverages an existing English language online computer simulation, Leading Change for Sustainability in Schools (LCSS), to study the effects of SBL on prospective school leaders in five societies (Mainland China, Hong Kong Thailand, Malaysia, and Australia). In the development stage, the research team will translate, culturally adapt, program, and test five new versions of the LCSS simulation in the five societies. Next, the research team will examine the effectiveness of an LCSS instructional module (i.e., intervention) with students in EDLM preparation programs. The learning engagement and outcomes of participant samples will be evaluated and compared across the five societies using a quasi-experimental, non-equivalent groups, time-series research design. Data on the participants’ simulation usage, decision-making, and results will be used to examine changes in behavioral engagement, knowledge, skills, and attitudes. Qualitative data extracted from an online discussion forum will be analyzed with sentiment and thematic analysis to gain further insights into students’ cognitive and behavioral engagement and responses to simulation-based learning. Finally, the research will explore how individual-and societal-level factors influence the effectiveness of SBL. The project will make several significant contributions. First, it will yield five culturally-adapted, validated versions of the online simulation on two essential EDLM curriculum topics: sustainability and change management. Second, the research will yield insights into the use, acceptance, and effectiveness of learning technologies in EDLM programs. Finally, the multi-society, quasi-experimental study conducted in the Asia Pacific region will break new ground in SBL research by analyzing learning engagement and outcomes within a cross-cultural framework. N/A

titleEnglish: A Corpus-Assisted Multimodal Discourse Analysis on Green Burial Promotion in Hong Kong
department: Department of Linguistics and Modern Language Studies
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Lau, Chaak Ming Dr LU, Fangcao Prof Zhu, Hongqiang
abstract: Cremation has been the most popular means of body disposition in Hong Kong; however, this practice raises concerns about pollution, public health, and land shortages, thereby threatening the sustainability of the city’s development. Green burial practices (GBP), such as using biodegradable materials and scattering cremated remains at green burial sites (e.g., gardens or at sea), offer environmentally friendly alternatives that can address the urgent demand for land resources and ease the financial burden on families. Since 2021, the SAR government has launched a campaign to promote GBP. The promotion involves textual and multimodal discourses, including webpages, videos, posters, and online articles. Prior research on the popularisation of GBP has tended to focus on public opinions toward these practices. However, crucial arguments, and linguistic and audio-visual elements that constitute the advertising discourse, have been overlooked, and the effectiveness of such discourse has remained unmeasured. This study perceives the green burials campaign as a form of strategic public communication, where the discourses aim to reformulate addressees’ beliefs, values, attitudes, and behaviours. Adopting a mixed-methods approach, the research will examine textual and multimodal discourses. Specifically, corpus-assisted discourse analysis, with the assistance of large-language models, will be conducted to reveal the discourses/themes and metadiscourse features in the textual data. Moreover, multimodal discourse features of the webpages, videos, and psoters related to GBP will be revealed and examined. These analyses elucidate how linguistic and semiotic patterns, as well as key themes/discourses, are utilised and constructed to (re)contextualise and (re)frame GBP in ways that are compatible with traditional Chinese funeral cultures and to influence addressees’ view towards GBP. To supplement the discourse studies, a survey will be distributed to investigate addressees’ perceptions of the promotional discourse and to measure its effectiveness. The significance of this project is three-fold. First, it contributes to the growing literature on death concepts, funeral culture, and environmental sustainability by explicating how GBP can be made compatible with traditional funeral cultures and help enhance environmental and socio-economic sustainability. Second, it elucidates the dynamic relationships between metadiscourse and contradictory ideologies (i.e., traditionalism and environmentalism) in advertising discourse. Third, it offers practical implications for enhancing the green burial promotion in terms of language style, content organisation, arguments, and the use of audio-visual resources. N/A

titleEnglish: Spatializing STEM Learning for Kindergarteners through Spatial Play Activities: A Controlled Experiment
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chiu, Ming Ming Prof Edwards , Susan Dr Lin, Dan Prof Newcombe, Nora S. Prof Yelland, Nicola
abstract: Young children’s spatial skills influence their later achievements in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM), but early childhood education in Hong Kong often neglects spatial skills. Children are more likely to achieve long-term development of spatial skills through a contextualised curriculum with spatialisation elements than through short-term decontextualised spatial training. Hence, it is important to develop an age-appropriate, play-based curriculum that incorporates spatial learning to develop kindergarteners’ spatial skills. Our pilot studies indicated that spatialized STEM content (e.g., creating a 3D map) enables children to navigate, recognise, visualise, and represent spatial relationships within their physical and visual environments, which can improve their spatial skills (*Hu et al., 2024). However, no controlled experiments have tested the effectiveness of a spatializing STEM curriculum on young children’s spatial learning. In addition, teachers often lack knowledge in spatialized curriculum, which must be addressed through a situated professional development (SPD) program that supports the coherence of knowledge and application. To address this research gap, this mixed-methods study will determine the effectiveness of a spatializing STEM curriculum, alongside an SPD program for teachers, in enhancing kindergarteners’ spatial skills. Three levels of the SPD program will be implemented: 1) understanding the spatial learning; 2) comprehensive implementation; and 3) projection level to develop spatial learning in kindergarten classes. This controlled experiment will examine the effectiveness of our proposed spatializing STEM curriculum and SPD program by evaluating children’s spatial learning outcomes across three conditions: (a) teaching as usual (control), (b) teaching with a spatial concept package (treatment 1), and (c) teaching with an SPD package and spatializing STEM curriculum (treatment 2). We will recruit 54 teachers to support the spatial learning of 549 kindergarteners. All children will complete pre- and post-test spatial tests, and we will analyse these data using difference-in-differences multilevel structural equation modelling with residual centring. In the qualitative part, we will collect classroom recordings during and after the intervention and interview teachers about their implementation processes and outcomes. We will analyse these qualitative data using thematic analysis. This proposed project will fill a knowledge gap by determining the impact of spatialized STEM curriculum on young children’s spatial skills. Additionally, this initiative will empower teachers to integrate spatial learning into children’s daily lives. The evidence-based findings will provide significant benefits to kindergarteners, teachers, and curriculum policy-makers in preparing future STEM learners from an early age. N/A

titleEnglish: Developing an AI-powered Platform for Teacher–AI Co-creation of Instructional Materials for Mathematical Modelling in Secondary Education: A Design-based Research Project
department: Department of Mathematics and Information Technology
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Bai, Shurui Tiffany Dr Ling, Man Ho Dr Ng, Davy Tsz Kit Dr Yee, Tat Leung, Tony Prof Zhang , Qiaoping
abstract: To enhance the relevance of mathematics in real-world applications, the government advocates for engaging students in tackling real-world problems through mathematical modelling. However, its instructional materials must be carefully contextualised and designed to ensure authenticity and feasibility for students. Relying solely on teachers to manually create these materials increases their workload, resulting in a limited availability of comprehensive instructional materials that hinders teaching practices. This project leverages Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) to address this barrier in mathematics education without depending on general-purpose AI tools, as these tools pose a risk of AI hallucination. The primary objective of this design-based research (DBR) project is to develop and optimise an AI-powered platform that supports teacher–AI co-creation of instructional materials for mathematical modelling. Its design draws on the usefulness theoretical framework covering technological, educational, and social aspects. Particular emphasis is placed on instructional design theories in the educational aspect, encompassing modelling perspectives, levels of learning experiences, activity-design principles, and key elements of modelling cycles. These theoretical resources will be embodied in AI and progressively refined through three DBR cycles. Teacher professional development (TPD) activities will first be provided to familiarise teachers with mathematical modelling instruction and the AI-powered platform. Each DBR cycles will involve approximately 20 teachers and 450 junior secondary students across six schools. Teachers will engage in creating instructional materials with AI and improving them based on peer feedback. They will then pilot the co-created materials, revise them, and repilot the revised materials in their schools. Data will be collected during the TPD period, teacher engagement in material development, and school trials. Qualitative content analysis will be conducted on teacher–AI interactions and their co-created materials to gain insight into how teachers utilise the platform and to identify areas for enhancement. Teacher interviews will provide further insights for improving the platform's functionalities and support mechanisms. During the school trials, classroom observations and teacher interviews will facilitate an investigation regarding the actual implementation of the co-created materials, including how teachers deliver the instructional content and how students respond. Changes in effects between the pilot and repilot of the instructional materials will be examined through student tests and interviews. Analysing both significant and non-significant improvements, alongside participants' perspectives, will inform the identification of effective instructional strategies and challenges. These insights will contribute to optimising the theoretical resources underpinning AI to assist teachers in future material development and teaching practices. N/A

titleEnglish: Opening the black box of principal leadership literacy: Measures, drivers, and outcomes
department: Department of Education Policy and Leadership
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Gurr, David Prof Walker, Allan David
abstract: The work of school principals has become more complex and challenging than ever due to unprecedented uncertainties, constant changes, and overwhelming digitalization (Fullan, 2025; Walker et al., 2024). To address this phenomenon, the UNESCO (2024) Global Education Monitoring Report on leadership in education calls for a new effective leadership paradigm involving 'new leadership literacies' not only to handle intricate issues but also to foster school transformation and sustainability in the new era (Arnold, 2021; Flaxman, 2023). Moreover, school principals are the gatekeepers and role models for all school initiatives. Informed by the notion of 'principal first' (PI, 2025a), principals are encouraged to cultivate their leadership literacy to 'self-lead, lead followers, and lead schools'. Hence, principals need to be positioned firmly at the center of 'futures-literate' initiatives from OECD (2025) and UNESCO (2023). However, our literature review found that principal leadership literacy, regarded as one of the crucial new literacies, has attracted little attention. This project will aim at (1) developing and validating a Principal Leadership Literacy Instrument (PLLI); (2) identifying salient individual, organizational, and contextual drivers of principal leadership literacy; (3) investigating positive outcomes of principal leadership literacy; and (4) exploring the relationships among principal leadership literacy and its said drivers and outcomes. To achieve the four aims, the project will employ an exploratory sequential mixed-methods design with qualitative and quantitative strands. The qualitative strand includes 2 interview studies with 24 principals and a panel of 3 experts. The quantitative strand involves 2 survey studies with approximately 200 and 350~400 Hong Kong school principals. This project will make four major contributions. First, the project will provide empirical insights to advance the theoretical framework on principal leadership literacy by opening the 'black box' (e.g., principal leadership literacy) between its drivers and positive outcomes. Second, the project will produce a PLLI, advancing quantitative investigations into leadership literacy and offering a rigorous tool for future international comparisons. Third, this project will enable policymakers to establish priorities to better craft related policies for principal preparation, selection, and standardization (e.g., Hong Kong professional standards for principals) to enhance principal literacy development in Hong Kong. Fourth, this project will yield a practical blueprint to foster principal leadership literacy, particularly as individual, organizational, and contextual drivers identified are malleable for future solution-focused interventions aiming for establishing 'futures-literate' schools in Hong Kong. This will advance international and local initiatives for a 'futures-literate' society in Hong Kong and beyond. N/A

titleEnglish: Teacher Buoyancy: Advancing Understanding of What, Where, How, Why, and When
department: Department of Special Education and Counselling
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chiu, Ming Ming Dr COLLIE, J. Rebecca Prof Klassen, Robert
abstract: Teachers in Hong Kong face exceptionally high job demands, routinely working longer hours than their international counterparts. While their dedication has supported outstanding student achievement and a strong global reputation for educational excellence, it has also led to increasingly severe burnout and job dissatisfaction. Alarmingly, nearly one-third of all teachers have left the profession in the past three years, mostly unrelated to retirement. This trend poses significant risks to the stability and quality of Hong Kong’s education system. To address these challenges, this research systematically investigates teacher buoyancy—the ability to cope with and recover from everyday work challenges—by focusing on five guiding questions: what are the types and dimensions of teacher buoyancy in Hong Kong; where is buoyancy particularly needed in our educational context; how can it be effectively enhanced; why is it so important for our teachers; and when does it fluctuate during daily professional experiences. By structuring the inquiry around these core questions, the research seeks to provide a comprehensive understanding of buoyancy’s role, mechanisms, and practical implications within Hong Kong’s educational environment. The project consists of four interconnected studies. Study 1 involves qualitative interviews and panel discussions with 45 teachers to generate context-specific scenarios that deepen our understanding of teacher buoyancy in Hong Kong schools. Study 2 uses a quantitative cross-sectional survey of 300 teachers to examine the structure and key factors of teacher buoyancy. Study 3 adopts a longitudinal design, surveying 588 teachers twice to explore how job demands and resources influence buoyancy over time, and how buoyancy relates to well-being, perceived teaching quality, and intentions to leave. Study 4 implements a 10-day diary approach with 138 teachers to capture the dynamic, state-like aspects of buoyancy and its daily antecedents, coping strategies, and teaching outcomes. The significance of this research is threefold. First, it will advance theoretical understanding of the dimensions and contextual features of teacher buoyancy in Hong Kong. Second, the findings will inform evidence-based, buoyancy-focused interventions for school leaders and policymakers to promote teacher well-being, sustain teaching quality, and reduce attrition. Finally, the project will identify effective coping strategies that empower teachers themselves to navigate daily setbacks and challenges. By investigating the processes that foster better buoyancy among teachers, this research aims to support a healthier, more engaged, and more effective teaching workforce, ultimately enhancing student outcomes and broader educational landscape in Hong Kong. N/A

titleEnglish: Mathematics and Reading Integration: Overlapping Skills Development among Kindergarteners
department: Department of Special Education and Counselling
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Cheung, Sum Kwing Prof Chung, Kevin Kien Hoa Dr KWAN, Lok Yin Joyce
abstract: Mathematics and reading are two core subjects in school learning. Both are strong predictors of one’s achievement in later life. Prior to formal instruction in elementary school, children have already started to acquire some skills in mathematics and reading in kindergarten which lay the foundation for learning in later years. Hence, it is important to provide quality support for early development in both mathematics and reading. Although each subject has its own specific skills that children need to develop during early years (e.g., verbal counting for mathematics and phonological awareness for reading), previous studies have shown that the two subjects are interrelated. Some core skills specific to one domain are related to the achievement in the opposite domain (e.g., verbal counting is associated with reading achievement whereas phonological awareness is associated with mathematical achievement). However, we still do not have a full picture of this cross-domain relation. Hence, this project aims to discover how fundamental skills in one domain are associated with the development in the other domain, i.e., mathematics versus reading, during the kindergarten years. Its findings will offer important implications for screening and support for early difficulties in mathematics and reading. To trace children’s development in mathematics and reading, this project is a longitudinal study with four phases across the second and third years of early childhood education in Hong Kong. While most of the previous studies addressing the interrelation between mathematics and reading have focused on inter-individual differences (e.g., why some children have better or worse performance than others), this project takes a different approach by focusing on intra-individual growth (e.g., what predicts the rate of change over time for individual children). Our approach is better aligned with the goal of education, which highlights the importance of supporting individual children to realize their potential. Hence, intra-individual growth is the key. This project aims to discover the “cross-domain” skills which are associated with the growth trajectory of mathematical and reading performance. In particular, it asks whether the initial achievement in one domain predicts the growth of achievement in the other domain. Furthermore, it asks which specific skills in one domain predict the growth of abilities in the other domain, and whether family SES has a role to play in the predictive relations. By addressing these questions, this project will offer a fuller picture of the reciprocal relation between mathematics and reading. N/A

titleEnglish: Mindfulness-Based Strengths Practice for Couples to Reduce Work-Family Conflict and Promote Wellbeing: A Cluster Randomized Controlled Trial
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chan, Ka Shing Kevin Prof Chiu, Ming Ming Dr KWOK, Jojo Yan Yan Dr Law, Wilbert Prof YEUNG, Yuen Lan Dannii
abstract: Dual-earner couples with childrearing responsibilities experience higher levels of work-to-family conflict (WFC) compared to other workers. WFC, defined as the pressures and demands of work interfering with family life, can lead to marital conflict and various mental health issues, including burnout, psychological distress, and depression. More than half of dual-earner couples report experiencing WFC, yet many do not receive family-friendly support from employers, who often are not sympathetic to their needs. Given dual-earner couples represent a vastly growing family structure and their detriment impedes fertility rates and economic growth, identifying effective solutions to alleviate WFC and promote wellbeing is of paramount importance. This project aims to broaden the work-family research paradigm by not only focusing on the impairments of multiple role responsibilities but also incorporating the foundational elements of marital, family, and psychosocial functioning that contribute to thriving families. Drawing on the spillover-crossover model and mindfulness-to-meaning theory, we propose a mindfulness-based strengths practice (MBSP) program that combines mindfulness techniques with a focus on character strengths, applied to work-family context. This program is designed to help dual-earner couples mitigate the detrimental impact of WFC, enabling them to thrive within the work-family interface. MBSP encourages dual-earner couples to focus on the present moment, regulate thoughts and emotions, and leverage partner strengths to navigate challenges. We hypothesize that MBSP will have stress-attenuating, stress-buffering, and wellbeing-amplifying effects on dual-earner couples’ WFC and wellbeing. A dyadic, three-group, four-wave, cluster randomized controlled trial will be conducted on 180 dual-earner couples with childrearing responsibilities (360 participants). Participants will engage in an 8-week MBSP web-based training program, which will be compared against an active control receiving a healthy lifestyle program and a waitlist control. Effectiveness will be evaluated through measures on individual and interpersonal mindfulness, character strengths, WFC, marital satisfaction, family satisfaction, and subjective wellbeing, assessed at pre-intervention, interim, immediately post-intervention, and 6-month follow-up. Linear mixed modeling will be conducted to compare changes in outcome variables across groups and structural equation modeling will test the hypothesized actor-partner interdependence model. Completion of this project will yield significant contributions: (1) theoretical advancements by determining whether mindfulness and character strengths effectively reduce WFC and promote wellbeing for individuals and their partners; (2) methodological contributions by generating experimental intervention data that rigorously test the theoretical assumptions underlying existing correlational research on WFC; and (3) practical implications through the development of a feasible, effective, and sustainable MBSP program tailored for dual-earner couples. N/A

titleEnglish: Enhancing language learning through AI-empowered, LLM-supported interactive social robots: A mixed-methods study within a three-phase intervention design framework
department: Department of Special Education and Counselling
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Fung, Ka Yan Dr Lin, Dan Prof Song , Sheng Hui
abstract: In Hong Kong, approximately 42% (N=28,600) of the 67,870 integrators in the 2024–2025 academic year are students diagnosed with specific learning disabilities (SpLD) (Education Bureau, 2025). Since SpLD was formally recognized under Special Educational Needs (SEN) in 2008, targeted resources and support mechanisms have been introduced to address their unique challenges. Teacher education programs now emphasize understanding the cognitive and behavioural traits of SpLD students, equipping educators with evidence-based strategies to support their learning. With advancements in educational technology, researchers and practitioners are increasingly exploring how digital tools can improve learning outcomes and accessibility for SEN students. Social educational robots have shown promise in providing feedback and assistance, helping students overcome learning barriers and enhance academic performance. However, many current solutions follow a “one-size-fits-all” model, which may not meet individual needs. The potential of AI-empowered social robots, supported by large language models, to foster social, emotional, and communication skills in students with dyslexia remains underexplored. The team has initiated some small-scale studies on using robots to support learning among students with SEN. (Fung et al., 2025; Chung et al., 2024). Supportive interactions have been shown to positively influence student engagement, motivation, and language proficiency, addressing both the emotional and functional dimensions of learning. This study further investigates how social robots are designed to engage students with dyslexia from both functional and emotional perspectives. A robot-assisted language learning system will be developed specifically for Chinese language acquisition, following a three-phase framework of invention: Phase 1 (Formative Study), Phase 2 (System Development), and Phase 3 (Pilot Study). Guided by the Self-Determination Theory framework, the study will examine outcomes such as behavioural, emotional, and cognitive engagement, along with intrinsic motivation. In Phase 3, a mixed-methods study will be conducted with 240 primary school students, divided into a control group and two experimental groups. The control group will use traditional paper-based materials, Experimental Group 1 will engage with PowerPoint-based content, and Experimental Group 2 will interact directly with the social robot. To ensure robust findings, a triangulation methodology will be employed, incorporating questionnaires, classroom observations, semi-structured interviews, and language proficiency assessments. The insights support Chief Executive’s 2025 Policy Address, which envisions the integration of artificial intelligence into future education. These findings could inspire theoretically advancement, inform policy decisions, guide resource allocation and shape curriculum design that contribute to a more inclusive and technologically empowered educational landscape. N/A

titleEnglish: Integrating Chatbot-facilitated Scaffolding with Self-Regulated Learning to Develop Teachers’ Intelligent-TPACK: A Randomised Controlled Trial
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Chen, Chen Logan Prof Chiu, Kin Fung, Thomas Dr Ismail, Celik Prof Lai, Chun Prof Looi, Chee Kit
abstract: Teachers’ intelligent-TPACK (I-TPACK) refers to their knowledge of how to make effective and ethical use of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) to enhance curriculum design and instruction (Celik, 2023; Chiu, 2025). Despite growing international emphasis on cultivating teachers’ I-TPACK (UNESCO, 2024; Vidal et al., 2023), current professional development programmes seldom embed self-regulated learning (SRL) principles that help teachers plan, monitor, and reflect on their GenAI integration. Previous SRL-based interventions have demonstrated positive effects on learning outcomes, yet few have extended to teacher professional learning or employed GenAI-enabled scaffolding to deliver adaptive and personalised support. To address this gap, the proposed project will develop and test a chatbot-scaffolded SRL (CSSRL) model integrating Zimmerman’s three SRL phases with four types of GenAI-enabled scaffolding (representational, cognitive, metacognitive, and motivational) to support teachers’ personalised learning. Building on the PI’s pilot work, a 15-week online professional development programme comprising five I-TPACK modules will be developed, consisting of an experimental condition (CSSRL) and a control condition (flipped professional development, [FPD]). Both conditions cover identical learning content, but will differ in instructional design. In the CSSRL condition, each module will guide teachers through a full SRL cycle supported by a large language model-based chatbot providing real-time, phase-aligned, and context-responsive scaffolding, whereas the FPD condition will follow a conventional flipped format without SRL structuring or chatbot support. The programme will be evaluated through a randomised controlled trial with 220 secondary school teachers in Hong Kong. Participants will be individually randomised to an experimental condition (CSSRL; n =110) or a control condition (FPD; n =110). Data sources will include three survey waves assessing teachers’ I-TPACK at T1 (pre-programme), T2 (post-programme), and T3 (two-month follow-up), as well as a performance-based task, interviews, artefacts, reflective journals, and chatbot logs. Quantitative, qualitative, and learning analytics approaches will be integrated to evaluate programme effectiveness, identify enabling and constraining factors, and refine the CSSRL model by testing how different scaffolding types align with SRL phases and shape learning outcomes. The project contributes theoretically by establishing an evidence-based CSSRL model integrating chatbot-facilitated scaffolding with SRL phases to enhance professional learning. Building on this model, it represents the first professional development programme offering teachers personalised and adaptive SRL support. Beyond the local context, this low-threshold, scalable, and research-informed programme offers a benchmark for professional development and generates transferable insights to guide international efforts to strengthen teachers’ GenAI competency. N/A

titleEnglish: Investigating Parents’ GenAI Feedback Literacy and Its Effects on Students’ Academic Learning: Instrument Development and Validation
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Prof Chen, Gaowei Prof CHIU, Ming Ming Dr Lam, Chun Bun Ian Prof Li, Sandy S C Prof Yan, Zi
abstract: Children do activities at home, and those who get better feedback from their parents learn more. However, most parents do not know how to supervise their students’ daily chats with Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI)—they lack Parent-GenAI feedback literacy (P-GenAIFL). So, they can be overprotective, overcontrolling, or overly critical of their children’s GenAI use and obstruct their learning. Although many researchers examined students’ and teachers’ GenAI feedback literacies, no one designed and validated a survey instrument to assess P-GenAIFL to show its effects on student learning outcomes. Without such an instrument, we cannot examine P-GenAIFL and determine its effect on student learning, let alone test the effectiveness of parent intervention programs. To address this issue, we will do a study with three parts. First, we create and validate an instrument to measure P-GenAIFL knowledge, beliefs, and practices. Second, we assess the P-GenAIFL of parents in Hong Kong. Third, we explore the effects of P-GenAIFL on students’ learning outcomes. Guided by our definition of P-GenAIFL and individual interviews with experts and parents, we identify the subdimension(s) of each P-GenAIFL dimension and create items for them. Experts check these items for substantive and content validity. Parent feedback help us refine the items. The parents/caregivers of 300 junior secondary students in Hong Kong will pilot test and semi-finalize the P-GenAIFL instrument. In the main study, another 300 parents will collect their children’s school grades and complete the P-GenAIFL. Using these P-GenAIFL responses, we determine the dimensionality of the P-GenAIFL items, each item’s quality, each subdimension’s reliability, and the scales’ external validity (against actual daily practices) in our Rasch model. Next, we assess their P-GenAIFL profiles and test whether P-GenAIFL is linked to their children’s school grades via structural equation modeling, accounting for the nested data structure. Pilot study data will be utilized in the main analysis if at least four items per subdimension remain, as common items to link the pilot and the main scales. Scholars will use our validated P-GenAIFL instrument to conduct quantitative studies determining the antecedents, mechanisms, and moderators of parents’ P-GenAIFL across contexts. Our findings regarding the local P-GenAIFL profiles and its link to students' learning outcomes will inform (a) stakeholders seeking to understand Hong Kong parents’ P-GenAIFL strengths and weaknesses and (b) corresponding parent intervention programs to boost students' learning. N/A

titleEnglish: Efficacy, Mechanism, and Economic Evaluation of Mindfulness-Enhanced Behavioral Parent Training for Families of Autistic Children: A Randomized Controlled Trial
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Cheung, Amanda Kingsze Prof Krägeloh, Christian Prof Lam, Gary Yu-hin Prof Zhang, Dexing
abstract: Autistic children often exhibit externalizing behavior problems that can affect their daily functioning and well-being. Early intensive behavioral interventions are crucial for improving their long-term outcomes, and Behavioral Parent Training (BPT) supports these intervention efforts by training parents to implement behavioral management strategies for their children in everyday life. However, many parents of autistic children experience significant stress, which can hinder their effective implementation of these strategies and impede behavioral improvements in their children. Conventional BPT primarily focuses on child behavior outcomes and does not directly address parental psychological well-being. However, child behavior problems and parenting stress often co-occur and influence each other reciprocally. Without addressing both concerns concurrently, families of autistic children may be trapped in a vicious cycle where parental stress reduces BPT fidelity and efficacy, while persistent child behavior problems further exacerbate parental stress. To break this cycle, it is essential to integrate psychotherapeutic support into BPT to promote parental mental health, enabling parents to effectively implement behavioral management strategies. A particularly promising psychotherapeutic approach to complement BPT is mindfulness training. As mindfulness emphasizes nonjudgmental awareness, acceptance, and non-reactivity, it can help parents alleviate stress and foster emotional equanimity. By enhancing mental wellness, parents can more calmly and consistently implement behavioral management strategies for their children. Given these potential benefits, mindfulness training is a valuable approach for integration into BPT to create synergy. The proposed project aims to establish an innovative, dual-focus intervention for families of autistic children by incorporating mindfulness training into BPT. While conventional BPT focuses on improving child behavior, Mindfulness-Enhanced BPT (ME-BPT) additionally targets reducing parenting stress to provide more comprehensive family support. Specifically, we will conduct a randomized controlled trial to investigate: (1) whether ME-BPT is more efficacious than BPT in reducing child behavior problems and parenting stress; (2) whether parental mindfulness mediates these effects; and (3) whether ME-BPT is more cost-effective than BPT. Theoretically, this project will create novel knowledge about the comparative efficacy of ME-BPT and BPT in improving child and parental outcomes. It will also advance understanding of the specific psychological mechanism through which ME-BPT provides distinct benefits compared to BPT. Practically, ME-BPT, if proven efficacious and cost-effective, can be applied as an evidence-based method to simultaneously address child and parental well-being, offering long-term benefits for families of autistic children. Supported by these favorable outcomes, we could promote ME-BPT to government and community organizations for incorporation into family support services. N/A

titleEnglish: Good Employment or Good Place? Job Preference for State Sector Employment and the Destination Choice of Highly Educated Youths in China
department: Department of Social Sciences and Policy Studies
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
coInvestigators: Dr Jiang, Ronghao Prof Lin, Sainan Dr Wu, Alfred Dr Yang, Fan
abstract: The employment landscape of highly educated youths in China is characterized by a distinctive pattern of sectoral preference with around two-thirds of university students expressing a strong intention to secure a stable career in state sector. Such sectoral preference is increasingly difficult to be met in China’s top-tier cities dominated by volatile private sector jobs. Whether to prioritize the access to state sector employment or the location in vibrant urban centers in the employment decision-making process of university students upon graduation is an issue of significant policy implications for both urban/regional governments concerned about the battle for educated talents and national economic planners wary of a potential brain drain from private to state sector. Existing literature on the university-to-work migration of university graduates is dominated by the debate over the relative importance of economic returns, urban amenities and social network as the attractive factors of the destination locations, with little attention paid to the role of job preference in China’s institutional context. Meanwhile, studies on the occupation choice and labor market outcomes of China’s university graduates are concerned with the impact of higher education and social origin on their choice between state and private sector employment without probing further into their complex and diversified location choices. Knowledge gaps are apparent in the missing link between these two bodies of literature warranting a serious investigation of the interaction between sectoral preference and spatial preference in shaping the destination choices of highly educated youths in China. This research will include a quantitative analysis of the choice experiment survey of final-year university students and a qualitative analysis of university graduates eventually working in state sector through data collected from universities in Wuhan and Guangzhou. It aims to 1) identify the destination choice pattern of university students driven by the preference for state sector employment; 2) investigate the individual and social factors conditioning the destination choices of public career-oriented university students; 3) excavate the variegated spatial pathways and strategies leading to the job attainment in state sector by university graduates. The findings of this proposed project will contribute new knowledge to advance theoretical inquiry into the complex nature and dynamics of the destination choices of highly educated youths in different institutional and cultural contexts and will be of direct relevance to Hong Kong with strategic interests in attracting and retaining mainland students. N/A

titleEnglish: Bridging Community and Individual: Dual Approaches to Variation and Change in Hong Kong Cantonese
abstract: Variability and change are fundamental features of human language. Language is always changing and no two people speak in exactly the same way. However, variability and change are not random. They follow systematic rules. For instance, in Hong Kong Cantonese, the two sounds /n/ and /l/ are merging so that the word for YOU can be pronounced as either nei5 or lei5. But the choice of nei5 or lei5 is not random and can be shaped by many social and linguistic factors. A Cantonese speaker in casual conversations with their local friends may prefer lei5. The use of /l/ may also be preferred before one vowel over another. However, a major mystery remains: Where do these systematic rules come from? What knowledge do individual speakers need to have for these patterns to manifest at the community level and how do their own production patterns and perceptual abilities contribute? To tackle these questions, this project adopts a dual approach, utilizing both corpus analysis and behavioral experiments to understand ongoing sound changes in Hong Kong Cantonese, bridging community-level patterns and individual-level processes. Hong Kong Cantonese is a perfect living laboratory because it is currently undergoing several rapid sound changes within a vibrant multilingual environment, offering a fresh perspective beyond studies focused only on English or other Indo-European languages. This project uses the aforementioned n-l merger as a test case. First, a large-scale corpus of natural speech from 120 native speakers of Hong Kong Cantonese will be compiled to map production patterns at both the community and individual levels. Subsequently, three perception experiments will be conducted with the same 120 participants to probe how individuals leverage their sociolinguistic knowledge of language contact to resolve ambiguity. This approach bridges the gap between community-level patterns and individual cognitive processes, and critically, forges a direct link between production and perception in sound change. The results of this project will document ongoing sound changes in Hong Kong Cantonese amidst rapid socio-cultural shifts, informing theories of language change by bridging patterns observed at community and individual levels. Additionally, this project will have the long-term potential to train Large Language Models to better accommodate the linguistic variability used by diverse speakers and to select socially appropriate variants to meet the communicative needs of users in different social situations. Ultimately, this work will contribute to scientific and cultural understanding of how humans communicate across a diverse range of language backgrounds. N/A

titleEnglish: The regulation of gambling-like video game loot boxes in Hong Kong: Risky product characteristics and compliance with relevant Ordinances
abstract: Playing video games is now a highly popular activity worldwide and in Hong Kong (HK) specifically: 94% of secondary school students play at least weekly. Video games may cause harm to young people: 15.6% of students experienced ‘video game addiction.’ Many video games sell gambling-like ‘loot boxes.’ Players can buy these virtual items with real-world money to get random rewards allowing them to look cooler and fight more powerfully. Players obtain desirable rewards very rarely (less than 1% chance) and so must spend large sums of money to guarantee obtaining what they want. These products are psychologically similar to gambling but are not regulated as such by governments, meaning young people who are not permitted to participate in traditional gambling are currently allowed to purchase loot boxes. More than 50% of HK video game players likely buy loot boxes. Some reported extreme spending of over HK$100,000 monthly. Besides financial harms, these products may exacerbate video game addiction and encourage children to gamble. Other countries have regulated loot boxes, including bans and minimum age requirements to better protect players. However, these products have not been regulated in HK. This project provides crucial, but presently lacking, local evidence on loot box availability, product characteristics, and regulation. Firstly, relevant HK laws will be identified. Consumer protection and gambling laws will be reviewed to identify how they apply and what obligations they place on companies. For example, the Trade Descriptions Ordinance requires that sellers must not hide important information, which when applied to the loot box context means that companies must tell players the probabilities for getting specific random rewards. Secondly, we will examine the 100 most popular games in HK through gameplay to identify how many of them sell loot boxes and how, thus providing evidence on how widely available these products are. Thirdly, we will assess whether the loot boxes identified comply with applicable HK law. For example, are players given information about their probabilities of winning rare rewards as required? We will also assess video game advertising materials published on HK social media as to whether they comply with consumer law. Our findings will be shared with regulators to encourage stricter enforcement against companies that do not follow rules. We will also create a public website that shares information about how loot boxes are regulated in different regions globally to help companies understand their regulatory obligations. N/A

titleEnglish: 魯迅思想與文學中的復仇 Revenge in Lu Xun’s Thought and Literature
abstract: This project examines a crucial yet underexplored dimension of Lu Xun’s thought and writing: the theme of revenge. Lu Xun has often been portrayed as an advocate of vengeance—an image shaped by his uncompromising stance toward adversaries, his later leftist orientation, and the combative tone and fascination with revenge narratives evident in his fiction. Yet this dimension has frequently been oversimplified or distorted. Scholarship has tended either to collapse his reflections on revenge into a generalized discourse of struggle, sometimes conflating it with revolution, or to abstract the theme into a vague notion of poetic justice. Moreover, few studies have situated Lu Xun within the comparative and world‑literary framework, exploring how he reworked Chinese and foreign traditions of revenge writing and how this process informed the making of Chinese modernity. Focusing on Lu Xun is particularly vital. As the central figure of modern Chinese literature and a thinker deeply attuned to the ethical and political crises of his time, he offers a unique vantage point for examining how revenge—at once a moral impulse and a narrative form—mediated tensions between tradition and modernity, individual development and social reform, Enlightenment ideals and Anti‑Enlightenment critiques. Unlike many of his contemporaries, Lu Xun not only reinterpreted classical Chinese motifs of vengeance but also actively engaged with global literary and philosophical traditions, embedding the theme of revenge into the very fabric of modern Chinese literature and intellectual history. To pursue these questions, the project combines intellectual history, literary analysis, and political philosophy to reconstruct the genealogy of revenge in Lu Xun’s thought, clarifying its content and distinctive features. Through comparative readings of Chinese and Western sources, it investigates how revenge intersects with issues of justice, responsibility, sincerity, and authenticity, and how it illuminates the ethical and political conditions of modern life. Ultimately, by reassessing Lu Xun’s place in both Chinese and global traditions of revenge literature, the study not only recovers a neglected aspect of his oeuvre but also demonstrates how the problem of revenge opens new horizons for understanding the formation of Chinese modernity. N/A

titleEnglish: Nature-inspired design of high-performance building cantilever structures
department: Department of Architecture and Civil Engineering
abstract: The research proposal aims to revolutionize structural design by drawing inspiration from the geometric and mechanical properties of natural structures. Natural structures, such as insect wings and plant rhizomes, exhibit lightweight yet highly efficient structural networks, characterized by primary and secondary veins in a statically determinate system optimized for their in-plane and out-of-plane stiffness. This study proposes to adapt these natural principles to the design of cantilevered building structures, a critical architectural element that often faces challenges in balancing weight, stiffness, and load-bearing capacity. Building upon recent advancements in machine learning and graphic statics, this research will employ Maxwell's reciprocal diagrams to analyze and replicate the force equilibrium of natural structural networks. These diagrams will be used to train artificial intelligence models capable of generating structural networks for arbitrary boundary geometries, specifically, cantilevered structures. The machine learning framework will integrate image-text-based Diffusion Models to predict form and force diagrams, ensuring that the generated designs maintain static equilibrium and geometric efficiency. The generated structural patterns will be evaluated for their mechanical performance under point loads and distributed loads, simulating real-world conditions such as wind and seismic forces. Our preliminary studies on scaled-down 3D-printed prototypes of dragonfly-inspired wing cores have demonstrated up to a 25% improvement in out-of-plane stiffness compared to traditional designs, with a significant reduction in material usage. The proposed research seeks to extend these findings, focusing on developing lightweight, high-performance cantilever designs that optimize stiffness-to-weight ratios while maintaining architectural flexibility. This work has broader implications for sustainable construction practices, as it leverages bio-inspired principles to minimize material usage without compromising structural integrity. The methodology is also adaptable to other natural structures, such as the vein networks of grasshopper wings or the rhizomes of Amazon water lilies, opening avenues for further exploration. The project will culminate in the development of a computational design platform, enabling architects and engineers to generate efficient cantilever structures based on user-defined boundary geometries. By bridging biology, computational design, and structural engineering, this research aims to redefine how we design lightweight and efficient cantilever systems, contributing to more sustainable and resilient architectural innovations in the built environment. N/A

titleEnglish: Caring Inside Out: Interpersonal Emotion Regulation, Sleep, Health, and the Role of Cultural Values among Family Caregivers
department: Department of Social and Behavioural Sciences
abstract: Background: Aging societies worldwide, including Hong Kong, face increasing care demands, with family caregivers playing a vital role. Supporting their well-being is therefore increasingly critical. The dual emotional burden of caregiving—regulating one’s own emotions while managing those of care recipients—may contribute to the high prevalence of sleep problems that further undermine health. This study will be the first to examine how family caregivers’ engagement in interpersonal emotion regulation (IER)—specifically, intrinsic IER (managing one’s own emotions through others) and extrinsic IER (helping others manage theirs)—affects sleep and, in turn, physical and psychological well-being. Recognizing that emotion regulation is shaped by cultural values, this study will also leverage Hong Kong’s multicultural context to examine how interdependence (vs. independence), a cultural value that emphasizes social connectedness and harmony, influences caregivers’ intrinsic and extrinsic IER and their implications for sleep and health. This study includes three key research questions: Q1) Are higher levels of intrinsic IER associated with better sleep, and higher levels of extrinsic IER associated with worse sleep? Q2) Do sleep patterns mediate the relationships between intrinsic and extrinsic IER and caregivers’ physical and psychological well-being? Q3) Does interdependence (vs. independence) influence caregivers’ engagement in intrinsic and extrinsic IER, which in turn are linked to sleep and health? Methods: The study will adopt a multimethod, three-part design involving family caregivers in Hong Kong. (1) A cross-sectional survey will measure intrinsic and extrinsic IER, sleep quality, physical and psychological health, and the cultural values of interdependence and independence. (2) A two-week daily diary (i.e., daily surveys) will capture caregivers’ everyday experiences of intrinsic and extrinsic IER, self-reported sleep and health, and objective sleep indicators via actigraphy watches. (3) A two-month follow-up survey will reassess sleep quality, and physical and psychological health. Together, these complementary assessments will provide cross-sectional, daily, and longitudinal perspectives to address the research questions. Expected Deliverables: This project will generate a unique dataset on family caregivers’ IER, sleep, and health. The findings will enhance family caregivers’ awareness of how emotion regulation influences sleep and health, and will offer practical guidelines for community organizations and health professionals to strengthen caregiver support. Ultimately, the study will inform culturally sensitive practices and interventions, support policies that sustain family caregiving, and contribute to a healthier aging society in Hong Kong and beyond. N/A

titleEnglish: Intergenerational support during parental widowhood in East Asian and Western aging societies
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
abstract: East Asian societies are rapidly aging, and widowhood is becoming a major social challenge. Losing a spouse brings profound psychological distress, with adverse effects on mental and physical health, and often threatens financial security. These risks are especially acute in East Asia, where high rates of poverty and depression in later life intersect with rigid gender norms that make adaptation after widowhood more difficult. Across countries, children are generally regarded as the primary source of support for widowed parents, but this expectation is even stronger in societies with deeply rooted familial cultures. Existing research has tended to frame widowhood primarily as a personal crisis and, less often, as a social problem. What has been largely overlooked, however, is a family-level perspective—how widowhood reorganizes the relationships between parents and all their adult children. We know that widowhood generates enduring financial, caregiving, and social needs, but far less is understood about how siblings in families collectively or unequally share these responsibilities, how these patterns evolve across different stages of widowhood, and why some families arrive at more balanced and intensive support arrangements than others. This project pioneers a family-centered approach to widowhood, reframing it as a multistage process that reshapes relationships both between generations (parents and children) and within generations (among siblings). By harmonizing large, nationally representative aging surveys from Europe, the United States, the Chinese Mainland, South Korea, and Japan, it will examine intergenerational support across four stages: pre-widowhood, pre-terminal phase (before parental death), early widowhood, and later widowhood. The study has three goals: (1) to map how adult siblings share practical and social support for their parents across these stages; (2) to identify factors that shape these support patterns at both the family level (e.g., sibling composition) and the societal level (e.g., policy and cultural contexts); and (3) to understand how widowhood and support across generations are influenced by gender. The project will make both theoretical and practical contributions. First, by examining how siblings share support for widowed parents, it will shed light on fairness and intergenerational solidarity, with implications for family conflicts, family caregivers’ well-being, and older adults’ mental health. Second, it will examine how different welfare systems and cultural contexts shape family support, offering insights into the balance between strong family obligations and expanding social welfare in East Asia. This will inform discussions on meeting the needs of widowed older adults and their families. N/A

titleEnglish: Augmenting Hong Kong Paper Crafting Technique: Interactive Heritage Preservation and Transmission with Augmented Reality and Generative AI
abstract: The Hong Kong Government's 2025 Policy Address highlights strengthening district-based promotion of Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH) and advancing the "ICH Around Town" initiative. Hong Kong's traditional paper craft technique, known as Za-zo, is a form of dynamic handcrafting that combines bamboo frameworks and colored paper to produce decorative structures for festivals and rituals. Recognized as Hong Kong's ICH in 2014 and officially inscribed on the Representative List in 2017, Za-zo has received growing attention through government and stakeholder initiatives. Despite these efforts, significant challenges remain in its preservation and transmission: (C1) lack systematic understanding of its under-documented dimensions and difficulties hinders broader transmission; (C2) current documentation inadequately represents its 4D information (3D form and dynamic process); (C3) crafting heavily relies on 2D references and intensive mentorship, hindering easy grasp of 4D forms, limiting scalability, broader participation, and personalized learning/creation; (C4) unclear criteria and methods to evaluate the new digital preservation and transmission tools. This project therefore aims to explore how Za-zo crafting techniques be better preserved and transmitted with emerging technologies, especially Augmented Reality (AR) and Generative AI (GenAI), to address the challenges. First, we will conduct literature review and in-depth interviews with key Za-zo stakeholders to identify under-documented dimensions of Za-zo and current transmission challenges (C1). Second, we will construct a multimodal 4D dataset to digitally represent and archive 3D Za-zo works and techniques, capturing external geometry and appearance, internal bamboo frameworks, and dynamic crafting processes through automatic reconstruction methods and interactive mobile AR capture (C2). Third, we will design an interactive tool with AR and GenAI to support easy, customizable 3D Za-zo creation and learning (C3). It will accept multimodal inputs (e.g., text descriptions, image references) and generate editable 3D Za-zo designs with decomposable components and AR-guided crafting instructions, allowing users to in-situ design and practice Za-zo in virtual and physical forms. Input and feedback from artisans, learners, and general users will be incorporated throughout. For validation, we will co-organize Za-zo creation and transmission activities with practitioners and industries, embedding them into common practice (C4). With strong local support, promising preliminary results, and solid expertise, this project aims to set a benchmark for digital heritage preservation of 3D and dynamic handcrafting. Through easy, customizable AR and GenAI solutions, we envision broader participation, craftsmanship revitalization, and new opportunities for cultural tourism and creative practice of Hong Kong–specific applications. N/A

titleEnglish: The Prescribed Image: Art as Medicine in Early Modern China and Its Contemporary Reactivation
abstract: This project investigates the practice of using visual art as medicine—what I term “art as prescription”—in early modern Chinese print culture and proposes its continued transhistorical influence in contemporary Chinese art. As early as the 14th century, the dietary and nutritional manual Yinshan zhengyao (YSZY, Proper and Essential Things for the Emperor’s Food and Drink) documented the practice of prescribing art as medicine. Originally dated 1330, though surviving only in a Ming printed edition of 1456, this manual vividly depicts the act of looking as a form of embodied care, most strikingly in several illustrated scenes showing pregnant women admiring paintings of carp, peacocks, eagles, jade, and etc, designating various effects on the unborn child such as intelligence, beauty, and strength. It suggests that observing certain objects, and equally the images of these objects, would help the maternal body to internalize and transfer the qualities of those objects to her offspring. This practice became increasingly popular thanks to Ming imperial sponsorship and a booming print industry, which together spurred rapid commercialization and democratization of medicinal knowledge through visual imagery. Similar prescriptions also appear in later Ming pharmacopeias, such as those illustrated in the seminal sixteenth-century text Bencao gangmu (BCGM, Compendium of Materia Medica). Although art as prescription has rarely been noted by art historians, contemporary Chinese art has reactivated this inheritance.
