Works by artists such as Huang Yongping and Cai Guo-Qiang directly borrow from these early modern sources, applying their medicinal imaginations to new socio-political and cultural contexts. This project therefore takes a transhistorical approach to examine this early modern practice and its afterlife in contemporary art. Drawing extensively on primary sources such as medical treatises, nutritional manuals, encyclopedias, illustrated guides, and painting manuals, this project is comprised of three parts: 1) tracing and analyzing the discourse of art as prescription illustrated in YSZY along with other Ming-era canonical texts; 2) establishing a visual archive of paintings prescribed as medicine in these sources; 3) recontextualizing and analyzing this phenomenon as it appears in contemporary Chinese art in light of this long lineage of belief in the medicinal function of art. To do so, this project takes an interdisciplinary approach to reveal the medicinal meanings and values embedded in images and acts of visualization, shedding light on the enduring connections between Chinese medicine and visual art. N/A

titleEnglish: Predicting Pedestrian Exposure to Nitrogen Dioxide in Transit Walksheds
department: Academy of Geography, Sociology and International Studies
abstract: Background: Ambient nitrogen dioxide (NO2), largely from vehicular emissions, is a critical urban health challenge, with each 10 μg/m³ increase in annual NO2 exposure associated with a 12.7% rise in all-cause mortality. Among urban populations, pedestrians are uniquely vulnerable to NO2 exposure due to their proximity to traffic emissions without physical protection. This vulnerability intensifies in transit walksheds (areas around public transit stations), where concentrated pedestrian activity coincides with heterogeneous pollution patterns, creating potentially elevated exposure risks. Despite this critical nexus of mobility and health, existing research focuses on ambient concentrations or residential population-based exposure, overlooking the collective exposure burden experienced by pedestrians on urban sidewalks. Understanding the distribution of pedestrian exposure in transit walksheds is therefore essential for identifying priority hotspots and informing targeted interventions for public health protection. Objective and Methodology: This research aims to develop a methodological framework to estimate pedestrian-weighted exposure to nitrogen dioxide (PEND) in transit walksheds. Leveraging our expertise, the framework will integrate mobile sensing, computer vision, and graph-based deep learning for accurate exposure modeling. First, a mobile sensing workflow will collect fine-grained data on NO2 concentrations and pedestrian volumes, delivering PEND measurements across extensive spatial coverage at high resolution. Second, a hierarchical feature engineering pipeline will integrate predictors across multiple spatial and temporal scales, including features extracted from multi-modal data (e.g., 3D urban models, road network graphs), to capture overlooked exposure factors. Third, a graph-based deep learning model will predict sidewalk PEND by explicitly capturing spatial dependencies of pedestrian mobility and pollution dispersion across road networks. The framework will be applied in Shenzhen's Futian District across transit walksheds with diverse built environments. Impact: By advancing pedestrian-focused exposure assessment in transit walksheds, this research will reveal micro-scale exposure hotspots overlooked by conventional assessments and identify key determinants of PEND patterns. These findings will enable targeted interventions tailored to specific exposure mechanisms (e.g., pollution-driven or activity-driven), maximizing public health protection with efficient resource allocation. This research can also inform transit-oriented development (TOD) by guiding design strategies that reduce exposure risks while promoting walkability and urban vitality. The replicable framework can be adapted to similar regions, contributing to a new paradigm for pedestrian-centered planning that safeguards air quality and public health in transit environments. N/A

titleEnglish: From Mediterranean to Pearl River: Greek Migration, Maritime Networks, and Community Building in Hong Kong, 19-20 centuries
department: Academy of Chinese, History, Religion and Philosophy
abstract: In the late nineteenth century, widespread economic hardship prompted many Greeks to seek opportunities abroad—a classic example of push-pull migration. While most European migrants set out for their colonies, the Greeks had to take a different path. In Hong Kong the Greeks built a distinct community among various ethnic groups, positioned between British rulers and the local Chinese. They fostered an identity that balanced integration and disintegration, assimilation and preservation, internationalism, and diasporic nationalism. Hong Kong’s stable business and political environment attracted Greek merchants from Greece, established diaspora regions, and even the Chinese mainland, where Greek communities were already present by the nineteenth century. The Greeks distinguished themselves among colonial ethnic groups due to their unique religious and cultural identity, situated between East and West. As a cosmopolitan trade diaspora, Greeks shared more similarities with Jewish or Armenian communities than with Western imperialists. Lacking support from a nation-state, they depended on close-knit kinship ties and familiar commercial norms to survive (Cohen, 2023). Most Greeks in Hong Kong worked as merchants, sailors, and business owners in Victoria (Central), thriving in spaces where intense commercial activity and cross-cultural interactions abounded, blurring ethnic distinctions and fostering a sense of belonging to the colony. Colonial capitalism provided minorities with economic opportunities while keeping them politically subordinate. Those who prospered tended to assimilate, resulting in class differences reflected in wealth, attire, and lifestyle within their small communities, as also seen in Dutch Indonesia, and French Indochina (Lim, 2023). As a globally mobile diaspora, Greeks continually negotiated challenges and shifting allegiances. This study centers on their economic roles, daily experiences, and communal bonds in nineteenth and twentieth century Hong Kong. This is the first major study to focus specifically on Greeks in Hong Kong, filling a significant gap in diaspora studies and the history of colonial Asia, through primary sources in Greek, Chinese, and English. It will uncover neglected stories and deepen our understanding of migration’s impact on collective identities, self-representation among marginalized groups, colonial entrepreneurship and maritime history on a global scale. The field of comparative studies will benefit through the contrast of this case to other phenomena. Based on existing literature, somebody could argue that the Greeks never set foot there, but the archival sources tell a different story. The historiography concerning the circulation of ideas, products, and people in modern times is incomplete without the Greek contribution. N/A

titleEnglish: Sound First Simulations and Virtual Environments for Blind and Low Vision Users in Hong Kong: Pattern Library, Cantonese Earcons, Open Software development kit, and Community Trials.
abstract: In Hong Kong, 199,600 people (2.7% of the population) are blind or have low vision (Statistics Department, HKSAR, 2021). Current orientation and mobility training often relies on instructor narration, physical mock-ups (e.g. bus stops), and ad hoc audio descriptions, before exposing learners to real situations. These real world exposures are hard to standardize, difficult to repeat independently, and costly to scale. Many learners require prolonged practice to discriminate sounds in situ (e.g. street crossings) and some disengage before achieving independence. Immersive virtual environments offer safe, repeatable, and engaging simulations for training, education, and leisure. Yet most systems remain sight-first, built primarily for vision with minimal audio retrofits, which excludes blind and low vision users from simulators, learning, and play (Galindo Esparza et al., 2025; Ghali et al., 2012; Zwoliński et al., 2025). We propose a sound-first alternative: immersive experiences designed to be used primarily through spatial audio, sonification, and light haptics. Three use cases anchor the work, learning (orientation and mobility), training (safe practice and rehearsal), and entertainment (accessible, agency-centered play). Our practical aim is to replace ad hoc drills with standardized, on-demand modules that learners can practice safely and as often as needed, accelerating skill acquisition, reducing risk, cost and dependence on physical space. In partnership with the Rehabilitation Centre of the Hong Kong Society for Blind, we will co-design a toolkit comprising: (i) an open interaction pattern library for non-visual navigation; (ii) a culturally aligned set of auditory cues and prompts for consistent learning; (iii) an open-source software development kit with templates; and (iv) three exemplar simulations spanning learning, training, and entertainment. Effectiveness and safety will be evaluated with blind and low-vision participants through controlled tasks and community trials, measuring success rates, time, errors, usability and workload, confidence, and intent to adopt. Development will follow an immersive-ethics protocol (Madary and Metzinger, 2016). Contributions include sound-first interaction grammar for non-visual immersion; rationales for spatial-audio mappings and prompts; comparative evidence against audio-afterthought experiences on performance, workload, and safe engagement. Building on findings that immersive perspective-taking can enhance motivation and processing (Banakou et al., 2016; Peck et al., 2013) and that audio motor training benefits spatial cognition and mobility (Cappagli et al., 2019), we expect inclusion of blind and low-vision learners across training, education, and play; providing evidence and guidance for Hong Kong and global accessibility practice. All assets will be openly released under permissive licenses to enable adoption. N/A

titleEnglish: Play your way to success: How playful study design enhances university student well-being and performance in the digital age
abstract: In today’s digitalized educational context, study processes are being redefined, requiring individuals to show higher proactivity than ever before. But how can university students proactively redesign their study processes to make learning more engaging in the digital age? This project introduces Playful Study Design (PSD), a set of student-led, proactive behaviors aimed at modifying the process of studying to be more fun and enjoyable without altering the tasks themselves. PSD consists of two key strategies: designing fun, which incorporates playful and creative elements into studying, and designing competition, which introduces self- or peer-based micro challenges to make studying more stimulating. Drawing on the Study Demands-Resources (SD-R) Model, this project explores how PSD improves student well-being and performance by activating study resources (e.g., autonomy, peer support, and feedback) and fostering positive challenge appraisal (e.g., perceiving tasks as manageable and growth-promoting). It also investigates the moderating role of digital competence, which is crucial in today’s tech-driven world, in strengthening these processes. The research involves two studies. Study 1 adopts an experience sampling approach to capture PSD’s daily effects, testing the mediating roles of study resources and challenge appraisals and the moderating effect of digital competence. Study 2 employs a Group Actor-Partner Interdependence Model (GAPIM) to investigate PSD’s peer spillover effects in team contexts, estimating actor, partner, and team effects, and testing digital competence’s role in amplifying PSD’s diffusion processes. This project’s novelty lies in three aspects. First, it positions PSD as a bottom-up, student-led study strategy in digital learning environments, complementing traditional top-down, teacher-led gamification approaches. Second, it extends the SD-R model by showing how playful, proactive behaviors transform study demands into motivating challenges and how personal resources (digital competence) boost proactive studying. Third, it applies GAPIM to quantify PSD’s social diffusion effects in group-based learning, an innovative approach rarely used in existing research. This project anticipates three major impacts. First, it will offer actionable PSD strategies that students and instructors can implement immediately to improve well-being and on-time performance. Second, institutions can apply the SD-R model to diagnose and review study resources/demands at course/program levels and inform targeted support for digitally disadvantaged learners. Third, beyond university students, the project expects to generalize this strategy to other learners and populations in tech-intensive environments, fostering their sustained engagement and well-being. N/A

titleEnglish: Gendering Electromobility: Exploring Chinese Women's Mobilities and Challenges in China's Male-dominated Platform Work
abstract: The rise of electromobility in China has opened doors for women in traditionally male-dominated platform work and transformed their mobility patterns and everyday lives. Yet scholarship on gendered electromobility in China remains sparse. When electric vehicles (EVs) first appeared in China a decade ago, scholars anticipated their adoption would reflect specific gender and class dynamics. Today, women using EVs in platform work are typically migrant and working-class rather than elite or middle-class consumers. To examine gendered electromobility in China, we need a deeper understanding of how EVs affect working-class and migrant women's experiences in platform work. This research aims to explore how China’s rising electromobility impacts women’s lived experiences in male-dominated platform work and how the transition to electromobility influences gender equality within platform work. The PI conducted a pilot study of 40 Chinese female ride-hailing drivers from Didi Chuxing between January and April 2025. The pilot study documents how female ride-hailing drivers experience increased work, social, and physical mobility alongside various frictions and provides a conceptual framework for understanding Chinese electromobility's socio-political infrastructure, which is embedded in gig labour processes, patriarchal culture, social reproduction, and social policies. While offering valuable initial insights, the pilot study raised new questions that led the PI to broaden the research through case studies of both Chinese female ride-hailing drivers and food-delivery riders, and to integrate gender and feminist theories to refine and expand research questions and further explore alternative narratives about power dynamics in Chinese electromobility and complicate the discussion on gender equality and social justice. This research combines Chatnography and Feminist Ethnography to address four key research questions. Through chat interviews and field observations, the first two questions explore how the Chinese electromobility transition and infrastructure affect the mobility patterns and challenges faced by female ride-hailing drivers and food-delivery riders. The third question uses intersectional frameworks to examine how various social factors shape cultural identities, allowing Chinese women to challenge traditional gender norms and transform power dynamics. The fourth question applies social reproduction frameworks to investigate how tensions between caregiving responsibilities and gig work influence cultural identities through everyday mobilities, and how these shared experiences create solidarity among Chinese women in platform work. This research makes empirical and theoretical contributions by enriching gender and feminist scholarship by providing situated knowledge about Chinese women in male-dominated platform work, while strengthening theoretical approaches to studying Chinese women, work, and labour. N/A

titleEnglish: Advancing the Chronospatial Revolution in the Study of Cultural Individualism–Collectivism
department: Department of Sociology and Social Policy
abstract: This project advances one of the most influential concepts in the study of cultural differences: individualism–collectivism (I-C). Since its introduction by Geert Hofstede in 1980, I-C has guided decades of academic research. The idea that societies differ in whether people are seen primarily as autonomous individuals or as members of groups bound by obligations has become a cornerstone cultural dimension. Recent advances in I-C research have included refining the theoretical definition and replacing Hofstede’s initial scores with more recent and nationally representative survey data. But progress so far remains limited in two crucial ways. First, existing indices are static. They cannot tell us whether and how cultures are changing over time, whether societies are converging or diverging, or what forces are driving any temporal shifts. Second, they equate nations with cultures, overlooking the substantial cultural variation across regional, rural–urban, religious, and linguistic lines that exists within societies. This project responds to these gaps by advancing what has recently been termed a chronospatial revolution: the move toward studying psychological and cultural phenomena as dynamic both across time and space. Using a harmonized World Values Survey / European Values Study dataset, I will draw on more than 500 nationally representative surveys conducted in several waves since 1981 in over 120 societies. The project has six objectives: (1) to construct the first validated longitudinal index of I-C at the national-level; (2) to track cultural trends across societies and test whether they reveal convergence, divergence, or regional clustering; (3) to identify the macro-level drivers and moderators of change, such as modernization, globalization, civilizational identity, and politics; (4) to map within-country variation in I-C across regions, rural–urban areas, and major religious and linguistic groups; (5) to develop a Territorial Heterogeneity Index (THI) that quantifies the extent of spatial variation in I-C within nations; and (6) to explain cross-national differences in THI using structural, institutional, and ecological predictors. The outcomes will be both theoretical and practical. The project will provide a new public dataset and measurement tool for the study of cultural change, clarify longstanding debates over patterns and drivers of cultural change, and generate systematic evidence on the cultural diversity within nations. Integrating temporal dynamics with internal variation, this research will move the study of I-C, and of culture more broadly, beyond the conventional static approaches toward a chronospatial paradigm that more adequately captures cultural dynamics. N/A

titleEnglish: The Hybrid Nature of Chinese Trusts
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: The public law–private law divide has long served as a key organising principle for legal classification. In general, private law governs interpersonal relationships, enabling individuals to arrange their affairs freely, while public law regulates the administration of society, constraining individual autonomy in pursuit of collective interests. In the common law tradition, trusts are firmly embedded in private law. By contrast, China has adopted a policy-driven approach to the trust. Chinese legislators transplanted the trust as a private law instrument but deployed it to advance state policy objectives. Following the enactment of the Chinese Trust Law in 2001, regulators promptly issued numerous regulations to align trust practices with macroeconomic priorities and social welfare goals. As a result, China’s trust industry bears little resemblance to its Western counterparts, revolving around commercial trusts managed by licensed trust companies. Many of these trusts were exploited as shadow banking vehicles, allowing financial institutions to channel credit to enterprises unable to obtain loans from the state banking system. These practices heightened systemic financial risks and disrupted the state’s macroeconomic policies, obviously deviating from the policy objectives for which the trusts were intended. This project examines China’s trust regime from three perspectives: law in books (the statutory framework), law in action (the operation of trusts in practice), and law in perception (how trusts are understood by legislators, regulators, and other stakeholders). Through these lenses, it identifies the hybrid public law–private law nature of Chinese trusts: a distinctive fusion of private law principles and state-driven policy objectives. The project makes three key contributions. First, it uncovers significant discrepancies among law in books, law in action, and law in perception, analysing how these discrepancies have created confusion and hindered the development of both trust law and the industry. Second, it demonstrates that China’s policy-driven approach is the root cause of these discrepancies. It further evaluates the hybrid model of Chinese trusts, considering both its effectiveness in advancing policy objectives and its ability to unlock the potential of the trust as a private law device. These findings will provide a foundation for constructing a sustainable trust law framework that balances state policies with party autonomy. Third, the project contributes to comparative law scholarship by presenting China’s experience as a cautionary lesson for other civilian jurisdictions that have adopted or are contemplating the introduction of trusts. N/A

titleEnglish: Instituting Art and Tech: Inter-Asian Networks and Collaborative Practices of Digital Studios and Artist Collectives
department: Dept of Cultural and Religious Studies
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Arts Tech has become a buzzword in recent cultural policy in Hong Kong (The Government of Hong Kong Special Administrative Region [HKSAR], 2021; Li, 2024), but there is a lack of a nuanced understanding of how artworks are created, and the cultural ecologies of how artists and practitioners operate in the field. This research examines the practices of digital studios and collectives, which serve as organisational entities that enable collaborative artistic practices with technology. By studying the practices of digital studios, I draw attention to alternative and emerging infrastructures for art and technology, as well as the challenges and needs of artists and organisations working in this field. A deeper understanding of these practices will enable more appropriate policymaking to support and enable these groups and practices. This research will extend beyond Hong Kong to networks in neighbouring cities in East and Southeast Asia and more broadly the Asia Pacific (including Australia). By looking at studios in Hong Kong, Taipei, Shanghai/Beijing, Singapore, and Melbourne, the intention is to understand the situated practices of how these entities respond to their local social, political, and economic environments, and develop projects and collaborations as producers, curators, and artists themselves. The aim is to initiate an inter-Asian network of studios and collectives to facilitate the sharing of knowledge and resources, and develop cultural exchanges within the region. As co-founder of the digital studio MetaObjects, I have gained firsthand experience through my own practice and engagement in the field. This research extends past research developed in my monograph, titled Ecologies of Artistic Practice: Rethinking Cultural Economies Through Art and Technology (The MIT Press, 2025), which sets a foundation for my thinking through international case studies. This research firstly aims to highlight digital studios as a cultural institutional form for incubating artistic practices engaging with technology, and secondly, focuses on initiating dialogue and exchange among studios and their networks in the Asia-Pacific region, where research on art and technology histories and practices is limited. While research is situated in the Asian context, it aims to bridge a wider dialogue through international connectivity and diasporic linkages, which characterises how practitioners navigate a globalised cultural economy. To complement this research, I have received a CUHK Direct Grant (2024-25) exploring the narratives on Arts Tech in Hong Kong, and a grant for the Fund for Innovative Technology-in-Education (FITE), which aims to produce digital humanities resources for teaching and learning. N/A

titleEnglish: Managing Pedagogic Disruptions in the Age of AI: A Pilot Case Study of Faculty Responses to Assessment Challenges
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: The accelerating uptake of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education presents both opportunities and challenges. Among the most pressing is AI-related academic dishonesty, such as the unauthorised use of generative AI to complete assignments. These practices destabilise long-standing rules and ethical values, leaving many academics uncertain not only about safeguarding integrity but also about the broader purposes of university education. In Hong Kong and elsewhere, this has produced a climate of regulative ambiguity: universities actively promote AI for research, teaching, and administration while restricting student use and imposing regulations whose effectiveness is unclear. This study will address three key research challenges: how assessment can maintain integrity while recognising new technological realities; how academics will negotiate contested understandings of academic integrity, AI literacies, and AI ethics; and how AI reshapes power and control relations in assessment design and governance. The project will conceptualise AI not simply as a detriment but as a site of productive tension that illuminates shifting modes of teaching, learning, and assessment. It will take a qualitative case study approach in three stages. Stage 1 will comprise 15–20 in-depth, artefact-elicited interviews in which academics compare ‘old’ and ‘new’ assessment practices using course outlines, rubrics, and tasks. Stage 2 will analyse these accounts to examine how modalities of pedagogic control are reconfigured. Stage 3 will involve brief follow-up interviews and a concluding workshop to exchange perspectives and validate findings. The analysis will be informed by cultural–historical activity theory, which conceptualises assessment as a human activity mediated by subjects, rules, tools, divisions of labour, communities, and outcomes. Contradictions within and between these elements provide analytical entry points for understanding how AI destabilises assessment design and propels change. Complementarily, Basil Bernstein’s theory of the pedagogic device will be used to analyse how AI-induced disruptions and concerns over academic misconduct reshape power and control relations in assessment. The project will generate new knowledge by documenting how faculty in Hong Kong higher education are rethinking assessment in response to AI, offering empirical insight into their strategies, dilemmas, and innovations. Conceptually, it will extend CHAT and Bernstein’s framework to the emerging field of AI in education. Practically, it will provide evidence-based guidance for institutions seeking to balance integrity with innovation. More broadly, it will position AI disruption as both a challenge and an opportunity for renewal in assessment and higher education governance. N/A

titleEnglish: More features, more resilience? Examining how citizens navigate surveillance concerns and maintain political expression in the multi-platform and multi-modal social media environment
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: The National Security Law has redefined the boundaries of freedom of expression in Hong Kong, prohibiting acts that threaten state security, including online speech. Although the government has emphasized its minimal impact on ordinary citizens, concerns have been raised about increased surveillance and potential chilling effects on online political expression. The limited research on online expression following the implementation of the law and the broader literature on repression and self-censorship suggest that citizens concerned about surveillance adapt their online political expression rather than refraining from it entirely. They leverage social media features to maintain their political expression, such as adjusting privacy settings and removing politically dissimilar contacts from their online networks. However, these studies often focus on specific strategies or single platforms and thus lack a holistic consideration of individuals’ ability to switch between platforms for different modes of expression, despite the proliferation of different social media platforms and new features. For example, individuals may use encrypted messaging applications like Telegram or ephemeral messaging like Instagram Stories to express opinions on sensitive topics, while publicly posting less sensitive opinions on platforms like Facebook to interact with more contacts. To understand how Hong Kong citizens navigate and employ different modes of expression across social media platforms to maintain political expression amid heightened surveillance, this project introduces the concept of social media political expression repertoire (SMPER), which refers to the ability to use various social media features across platforms for different modes of political expression. Based on this concept, interviews will be conducted to explore the range of features across social media platforms that Hong Kong citizens employ for political expression and their rationales for selecting these features in different situations. The survey component of the project will explore whether a richer SMPER (i.e., greater ability to engage in different expression modes) relates to higher political expression on social media and weakens the negative association between surveillance concerns and political expression. This project will make important contributions. Theoretically, its focus on SMPER responds to recent calls in the field of political communication to examine individuals' use of multiple platforms, addressing the bias toward single platforms or specific platforms features in past studies. Moreover, it sheds light on how a richer repertoire of online expression options, enabled by the multi-platform and multi-modal social media environment, helps sustain citizens’ political expression amidst growing sanctions—a sign of citizens’ political resilience. N/A

titleEnglish: Commodified Trauma: Global Afterlives of the Girl Statue, Memorial to “Comfort Women”
department: Dept of Cultural and Religious Studies
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: This project investigates the global proliferation of the Girl Statue, a memorial to the “comfort women,” victims of sexual violence by the Japanese Empire (c.1931-1945), and its transformation into a commodity. Since its first installation in Seoul in 2011, the statue has circulated across diverse media: monuments, miniatures, LEGO toys, digital AR games, silver coins, and tattoos, originating in East Asia and spreading worldwide. The project asks: how does a symbol of historical trauma become an object of consumption and leisure? By tracing the statue’s transformation from sacred monument to circulating meme, it reveals the tension between its commemorative purpose and its reality as a commodity. I argue that the statue’s global reproduction, though well-intended, has generated a commodity economy that risks reenacting the very logic of violence it seeks to redress—extending the violence of the Japanese empire into the U.S. neoliberal empire today. As the first scholarly work to shift focus from testimony-based studies of “comfort women” to the memorial’s afterlives in visual and popular culture, this project examines the neoliberal commodification of trauma in the name of justice and humanitarianism. Combining interviews with artists, activists, and consumers with analysis of visual archives and community-based art, the methodology contributes to the anthropological turn in contemporary art history. Grounded in rigorous visual analysis, it critically engages with the “artist-as-ethnographer” model to interrogate the unexamined visual economy that now dominates global memory. Advancing a framework of ethnic and feminist spectatorship that foregrounds racial and sexual minoritarian subjectivities, the project repositions close visual analysis as a mode of ethical care. These findings offer a new lens for understanding the global memorialization of trauma, with implications for visual culture, memory studies, and cultural studies grappling with war and imperial violence. The proposed two-year grant will support completion of a monograph and two journal articles. Funding is sought for fieldwork, archival research, and interviews, and a teaching relief for writing and revision. The book manuscript is under discussion with Duke University Press and requires final research. Two chapters have been refined through workshops at Harvard and Tulane Universities, and two journal articles intended for Asia-Pacific Journal: Japan Focus and positions: asia critique will complement the monograph’s frameworks. Ultimately, this project demonstrates how the “comfort women,” in becoming images, continue to comfort the present. N/A

titleEnglish: From Minds to Machines: Architecture of Control for AI Agents
department: Dept of Cultural and Religious Studies
institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have redefined the landscape of artificial intelligence (AI). These systems now generate text, code, and scientific hypotheses, exhibiting remarkable linguistic and problem-solving abilities. Yet, their behavior remains unreliable: they hallucinate, contradict themselves, and reproduce human biases. Scaling models further has reached diminishing returns, and progress increasingly depends on developing language agents—LLMs embedded in architectures capable of perception, memory, and planning. These agents resemble modular cognitive systems, raising a central question in both cognitive science and AI—the control problem: how do multiple specialized components coordinate with each other to produce coherent, intelligent behavior? This project develops a unified framework for understanding and implementing control in cognitive systems, both biological and artificial. Drawing on my previous work on control and cognitive architecture (Bechtel & Huang, 2022; Huang, 2017; Huang, 2019), the project proceeds in two stages. First, I will develop a mechanistic account of how the brain achieves coherent, intelligent behavior without relying on a single central controller. Integrating recent findings from decision neuroscience and reinforcement learning, I will revise and advance a “Society-of-Mind” account that treats the mind as a multi-agent system. Crucially, diverse neural processes cooperate with simple control signals—akin to a “democratic” procedure that leverages wisdom-of-the-crowd effects. This model explains how flexible and systematic behavior emerges from the coordinated interaction of specialized subsystems implicated in working memory, episodic memory, and hierarchical representations of categories and relations. Second, I will translate this account into design principles for language agents. Through a structured review of existing agent architectures and targeted analyses of their control strategies, I will identify which strategies enhance the reliability, coherence, and adaptability of language agents, and how biologically-inspired design principles can complement them. The outcome is a clear set of implementable principles to guide the next generation of AI systems. Each stage will produce a peer-reviewed article and a conference presentation. This research will be conducted with collaborators specializing in cognitive neuroscience and computer science at National Taiwan University and Microsoft Research Asia, respectively. Situated within the interdisciplinary strengths of AI research at the Chinese University of Hong Kong, it will bridge philosophy, cognitive science, and AI design to advance our understanding of the control architectures of human and artificial agents. In turn, this understanding will enable us to build more capable and safer AI systems for the future. N/A

titleEnglish: "Hurddle together to stay warm": Social identification and positive adaptation among parents of disabled children in China's economically disadvantaged areas
abstract: Globally, the prevalence of intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) is 3.1%, with 80% of them living in the Global South contexts. In China’s economically disadvantaged areas (and many Global South contexts), family care is overwhelmingly drained as the default source of support for children with IDD (CwIDD), as formal and informal support is often compromised by inadequacies in social welfare and social stigma. It is hence critical to explore alternative forms of coping resources for these underserved families. The social identity approach to health literature suggests that meaningful social identification derived from social group connection can be a form of community-level coping resource that facilitates individuals’ adaptive growth following adversities. Inspired by the community’s rich self-help and mutual aid experiences in under-resource contexts, this study proposes a social identity model to family adaptation to examine the “social cure” potential of social identification derived from meaningful social connections with fellow parents of CwIDD in promoting positive adaptation. The study aims to develop a rich, contextualized, and rigorous understanding of the characteristics, socio-familial determinants, influence, and changing pathways of parents’ social identification in China’s economically disadvantaged areas. A community-engaged, three-phase explanatory sequential mixed-methods design is proposed. In Phase I, a quantitative study will examine the characteristics and socio-familial determinants of parents’ social identification, as well as the relationships among caregiving demands, social identification, and adaptation outcomes. To explain why and how social identification can be enacted as a collective coping resource and to inform service development, Phase II will conduct a qualitative constructivist grounded theory inquiry to theorize the pathways to parents’ social identity changes in the caregiving journey, focusing on the meanings, stages, triggers, and precipitating events of identity transformation. In Phase III, a co-production workshop will be organized to co-create a social identification-building service model. Partnership with three community partners will enhance the sustainable community benefits of the study. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to examine social identification as a community-level coping resource in the context of the family care of CwIDD. Academically, this study will contribute to the theoretical understanding of the social identity processes among socially stigmatized population. Practically, it will inform the development of a culturally competent social service intervention that is grounded in the community’s internal strengths and assets for underserved families in the Global South. N/A

titleEnglish: Efficacy of online Meaning-Centered Psychotherapy for Cancer Caregivers (MCP-C) in alleviating depressive symptoms among Chinese caregivers of patients with advanced cancer: A randomized controlled trial
abstract: Informal caregiving for advanced cancer patients is a global public health issue. Although sometimes valuable, long hours of caregiving are generally detrimental to the physical and psychological health of caregivers. Caregiving is complex and challenging, and associated with significant depression (42.3%), exceeding rates observed in other caregiver groups. Poor caregiver mental health could diminish care quality and negatively impact outcomes for care recipients. Current interventions have shown some effects on improving the mental well-being of caregivers of advanced cancer patients. However, these were not sustained over time, as the interventions did not address the root cause of persistent depression in this population—namely, existential distress. A culturally appropriate and novel Meaning-Centered Psychotherapy for Cancer Caregivers (MCP-C) was adapted for Chinese caregivers of patients with advanced cancer and pilot-tested by our team. The feasibility, acceptability, and preliminary effects of the MCP-C have been confirmed. A potential intervention mechanism on meaning in caregiving and positive appraisal of caregiving were supported by a theoretical model and pilot study, but have not been fully explored. The proposed two-arm randomized controlled with a sequential mixed-methods design will examine the effects of an online Chinese MCP-C on depressive symptoms and health-related outcomes. One hundred and fifty caregivers will be randomized to receive 7 weekly online MCP-C sessions delivered by trained interventionists or dosage-matched online psychoeducation sessions as the active control. Outcome measures include depressive symptoms (primary outcome), anxiety, meaning in caregiving, caregiver appraisal, spiritual well-being, and benefit finding. A linear mixed-effects model will be used to compare the effects of the intervention immediately post-intervention (T1) and at the 3-month follow-up (T2). Both quantitative (path analysis) and qualitative methods (semi-structured individual interviews) will be used to explore the mechanism of change. It is hypothesized that the MCP-C participants will experience significantly greater improvements in depressive symptoms and in several secondary outcomes at T1 and T2. Caregivers will experience enhanced meaning in caregiving and positive appraisal of caregiving. The changes in meaning in caregiving and positive appraisal of caregiving will mediate the effects of the MCP-C on caregiver depressive symptoms. This culturally appropriate and novel approach can be adopted by community mental healthcare services, incorporated into online caregiver support services in Hong Kong, and replicated in other Asian populations in other countries, to improve the mental health of informal caregivers of advanced cancer patients, which in turn will benefit the patients’ quality of care. N/A

titleEnglish: Transnational Storytelling Networks and Health Discourses among Indonesian Migrant Domestic Workers in Hong Kong
abstract: Hong Kong employs over 367,000 migrant domestic workers (MDWs), mostly from Indonesia and the Philippines, who provide indispensable care and household labour. Yet despite their centrality to the city’s economy and households, MDWs face disproportionate health vulnerabilities. Indonesian MDWs, in particular, encounter restrictive employment structures, limited healthcare access, and linguistic barriers that render them “information poor,” especially concerning health. In the absence of equitable institutional support, MDWs turn to transnational storytelling agents: interpersonal ties, community organisations, and media outlets across Hong Kong and Indonesia that circulate health-related discourses. These networks provide solidarity and information, yet they also expose workers to misinformation and harmful practices. Hence, understanding what these discourses are, how MDWs evaluate the reliability of these agents and their discourses, and how such evaluations influence their everyday health practices, is particularly important. Guided by the frameworks of migration linguistics and communication infrastructure theory (CIT), this study examines three interrelated dimensions: (1) the storytelling agents across Hong Kong and Indonesia in migrants’ networks, (2) health discourses within these networks, and (3) how MDWs evaluate the quality of both agents and discourses, and how these evaluations shape their health experiences. The interdisciplinary and intersectoral team will employ a sequential mixed-methods design, combining a cross-sectional survey with in-depth interviews. Phase 1 involves a survey of 300 Indonesian MDWs to map their storytelling agents, information needs, structural conditions, language repertoires, and health outcomes. Phase 2 consists of narrative interviews with 40 participants, allowing for a discourse analysis of how health narratives are constructed, assessed, and enacted. Thematic analysis combined with analysis of stance-taking will illuminate how migrants evaluate health information, position themselves within networks, and align with or resist particular sources of advice. Theoretically, the project contributes significantly to the development of CIT, which has long been used to analyse (im)migrant consumption of health information. More specifically, by gathering empirical data related to migrants’ evaluations of storytelling networks and using discourse analytic tools to analyse the stories themselves, this research illuminates how CIT can be used, not only to understand the links between different agents, but to demonstrate the actual impact these links have on the lives and health of marginalised migrants. Practically, it informs best practices for the development of health communication programmes targeting migrant communities, especially regarding the effective delivery of health messages and the use of effective communication channels to disseminate health information. N/A

titleEnglish: Targeting Sleep and Circadian Disturbance to Reduce Post-Stroke Depressive Symptoms: A Randomised Controlled Trial
abstract: Post-stroke depression (PSD) affects approximately 33% of stroke survivors, over twice the rate observed in age-matched adults without stroke (13%). PSD is strongly associated with a higher recurrence rate, increased mortality, and reduced quality of life. Affecting over half of stroke survivors, sleep and circadian disturbance represents a key modifiable contributor to PSD that predicts depressive symptoms and often remains as a residual problem after depression treatments, increasing relapse risk. Existing sleep interventions, such as cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I), demonstrate reduced efficacy in stroke populations. Our qualitative work highlights that the distinct and multifaceted nature of post-stroke sleep and circadian disturbances precludes easy alignment with disorder-focused sleep interventions. To address this gap, we developed a transdiagnostic intervention targeting shared behavioural mechanisms across diverse sleep and circadian disturbance in stroke survivors, guided by the National Institutes of Health’s Stage Model for Behavioural Intervention Development. The intervention integrates evidence-based approaches: CBT-I (stimulus control, sleep restriction, and cognitive strategies), chronotherapy (regularisation of sleep–wake timing and other non-photic zeitgebers), behavioural activation (scheduling and self-monitoring of meaningful daytime activity), and self-determination theory (enhancing self-determined motivation). The intervention is delivered over six weeks via modular-format 1-hour sessions comprising cross-cutting, core, and optional modules. Pilot data provide preliminary support for the hypothesized target engagement. Depressed stroke survivors who received the intervention showed reduced depressive symptoms and sleep disturbances, increased engagement in actigraphy-measured daytime activities, and improved circadian alignment, indexed by the dim light melatonin onset (DLMO)-to-bedtime phase angle. To confirm the intervention’s efficacy and the antidepressant mechanism, we propose an assessor-blinded, two-arm randomised controlled trial involving 118 community-dwelling stroke survivors with clinically significant depressive symptoms and sleep and circadian disturbance. The participants will be randomly assigned (1:1) to the intervention or an attention-matched control, with assessments conducted at baseline, post-intervention, and a three-month follow-up. The primary outcome is depressive symptom severity. Secondary outcomes include subjective and objective circadian and sleep metrics from questionnaires, actigraphy, and sleep diaries, and biological circadian phase measured by DLMO. The analyses will follow intention-to-treat linear mixed models for efficacy and mediation models to examine the antidepressant mechanism. If effective, this intervention will provide a cost-efficient and low-stigma approach for reducing PSD by addressing the often-overlooked domain of sleep and circadian health. N/A

titleEnglish: Understanding and Enhancing Road Crossing Safety for People with Disabilities
department: Department of Land Surveying and Geo-Informatics
abstract: Disability has become a prominent global social issue, with an estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide experiencing significant disabilities, including over 534,200 in Hong Kong. For many people with disabilities, walking with or without aids and wheelchair use are essential for daily mobility, providing a means to access destinations and serving as a first- and last-mile connection to public transport. However, these activities often require crossing roads, which presents significant safety concerns that can discourage people from leaving their homes. Pedestrian crossings are particularly hazardous, accounting for nearly one-third of all injury-related traffic collisions in Hong Kong. International research confirms that disabled pedestrians face disproportionately high risks, which transforms a routine activity into a dangerous obstacle course and a barrier to independent living. Although Hong Kong has established policies and design manuals to promote barrier-free access, their implementation is inconsistent. Empirical studies indicate that existing interventions are often ineffective, yet the prevalence and nature of the safety concerns faced by disabled pedestrians at road crossings remain poorly understood. This project addresses this gap by developing a novel, human-centred, and AI-powered framework to systematically assess and improve the safety of road crossings for people with disabilities, focusing on Hong Kong's two most prevalent impairments: visual and mobility impairments. The research is structured around three core objectives. First, working closely with 40 participants (20 for each impairment group), it will utilise multiple qualitative approaches, such as video diaries, go-along interviews, and in-depth interviews, to explore their lived experiences. This aims to identify the specific physical, social, and environmental barriers they encounter at different types of crossings, determine which factors facilitate or deter their mobility, and understand their design preferences for safer crossings. Second, building on these insights, this project will co-design potential interventions with participants and use advanced AI algorithms, such as controllable diffusion models, to propose actionable, context-sensitive design improvements. Finally, the project will integrate multiple geographic data sources to characterise the key features of road crossings that participants identify as concerning. By building upon the qualitative findings and integrating quantitative data sources, the research will develop a scalable, data-driven assessment tool. This tool will evaluate and map the inclusivity of road crossings across Hong Kong, visually identifying those that are unsafe or unsuitable. The outcome will be tangible, evidence-based improvements, fostering a safer and more equitable transport system for all. N/A

titleEnglish: Boosting Mathematical Modelling Outcomes with an AI-Powered Computational Design Platform: A Randomized Controlled Trial in Hong Kong Secondary Schools
abstract: Solving many problems in our world (e.g., wars, floods, climate change) requires mathematical modelling (MM) of real-world situations, but most students lack such skills. Only 9% of 15-year-old students can model complex situations (PISA, 2022). Although the Hong Kong Education Bureau introduced MM into the curriculum in 2017, many students do not understand MM, and teachers do not teach it effectively. Additionally, most current educational technologies primarily address fixed MM problems rather than supporting MM processes. Hence, we urgently need to construct a theoretical framework to guide MM learning and teaching. As computational design (CD) is closely aligned with MM, I enhanced a CD theoretical framework that blends design thinking, computational thinking, and mathematical modelling. Then, I used it to create a prototype of an AI-powered platform, CD2MM, to help learn MM. This platform will co-create teaching materials with teachers by converting real-world situations into different phases of MMs in both visual and analytical formats. Then, students will work on these materials to co-create models with multiple agents on CD2MM. This project has two stages. First, I will develop the CD2MM platform based on the current prototype by adding the main features of visual-logical scaffoldings and multi-agent studios. I will conduct eight rounds of agile development cycles involving feature development, rapid user testing, and collaborative evaluation to enhance the platform. Then I will conduct the feasibility study to test the CD2MM. Second, I will partner with the “Operation SoInno” program by Jockey Club Design Institute and local secondary schools for a field experiment. 300 junior secondary students from 12 classes across 3 schools will participate in a randomized controlled trial with pre- and post-tests conducted in the schools’ project-based learning courses. The classes will be randomly assigned to the CD2MM group or the traditional MM group. I will analyse their MM competency, self-efficacy, and experiences with multilevel linear models to evaluate the effectiveness of the CD2MM platform. I will further understand students’ epistemic patterns on the platform using ordered network analysis. This study will advance theory, practice, and policy in MM education. It will deliver (1) a CD theoretical framework for education, and (2) an evidence-based platform, CD2MM, to enhance MM education in Hong Kong’s secondary schools, with potential for global adoption. Our findings will benefit students, teachers, future employers, and educational policy makers by promoting creative mathematical education powered by AI at scale. N/A

titleEnglish: How Asian Immigrants Think Americans See Them and How It Shapes Political Engagement
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
abstract: Asian immigrants are one of the fastest-growing groups in the United States, yet questions about their acceptance and belonging remain. During the COVID-19 pandemic, anti-Asian hate crimes spiked after rhetoric about the “China virus,” and East Asian communities—especially Chinese, Korean, and Japanese Americans—have faced suspicion as U.S.–China tensions spill into domestic politics. These moments highlight the persistence of the “perpetual foreigner” stereotype, even as Asian Americans become a more visible part of American life. Research shows that inaccurate beliefs about immigrants—such as inflated estimates of their numbers or misconceptions about crime and welfare—fuel prejudice and restrictive policies. But most work examines only how native-born Americans misunderstand immigrants. Much less is known about how immigrants themselves view the majority population. This project centers on meta-perceptions—what Asian immigrants believe Americans think of them. These beliefs matter: they shape whether immigrants feel accepted, trust institutions, and engage politically. Studies in partisan politics show that people often exaggerate how negatively others see them. If Asian immigrants similarly overestimate prejudice, they may feel excluded and powerless, even when majority views are more favorable. Such distortions could dampen participation and deepen marginalization. East Asians are a critical case. They account for the largest share of recent Asian immigration, are highly visible in education and the economy, and sit at the center of debates over globalization and national security. Their status is paradoxical: celebrated as a “model minority” yet treated with suspicion in times of geopolitical strain. This tension makes East Asians uniquely positioned to reveal how belonging and exclusion operate side by side. The project uses surveys and experiments to measure Asian immigrants’ perceptions of how Americans view them, compare these perceptions to actual opinion, and test whether correcting misperceptions shifts outcomes. Corrective information will allow evaluation of whether greater accuracy reduces perceived discrimination, strengthens belonging, bolsters dual identification as Asian and American, and increases political engagement. By uncovering how distorted meta-perceptions shape immigrant incorporation, the study advances research on misperceptions and democratic inclusion. It shows that fostering integration requires not only reducing majority prejudice but also addressing how immigrants themselves imagine they are seen. N/A

titleEnglish: Towards Trustworthy Generative AI in Legal Services: A Governance-Guided Legal Reasoning System with Explainability and Verifiability
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
abstract: Generative AI often makes errors in legal contexts, such as providing false information, outdated or incorrect references, or reasoning that does not align with the legal system. This project aims to develop Verified Professional Services (VeriPS) to address these issues in legal AI systems. VeriPS will ensure that AI-generated legal answers are accurate, clear, and trustworthy. Unlike traditional AI, VeriPS focuses on delivering evidence-based answers. This approach will help reduce errors, improve accuracy, and make the system reliable and easy for people to review. The research involves four key stages. First, VeriPS will develop a system to verify AI-generated legal claims by linking them to authoritative sources, with strict adherence to jurisdictional and temporal parameters. Second, a bilingual legal knowledge base for Hong Kong will be created, featuring version histories and benchmarks for consistency. Third, the system will break down complex legal questions into smaller, verifiable components, using specialized tools to improve reasoning and precision. In the third stage, only outputs that meet high-confidence thresholds will be approved, with uncertain cases flagged for human review to prevent unsupported claims. Finally, in the fourth stage, the project will focus on governance and real-world application, integrating risk assessment tools, audit trails, and pilot testing with legal and corporate partners. The platform will be open sourced to promote transparency and broad adoption. Expected research outputs of the project is six-fold: 1) a governing framework will ensure that all outputs are legally sound and compliant with Hong Kong law; 2) a bilingual knowledge base comprising approximately 50,000 legal documents will be provided, with clear versioning, update histories, and timelines; 3) two datasets, Legal Verify with over one million real-world legal scenarios and Legal Decomp with over 150,000 expert-validated legal questions, will support the development and rigorous evaluation of AI systems for legal reasoning, enhancing accuracy, reliability, and practical applicability; 4) a suite of user-friendly tools will decompose legal problems, detect errors, and generate clear, accurate responses. 5) an AI platform (VeriPS), developed in partnership with industry, will offer risk management features, decision explanations, APIs, pilot deployments, security testing, and an open-source variant; and 6) an educational program will train lawyers, law students, and related professionals to effectively integrate these AI tools into legal practice. While tailored for Hong Kong's unique bilingual common law system, the framework is adaptable to other jurisdictions, providing a scalable model for trustworthy AI in legal and regulated environments. N/A

titleEnglish: China (Un)Plugged: Digital Explorations of the Northwest, 1937–1957
institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
abstract: This project investigates the transformation and significance of Northwest China from 1937 to 1957, a period shaped by the Sino-Japanese War and its aftermath. As the war forced a re-imagination of the country’s centre and peripheries, the Northwest emerged as the critical “rear area” (houfang) in Chinese discourse. To address gaps in existing research, this study focuses on the interplay between infrastructural actors, spatial discourses, and material infrastructures, all of which influenced the region’s development. By employing digital methods such as network analysis, topic modelling, and transport simulation, the project examines how personal and institutional relationships, historical narratives, and material infrastructures intersected to shape the spatial dynamics of national integration during this transformative period. Central to the research is the exploration of how key actors shaped and were shaped by spatial imaginations and infrastructural development. The project traces these interrelationships through archival sources, historical maps, and generated datasets, reconstructing transportation routes and uncovering the dynamics of movement and interaction in and with the Northwest. By combining spatial analysis with historical network studies, the project provides a systematic account of the Northwest’s evolving role under changing geopolitical conditions. Thereby, the study aims to demonstrate how historical ruptures influence imaginations and materialisations of space and infrastructure. It uncovers the Northwest’s strategic importance in fostering national integration and highlights the reciprocal influences between spatial discourse and material outcomes. The use of innovative digital methods enhances traditional historical research and offers new insights to the broader field of digital humanities. By examining the historical context of spatial relations, the project provides a framework for understanding how the dynamics of infrastructure, discourse, and space continue to influence contemporary practices. Ultimately, this project advances our understanding of the Northwest’s historical significance and demonstrates the transformative potential of integrating digital methods with historical inquiry. N/A

titleEnglish: Can Relaxing Building Height Restrictions Reduce Housing Costs and Enhance the Quality of Housing?
department: Department of Real Estate and Construction
abstract: Housing has become expensive almost everywhere across the world. Urban economists argue that overly stringent land use regulations are the primary reason for house price growth. With formal housing costs going up, the prevalence of inferior-quality informal houses or slums has increased in recent decades, as they provide cheaper housing alternatives to lower-income households. Building height restrictions are arguably the most widespread form of land use regulations used by planners the world over to regulate population density. The extant urban economics literature has shown that building height regulations make housing expensive. This is because in a free-market environment, higher land values predict taller buildings. Under binding building height regulations, developers are unable to build as tall as land values would dictate, leading to fewer but more expensive units. Nowhere are building height restrictions stricter than in Indian cities, where the average building height is less than two floors. Indian cities are also known for large slum populations and rapidly growing formal house prices. This makes India a great laboratory for studying the relationship between building height restrictions and housing outcomes. Can relaxing building height restrictions reduce housing costs and enhance the quality of housing? This question has become pertinent in recent years as more local governments across the world have started implementing “upzoning” reforms—a common term for deregulation of land use. I intend to investigate whether relaxing building height restrictions or upzoning can improve housing outcomes in India. To accomplish this, I plan to first develop a theoretical model explaining the housing and labor market mechanisms through which upzoning impacts housing costs and the prevalence of poor-quality slum housing. Such a model would integrate housing outcomes with labor market forces, like migration and wage earnings. I then plan on gathering survey datasets to empirically estimate the impact of upzoning reforms on housing rents, slum housing prevalence, floor space consumption, and wage earnings. I will also estimate the impact of upzoning on house prices using a property transaction dataset. For this empirical estimation exercise, I plan on using a staggered difference-in-difference design by exploiting several upzoning reforms implemented across large Indian cities. The sample of the study will be limited to the ten largest metropolitan areas of India, partly because they are comparable, and upzoning data on these cities is readily available. The findings from this project will potentially contribute to the urban economics literature on upzoning. N/A

titleEnglish: Architecture and Television in 1980s Milan
abstract: This is a study of architecture in television and, conversely, television in architecture. On one hand, it examines set designs and the material network of broadcasting centres and antennas required for the production and dissemination of TV content. On the other, it investigates the architectural consumption of television, or how the TV screen transformed the design and experience of domestic and public space. The historical and geographical focus of this project is specific: 1980s Milan. During this decade, the city shifted from a manufacturing to a service-based economy, becoming, among other things, a hub for television. After the liberalisation of private broadcasting in 1974, numerous television channels emerged in the city. By 1978, around thirty were operating, including Silvio Berlusconi’s TeleMilano, which broadcast from Milano 2, the suburb he completed in 1976. TeleMilano marked the end of Berlusconi’s real estate career and the beginning of his media empire. By 1982, he owned three channels all managed by Mediaset, his company. By 1985, Mediaset had consolidated its operations into a purpose-built production centre. This project examines the design of Mediaset’s 1980s TV shows, alongside the work of Milan-based artists, architects, and interior and furniture designers, many of whom worked with and for the medium. In essence, the project analyses the architectural manifestations of a dramatic shift in Italian culture and politics, one that arguably paved the way for Berlusconi’s rise to power in 1994. Through archival and library research and interviews, it explores the relationship between architecture and television in 1980s Milan, focussing on its epistemological dimensions and political implications. This project arises from an urgent need both within and beyond architectural history. Within the discipline, analyses of television have largely focussed on the mediatisation of architecture on screen—in documentaries, news broadcasts, films, and series. Yet we still know comparatively little about the architecture of television itself (the design of television shows) and the architecture of television viewing (furniture, domestic interiors, collective public spaces). Beyond the discipline, the project responds to the growing interest in how media technologies shape environments and cultural landscapes. And in 1980s Milan, television was not just entertainment but a force shaping opinion and social behaviour, with architecture playing a critical role as both content and framework for its reception. In short, this project offers a comprehensive study of television through the lens of architectural history. N/A

titleEnglish: Opening Urban Gateways to Global Connectivity? Exploring how the Belt and Road Initiative Is Driving Logistics-Led Urbanisation
abstract: This project will investigate the relationship between logistics-led urbanisation (LLU) and the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), with the objective of generating empirical, theoretical, and policy-relevant insights into how global connectivity reshapes urban governance, spatial development, and local livelihoods. The BRI has become one of the central strategies shaping global connectivity and urban transformations, and a particularly strong articulation of “global China”. Yet the dominant geopolitical and political-economic interpretations of the BRI pay less attention to its urban dimension. In response, the project will interrogate global China and the BRI from a local and urban perspective, investigating the phenomenon of logistics-led urbanisation (LLU), understood as the development of logistics infrastructure through which urban actors connect with transnational supply chains to stimulate local growth. Specifically, the research will capture infrastructural, spatial, governance, and social changes produced by LLU, analyse the strategies of Chinese actors and their navigation of local governance structures, examine how local stakeholders negotiate and contest LLU, and assess its impact on local livelihoods and labour. The rapid development of BRI-inspired projects worldwide underscores the significance of the proposed research. It will explore two European cases of BRI-inspired LLU, located in Liège (Belgium) and Małaszewicze (Poland). Both sites have recently emerged as strategic nodes in China–Europe trade corridors, catalysing profound transformations in “left-behind” regions historically marked by deindustrialisation and economic decline. Through a comparative, multi-scalar, and qualitative research design, the project will combine systematic analysis of governance and planning documents, semi-structured interviews with Chinese and local actors, focus groups with community representatives, and on-site observations of logistics zones. Spatial analysis will complement qualitative data to visualise infrastructural transformations and planning strategies. The outcome of the project will address existing research gaps by making four empirical, theoretical and policy-related contributions. First, it will re-centre urban governance and social dynamics in BRI research, complementing dominant state-centric and political-economic approaches. Second, it will advance theoretical debates about infrastructure-led urbanisation by conceptualising LLU as a distinctive mode of urban development under global connectivity shaped by the BRI, thus capturing the specificities of BRI-inspired urbanism. Third, the project will explore LLU as a concept that connects the BRI with critical perspectives on transport and logistics. Finally, it will produce actionable recommendations for stakeholders and communities engaged in LLU, outlining strategies for harnessing opportunities and mitigating risks to ensure socially and economically sustainable urban futures. N/A

titleEnglish: Simulating Peer Students with Generative AI for Learning by Peer Teaching in Higher Education
abstract: Generative AI (GenAI) is reshaping how students approach coursework in higher education. GenAI systems (e.g., ChatGPT) allow students to complete assignments individually with minimal peer interaction, which can reduce the communication and self-reflection that are core career-readiness skills. The learning-by-peer-teaching approach offers promise to address this risk as it has been shown to help students learn more when required to explain ideas as they teach a novice peer. It strengthens social interaction and metacognitive processes. Prior research has leveraged the learning-by-teaching approach to build teachable bots/agents (software “students” that learn from what human students teach them). Although these bots showed learning benefits, they were costly to build and limited to fixed design rules. GenAI offers opportunities to create teachable bots that simulate peer students and embody diverse student characteristics, including different levels of prior knowledge and communication styles (e.g., questioners or answer-givers). These variations create learning conditions that require students to adapt explanations, monitor understanding, and regulate strategies; the dialogue with the bots also gives students practice with communication and self-reflection. The project aims to develop and test design principles and methods for using GenAI-powered teachable bots that function as peer students. By calibrating GenAI behaviour, the bots will reflect variations in prior knowledge and communication styles. Their effectiveness will be evaluated through randomised controlled trials in university classes that test learning gains, motivation, metacognition, and how students participate and explain ideas in both one-to-one tutoring and triads. Triads will include both two students with one bot and one student with two bots. The trials will also examine how variations in the bots’ prior knowledge and communication styles affect learning processes and outcomes. The project will investigate interaction patterns over time to guide adaptive mechanisms that sustain engagement and support regulation. The contributions of this research will be multifold. Conceptually, it will extend the learning-by-teaching approach into GenAI settings. Methodologically, it will provide design principles for configuring GenAI models to simulate diverse student characteristics, together with process-sensitive measures that capture how students teach and interact with simulated peers. Empirically, it will offer evidence from randomised trials on effects for learning gains, metacognition, and communication. Practically, it will produce design principles for teachable bots and guidelines for integrating them into classes. All contributions will be implemented in an open-source platform to maximise impact in higher education and strengthen students’ collaborative learning in the AI era. N/A

titleEnglish: Beyond Transaction and Anonymity: Unveiling the Practice of Ghostwriting in Tang Dynasty
abstract: Although widespread, ghostwriting tends to be regarded as a somewhat underhanded practice in contemporary society. In contrast, under the Tang 唐 dynasty (618–907), the practice of daibi 代筆 or daizu 代作—literally, “writing on behalf of another” and commonly translated as “ghostwriting”—permeated all strata of literate society. A significant portion of surviving Tang literature consists of ghostwritten works by a diverse array of authors, catering to diverse putative authors, and serving distinct purposes. Among them, indeed, are literary masterpieces. However, this significant Tang phenomenon remains under-researched, perhaps due to our contemporary disparagement of ghostwriting. This project, as the first comprehensive examination of Tang ghostwriting, attempts to reveal its prevalence, literary features, sociopolitical roles, and broader cultural implications. By undertaking a close reading of representative texts across genres, combined with theoretical frameworks and comparative perspectives, this study will uncover the literary and rhetorical features of Tang ghostwritten works. It will thoroughly investigate ghostwriters from diverse social backgrounds and, given that their identities were often known to the public, reveal the dual voices—that of the putative author and their own—conveyed in their works. Additionally, this project will demonstrate that ghostwriting extended beyond a mere service for the literarily unskilled or commercial transaction of literary expertise. By exploring the roles played by ghostwriting in Tang dynasty society and politics, this study will reveal the vital functions it served, such as seeking patronage, consolidating friendships, and legitimizing authority. Highlighting the roles of delegation, cooperation, and negotiation in Tang dynasty ghostwriting, the study will also draw parallels with contemporary discussions of authorship, authenticity, and the nature of writing. These insights will in turn contribute to modern debates on ghostwriting, including the emergence of ghostwriting facilitated by large language model (LLM)-based agents. Ultimately, this project will enhance our understanding of a neglected area of Tang literature and culture, the practice of ghostwriting, and the concepts of authorship and authenticity in both premodern and modern contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Evaluating the Necessity and Feasibility of a Sentencing Council in Hong Kong: Lessons from Comparative Perspectives
abstract: Sentencing decisions are one of the most public-facing acts of the judiciary. Yet in many common law jurisdictions including Hong Kong, sentencing and its reform remain opaque, inconsistently structured, and undertheorised at the institutional level. The proposed project examines the necessity, feasibility and desirability of establishing a sentencing council or similar body in Hong Kong. Sentencing councils are bodies that provide guidance, advice, and information on sentencing to courts, governments, and the public. Sentencing Councils have been established in England and Wales, Scotland, Australia, and elsewhere to enhance consistency, transparency, and public confidence in the criminal justice system. However, jurisdictions such as Canada, New Zealand, and Hong Kong have either resisted such reforms or failed to institutionalise them. The reasons for this divergence and their implications remain underexplored. The proposed project fills in the literature gap. Drawing on legal doctrinal analysis and comparative studies, the project will map the institutional design and functions of sentencing bodies across selected jurisdictions. The proposed project will: 1) analyse why sentencing councils have emerged in some jurisdictions and not others; (2) develop a typology of existing sentencing institutions and evaluate their structures, functions, and limitations; (3) assess the existing sentencing framework in Hong Kong and identify institutional gaps; and (4) propose reform models suitable for Hong Kong’s legal and constitutional context. The research will map the institutional design, membership and mandate of sentencing bodies worldwide. Empirical methods, including surveys of participants in the criminal justice system and interviews, will examine stakeholder attitudes towards sentencing reform.
