The project will create a normative typology of sentencing institutions by categorising them according to structural and functional criteria, evaluating each jurisdiction with its unique sentencing reform mechanisms. The typology will assess indicators of institutional success, such as sustainability, adaptability, and insulation from short-term political pressures. Findings will be used to assess whether Hong Kong possesses the structural prerequisites for adopting a statutory sentencing council, a non-statutory advisory body, or a hybrid model to help the city develop its own unique path for sentencing reform. Hong Kong’s sentencing system, while rooted in appellate guidelines and precedent, lacks clear mechanisms, a data information system, or avenues for public consultation. Debates about sentencing consistency, transparency, and judicial discretion have intensified in recent years. By exploring feasible institutional reform options, this research will contribute significantly to both legal scholarship and sentencing reform development. N/A

titleEnglish: Mortar: Material Weakness and the Scientific Value Revisited
abstract: This project applies a history of science and knowledge approach to the weak material mortar. Mortar is a mixture of sand, water, and lime/cement, which hardens to bind masonry blocks, seal holes and gaps, and cover the surfaces of structures. Mortar joints, fillings, and coatings often appear to be the weakest points in buildings and sites in the inevitable processes of deterioration and damage. While industrial interests have driven scientific research to produce stronger mortar materials since the 18th century, conservation activities have also uncovered the diversity of natural knowledge in making and using mortars in human history. Yet this body of conservation knowledge remains on the margins of major sciences and the historiography of science. This project will revisit and recontextualise this body of knowledge anew. There are two problems the project aims to tackle. The first is the conceptual framework of “weak knowledge” discussed by some historians of science, which has overlooked material weakness as an epistemic dimension in knowledge production. The second concerns scientific value, which historians have often characterised through discourses on precision, quantification, and reproducibility—such concepts that reflect modern Western epistemology. Focusing on weak mortar, this project will conduct a historical examination of mortar technology not only within spheres of modern science but also in the conservation experiences of late imperial China. It will demonstrate how material weakness generated new knowledge and innovation under distinct epistemic conditions, and argue that care for vulnerability is a key value integral to the productions of scientific and rational knowledge under examination. Two primary sources will be used, complemented by fieldwork. The first is a little-studied corpus produced by modern scientists and engineers since 1839, focusing on weathering, erosion, decay, degradation, and deterioration of built environments. The second consists of Chinese local gazetteers compiled by literati elites in the 8th-20th centuries, containing records of decay, damage, and maintenance of historic sites. Historical methods and digital techniques will be employed to explore links between these sources. Fieldwork aims to situate the interplay of local and global knowledge and to further decolonise Western epistemologies. Particular attention will be paid to the World Heritage site The Palace Museum of Beijing and the marginalised countryside areas of Hong Kong. This project will historicise different perceptions and understandings of material weakness, decolonise sciences in China, and make a case for an inclusive historiography of knowledge. N/A

titleEnglish: Lyric Engines: Romantic and Early Victorian Women's Poetry and the Industrial Imagination
abstract: This project situates women’s poetry in Britain within the industrial, scientific, and technological transformations of the Romantic and early Victorian periods. While scholarship on literature and the Industrial Revolution has tended to foreground male authors and prose genres, women’s poetic engagements with industrialisation, mechanisation, and expanding knowledge networks remain critically underexplored. By bringing together better-known figures, such as Joanna Baillie and Elizabeth Barrett Browning, with lesser-known poets, many of whom are from the labouring classes, this study reconstructs the various ways in which women challenged the nineteenth-century masculinisation of technology and probed the imagined boundaries between human and nonhuman minds and bodies. Methodologically, the project combines close reading, archival recovery, and historicist contextualisation with perspectives from feminist theory, posthumanism, and the history and philosophy of technology. It examines how women’s poetry reimagined the relationship between humans and machines by using lyric (and other poetic) modes as a kind of technology, with rhythms and rhetorical framings that could both reflect and resist the age’s increasingly mechanical culture. Case studies range from the mythologised industrial landscapes of Anna Seward’s ‘Colebrook Dale’ (1810) and Eleanor Anne Porden’s 'The Veils' (1815); to descriptions of energetic bodies in Mary Robinson’s 'The Progress of Liberty' (1801) and Ann Yearsley’s 'A Poem on the Inhumanity of the Slave-Trade' (1788); to poetry by Letitia Elizabeth Landon, Caroline Norton, and Caroline Bowles Southey denouncing the factory system’s dehumanisation of women and children (Bowles, using the orientalist image of the Juggernaut in her poems, further brings wider questions about empire into the frame); to comparisons between industrial labour and poetic production in Elizabeth Barrett Browning’s 'Aurora Leigh' (1856) and poetry by women factory workers; to speculations about the “poetical science” of algorithms in the ground-breaking work of Ada Lovelace. By historicising nineteenth-century intersections of gender, imaginative writing, and technology, the study reframes contemporary debates on artificial intelligence, creativity, authenticity, and agency, revealing their deep Romantic roots. It contributes to the growing field of literature and technoscience, to the recovery of women’s writing, and to feminist and posthumanist understandings of our technological condition. Ultimately, it argues that Romantic thinking, defined not by nostalgia but by imagination and productive uncertainty, offers vital resources for engaging with the machines of our present and future. N/A

titleEnglish: Using Large Language Models to Capture and Understand Human-like Representations in Everyday Decisions
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming societies and industries, and it is also reshaping how cognitive and behavioral scientists study the mind. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) provides researchers with unprecedented opportunities to develop theories and tools that extend beyond human cognition. We argue that two key properties of LLMs make them especially valuable for understanding human decision-making and for training intelligent agents that behave in more human-like ways: (i) their capacity to rapidly simulate human decisions in silico, enabling scalable human experiments, and (ii) their flexibility to adapt quickly to specific scenarios through in-context learning and lightweight interventions that can steer behavior without retraining. Building on these properties, we aim to leverage LLMs to unlock large-scale human data collection in more naturalistic decision-making scenarios, without imposing additional time or effort on participants. Specifically, we will examine decisions with real-world relevance that involve risk, time, morality, and social interaction. To accomplish this, we will develop automation tools for running online behavioral experiments at scale, use crowdsourcing platforms for participant recruitment, and integrate LLMs to provide informative priors that guide human data collection. Our focus will be on characterizing how uncertainties are represented in these decisions and on using Bayesian models both to optimally design human experiments and to provide mechanistic accounts of human and LLM behavior. The resulting human data will also support closer alignment between humans and LLMs. We will explore sample-efficient and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods to distill human data into LLMs, creating a mutually reinforcing research cycle: better-aligned LLMs will further accelerate human experimentation, while realigning AI models on improved human data will promote safer and more effective deployment in real-world contexts. Ultimately, the proposed project seeks to advance our theoretical understanding of both LLM and human behavior, enhance the design of automated behavioral experiments, and improve the human-likeness of LLMs, thereby contributing to the development of safer and more robust AI systems. The PI’s prior experience --- spanning (i) Bayesian models of cognition, (ii) large-scale online experiments on human strategic decisions, (iii) eliciting latent representations from humans and LLMs using Markov chain Monte Carlo, (iv) post-training LLMs with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, and (v) activation editing to steer LLM behavior --- provides a strong foundation for carrying out the proposed research. N/A

titleEnglish: Frontier Effects: the Japanese Colonization of Hokkaido, 1869-1905
department: School of Modern Languages and Cultures
abstract: My project examines the history of settler colonialism in Hokkaido: today Japan’s northernmost island and the unceded territories of the Indigenous Ainu people. While the Ainu were long recognized by the Tokugawa bakufu, in 1869 Japan's new Meiji government annexed Hokkaido outright, claiming their territories as terra nullius (“empty” or “ownerless” land). Thereafter, Japan’s colonization functioned through the dislocation and displacement of the Ainu. This included violent forms of physical removal such as forced migrations. However, it also took the form of discursive erasures including romantic pioneer myths that claimed settlers were heroically “reclaiming” the “virgin soil” of Ainu villages and tropes of the Ainu, spread throughout popular media and national textbooks, as stateless “barbarians”. I argue that, because the Ainu’s continuing presence revealed a fundamental contradiction at the heart of the terra nullius doctrine and thus undermined Japanese sovereignty in Hokkaido, this discourse became a crucial means for the Japanese state and common settlers alike to reaffirm claims to Ainu territories. In turn, it served to naturalize the forced removal of the Ainu from the land and legitimize the presence of Japanese settlers. Troublingly, conventional scholarship on Japanese colonialism in Hokkaido focuses overwhelmingly on the experiences of settlers, ignoring the Ainu or treating them purely as victims. Such scholarship, I argue, often tacitly reproduces the logics of terra nullius. Challenging received historiography, my research demonstrates that the Ainu not only persisted on the land but actively resisted Japanese colonization. Drawing on newspaper articles, petitions, interviews, and 20th century Ainu memoirs, I document the often-overlooked history of Ainu resistance against and non-compliance with colonial policy. This included physical confrontations between the Ainu and settlers, sizable organized protests in Hokkaido and Tokyo, and patterns of deliberate subversion. This resistance constituted a tangible threat to Japanese rule and, as a result, prompted the state to intensify its suppression of Ainu sovereignty. This led to other forms of dislocation which aimed to suppress the emerging Ainu anti-colonial movement, such as the state’s designation of the Ainu as “former aborigines” in need of “protection” and, on this basis, its suppression of their civil rights. N/A

titleEnglish: When Work Disrupts the Body’s Clock: Trajectories of Working-time Arrangements, Epigenetic Ageing, and Gene–Environment Interplay
department: Department of Social Work and Social Administration
abstract: Working-time arrangements, including nonstandard work schedules (e.g., evenings, nights, split/rotating shifts, and irregular hours) and long hours, are increasingly prevalent in 24/7 economies in the world and important contributors to health disparities. Yet our understanding of how such schedules “get under the skin” remains limited. Existing research has focused largely on self-reported outcomes such as stress, sleep quality, or subjective wellbeing, leaving molecular pathways to accelerated ageing and chronic disease in the dark. Moreover, most studies treat work schedules as static, single-point exposures and neglect household dynamics that lead to changes in work time arrangements over the life course. Much less is known on how the health risks of working-time arrangement interact with genetic predispositions across diverse social and demographic contexts. This project addresses these limitations through two complementary, interdisciplinary studies. We focus on two interrelated outcomes: (1) biological ageing, measured with epigenetic clocks capturing cumulative physiological wear-and-tear, and (2) obesity, a chronic disease with strong genetic underpinnings and established links to work stress and schedules. Study 1 investigates how trajectories of working-time arrangements, captured through repeated measures of weekly hours and timing over 18 years, relate to biological ageing. Using sequence analysis, we identify patterns such as stable standard hours, persistent long hours, or irregular shifts, and link these to DNA methylation data from about 3,600 participants in the UK Household Longitudinal Study. Study 2 examines whether long-run working-time trajectories moderate genetic susceptibility to higher body mass index (BMI). The BMI polygenic score, derived from UK Household Longitudinal Study genetic data, aggregates the weighted effects of 480 genome-wide significant genetic variants associated with BMI, providing a validated measure of genetic propensity to adiposity. The contribution of this project is threefold. First, it provides the first evidence on how dynamic, long-term work schedules affect molecular ageing and obesity, moving beyond static, self-reported outcomes. Second, it advances theories of life course inequality by demonstrating whether working-time arrangements accelerate biological wear-and-tear. Third, it generates evidence relevant for policy, including labour regulation, occupational health, and family wellbeing in societies with 24/7 economies. The insights offer a valuable test case for Hong Kong, where employees work the longest hours among OECD economies, while recognising institutional differences may shape how patterns are perceived. The project fills a critical gap in understanding how work and biology intersect and makes a unique contribution by bridging social science and molecular genetics to inform scholarship and practice. N/A

titleEnglish: RAISE Liver Triage From Primary Care: Risk-Stratified Precise Referral for Early Liver Assessment in Adults with Diabetes
department: Department of Family Medicine and Primary Care
abstract: Liver disease is a rising but under-recognized burden among adults with diabetes, often progressing silently to advanced stages such as cirrhosis or hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Despite high prevalence—73% with fatty liver and 18% with fibrosis in Hong Kong Diabetes Register data—adherence to liver assessment remains poor, and hospital imaging capacity is constrained. Earlier precise referrals of liver assessment (for liver scarring or cancer risk) from primary care clinics—where people with diabetes already attend (e.g., RAMP-DM)—can overcome poor adherence and limited hospital capacity, raise timely scan completion and yield. The RAISE Liver Triage project proposes a scalable, risk-stratified referral pathway embedded within current RAMP-DM primary care program to address these gaps, by leveraging routine diabetes visits to prioritize liver assessment referrals using readily available clinical data. Objective 1 describes current liver assessment referral processes for adults with diabetes in Hong Kong Hospital Authority (HA) settings. This includes identifying who is referred, from which care settings (e.g., RAMP-DM, GOPC, Family Medicine Clinics), for which tests (e.g., ultrasound, FibroScan, biomarkers), and how long it takes to complete assessment. Objective 2 characterizes downstream outcomes among referred patients, including diagnosis stage, waiting time, treatment delays, hospital length of stay, readmission, and mortality. Objective 3 develops and validates a machine learning–based risk score and nomogram using routine data to guide precise referrals. The score will be embedded in the EHR to auto-populate referral forms and trigger reminders. Internal validation will include calibration, discrimination, and decision-curve analysis, while external validation will be conducted across three partner hospitals: HKU–Shenzhen Hospital, Guangdong Second Provincial General Hospital, and Gansu Wu Wei Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital. Objective 4 evaluates equity and subgroup differences in referral completion and outcomes by sex, age, socioeconomic status, comorbidity profiles, and care setting. Stratified models and fairness checks will guide targeted interventions and safeguard against disparity amplification. Analytical methods will include descriptive statistics and multivariable models suited to outcome types—linear, logistic, Cox, and Poisson/negative binomial upon fitting. Success will be measured by increased scan adherence, reduced diagnostic delays, and improved patient outcomes. Dashboards will track key metrics to support continuous improvement and budget-impact planning. Aligned with the Primary Healthcare Blueprint and CDCC initiatives, this project offers a data-driven, policy-relevant solution to enhance liver health risk early detection and management from primary diabetes care. N/A

titleEnglish: Kant, Sympathy, and the Moral Life
abstract: Human relationships are foundational to living a good life. Yet it is becoming increasingly difficult to know what good human relationships look like. How should one relate to others amidst deep political uncertainty or the likely significant social changes that will come with the implementation of new technologies like generative AI? To effectively answer questions like these it is necessary to conduct a philosophical investigation into the nature of human relationships and to articulate their ideal moral foundations. In this project, I will conduct such an investigation and articulate a philosophical framework for human relationships based on the work of Immanuel Kant. This framework will produce a monograph outlining character traits that are crucial to human flourishing and applying them to key features of everyday life. Kant’s published work is known for its emphasis on human dignity and rational thought. The framework developed here is grounded in these elements. Chapter one extends them further, reconstructing a virtue of rational sympathy based on material from Kant’s lectures and lesser-known texts. I argue that rational sympathy is an intellectual master virtue and is central to good moral character in the Kantian sense. In chapter two, I articulate an account of moral habit and improvement consistent with this account of sympathy, showing that Kantian ethics has the resources to explain ordinarily desirable features of the good life, contrary to objections from historical and contemporary thinkers. Chapter three then develops a novel account of moral worth, the core of virtuous action, consistent with this. Chapter four reconstructs an account of forgiveness as an imperfect duty and Kantian virtue that requires the development of humility, self-respect, and sympathy. Chapters five through seven focus on the moral features of everyday life: family, friendship, and professional roles. Chapter five reconstructs Kant’s account of marriage and the family to illuminate what Kantian moral psychology can say about these topics today. Chapter six examines the role of pleasure alongside moral duty in friendship. In it, I argue that Kantian duty is necessary to maintain worthwhile friendships and does not threaten or diminish their non-moral value. Chapter seven examines what it looks like to live with integrity in professional environments with significant moral uncertainty and distress, arguing that a Kantian conception of conscience is both necessary and well-suited to establish boundaries for moral action and responsibility. N/A

titleEnglish: Does Career Development Learning Influence Early Career Success? A Longitudinal Study Examining University Students’ Transition to Work.
abstract: University students today face an increasingly competitive and unpredictable economic environment. Given the role of higher education in shaping young people’s career trajectories and life plans, are universities dedicating the right resources to career development learning (CDL)? Does behavioural, cognitive, and affective engagement in CDL activities, such as internships and career coaching, effectively equip students with career development self-efficacy, clarify their career goals, and help them achieve early career success? Conversely, are students who are less proactive in CDL at a disadvantage, or do they achieve early career success via other pathways and experiences? In light of the rapidly evolving career landscape, there is an urgent need to update our understanding of CDL experiences, career development self-efficacy and early career success and setback, particularly from the perspectives of students and graduates. These key stakeholders can provide first-hand insights into the dynamic changes occurring within contemporary society. This proposed project will be a longitudinal mixed-methods study to investigate the impact of CDL on Hong Kong (HK) university students’ early career success/setback. Survey data will be collected in two waves from 1800 university students across four universities. A quantitative approach will be used to examine the relationship between students’ engagement in CDL, their self-efficacy in career development, career goal clarity, and early career success. At the same time, a qualitative multiple case study approach will be used to examine the university-to-work transition experiences of at least 13 students exhibiting different levels of engagement in CDL. These students will be tracked for a year, during which video reflections will be collected using an innovative online platform (i.e. Indeemo), which allows for the collection of real-time insights into the lived experiences of graduates navigating the early stages of their career. The analysis will focus on their paths to achieving early career success or their experiences with early career setback,and how these outcomes are influenced by their level of engagement with CDL,their self-efficacy in career development,and their career goal clarity. While previous research tends to focus on career success,the proposed project challenges this “success-only” narrative by exploring both early career success and setbacks. Findings will not only contribute to the conceptualization of youth career development in HK but can also inform education and government policy recommendations.This will ensure resources are directed in a targeted and meaningful way to better assist the next generation in realizing their aspirations and potential. N/A

titleEnglish: Powering Survey Research with Census Data and Predictions from Machine Learning and LLM
abstract: Surveys are increasingly costly with falling response, yet many analyses need tighter confidence intervals than budgets buy. Meanwhile, census-scale microdata and modern prediction techniques—including zero-/few-shot LLMs—are becoming more accessible. Our project targets this tension directly: we aim to increase effective sample size of surveys not by conducting more interviews but by utilizing auxiliary data and computational capacity. Its core purpose is to increase statistical power and reduce standard errors for widely used survey estimands by combining shorter-but-wider survey-like data with larger-but-narrower census‑like data using prediction‑powered inference (PPI), a recently-developed statistical framework. Specifically, we aim to test PPI on a diverse range of widely-used datasets and outcomes, and document the improvement in statistical power PPI brings to them. Building on this, we would release our predictions which would (1) immediately benefit the users of all these datasets by increasing their effective sample size; (2) inform future data collections to focus more on harder-to-predict outcomes and less on easier-to-predict ones. Methodologically, we plan to develop design‑consistent PPI for complex surveys: weighted estimating‑equation representations and variance estimators that respect weights, strata, PSUs, replicate schemes, and common two‑phase subsamples. Substantively, we evaluate gains not only for means but for associations and interactions central to sociological inference, with special attention to intersectional and small‑domain targets where data sparsity is acute. A pilot on NHIS 2017 (42 outcomes) demonstrates feasibility. Using harmonized ACS covariates, we compare cross‑fitted within‑year predictors, models trained on prior NHIS waves, and a calibrated zero‑shot LLM benchmark. PPI delivers average effective‑sample gains of about 10% for means and ~6–8% for OLS coefficients (age, sex, education, race), with substantial heterogeneity: several outcomes (e.g., self‑rated health, activity, functional limitation) show >20–30% gains, while others (e.g., smoking intensity, depressive‑feeling items) see little improvement. Because analysts work one outcome at a time, these outcome‑specific gains are practically decisive. If this pattern generalizes, design‑consistent PPI offers a standards‑compatible route to tighter survey statistics, credible intersectional analysis, and smarter use of scarce interviewing resources, all without altering questionnaires or inflating budgets. N/A

titleEnglish: Computational observatory for tracking and understanding research impact within and beyond science
department: Department of Social Work and Social Administration
abstract: Science is a public good, and the study of science is a social process. While scientific research is essential for driving economic growth, improving human health, and raising living standards, quantifying the impact of science within science on research and its effects outside of science on society remains a challenge. The convergence of three recent developments, including the availability of large-scale datasets, rapid advances in AI, and the emergence of the science of science research, allows us to develop a computational observatory to track and understand the broader impact of science. The proposed study contains three interrelated thrusts. Thrust one will quantify the research use of science. Specifically, from large-scale publications, we will identify new research methods and tools developed in science, analyze their adoption across different fields, quantify their impact on producing novel research, and study their effects on the careers of researchers. We will focus particularly on exploring the use of generative AI in scientific research. Thrust two will quantify the medicine use of science. Specifically, utilizing new datasets of drug development and research outputs, we will assemble a first-of-its-kind corpus of papers behind each marketed drug, mapping the translational pathway from sciences to medicines. We will employ network modeling methods to study the regularities in the evolution of science that lead to each drug’s success, with a particular focus on Alzheimer’s drugs. Thrust three will quantify the policy use of science. Specifically, we will study how scientific evidence is utilized in policy-making by analyzing large-scale datasets of policy documents. There, we will characterize the science used by policy and study the interactions between policy-making institutions. We will focus on mental health policy to examine the alignment and discrepancies between scientific evidence and policy application. The proposed study has implications for multiple stakeholders, including researchers, innovators, and policymakers. It will not only provide actionable insights to leverage new technologies to augment research capabilities, but also inform more effective strategies for health investments and support the creation of evidence-based policies. The computational observatory will enable continuous monitoring of research impact, fostering an evidence-based approach to decision-making. Ultimately, insights from this study have the potential to enhance the societal benefits of science, contributing to the advancement of scientific research, human health, and effective governance. N/A

titleEnglish: An Ecological Approach to Youth Wellbeing in Esports Participation: Development and Validation of Multidimensional Assessment Measures
department: Academy for Educational Development and Innovation
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: The global esports industry is expanding rapidly driven by the massive player base and enormous revenue generated from video gaming. In Asia, esports have gained widespread legitimacy as a recognised sport, especially in China, which is a prominent global leader in the esports market because of its largest pool of players, extensive government initiatives, and substantial funding from various sectors. Hong Kong, which benefits from its strategic location, acts as a crucial link between Eastern and Western esports communities. Since 2017, the Hong Kong SAR Government has actively promoted the industry by allocating HK$100 million in the 2017/18 and 2018/19 budgets to drive its growth and ecosystem development. While esports participation offers economic, social, and cognitive benefits, esports participants frequently face wellbeing challenges that are influenced by various interconnected factors, including physical health, academic performance, family dynamics, and societal impacts. Despite growing awareness, validated tools to assess and manage these dimensions of wellbeing in esports contexts systematically are lacking. The governments and the wide range of stakeholders of the esports industry are in urgent need of scientific and culturally relevant assessments to foster the healthy and sustainable development of esports while safeguarding the overall wellbeing of young people. This research project draws on ecological systems theory to develop and validate a multidimensional measure of wellbeing for youth aged 15 to 35 who are engaged in esports in Hong Kong and Mainland China. The research involves three phases: 1) semi-structured interviews with esports participants (n = 30) to identify the key ecological influence on well-being and inform the development of a culturally relevant, youth-centered item pool; 2) pilot testing (n = 200) followed by a large-sample validation (n = 1500) using Exploratory and Confirmatory Factor Analyses to examine the scale’s factor structure and establish reliability and validity of the assessment; and 3) exploration of cross-regional differences in wellbeing profiles and test for the variation in the relationships between ecological factors and wellbeing outcomes between the two populations using Multi-Group Structural Equation Modeling. This project will provide the first ecologically grounded, psychometrically sound tool for assessing youth wellbeing in esports, shedding light on how wellbeing profiles differ between regions. The findings will provide valuable insights for game developers, educators, and policymakers who are seeking to promote healthy and supportive esports gaming environments. Furthermore, they will offer culturally informed strategies that are tailored to both regions for effectively promoting youth wellbeing. N/A

titleEnglish: Ethical Co-Creativity with Generative Artificial Intelligence: Investigating Visual Arts Students’ Acceptance, Agency and Responsibility in the Creative Process
department: Department of Cultural and Creative Arts
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: The widespread public adoption of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) marks a pivotal moment in the intersection of technology and visual arts education. GenAI offers new avenues for innovation to students exploring their creative potential. However, it raises critical ethical questions about the future of creativity and the evolving role of GenAI in artistic practices. These issues are especially relevant for undergraduate students as they navigate key stages of their artistic development and prepare for professional careers. This project will investigate how Hong Kong fine arts and graphic design students engage with GenAI as a creative and ethical partner. It will introduce the Ethical–Aesthetic Technology Acceptance Model (EA-TAM), extending the classical model by integrating creative agency and ethical awareness, which are core factors shaping GenAI adoption. The PI will (1) examine students’ attitudes towards GenAI; (2) document how GenAI is applied within creative workflows; (3) identify key ethical issues; and (4) develop a theoretical framework explaining ethical co-creativity in human–AI artmaking. The EA-TAM will provide a structured lens to help students reconcile artistic creativity with responsible AI use, balancing innovation, authorship and ethical integrity in contemporary art practice. This project will use a mixed-methods design. The first phase will include a survey of 200 second- and third-year fine arts and graphic design students to measure perceived usefulness, ease of use, creative control and ethical awareness, as predictors of behavioural intention. The second phase will comprise ethnographic case studies with 54 students. Following an eight-hour GenAI workshop, the participants will create artworks over three months. Studio observations, portfolio analyses and individual interviews will document how GenAI shapes artistic agency, authorship and sense of authenticity. Artwork originality will be evaluated by three independent professors using validated rubrics. The third phase will involve focus group discussions with 30 students (six groups) to explore shared ethical and social issues such as plagiarism, copyright and bias. The interdisciplinary team will be led by the PI, who has a strong research record in visual arts education, and will comprise an AI art ethics scholar, a practising art-technology artist and two pedagogically experienced instructors. This will offer the ideal expertise for advancing theory, policy and curriculum design for responsible GenAI integration. By integrating perspectives from art, technology and ethics, the project will generate insights that help educators foster innovation while upholding artistic integrity and social responsibility. N/A

titleEnglish: More than One Reality: Photo Pictorial and the Making of Hong Kong Documentary Photography in the 1960s and 1970s
department: Department of Cultural and Creative Arts
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: Scholarship on post-war Hong Kong photography has received less attention compared to cinema. This project addresses that gap by examining Photo Pictorial (攝影畫報 1964–2005), one of Hong Kong’s few dedicated photography magazine during the 1960s and 1970s, as a key site where documentary photography was constituted as both practice and institution. Prior to the 1960s, photography in Hong Kong was limited to the elite colonial circles, where photographers favored pictorialist salon aesthetics and rarely engaged with social realities. In the postwar decades, urban renewal cleared old neighborhoods, while an influx of mainland émigrés fleeing political turmoil created densely populated urban districts. These social and urban transformations, together with the Cultural Revolution, the 1967 leftist riots, reshaped the city’s political climate and compelled photographers to reconsider the potential of the medium. This project examines three aspects. First, the project examines how Photo Pictorial facilitated various documentary approaches to represent local social realities. The magazine’s photographers began turning their attentions to subjects overlooked by salon aesthetics, including overcrowded tenements and everyday street life. Mainland émigré photographers like Meng Minsheng (蒙敏生 1919-2007) and James Chung (鍾文略 1925-2018), as well as locally born photographers like Mak Fung (麥烽 1918-2009) and Yau Leung (邱良1941-1997), brought different perspectives shaped by their varied identities and positions within the city. Second, the study examines the magazine’s role in cultivating a growing community of local amateur photographers. Reflecting the rise of an urban middle class who could afford cameras, Photo Pictorial provided both technical instruction and aesthetic models, helping shape a generation of practitioners who contributed to documenting the changing urban landscape. It became a key platform where émigré photographers, local professionals, and self-taught amateurs could learn, exchange ideas, and make their work visible. Third, the project explores how the magazine worked as bridge between local practice and transnational networks. As the only photography magazine accessible to mainland readers in the 1970s, Photo Pictorial acted as a key conduit between Hong Kong and mainland China, while its circulation across Southeast Asia created the cultural exchanges that crossed Cold War boundaries. By utilizing archival research, oral histories, and visual analysis, this project argues that Photo Pictorial constituted a vision of what documentary photography could be in a city navigating urban transformation and political struggle. N/A

titleEnglish: Unmasking Deception: The Fight Against Fake Research Papers
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: Fake research publications can be deadly, as shown by the many who believed Andrew Wakefield's fraudulent claim that a measles vaccine causes autism, which led to vaccine refusal, measles outbreaks, and numerous deaths worldwide. In the 25 years following the retraction of Wakefield’s work, paper mills have produced over 10,000 fraudulent journal articles, causing a spike in retractions, and eroding trust in research. Our project aims to support the scientific community’s quality assurance efforts in combatting paper mills, which are profit-driven and potentially illegal organizations that generate fake manuscripts resembling genuine research. In the first phase, we will employ the Delphi technique by inviting 30 experts, including 10 representatives each from the following fields (1) journal editors, (2) publishing managers, and (3) academics researching peer review processes or scientific publishing. They will be asked to share their expert opinions on good practices for detecting and preventing paper mills’ fake manuscripts in the peer review process, and the challenges that make it difficult to differentiate fake papers from genuine ones. This collaborative effort aims to design a framework that enhances the current peer review process in detecting and preventing paper mill infiltration (RO1). In the second phase, we will build on routine activity theory to create and test a theoretical model of key identifiers of fake papers (examining attributes include authors, journal, reviewer and editor, and paper content) and model its diffusion pattern (RO2). We will utilise all retracted paper mill articles available in the Retraction Watch Database from inception to present as “fake papers” and match with “genuine papers” published in Q1 journals across various fields to test our model (n=20,450), analysing these data with computational linguistics, advanced statistics, and three machine learning models. Our pilot test (n=300) confirmed significant differences between genuine and fake papers among some attributes. Armed with an evidence-based framework, theory and vast data, we will develop an AI screening tool to detect fake research to aid researchers, editors, reviewers, publishers, and general public. Removing fraudulent studies will enable researchers to base their work more on genuine and reliable research. Journal editors and reviewers can save time and effort by easily removing submissions from paper mills. Publishers can maintain the quality and prestige of their journals, allowing the scientific community to rebuild credibility and disseminate extant knowledge, rather than misinformation to the research community and wider public. N/A

titleEnglish: Playful Urbanism for Active Ageing: Nurturing Older Adult Play and Social Connectedness in Hong Kong’s Public Open Spaces
department: Department of Social Sciences and Policy Studies
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: As populations age, urban loneliness intensifies. This study argues for a paradigm shift, positioning outdoor play as a core dimension of active ageing and embedding it within urban design as a potent strategy against social isolation. In dense cities like Hong Kong, cramped living spaces push older adults' activities into the public realm, creating reliance on public open spaces (POSs). Paradoxically, these community amenities are scarce and often lack age-friendly design for playful engagements, creating disjointed landscapes that perpetuate social isolation. Although play is fundamental to well-being across the life course, age friendly approaches largely prioritise accessibility and functionality over play needs, while research on older adult play remains mostly indoor and programmatic, yielding limited insight into everyday outdoor playful practice or enabling spatial affordances. Consequently, three gaps persist: outdoor play repertoires and cultural variations for older adults are poorly understood; enabling and inhibiting environmental attributes are under-specified; and pathways to translate evidence into urban design and policy remain unclear. Addressing the question "How can POSs achieve a better recreationist–environment fit to nurture older adult play in high-density Asian urban contexts?", this research employs a four-stage mixed-methods design. Building on literature review and pilot study that established a five-dimensional play conceptualisation model, 15 LCSD-managed POSs in Hong Kong are selected as case studies. Stage 1 integrates environmental audits using the Play-Oriented Age-Friendly Environment Audit (PAEA) framework with behavioural observations via SOAPS.hk methodology across multiple time periods and weather conditions. A pre-validated Enhanced YOLO-Pose model provide machine-learning validation of collected behavioural data. GIS spatial statistics identify statistically significant behaviour-environment hotspots and associations. Stage 2 conducts walking interviews with 20 participants across 5 sites exploring subjective experiences and latent play desires, followed by questionnaires administered to current (n=500) and future (n=200) older adult cohorts. Bayesian Network Modelling captures interdependencies between environmental attributes, play behaviours, and social outcomes. Stage 3 develops "Playful Urbanism for Active Ageing" framework through two-round Modified Delphi process with 15-20 interdisciplinary experts, utilising AI-assisted Natural Language Processing for systematic qualitative synthesis and expert consensus achievement. Stage 4 validates framework applicability through retrospective spatial analysis whilst translating findings into evidence-based policy briefs and design guideline toolkits for urban planning authorities and practitioners. By bridging the gap between urban design and gerontology, this study pioneers a playful urbanism framework that reframes age-friendly design, empowering policy and design practitioners to cultivate vibrant, playful ageing in high-density Asian urban contexts. N/A

titleEnglish: Amateur Esports to Enhance Older Adults' Well-being: A Serious Leisure Perspective
department: Academy for Educational Development and Innovation
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: By 2030, over 30% of Hong Kong's population will be aged 65 and above. This rapid ageing trend necessitates innovative ways to enhance older adults' well-being whilst stimulating economic growth. Although Hong Kong's silver economy policy aspires to meet these twin challenges, it has largely overlooked the potential of digital leisure because most existing studies position IT consumption as a compensation for age-related decline, overlooking its potential to augment meaningful experiences that promote well-being in older adults' leisure consumption. Shifting the focus from compensation to augmentation, this project investigates amateur esports—a structured form of digital leisure—as a pathway to enhance older adults' well-being. Unlike other forms of digital leisure (e.g., casual gaming), amateur esports—the professionalized and organized form of video game competition designed for broad participants—offers a structured, multifaceted pathway to well-being, including cognitive stimulation through strategic gameplay, social bonding via team competition, and personal growth through skill development. To achieve a comprehensive understanding, this project takes Serious Leisure Perspective as a conceptual foundation and extends it to identify how valuable experiences develop in amateur esports consumption as a structured, benefit-rich pursuit among older adults. Specifically, to identify serious leisure-based experiences in amateur esports and how its digital elements boost these experiences, hence enhancing older adults' well-being, this project employs a mixed-method design comprising two interconnected studies: Study 1 characterises and classifies serious leisure-based experiences through interpretive qualitative research, utilising semi-structured interview and participant observation. Study 2 develops an affordance–experience model to examine how IT affordances translate these experiences into psychological well-being, employing an online questionnaire-based survey analysed through structural equation modelling (SEM) and fuzzy-set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA). An applicability check will translate findings into actionable policy recommendations. Thus, this project will deliver: A comprehensive framework for understanding older adults' experiences in amateur esports consumption. A validated theoretical model explaining how amateur esports enhances psychological well-being in later life. Evidence-based recommendations for digital leisure policy and platform design to foster silver economy. This project is the first to theorise and categorise serious leisure-based experiences among older adults' amateur esports participation and to empirically validate an affordance–experience model explaining how IT affordances build these experiences to augment psychological well-being, addressing the paucity of research on the mechanism linking digital leisure to older adults' well-being and shaping digital leisure demand for silver economy success. N/A

titleEnglish: Orchestrating Co-Creation Synthesis: Examining How AI Mediates Independent and Collaborative Writing for ESL Learners in Hong Kong Primary Schools
department: Department of Mathematics and Information Technology
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: In Hong Kong’s primary education, creativity and collaboration are prioritized as key generic skills. Yet their cultivation within English language learning remains limited. Despite English writing being a key learning area, decades of exam-oriented practices have stifled imagination, discouraged peer interaction, and left 36% of pupils below basic proficiency. Particularly, few instructional models leverage collaborative writing to cultivate creativity in ESL. In response to this challenge, this 20-month study will design, implement, and evaluate CWrite, an AI-mediated collaborative writing platform. Rather than replacing teachers or peer interaction, CWrite positions AI as a mediator of human–human co-construction, scaffolding learners through reflective thinking, deliberative dialogue, and joint text creation. The study integrates independent and collaborative writing within a structured instructional framework, Initiate–Pair–Collaborate–Apply (IPCA). Students first compose individual drafts to articulate ideas and identify linguistic gaps. They then pair up and upload their texts to CWrite, where the AI generates open-ended, text-contingent prompts to stimulate peer discussion and facilitate creative synthesis. Through guided collaboration, learners merge their most effective ideas into a jointly authored text and later apply refined skills in an individual task. A quasi-experimental design will be implemented with 412 Primary 5 students from 18 classes across six Hong Kong schools over two semesters. Classes will be randomly assigned to either a traditional teacher-led writing condition or an experimental group integrating CWrite-supported collaboration. Data include: (1) scores of students’ pre-, during- and post-intervention writing; (2) learners empowerment from pre- and post-survey responses (meaningfulness, competence, choice, impact); (3) learning behavioral logs on CWrite and discourse data from pair discussions which are coded for deliberative dialogue (questioning, justification, negotiation among peers and AI) and socially shared regulation (planning, monitoring, evaluation); and (4) semi-structured interviews with eight teachers and 40 students about retrospective insights. Data analyses will employ difference-in-differences multilevel structural equation modeling to assess intervention effects on writing and empowerment. To trace how human–AI interactions shape collaboration, statistical discourse analysis, epistemic network analysis, and process mining will be used to model dialogue dynamics and behavioral pathways. The relationship between process and outcome will be examined by testing whether deliberation network strength and its alignment with behavioral traces predict higher-quality collaborative writing. Thematic analysis of interviews will triangulate the quantitative findings. By examining human-AI collaboration, empowerment, and regulation, this project lays the groundwork for human-centered principles in scalable, AI-assisted ESL pedagogy. N/A

titleEnglish: Seeing and Feeling Representation: How Gender Cues in Bureaucracy Shape Citizen Attention, Emotions, and Trust
department: Department of Social Sciences and Policy Studies
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: Public trust in government often depends not only on what bureaucrats do, but also on who they are and how they appear to citizens. Research on representative bureaucracy suggests that having officials who reflect the population’s diversity can shape how people feel about public institutions in terms of legitimacy, performance, and trustworthiness, even beyond tangible policy outcomes. Yet, the psychological and emotional processes behind these “symbolic effects” remain unclear, especially when it comes to gender representation. Why do female officials sometimes inspire trust and fairness, but at other times trigger doubts about competence or authority? In answering this question, the proposed project will investigate how citizens perceive and react to gender cues in different bureaucratic settings, with a focus on China but offering broader relevance for other societies. Drawing upon recent advances in cognitive science, it employs a mixed-methods research design, combining two laboratory experiments and follow-up interviews. The first experiment examines organizational-level effects by presenting participants with group images of bureaucratic teams varying in gender composition and policy domain. The second experiment focuses on individual-level service encounters, manipulating bureaucrat gender, rank, and interactive scenarios to test how these cues affect perceptions of competence, empathy, and institutional legitimacy. In both experiments, in addition to routine post-treatment questionnaires that requires participants to self-report perceptions, eye-tracking and emotion recognition technology will be employed to measure where people focus their attention and how they feel when interacting with male versus female officials in different roles and policy areas. Follow-up interviews will then help uncover the cultural meanings and stereotypes that explain these reactions, provide qualitative insights into participants’ interpretations, and offer a richer understanding of the mechanisms driving trust or skepticism. The project’s contribution will be threefold. First, it advances the representative bureaucracy theory by producing a micro-founded model of symbolic representation and clarifying how gender cues interact with contextual factors such as bureaucratic hierarchy and policy domain to shape citizen attention, emotions, and trust. Second, it will deliver an innovative, validated methodological framework that integrates eye-tracking, affective analytics, and interviews, offering a transferable model for future research in behavioral public administration. Third, it will inform evidence-based strategies for public organizations, including communication guidelines, personnel deployment practices, and training modules to manage identity cues effectively. By translating behavioral evidence into actionable insights, the project will help public managers design more inclusive and trustworthy governance practices. N/A

titleEnglish: Effects of Physical Activity Interventions on Circadian Rest–Activity Rhythms and Executive Functioning in Children with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder: A Randomized Controlled Trial with Mediation Analysis
department: Department of Health and Physical Education
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a widespread neuropsychiatric disorder in children, characterized by persistent inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity inappropriate for the child’s age. Common comorbidities include sleep disturbances, circadian disruptions, behavioural problems and impairments in executive functions (EFs). There has been emerging evidence showing the beneficial effects of physical activity (PA) intervention in improving EFs (e.g., working memory, cognitive flexibility, and inhibitory control) and sleep quality in children with ADHD, but most existing research relies on self-reported or parent-reported measures, without incorporating objective assessments, including actigraphy-measured sleep parameters, circadian rest-activity rhythms, and physiological biomarkers of dim-light melatonin onset (DLMO). Given the crucial role of circadian rest-activity rhythms in sleep regulation and their association with EFs, which may serve as a key mechanism underlying the beneficial effects of PA engagement. Despite this theoretical basis, the potential mediating role of PA-induced alterations in circadian rhythms remains unexplored in this context. Our pilot 12-week intervention with 63 children with ADHD, following our previous protocol, showed weak correlations between changes in actigraphy-measured circadian rest-activity rhythms (e.g., relative amplitude) and changes in inhibitory control after intervention. These findings suggest that circadian rest–activity rhythms may causally contribute to executive dysfunctions in children with ADHD. The aim of the proposed project is to examine the effects of PA intervention on circadian rest–activity rhythms and EFs and to determine whether changing circadian rest–activity rhythms through PA can improve EFs, improve sleep quality, and alleviate behavioural problems. Based on power calculations, 150 children with ADHD aged 6-12 years will be randomly assigned to either a 12-week PA intervention (three sessions per week, 60 minutes per session) or a waitlist control group. Assessments will be conducted at four time points: baseline (T1), immediately after the intervention (T2, primary endpoint), at 3-month post-treatment follow-up (T3), and at 6-month post-treatment follow-up (T4). After T3, the waitlist control will receive the same PA intervention. Primary outcomes include actigraphy-measured circadian rest–activity rhythms and neurocognitive assessments of the three core EFs. Secondary outcomes will include behavioural problems, mental health, subjective and objective sleep and circadian measures (e.g., self-report and parental-report questionnaires, and DLMO). The results will deepen our understanding of how PA influences EFs via behavioural manifestation of circadian rhythms. These insights will help educators, psychologists, and policymakers develop early intervention strategies and school-based health practices, ultimately improving cognitive and behavioural outcomes for children with ADHD. N/A

titleEnglish: Understanding the Role of Preparatory Brain States in Processing Speed and Executive Function with a Network-based EEG Connectivity Approach
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: Why does the same person respond in 200ms on one trial but 600ms on the next? Traditional cognitive research averages hundreds of trials, treating this threefold variation as noise. We propose this variability reflects meaningful fluctuations in how the brain prepares before information appears—and these preparatory states fundamentally determine processing speed. Processing speed predicts outcomes ranging from academic achievement to driving safety, yet mechanisms generating speed differences remain unclear. Current theories focus on what happens after a stimulus appears, assuming faster processors have more efficient neural transmission or decision-making. This overlooks a critical question: what if speed is determined before processing begins? Our pilot analysis of public EEG datasets provides compelling initial evidence. We found that prestimulus connectivity between the Salience Network (detecting important information) and Dorsal Attention Network (directing focus) correlates with reaction time (r=0.47, p<.001). This suggests that when these networks communicate effectively before stimulus onset, rapid responses follow. This proof-of-concept study tests whether processing speed differences—both between and within individuals—arise from preparatory brain states rather than processing efficiency. Using high-density EEG, we will record 80 participants performing three validated tasks that measure sustained attention (GradCPT), processing speed (Arrow Task), and inhibitory control (Stop-Signal Task). This streamlined single-session design generates six performance conditions while minimising participant burden. Crucially, we employ trial-by-trial analysis rather than averaging. For each response, we examine brain activity during preparation (500 ms before stimulus), perception (0-200ms after stimulus), and decision-making (300-500ms after stimulus). We predict that optimal preparatory states—characterised by alpha oscillations in the Salience Network that enhance readiness—cascade through processing stages, enabling fast responses. Poor preparation necessitates compensatory executive control, producing slow responses. Our approach reveals why processing speed and executive function appear correlated in traditional studies: both are impaired when attention fluctuates. By classifying each trial's attention state based on neural markers, we can test whether these represent distinct capacities when preparation is optimal. Post-session questionnaires (ASRS, CFQ, MAAS) will validate whether neural instability predicts real-world attention difficulties. If validated, this framework transforms understanding from "some people are slow processors" to "suboptimal brain preparation causes slow responses." Rather than training people to process faster—attempting to accelerate fixed hardware—interventions could optimise preparatory control. This paradigm shift has implications for intervention (neurofeedback / brain stimulation), clinical assessment (identifying specific preparatory deficits), and lifespan cognitive health (maintaining preparation abilities). N/A

titleEnglish: Cultivating Advocate Identities of Ethnic Minority Teachers in Hong Kong: Emotional and Ideological Border-Crossings
institution: The Education University of Hong Kong
abstract: This project investigates how ethnic minority (EM) teachers in Hong Kong public schools construct their professional and advocate identities as they navigate emotionally and ideologically charged school environments. Positioned at the intersection of racial marginalization, linguistic stratification, and institutional gatekeeping, these teachers often negotiate complex tensions between community-rooted commitments and the dominant norms of Han-centric, monolingual school cultures. Their work involves not only pedagogical engagement but also emotional labor and ideological border-crossing, making them critical yet underrecognized agents of equity. While existing research has illuminated the educational challenges faced by EM students, little is known about how EM teachers experience and respond to these same structures from within. Frequently framed as cultural and linguistic intermediaries, EM teachers often support student inclusion while facing systemic exclusion themselves. There remains a gap in understanding how they cultivate agency, engage in resistance, and form professional identities grounded in justice, solidarity, and multilingual advocacy. This 24-month study adopts a multi-method design to examine the affective and ideological contours of identity construction among approximately 20 in-service EM teachers. Data will be collected across three phases through go-along interviews, classroom observations, pre- and post-intervention surveys with teachers, students, and parents, visually mediated identity texts, and focus group discussions. This design will generate a multilayered portrait of how EM teachers make sense of their roles, emotions, and positioning within racially and linguistically hierarchical schools. The study culminates in a six-part participatory professional development (PD) series that centers on emotional reflexivity, ideological clarity, and community-based advocacy. Rather than offering top-down training, the workshops will serve as dialogic and creative spaces for teachers to explore how race, language, power, and emotion shape their professional practices and ethical stances. Teachers will collaboratively generate strategies for sustaining emotional well-being and advancing equity within their schools and beyond. By foregrounding the emotional and ideological labor of EM teachers, this project contributes to critical scholarship on language teacher identity, raciolinguistic equity, and anti-deficit professional development. Findings will inform inclusive policy reforms and teacher education in Hong Kong, while offering insights into cultivating advocacy-oriented teacher identities in other multilingual and racially stratified contexts.
